topics.title: Core of the Embedded System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Core of the Embedded System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded System (KTU 2024 Definition):** An **Embedded System** is a specialized, purpose-built computing subsystem that is an integral part of a larger mechanical, electrical, or electronic system. It is a combination of **hardware**, **software** (firmware/application code), **firmware**, and **real-time operating constraints**, designed to perform a dedicated function under strict constraints of **power**, **cost**, **size**, **reliability**, and **real-time response**.

The term *"embedded"* itself signifies that the computing element is **embedded (deeply integrated)** within the host device, and is typically **invisible to the end user** as a "computer." The user interacts with the *system*, not the *computer inside it*.

### Formal Components of a KTU-Defined Embedded System
1. **Hardware Platform** — Processor (µC/µP/DSP/ASIC/SoC), Memory, I/O peripherals, Power supply, Clock.
2. **Software/Firmware Layer** — Application code, device drivers, RTOS kernel (if any), middleware.
3. **Real-Time Operating Constraints** — Deterministic response to external events (sensors/user inputs) within a bounded time.
4. **Communication Interfaces** — Wired (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, Ethernet) or Wireless (BLE, Wi-Fi, LoRa).
5. **User Interface (Optional)** — LEDs, LCDs, touchscreens, buzzers, buttons.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialized Kitchen Chef" 🧑‍🍳

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition for Students:** Imagine a large hotel with **one master chef (General-purpose computer)** and **ten small specialized kitchens (embedded systems)** — one in the bakery, one in the sushi bar, one in the ice-cream parlor, one in the coffee kiosk, etc.
>
> - The **general-purpose computer** is your laptop/PC — it can do *anything*: gaming, browsing, programming, video editing.
> - Each **embedded system** is like the *sushi chef* who *only* makes sushi — fast, perfect, economical, predictable, and never tries to make pizza.
>
> The sushi chef (embedded system) is **optimized for one task**, uses **less energy**, is **cheaper to hire**, **smaller in workspace**, and **predictable in output timing** (real-time). That is precisely what makes a washing machine controller, a car's ABS module, or a pacemaker "embedded" rather than "general-purpose."

---

## 1.3 Distinguishing Embedded System from General-Purpose Computer

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (High-Frequency KTU Question):** A general-purpose computer runs *user-installed applications of varied intent*. An embedded system is **pre-programmed at manufacture** to perform a *single* (or a small fixed set of) function(s) for the lifetime of the host product.

| Parameter | General-Purpose Computer | Embedded System |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Multi-purpose | **Single / Dedicated function** |
| **User Programming** | User installs software | Firmware fixed at factory |
| **OS** | Full OS (Windows/Linux/macOS) | **Bare-metal / RTOS / No OS** |
| **Power Consumption** | High (Watts to hundreds of Watts) | **Low (µW to few Watts)** |
| **Cost** | Hundreds to thousands of ₹ | **Tens to a few hundred ₹** |
| **Real-Time** | Soft (best-effort) | **Hard / Firm / Soft real-time** |
| **User Awareness** | Aware (it's the product) | **Hidden (it's part of the product)** |
| **Upgradeability** | High (software updatable) | **Rare / Field-updatable only** |
| **Examples** | Laptop, Desktop, Server | Washing machine, ECU, Pacemaker, IoT sensor |

---

## 1.4 The Five Core Elements of an Embedded System (KTU Module 1 Anchor)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **"Core of the Embedded System"** — the literal heart of the syllabus — is the **five-block functional core** that *every* embedded system is constructed from, irrespective of complexity:

### Core Element 1 — **The Processor / Controller (The Brain) 🧠**
The heart of the system. It executes the instructions, performs arithmetic/logic operations, controls data flow, and makes decisions.
- **Variants:** Microprocessor (µP), Microcontroller (µC), Digital Signal Processor (DSP), Application-Specific Integrated Circuit (ASIC), Application-Specific Instruction-set Processor (ASIP), System-on-Chip (SoC), Field-Programmable Gate Array (FPGA).
- **Key Specification:** *Clock Frequency (f<sub>CLK</sub>)*, *Architecture (CISC/RISC)*, *Word length (8/16/32/64-bit)*.

### Core Element 2 — **Memory (The Notepads) 📓**
Stores the **program code** and **runtime data**. Divided into two fundamental kinds:
- **Program Memory (ROM Family):** ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, **Flash** (most common in modern embedded).
- **Data Memory (RAM Family):** Static RAM (SRAM), Dynamic RAM (DRAM — rare in MCUs), Cache (L1/L2 in SoCs).

### Core Element 3 — **Input/Output Interfaces & Peripherals (The Senses & Limbs) 👁️🖐️**
- **Inputs:** Sensors, keypads, switches, ADC channels, communication ports.
- **Outputs:** LEDs, LCDs, motors, relays, DAC channels, communication ports.
- **On-chip peripherals common in microcontrollers:** Timers, Watchdog Timer, PWM, ADC, DAC, UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, GPIO.

### Core Element 4 — **Clock & Reset Circuitry (The Heartbeat) ⏱️**
- Provides the **synchronized timing reference** for the processor, peripherals, and bus fabric.
- Sources: Crystal Oscillator, Ceramic Resonator, Internal RC Oscillator, PLL.
- **Reset circuitry** ensures deterministic startup; includes Power-on Reset (POR), Brown-out Reset (BOR).

### Core Element 5 — **Power Supply & Management (The Life Support) 🔋**
- Converts mains/battery voltage to regulated DC levels (3.3 V, 5 V, 1.8 V, 1.2 V).
- Subsystems: LDO regulators, DC-DC buck/boost converters, battery management, sleep modes.

> [!NOTE]
> **A useful engineering rule:** When designing *any* embedded system, you **must** answer the question — *"Which Processor, with what Memory, talking to which I/O, clocked at what rate, and powered from where?"* — before you write a single line of code.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Foundational Processor Architectures

### 2.1.1 Von Neumann Architecture (Princeton Architecture)

- **Core Idea:** A **single shared bus** carries **both** instructions *and* data from a **single, unified memory**.
- **Bottleneck:** Known as the **Von Neumann Bottleneck** — the CPU cannot fetch an instruction and a data word in the *same* cycle because they share one bus.
- **Cost:** Cheaper (one memory, one bus).
- **Used in:** Most general-purpose CPUs (x86), and **some low-end MCUs** (e.g., 8051 family).

**Operational Steps (per instruction cycle):**
1. **Fetch (F):** Read instruction from memory over the bus.
2. **Decode (D):** Decode the opcode.
3. **Execute (E):** Read operands (over the *same* bus) and run ALU operation.
4. **Store (S):** Write result back to memory (over the *same* bus).

### 2.1.2 Harvard Architecture

- **Core Idea:** **Physically separate memories and separate buses** for *instructions* and *data*. The CPU can therefore **fetch an instruction and read/write a data operand simultaneously**.
- **Throughput:** Roughly **double** the bus bandwidth of Von Neumann at the same clock.
- **Cost:** More silicon area (two memories, two bus sets).
- **Used in:** Most modern DSPs, AVR (ATmega), PIC, ARM Cortex-M based MCUs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Modified Harvard (used in ARM Cortex-M, modern x86):** Physically Harvard (separate L1 caches/buses), but the **programming model** still presents a unified address space. Best of both worlds.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Embedded Processors

| Processor Class | Definition | Typical Use | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Microprocessor (µP)** | CPU only — needs *external* RAM, ROM, peripherals | Desktop, high-end embedded | Intel x86, ARM Cortex-A |
| **Microcontroller (µC)** | CPU + RAM + ROM + peripherals *on one chip* | Washing machine, toys, IoT | 8051, ATmega328, STM32, PIC16F |
| **DSP** | Optimized for **continuous data-stream arithmetic** (MAC units) | Audio, video, radar, motor control | TMS320C6000, SHARC |
| **ASIC** | Hardwired for **one specific function** — cannot be changed | Crypto miners, network switches | Custom silicon |
| **ASIP** | Custom instruction set tuned for a domain | Baseband processors | Tensilica Xtensa |
| **SoC** | *Entire system* (CPU + GPU + RAM controller + I/O + radios) on one die | Smartphones, IoT gateways, smartwatches | Qualcomm Snapdragon, Nordic nRF52 |
| **FPGA** | Reconfigurable logic fabric | Prototyping, parallel DSP, networking | Xilinx Spartan, Intel Cyclone |
| **GPU / VPU** | Massive data-parallel compute | AI/ML inference, vision | NVIDIA Jetson, ARM Mali |

---

## 2.3 Core Performance & Power Formula Sheet (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorize the following. KTU frequently tests these in *Part A* and embeds them inside *Part B* design/numerical questions.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Clock Period** | $T_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$ | seconds (s) | Base time unit of processor |
| 2 | **CPU Cycles per Instruction (CPI)** | $\text{CPI} = \dfrac{\text{Total Cycles}}{\text{Instruction Count}}$ | cycles/instr | Architectural efficiency |
| 3 | **Execution Time** | $T_{exec} = \dfrac{N_{instr} \times \text{CPI}}{f_{CLK}}$ | seconds | How fast a program runs |
| 4 | **MIPS (Million Instructions / s)** | $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$ | MIPS | Marketing/benchmark metric |
| 5 | **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{1}{T_{exec}}$ | tasks/s | Real-time schedulability |
| 6 | **Dynamic Power** | $P_{dyn} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f_{CLK}$ | Watts (W) | Battery-life estimation |
| 7 | **Static Power** | $P_{static} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{leak}$ | Watts (W) | Idle / sleep-mode design |
| 8 | **Energy per Instruction** | $E_{instr} = P \cdot T_{exec} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^{2}$ | Joules (J) | Energy-aware computing |
| 9 | **Amdahl's Law (Speedup)** | $S = \dfrac{1}{(1 - f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$ | dimensionless | Parallelism gain limit |
| 10 | **Memory Latency** | $t_{mem} = t_{access} + \dfrac{\text{Size}}{\text{BusWidth}} \cdot T_{CLK}$ | seconds | Cache / DMA design |
| 11 | **Deadline (RTOS)** | $T_{deadline} \ge T_{exec} + T_{interrupt\ latency}$ | seconds | Hard real-time guarantee |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols used:** $N_{instr}$ = number of instructions in the program, $\alpha$ = switching activity factor ($0 \le \alpha \le 1$), $C$ = total switched capacitance, $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage, $f$ = fraction of program parallelized in Amdahl's law, $N$ = number of parallel units.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CPI → MIPS → Execution Time** chain is used daily by firmware architects to **estimate battery life** of a wearable.
- The **$V_{DD}^{2}$** term in dynamic power is why modern MCUs aggressively *undervolt* and *clock-gate* unused peripherals — a **10% voltage drop gives ~19% power saving** with negligible speed loss.
- **Amdahl's Law** is the canonical answer to "Why doesn't adding 100 CPU cores give 100× speedup?" — a favorite KTU viva question.
- The **Von Neumann bottleneck** is precisely why modern embedded SoCs (e.g., STM32H7) use **multi-layer AHB/AXI bus matrices** to decouple instruction and data streams — a *Modified Harvard* on-chip.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Core Performance Equation

**Claim:** The time taken to execute a program of $N$ instructions on a processor with average CPI $= C$ and clock frequency $f_{CLK}$ is

$$T_{exec} = \dfrac{N \cdot C}{f_{CLK}}$$

### Derivation (Exhaustive Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Define one clock cycle.**
The clock generator emits a square wave of frequency $f_{CLK}$ (in Hz). One complete high-low period is *one cycle*.

By definition of frequency:

$$T_{CLK} = \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$$

This is the **time for one CPU cycle** (seconds per cycle).

**Step 2 — Define instruction count.**
Let the *compiled* program contain $N$ machine-language instructions to be executed. $N$ is dimensionless (just a count of instructions).

**Step 3 — Define average CPI.**
Different instructions take different numbers of cycles. The **average** cycles per instruction across the whole program is:

$$C = \dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (N_i \cdot C_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} N_i}$$

where there are $n$ instruction *classes*, each class $i$ has $N_i$ instances and consumes $C_i$ cycles per instance. The denominator is just $N$, the total instruction count.

**Step 4 — Compute total cycles.**
Each of the $N$ instructions consumes, *on average*, $C$ cycles. Therefore total CPU cycles used:

$$\text{Total Cycles} = N \cdot C$$

**Step 5 — Convert cycles to seconds.**
Multiply total cycles by the seconds-per-cycle:

$$T_{exec} = \text{Total Cycles} \cdot T_{CLK} = N \cdot C \cdot \dfrac{1}{f_{CLK}}$$

Rearranging:

$$T_{exec} = \dfrac{N \cdot C}{f_{CLK}}$$

**Q.E.D. — this is the canonical KTU execution-time equation.**

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 3-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A 16-bit embedded processor runs at $f_{CLK} = 50\,\text{MHz}$. A control loop has $N = 20{,}000$ instructions with an *average* CPI $= 1.5$. Find (i) $T_{exec}$ per loop, (ii) maximum loop rate, (iii) MIPS rating.

**Step (i) — Compute $T_{exec}$.**

$$T_{exec} = \dfrac{N \cdot C}{f_{CLK}} = \dfrac{20{,}000 \times 1.5}{50 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{exec} = \dfrac{30{,}000}{50 \times 10^{6}} = 6 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} = 600\,\mu\text{s}$$

**Step (ii) — Maximum loop rate.**

$$f_{loop} = \dfrac{1}{T_{exec}} = \dfrac{1}{600 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1666.67\,\text{Hz} \approx 1.67\,\text{kHz}$$

**Step (iii) — MIPS rating.**

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{C \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{50 \times 10^{6}}{1.5 \times 10^{6}} = 33.33\,\text{MIPS}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU often gives such numbers with units in **kHz**, **ns**, or **µs** — always normalize to *seconds* and *Hz* before plugging into formulas.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Core Processor Choices

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Core" Comparison Table — A KTU-favourite (frequently asked as a 7-mark design-comparison question in Part B).**

| Criterion | 8051 (8-bit) | PIC16F (8-bit) | ATmega328 (8-bit) | ARM Cortex-M0 (32-bit) | ARM Cortex-M4 (32-bit + DSP) | DSP (e.g., TMS320) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Architecture** | Von Neumann | Harvard | Harvard | Harvard (M-Harvard) | Modified Harvard | Harvard |
| **Bus Width** | 8-bit | 8-bit | 8-bit | 32-bit | 32-bit | 16/32-bit |
| **Typical f<sub>CLK</sub>** | 12–24 MHz | 4–20 MHz | 1–20 MHz | up to 50 MHz | up to 180 MHz | 100–1000 MHz |
| **On-chip Flash** | 4–64 KB | 1–28 KB | 32 KB | 16–256 KB | 64 KB – 2 MB | External/None |
| **On-chip RAM** | 128 B – 1 KB | 128–2 KB | 2 KB | 4–32 KB | 32–256 KB + CCM | External |
| **Typical CPI** | 12 (×12) | 4 (×4) | 1–2 | 1–2 | 1–2 + MAC | 1 + MAC |
| **Power (active)** | ~10 mA | ~5 mA | ~10 mA | ~3 mA | ~10 mA | 50–500 mA |
| **Cost (₹)** | ₹15–50 | ₹20–60 | ₹40–100 | ₹30–150 | ₹80–400 | ₹200+ |
| **Best For** | Legacy teaching, toys | Hobby, low-cost | Arduino-class IoT | Modern IoT, wearables | Motor control, audio | Audio/Video/Comms |
| **DSP capability** | None | None | None | None | Yes (MAC, FPU) | Excellent |

> **Mnemonic for exam:** *"Old Von has a clock of 12, Modern Harvard runs at 1–2."* — captures the CPI difference that drives the MIPS gap.

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Dynamic Power Reduction by Voltage Scaling

**Claim:** Reducing $V_{DD}$ from $V_1$ to $V_2$ reduces dynamic power by the ratio

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{2}$$

### Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the original dynamic power.**

$$P_1 = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_1^{2} \cdot f$$

**Step 2 — Write the new dynamic power after voltage scaling.**

$$P_2 = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_2^{2} \cdot f$$

**Step 3 — Take the ratio, observing that $\alpha$, $C$, and $f$ are unchanged.**

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{\alpha \cdot C \cdot V_2^{2} \cdot f}{\alpha \cdot C \cdot V_1^{2} \cdot f}$$

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{V_2^{2}}{V_1^{2}} = \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{2}$$

**Q.E.D.**

**Engineering Insight:** A 50% voltage cut gives $\left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{2} = 0.25$ → **75% power saving** (with proportional speed loss, which is the *Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling (DVFS)* trade-off central to modern RTOS power management).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — The Core of an Embedded System

> [!NOTE]
> The diagram below is the *master block diagram* you should be able to redraw from memory in any KTU exam. It shows the *five core elements* from §1.4 and their interconnections.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE["CORE OF THE EMBEDDED SYSTEM"]
        CPU["PROCESSOR OR CONTROLLER\nCPU, ALU, Registers, Program Counter"]
        MEM["MEMORY\nROM, Flash, RAM, Cache"]
        IO["INPUT OUTPUT PERIPHERALS\nGPIO, ADC, DAC, UART, SPI, I2C, PWM, Timers"]
        CLK["CLOCK AND RESET\nCrystal Oscillator, PLL, POR, BOR"]
        PWR["POWER SUPPLY AND MANAGEMENT\nLDO, DC DC Converter, Battery, Sleep Modes"]
    end

    EXT["EXTERNAL WORLD\nSensors, Actuators, User Buttons, Display, Network"]

    PWR -->|Vdd, Vcore| CPU
    PWR -->|Vdd| MEM
    PWR -->|Vdd| IO
    CLK -->|fCLK Clock| CPU
    CLK -->|Reset| CPU
    CPU <-->|Address Bus, Data Bus, Control Bus| MEM
    CPU <-->|Peripheral Bus APB AHB| IO
    IO <--> EXT
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Von Neumann vs Harvard Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VN["VON NEUMANN ARCHITECTURE"]
        CPU1["CPU"]:::core
        BUS1["SINGLE SHARED BUS"]:::bus
        MEM1["UNIFIED MEMORY\nInstructions AND Data"]:::mem
        CPU1 <--> BUS1
        BUS1 <--> MEM1
    end

    subgraph HV["HARVARD ARCHITECTURE"]
        CPU2["CPU"]:::core
        IBUS["INSTRUCTION BUS"]:::bus
        DBUS["DATA BUS"]:::bus
        IMEM["INSTRUCTION MEMORY\nROM, Flash"]:::mem
        DMEM["DATA MEMORY\nRAM"]:::mem
        CPU2 <--> IBUS
        CPU2 <--> DBUS
        IBUS <--> IMEM
        DBUS <--> DMEM
    end

    classDef core fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#118ab2,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Typical Microcontroller Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MCU["TYPICAL MICROCONTROLLER CHIP"]
        CORE_M["CPU CORE\nALU + Register File + PC"]:::core
        FLASH["FLASH PROGRAM MEMORY\nNon volatile Code Storage"]:::mem
        SRAM["SRAM DATA MEMORY\nRuntime Variables, Stack"]:::mem
        EEPROM["EEPROM\nNon volatile User Data"]:::mem
        TIM["TIMERS AND PWM\n8 bit, 16 bit, Watchdog"]:::peri
        ADC["ADC AND DAC\nAnalog IO Channels"]:::peri
        COM["COMMUNICATION CONTROLLERS\nUART, SPI, I2C, CAN, USB"]:::peri
        GPIO["GPIO PORTS\nGeneral Purpose IO Pins"]:::peri
        CLK_M["OSCILLATOR AND PLL\nSystem Clock Generator"]:::clk
        RST_M["RESET AND INTERRUPT CONTROLLER\nPOR, BOR, NVIC"]:::clk
        BUS_M["INTERNAL BUS MATRIX\nAHB, APB Fabric"]:::bus
    end

    EXT_M["EXTERNAL CIRCUITRY\nSensors, Motors, LED, Buttons, USB Connector"]

    BUS_M --- CORE_M
    BUS_M --- FLASH
    BUS_M --- SRAM
    BUS_M --- EEPROM
    BUS_M --- TIM
    BUS_M --- ADC
    BUS_M --- COM
    BUS_M --- GPIO
    CLK_M --> CORE_M
    RST_M --> CORE_M
    GPIO <--> EXT_M
    COM <--> EXT_M
    ADC <--> EXT_M
    TIM <--> EXT_M

    classDef core fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef mem fill:#118ab2,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef peri fill:#ef476f,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef clk fill:#06d6a0,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef bus fill:#f4a261,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

---

## 4.4 Functional Architecture Flow — From Sensor to Actuator (Signal Chain)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **canonical "embedded signal-flow"** that you should be able to draw in *any* design question (washing machine, pacemaker, IoT sensor node).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S["PHYSICAL PHENOMENON\nTemperature, Pressure, Motion, Light"]:::world
    SENS["SENSOR\nTransducer, Signal Conditioning"]:::peri
    ADC["ADC\nAnalog to Digital Converter"]:::peri
    PROC["PROCESSOR OR MCU\nRuns Firmware Algorithm"]:::core
    DEC["DECISION LOGIC\nThreshold, PID, ML Inference"]:::core
    DAC["DAC OR PWM\nDigital to Analog / Pulse Width"]:::peri
    ACT["ACTUATOR\nMotor, Heater, Valve, LED, Buzzer"]:::world

    S --> SENS --> ADC --> PROC --> DEC --> DAC --> ACT
```

**Reading the diagram:** The *physical world* (left) is sensed, converted to digital, processed by the embedded firmware, a *decision* is taken, a *control signal* is generated, and the *physical world* is actuated (right). The loop runs continuously at a rate determined by the **real-time deadline**.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Block Diagram — SoC (System on Chip) vs Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SOC["SYSTEM ON CHIP SoC"]
        CPU_S["APPLICATION CPU Cortex A series"]:::core
        GPU_S["GPU AND VPU\nGraphics, Video, AI"]:::core
        MCPU_S["MICROCONTROLLER CORE Cortex M series"]:::core
        RADIO["WIRELESS RADIOS\nBLE, Wi Fi, LTE, GPS"]:::peri
        MEMC["MEMORY CONTROLLER\nDDR, Flash, eMMC"]:::mem
        DSP_S["DSP AND NPU\nAudio, ML Inference"]:::peri
        IO_S["HIGH SPEED IO\nUSB, PCIe, Ethernet, HDMI"]:::peri
        SEC["SECURITY ENGINE\nCrypto, Secure Boot, TrustZone"]:::peri
        BUSA["SYSTEM BUS NoC, AXI"]:::bus
    end

    subgraph MCU["MICROCONTROLLER"]
        CPU_M["CPU CORE"]:::core
        FLM["FLASH AND SRAM"]:::mem
        PERIM["ON CHIP PERIPHERALS\nUART, SPI, ADC, Timers, GPIO"]:::peri
        BUSM["AHB APB BUS"]:::bus
    end

    BUSA --- CPU_S
    BUSA --- GPU_S
    BUSA --- MCPU_S
    BUSA --- RADIO
    BUSA --- MEMC
    BUSA --- DSP_S
    BUSA --- IO_S
    BUSA --- SEC

    BUSM --- CPU_M
    BUSM --- FLM
    BUSM --- PERIM

    classDef core fill:#ffd166,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef peri fill:#ef476f,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef mem fill:#118ab2,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef bus fill:#f4a261,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (KTU 2024 PECPT861):**
> - **Part A:** 3 marks each (short answer, no choice). Five questions per module out of which **two from Module 1**.
> - **Part B:** 14 marks each, with **internal choice** (Q1 or Q2, Q3 or Q4 …). One full 14-mark question from Module 1 in a typical end-semester paper.
> - Bloom's Levels: *Remember* → *Understand* → *Apply* → *Analyze* → *Evaluate*.

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] — 3 Marks**
**Define an embedded system. List any four characteristics that distinguish it from a general-purpose computer.**

**Model Answer:**

An **embedded system** is a computing subsystem that is *integrated into a larger host device* and is designed to perform a **single, dedicated function** under constraints of power, cost, size, and real-time response. It combines application-specific hardware and firmware.

**Four distinguishing characteristics:**

1. **Single-Purpose Functionality:** Performs one (or a few fixed) task(s) for the product's lifetime, unlike a general-purpose computer that runs varied user-installed applications.
2. **Tight Resource Constraints:** Limited **CPU speed** (kHz–hundreds of MHz), **RAM** (Bytes to MB), **ROM/Flash** (KB to MB), and **power** (µW–W). A general-purpose computer has GB RAM, multi-GHz CPUs, tens of watts to kilowatts.
3. **Real-Time Determinism:** Must respond to external events within a **bounded deadline** (hard/firm/soft real-time). A PC is best-effort; a missed deadline in an ABS brake or pacemaker is fatal.
4. **High Reliability and Harsh-Environment Operation:** Must run for years without failure in environments with temperature swings, vibration, EMI — conditions where a PC would fail.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Insight:** Vague definitions lose 1 mark. KTU expects the words *"dedicated function,"* *"tight constraints,"* and *"real-time"* explicitly.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] — 3 Marks**
**Differentiate between Von Neumann and Harvard architectures with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|---|---|---|
| **Memory Model** | **Single unified memory** holds both instructions and data | **Physically separate** instruction and data memories |
| **Bus** | One shared bus for instruction fetch and data read/write | Two **separate buses** (instruction bus + data bus) |
| **Cycle** | Cannot fetch instruction and data *in the same* cycle — the **Von Neumann Bottleneck** | Can fetch instruction and read/write data *simultaneously* |
| **Throughput** | Lower for a given clock | Higher — roughly double bus bandwidth |
| **Silicon Cost** | Lower (one memory, one bus) | Higher (two memories, two buses) |
| **Example** | 8051 family, x86 desktop CPUs | AVR ATmega, PIC, ARM Cortex-M, all DSPs |

> [!NOTE]
> A modern compromise, the **Modified Harvard architecture** (used in ARM Cortex-M, modern x86) uses *physically separate L1 caches and buses*, but presents a *unified address space* to the programmer.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — With Internal Choice

---

### **Question 3 (A) — 14 Marks — [KTU University Exam – 2024 ESE Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **five core elements of an embedded system** with a neat block diagram. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 based embedded controller operates at a clock frequency of $72\,\text{MHz}$. A real-time control task consists of $N = 45{,}000$ instructions with an average CPI of $1.25$. Calculate:
   (i) The execution time $T_{exec}$ of one task instance.
   (ii) The maximum achievable task rate.
   (iii) The MIPS rating of the controller.
   (iv) The total energy consumed by the processor in one execution, given that the average active current draw is $I = 12\,\text{mA}$ at $V_{DD} = 3.3\,\text{V}$. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks (explanation + diagram):**

The five core elements of every embedded system are:

1. **Processor / Controller (Brain):** Executes the program; can be a microprocessor, microcontroller, DSP, ASIC, ASIP, SoC, or FPGA. Key parameters: word length, clock, instruction set (CISC/RISC).

2. **Memory (Notepads):** Two fundamental kinds:
   - *Program Memory* (non-volatile) — ROM, PROM, EPROM, EEPROM, Flash.
   - *Data Memory* (volatile) — SRAM, DRAM, Cache.

3. **I/O Interfaces & Peripherals (Senses & Limbs):** Provide connectivity to the external world. Inputs: sensors, keypads, ADC channels, comm ports. Outputs: LEDs, LCDs, motors, DAC, comm ports. Common on-chip peripherals: Timers, PWM, ADC, DAC, UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB, GPIO, Watchdog.

4. **Clock & Reset Circuitry (Heartbeat):** Provides the *synchronized timing reference* (crystal/PLL/internal RC) and *deterministic startup* (POR, BOR, external reset).

5. **Power Supply & Management (Life Support):** Battery or mains-derived regulated DC; LDO, DC-DC converters; sleep/standby modes for power-aware design.

```
                +--------------------------------------+
                |       POWER SUPPLY & MANAGEMENT      |
                |   (LDO, DC-DC, Battery, Sleep Modes) |
                +-----+--------------+-------------+---+
                      |              |             |
                      v              v             v
   +----+    +----------------+   +---------+   +-------+
   |CLK |--->|    PROCESSOR   |---| MEMORY  |   |  I/O  |
   |RST |--->|  (CPU + ALU)   |   | ROM RAM |   | PERIPH|
   +----+    +-------+--------+   +----+----+   +---+---+
                |       ^             |              |
                |       |  Address/Data/Control Bus  |
                +-------+-------------+--------------+
```

**Part (b) — 7 marks (numerical):**

**Given:** $f_{CLK} = 72\,\text{MHz} = 72 \times 10^{6}\,\text{Hz}$, $N = 45{,}000$, CPI $= 1.25$, $V_{DD} = 3.3\,\text{V}$, $I = 12\,\text{mA}$.

**(i) Execution Time:**

$$T_{exec} = \dfrac{N \cdot \text{CPI}}{f_{CLK}} = \dfrac{45{,}000 \times 1.25}{72 \times 10^{6}}$$

$$T_{exec} = \dfrac{56{,}250}{72 \times 10^{6}} = 7.8125 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s} \approx 781.25\,\mu\text{s}$$

**(ii) Maximum Task Rate:**

$$f_{task} = \dfrac{1}{T_{exec}} = \dfrac{1}{781.25 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1280\,\text{Hz} \approx 1.28\,\text{kHz}$$

**(iii) MIPS Rating:**

$$\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}} = \dfrac{72 \times 10^{6}}{1.25 \times 10^{6}} = 57.6\,\text{MIPS}$$

**(iv) Energy per Execution:**

First compute the *active power* drawn:

$$P = V_{DD} \cdot I = 3.3 \times 12 \times 10^{-3} = 39.6 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{W} = 39.6\,\text{mW}$$

Then the *energy* consumed in one execution:

$$E = P \cdot T_{exec} = 39.6 \times 10^{-3} \times 781.25 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$E = 3.09375 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{J} \approx 30.94\,\mu\text{J}$$

**Final Answer:** $T_{exec} \approx 781.25\,\mu\text{s}$, max rate $\approx 1.28\,\text{kHz}$, **57.6 MIPS**, energy $\approx 30.94\,\mu\text{J}$ per execution.

---

### **Question 3 (B) — 14 Marks — [Internal Choice Alternative]**

**(a)** Compare **microprocessor, microcontroller, DSP, ASIC, and SoC** in terms of definition, integration level, flexibility, cost, power, and a typical application. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive Amdahl's Law for parallel speedup. A particular image-processing task in an embedded vision system spends **80% of its time in a parallelizable kernel** and **20% in sequential I/O setup**. If we add a co-processor that **quadruples (4×) the speed of the parallelizable portion only**, what is the *overall* speedup? Also compute the *theoretical maximum speedup* if the co-processor could be made infinitely fast. *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution

**Part (a) — 7 marks (comparison table):**

| Parameter | Microprocessor (µP) | Microcontroller (µC) | DSP | ASIC | SoC |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | CPU only | CPU + RAM + ROM + peripherals on one die | CPU optimized for streaming numeric compute | Hardwired for one specific function | Entire system on one chip |
| **Integration** | External RAM/ROM/I/O | Highly integrated | External memory | Fixed function | Highest |
| **Flexibility** | High (programmable) | High (programmable) | Medium (programmed via SDK) | Zero (post-tape-out) | High (programmable cores) |
| **NRE Cost** | Low | Low | Low | Very High (mask, fab) | High |
| **Unit Cost** | Medium | **Lowest** | Medium | Low (at volume) | Medium–High |
| **Power** | Medium | **Lowest** | Medium–High | Lowest | Variable |
| **Typical App** | Laptop, server | Washing machine, IoT | Audio codec, motor control | Network switch ASIC | Smartphone, smartwatch |

**Part (b) — 7 marks (Amdahl's Law):**

**Derivation:**

Let $T$ be the original (single-core) execution time. Split it into a *parallelizable fraction* $f$ and a *sequential fraction* $(1-f)$:

$$T = T_{seq} + T_{par} = (1-f) \cdot T + f \cdot T$$

If we use $N$ parallel units, the parallel part runs in time $\dfrac{f \cdot T}{N}$, while the sequential part is unchanged:

$$T(N) = (1-f) \cdot T + \dfrac{f \cdot T}{N} = T \cdot \left[ (1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N} \right]$$

The overall speedup is:

$$S(N) = \dfrac{T}{T(N)} = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$$

**Q.E.D. — Amdahl's Law.**

**Numerical computation:**

Given: $f = 0.80$, $N = 4$ (quadrupled speedup of the parallel portion).

$$S(4) = \dfrac{1}{(1 - 0.80) + \dfrac{0.80}{4}} = \dfrac{1}{0.20 + 0.20} = \dfrac{1}{0.40} = 2.5$$

**Theoretical maximum speedup** ($N \to \infty$):

$$S_{max} = \lim_{N \to \infty} \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}} = \dfrac{1}{1-f} = \dfrac{1}{0.20} = 5$$

**Final Answer:** Overall speedup = **2.5×**, theoretical maximum = **5×**. Notice the *diminishing return* — quadrupling only the parallel portion yields only 2.5× overall, *not* 4×, because of the 20% sequential overhead.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Mixing f and 1-f:** Many students incorrectly plug $f = 0.20$ (the sequential part) into the parallel slot. Read the question carefully — *"80% of its time in a parallelizable kernel"* means the **parallel fraction is 0.80**, the sequential is 0.20.
> 2. **Forgetting the N in the denominator** of Amdahl's law formula.
> 3. **Unit mismatch** in MIPS/execution time numericals — always convert kHz to Hz and µs to s *before* substituting.
> 4. **Skipping the diagram in part (a)** — 2 marks are awarded for the *neat block diagram* in KTU 2024 Part B (7-mark subparts). An answer without a diagram loses 2 marks automatically.
> 5. **Writing $\dfrac{f}{N}$ as $f \cdot N$** — a deadly sign error.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute KTU Module-1 Revision Punch-list — print this and revise before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Definition ready:** Embedded system = *"Computing subsystem integrated into a host product, dedicated to a single function, under tight power/cost/size/real-time constraints."*
- [ ] **Five Core Elements** memorized: **Processor, Memory, I/O, Clock/Reset, Power**.
- [ ] **Von Neumann vs Harvard:** single shared bus & memory vs separated instruction/data memories & buses.
- [ ] **Von Neumann Bottleneck** is the inability to fetch instruction and data in the same cycle.
- [ ] **Modified Harvard** (ARM Cortex-M, modern x86) = separate L1 buses + unified address space.
- [ ] **Processor taxonomy** (descending integration): µP → µC → SoC; **µP** needs external peripherals, **µC** has them on-chip, **SoC** has *everything* (CPU + GPU + RAM controller + radios).
- [ ] **DSPs** are processors with MAC units, Harvard memory, and zero-overhead loops — for streaming numeric data.
- [ ] **ASICs** are not field-programmable; **FPGAs** are; **ASIPs** are programmable *with a custom ISA*.
- [ ] **Execution-time equation:** $T_{exec} = \dfrac{N \cdot \text{CPI}}{f_{CLK}}$ — the most-tested formula in this module.
- [ ] **MIPS equation:** $\text{MIPS} = \dfrac{f_{CLK}}{\text{CPI} \times 10^{6}}$.
- [ ] **Dynamic power scales as $V_{DD}^{2}$** — DVFS saves 75% power at half voltage.
- [ ] **Amdahl's Law:** $S(N) = \dfrac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{N}}$; **maximum** = $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$.
- [ ] **Memory taxonomy:** Non-volatile = ROM/PROM/EPROM/EEPROM/Flash; Volatile = SRAM/DRAM.
- [ ] **Real-time deadlines:** $T_{deadline} \ge T_{exec} + T_{interrupt\ latency}$ — fundamental hard real-time constraint.
- [ ] **Block diagram of the core of an embedded system** — must be reproducible from memory (see §4.1).
- [ ] **The "sushi chef vs master chef" analogy** — perfect one-line opening for any definition question.
- [ ] **Keywords examiners scan for:** *"dedicated function," "real-time," "tight constraints," "single-purpose," "Von Neumann bottleneck," "Modified Harvard," "Amdahl," "DVFS."* Their presence in your answer = full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Memory
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Memory

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Advanced Embedded Systems**, **Memory** refers to the hierarchical collection of physical and logical storage elements used by a microcontroller/microprocessor (e.g., ARM Cortex-M, Cortex-A, RISC-V) to hold instructions, data, and configuration during program execution. It is organized in a strict **memory hierarchy** optimized for the trade-off between **speed**, **cost per bit**, and **volatility**.

According to the KTU 2024 scheme, embedded memory systems are classified along two axes:
- **By Volatility**: Volatile (loses data on power-off) vs Non-Volatile (retains data without power).
- **By Access Pattern**: Random Access Memory (RAM) vs Sequential/Serial Access Memory.
- **By Mutability during operation**: Read-Only Memory (ROM) vs Read-Write Memory (RWM).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Highlight**: "Memory in an embedded system is a tightly coupled, addressable storage resource governed by the system bus architecture and the Memory Management Unit (MMU) or Memory Protection Unit (MPU)."

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine a busy **hospital pharmacy**:
- **Registers** inside the doctor’s pocket are like the **Registers (CPU)**: instant access, tiny capacity.
- The **pharmacist’s front desk drawer** is the **L1 Cache**: fast, small, always open.
- The **main pharmacy storeroom** is the **Main Memory (RAM)**: large, slightly slower, must walk to it.
- The **central warehouse in another city** is the **Secondary Storage (Flash/SSD)**: huge, very slow to fetch.
- The **printed medical reference book** is the **ROM**: contains fixed, unchangeable procedures.

When the doctor needs a medicine, the system first checks the pocket (register), then the drawer (L1), then the storeroom (RAM). The further you go, the slower it gets — but the cheaper per unit. This **tiered model** is exactly what an embedded memory hierarchy does.

## 1.3 Key Physical / Engineering Constants & Metrics

- **Typical SRAM access time**: **1–10 ns** (flip-flop based, 6 transistors per bit).
- **Typical DRAM access time**: **50–100 ns** (capacitor + 1 transistor per bit, needs refresh).
- **Typical NOR Flash read time**: **50–100 ns**, byte-erasable sector **~1 s**.
- **Typical NAND Flash page read**: **25–50 µs**, block erase **~1–2 ms**, page program **~200–300 µs**.
- **Endurance (NOR/NAND Flash)**: **10⁴ to 10⁶ program/erase cycles**.
- **Data retention**: **10–20 years** for commercial Flash.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The single most exam-relevant metric is **Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)**, which combines hit ratio, hit time, and miss penalty. See SECTION 2 for the formula.

## 1.4 Block Classification Overview

| Tier | Memory Type | Volatility | Relative Speed | Typical Use in Embedded SoC |
|------|-------------|------------|----------------|------------------------------|
| 0 | CPU Registers | Volatile | Fastest | Operands, instruction pointer |
| 1 | L1 Cache (SRAM) | Volatile | Very Fast | Per-core instruction/data cache |
| 2 | L2/L3 Cache (SRAM) | Volatile | Fast | Shared, on-chip |
| 3 | Main Memory (DRAM/SRAM) | Volatile | Medium | Working memory, stack, heap |
| 4 | Embedded Flash / EEPROM | Non-Volatile | Slow | Firmware, calibration data |
| 5 | External Storage (NAND, SD) | Non-Volatile | Very Slow | Logs, multimedia, OS image |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Memory Hierarchy Principles

The memory hierarchy in any advanced embedded SoC (such as those based on **ARM Cortex-A53**, **RISC-V**, or **NXP i.MX8**) is designed on three fundamental principles:

1. **Locality of Reference (Temporal & Spatial)**:
   - *Temporal*: If a memory location is accessed, it is likely to be accessed again soon (kept in cache).
   - *Spatial*: If a location is accessed, nearby locations are likely to be accessed next (cache lines are block-fetched).
2. **Inclusion Property**: Contents of higher (faster) levels are a subset of lower (slower) levels.
3. **Cost-Per-Bit Optimization**: Smaller, faster memory (SRAM) is expensive; larger, slower memory (DRAM, Flash) is cheap. The system combines them optimally.

## 2.2 Types of Memory — Detailed Theoretical Breakdown

### 2.2.1 RAM (Random Access Memory)

- **SRAM (Static RAM)**:
  - Built using **6 transistors per bit** (6T cell) forming a bistable latch.
  - No refresh required → lower power in standby when designed with low-leakage cells.
  - Used for **cache, register files, internal SRAM of MCUs** (e.g., 256 KB SRAM in STM32H7).
  - Drawback: Low density, high cost per bit.

- **DRAM (Dynamic RAM)**:
  - Built using **1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit**.
  - Capacitor leaks charge → must be **refreshed every ~64 ms**.
  - Higher density, lower cost per bit.
  - Used as **external main memory** in high-end embedded SoCs (smartphones, gateways).

- **PSRAM / FRAM / MRAM** — modern variants:
  - **PSRAM**: DRAM cell with built-in refresh controller, serial interface (e.g., ESP32 uses external QSPI PSRAM).
  - **FRAM (Ferroelectric RAM)**: Non-volatile, ~ns access, used in ultra-low-power MCUs (e.g., TI MSP430FR series).
  - **MRAM (Magnetoresistive RAM)**: Non-volatile, fast, used in industrial/automotive.

### 2.2.2 ROM (Read-Only Memory) and Non-Volatile Storage

- **Mask ROM**: Programmed at fabrication; cheapest per bit, no field updates.
- **PROM (OTP)**: One-time programmable fuses.
- **EPROM**: Erasable via UV light through quartz window.
- **EEPROM**: Byte-erasable electrically, ~1M erase cycles.
- **NOR Flash**: Random read access (XIP — Execute-In-Place), high reliability, slow erase. Holds **firmware/bootloader**.
- **NAND Flash**: Sequential page read/write, high density, lower cost. Stores **mass data**.
- **eMMC / UFS**: Managed NAND with built-in controller, used in Linux-based embedded systems.

### 2.2.3 Specialized Embedded Memory

- **TCM (Tightly Coupled Memory)**: Deterministic, low-latency RAM in ARM Cortex-M7/M33 — no cache, no bus arbitration.
- **OTP Memory**: One-Time-Programmable for device keys, serial numbers, trim values.
- **Battery-Backed SRAM (BBRAM)**: Non-volatile emulation via external coin cell.

## 2.3 Cache Memory Organization

A cache is divided into:
- **Tag**: Stores upper address bits to identify which main memory block is cached.
- **Index**: Selects the cache line.
- **Block Offset**: Selects the byte within the block.
- **Data Block**: The actual cached bytes (typically 32 or 64 bytes).

Cache mapping schemes:
- **Direct Mapped**: Each main memory block maps to exactly one cache line. (Simple, fast, higher miss rate.)
- **Fully Associative**: Any block can be placed in any line. (Best hit rate, complex hardware.)
- **N-way Set Associative**: Compromise — N lines per set. (Modern CPUs use 4/8/16-way.)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all high-yield formulas** for this module. Memorize them in this exact form.

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|-------------------|-------|
| 1 | Addressable Memory Capacity | $C = 2^n$ | $n$ = number of address lines, $C$ = words (or bytes) | $n$-bit address bus can access $2^n$ unique locations |
| 2 | Word-Size vs Addressable Units | $C = 2^n \times w$ | $w$ = word size in bits | Total memory in bits |
| 3 | Average Memory Access Time (1-level cache) | $T_{avg} = T_{hit} + (1 - h) \times T_{miss}$ | $h$ = hit ratio, $T_{miss}$ = miss penalty | $T_{hit}$ is cache access time |
| 4 | AMAT (2-level cache, L1 then L2) | $T_{avg} = T_{h1} + (1-h_1)(T_{h2} + (1-h_2)T_{m2})$ | $h_1, h_2$ are hit ratios of L1, L2 | $T_{m2}$ is main memory access time |
| 5 | Miss Rate | $m = 1 - h$ | dimensionless | $h + m = 1$ |
| 6 | Effective Access Time w/ No Cache | $T_{eff} = h \cdot T_{c} + (1-h) \cdot T_{m}$ | $T_c$ = cache time, $T_m$ = main memory time | Basic two-level average |
| 7 | DRAM Refresh Burden | $F_{refresh} = \dfrac{C_{rows}}{T_{retention}}$ | rows = number of rows, $T_{retention}$ in seconds | Typically 8192 rows in 64 ms |
| 8 | NAND Flash Endurance Lifetime | $L = \dfrac{N_{blocks} \times P/E_{cycles}}{W_{writes/day}}$ | $L$ = lifetime in days | Used for SSD design |
| 9 | Speedup via Cache | $S = \dfrac{T_{no\ cache}}{T_{avg}}$ | dimensionless | Must be $>1$ for the cache to be useful |
| 10 | Cache Line Address Decoding | $\text{Address bits} = \text{Tag} + \text{Index} + \text{Offset}$ | $\text{Offset} = \log_2(\text{block size in bytes})$ | $\text{Index} = \log_2(\text{number of sets})$ |

> **Note on LaTeX**: In the table above, vertical bars are not used; for absolute value style, the formulas use parentheses.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Boot ROM** in SoCs (e.g., Qualcomm, NXP i.MX) is mask ROM storing the **First Stage Boot Loader (FSBL)**.
- **Automotive ECU** uses ECC-protected Flash for ASIL-D compliance.
- **IoT edge devices** use **MRAM/FRAM** to combine non-volatility with byte-level write capability, eliminating the need for an external EEPROM.
- **Cache coherence protocols (MESI, MOESI)** are critical in multicore embedded SoCs to prevent stale data across CPU cores.
- **Wear-leveling algorithms** in NAND Flash controllers extend device lifetime by distributing write/erase cycles uniformly across blocks.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Memory Access Time (AMAT) with a Single-Level Cache

We derive the AMAT used to evaluate whether adding a cache improves system performance.

**Step 1: Define the two possible events when CPU requests data.**

Let $h$ be the probability (hit ratio) that the required data is in the cache. Then the miss ratio is $(1-h)$.

**Step 2: Write the access time for the hit case.**

If hit, the CPU waits only the cache access time $T_{hit}$ (in **ns**).

**Step 3: Write the access time for the miss case.**

If miss, the CPU must:
1. Wait $T_{hit}$ to discover the miss in cache.
2. Transfer the block to cache, taking $T_{miss}$ (miss penalty) in **ns**.

So total miss latency = $T_{hit} + T_{miss}$.

**Step 4: Form the expected (average) access time.**

$$T_{avg} = h \cdot T_{hit} + (1-h)(T_{hit} + T_{miss})$$

**Step 5: Simplify algebraically.**

$$T_{avg} = h \cdot T_{hit} + T_{hit} + T_{miss} - h \cdot T_{hit} - h \cdot T_{miss}$$

$$T_{avg} = T_{hit} + (1-h) \cdot T_{miss}$$

> **Result**: $T_{avg} = T_{hit} + (1-h) \cdot T_{miss}$. This is the standard AMAT formula used by KTU.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — AMAT for a Two-Level Cache (L1 + L2)

**Step 1: Define parameters.**

- $T_{h1}, h_1$ = L1 access time, L1 hit ratio.
- $T_{h2}, h_2$ = L2 access time, L2 hit ratio.
- $T_{m2}$ = Main memory access time.

**Step 2: Start with L1.**

$$T_{avg1} = T_{h1} + (1-h_1)\cdot T_{L1\_miss\_penalty}$$

**Step 3: Express L1 miss penalty in terms of L2.**

When L1 misses, CPU consults L2. So:

$$T_{L1\_miss\_penalty} = T_{h2} + (1-h_2)\cdot T_{m2}$$

**Step 4: Substitute.**

$$T_{avg} = T_{h1} + (1-h_1)\left[T_{h2} + (1-h_2)\cdot T_{m2}\right]$$

**Step 5: Expand.**

$$T_{avg} = T_{h1} + (1-h_1)T_{h2} + (1-h_1)(1-h_2)\cdot T_{m2}$$

> This is the canonical form tested in KTU 14-mark problems.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Number of Addressable Locations & Memory Map

Given an address bus of width $n$ bits, the number of uniquely addressable locations is:

$$N = 2^n$$

**Step 1**: Each address line has 2 states.
**Step 2**: $n$ lines → $2 \times 2 \times \dots \times 2 = 2^n$ combinations.
**Step 3**: Total memory capacity in bits = $N \times w$ where $w$ is data bus width.

**Example**: STM32 with 32-bit address bus can address $2^{32} = 4\text{ GB}$ (in theory; MMU/memory map limits it).

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Cache Address Decoding

For a direct-mapped cache with:
- Block size = $B$ bytes
- Number of sets = $S$

**Step 1: Offset bits.**

$$b_{offset} = \log_2(B)$$

**Step 2: Index bits.**

$$b_{index} = \log_2(S)$$

**Step 3: Tag bits.**

$$b_{tag} = n - b_{offset} - b_{index}$$

where $n$ is the total address bus width.

**Worked Numerical Example** (KTU-style):

A 32-bit address, direct-mapped cache, 64 KB capacity, block size = 16 bytes.

**Step A**: Number of blocks = $64 \text{ KB} / 16 \text{ B} = 4096$ sets.

**Step B**: $b_{offset} = \log_2(16) = 4$ bits.

**Step C**: $b_{index} = \log_2(4096) = 12$ bits.

**Step D**: $b_{tag} = 32 - 4 - 12 = 16$ bits.

> **Verification**: $2^{16} \times 2^{12} \times 2^{4} = 2^{32}$ ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Memory Selection for Embedded Systems

| Selection Criterion | Preferred Memory | Engineering Justification |
|---------------------|------------------|---------------------------|
| Boot code storage | NOR Flash / Mask ROM | XIP capability, deterministic read |
| Frequent write logs | Battery-backed SRAM / FRAM | No wear-out, byte-addressable |
| High-density data | NAND Flash / eMMC | Lowest cost per bit |
| Cache level 1 | SRAM (on-chip) | Sub-ns latency, no refresh |
| Real-time deterministic interrupt context | TCM (Tightly Coupled Memory) | Zero bus wait-states |
| IoT sensor buffering | PSRAM (serial QSPI) | Low pin count, sufficient speed |
| Automotive ASIL-D firmware | ECC-protected NOR Flash | Single-bit correction, safety compliance |
| Wear-sensitive filesystem journal | MRAM | Effectively infinite endurance |

## 3.6 Numerical KTU-Style Problem Walkthrough

> **Problem**: A system has L1 cache access time = **1 ns**, hit ratio = **0.9**. Main memory access time = **100 ns**. Compute (a) AMAT, (b) speedup over a no-cache system, (c) required hit ratio to make AMAT ≤ 5 ns.

**Solution (a)**:

$$T_{avg} = T_{hit} + (1-h) \cdot T_{miss} = 1 + (1-0.9)\cdot 100 = 1 + 10 = 11 \text{ ns}$$

**Solution (b) — No cache system** (every access goes to main memory): $T_{no\ cache} = 100$ ns.

$$S = \dfrac{T_{no\ cache}}{T_{avg}} = \dfrac{100}{11} \approx 9.09$$

**Solution (c)**: Set $T_{avg} \le 5$:

$$1 + (1-h)\cdot 100 \le 5 \implies (1-h)\cdot 100 \le 4 \implies 1-h \le 0.04 \implies h \ge 0.96$$

> **Answer**: Hit ratio must be at least **96%** to keep AMAT ≤ 5 ns.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Embedded Memory Hierarchy Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU Core Registers"] --> L1["L1 Cache SRAM"]
    L1 --> L2["L2 Cache SRAM"]
    L2 --> L3["Main Memory DRAM or SRAM"]
    L3 --> L4["Internal Flash NOR"]
    L4 --> L5["External Storage NAND or eMMC"]
    L1 -.->|"TCM Deterministic Path"| REALTIME["Real Time Interrupt Service"]
    style CPU fill:#ffe4b5,stroke:#333
    style L1 fill:#ffd1dc,stroke:#333
    style L2 fill:#c1f0c1,stroke:#333
    style L3 fill:#add8e6,stroke:#333
    style L4 fill:#d8bfd8,stroke:#333
    style L5 fill:#f0e68c,stroke:#333
    style REALTIME fill:#ff7f7f,stroke:#333
```

> **Reading Guide**: Top-of-pyramid (CPU Registers) is **fastest and smallest**. Bottom (External Storage) is **slowest and largest**. Cost-per-bit is **inversely** proportional to speed.

## 4.2 Cache Read Operation Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["CPU Issues Address"] --> B{"Check Tag at Cache Index"}
    B -- "Tag Matches AND Valid=1" --> C["Cache HIT: Return Data in 1 Cycle"]
    B -- "Tag Mismatch or Valid=0" --> D["Cache MISS: Stall CPU"]
    D --> E["Fetch Block from Main Memory"]
    E --> F["Place Block in Cache Line"]
    F --> G["Update Tag and Valid Bit"]
    G --> H["Return Data to CPU and Resume"]
    C --> I["Next Instruction"]
    H --> I
    style A fill:#fafad2
    style C fill:#90ee90
    style D fill:#ffb6c1
    style H fill:#87ceeb
```

## 4.3 Address Decoding Architecture (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ADDR["32 bit Address Bus"] --> DECODER["Address Decoder Logic"]
    DECODER --> EXT1["External NOR Flash Region F0000000 to FFFFFFFF"]
    DECODER --> EXT2["External DRAM Region 00000000 to 0FFFFFFF"]
    DECODER --> PERIPH["On Chip Peripherals Region 40000000 to 5FFFFFFF"]
    DECODER --> SRAM["Internal SRAM Region 20000000 to 3FFFFFFF"]
    style ADDR fill:#ffe4b5
    style DECODER fill:#dda0dd
    style EXT1 fill:#f0e68c
    style EXT2 fill:#add8e6
    style PERIPH fill:#90ee90
    style SRAM fill:#ffd1dc
```

> **Note**: In real KTU problems, you may be given a partial memory map and asked to compute the address range of a peripheral given its size and base address. The formula is: $\text{Range} = [\text{Base}, \text{Base} + \text{Size} - 1]$.

## 4.4 Write-Back vs Write-Through Cache Policy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    WT["Write Through Policy"] --> WTC["Write to Cache AND Main Memory Simultaneously"]
    WTC --> WTR["Simple, Always Consistent, High Bus Traffic"]
    WB["Write Back Policy"] --> WBC["Write ONLY to Cache, Mark Dirty"]
    WBC --> WBR["Write to Main Memory ONLY when Block is Evicted"]
    WBR --> WBR2["Lower Bus Traffic, Needs Dirty Bit, Risk of Data Loss on Power Failure"]
    style WT fill:#c1f0c1
    style WB fill:#ffd1dc
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between SRAM and DRAM with respect to cell structure, refresh requirement, density, and typical use in embedded systems.**

**Model Answer**:

| Parameter | SRAM | DRAM |
|-----------|------|------|
| Cell structure | 6 transistors (6T) bistable latch | 1 transistor + 1 capacitor |
| Refresh | Not required | Required every ~64 ms |
| Density | Low (6T per bit) | High (1T+1C per bit) |
| Cost per bit | High | Low |
| Speed | Faster (1–10 ns) | Slower (50–100 ns) |
| Power | Lower in active use | Higher due to refresh |
| Embedded use | On-chip cache, internal MCU SRAM, TCM | External main memory in SoC with MMU |

(3 marks awarded for correctly tabulating at least 5 distinguishing points.)

---

### **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**What is a cache hit ratio? How does a higher hit ratio affect the Average Memory Access Time (AMAT)?**

**Model Answer**:

- **Cache hit ratio (h)** = (Number of cache hits) / (Total number of memory accesses). It is a fraction between 0 and 1.
- AMAT formula: $T_{avg} = T_{hit} + (1-h) \cdot T_{miss}$.
- As **h increases**, the term $(1-h)$ decreases, so the contribution of the slow miss penalty shrinks.
- When $h \to 1$, $T_{avg} \to T_{hit}$, meaning the system runs at cache speed.
- When $h \to 0$, $T_{avg} \to T_{hit} + T_{miss}$, meaning the cache is useless and acts as an extra cycle of latency.

(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for correct qualitative analysis.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**: Students very often **forget the $+T_{hit}$ term** in the AMAT formula and write only $(1-h)\cdot T_{miss}$. This loses **2 marks immediately**. Also, students frequently confuse **hit ratio with miss ratio** in numerical problems. Always state $h$ and $1-h$ explicitly before substituting.

---

### **Part B — Question A** (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the memory hierarchy in an advanced embedded SoC. List any **five** levels in order of increasing capacity and decreasing speed, with one example memory technology at each level. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A two-level cache system has the following parameters:
- L1: $T_{h1} = 1$ ns, $h_1 = 0.85$
- L2: $T_{h2} = 8$ ns, $h_2 = 0.95$
- Main memory access: $T_{m2} = 200$ ns

Compute:
1. The overall Average Memory Access Time (AMAT).
2. The speedup compared to a no-cache system (where every access goes to main memory).
3. The new AMAT if L1 hit ratio is improved to **0.95** (with L2 unchanged). **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (a)**:

The memory hierarchy in an advanced embedded SoC exploits **locality of reference** to balance speed, cost, and capacity. The five levels from fastest/smallest to slowest/largest are:

1. **CPU Registers** – few bytes, sub-ns access; example: R0–R12 in ARM Cortex-M.
2. **L1 Cache (SRAM)** – typically 16–64 KB per core; example: Harvard L1I/L1D in Cortex-A53.
3. **L2 Cache (SRAM)** – 256 KB – 2 MB shared; example: on-chip L2 in NXP i.MX8.
4. **Main Memory (DRAM / PSRAM)** – 64 MB – 4 GB; example: external LPDDR4 in a gateway SoC.
5. **Non-Volatile Storage (NAND / NOR / eMMC)** – GB scale; example: firmware in embedded NOR Flash, logs in eMMC.

**Key points** (1 mark each, 5 marks for the table; 2 marks for explaining the locality principle):
- The principle of **temporal locality** keeps recently accessed data in upper levels.
- The principle of **spatial locality** moves data in **blocks** (cache lines).
- The hierarchy ensures the **average access time** is dominated by the upper levels for hot data.

### **Model Solution (b)**:

**Step 1 — Use the two-level AMAT formula**:

$$T_{avg} = T_{h1} + (1-h_1)\left[T_{h2} + (1-h_2)\cdot T_{m2}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values**:

$$T_{avg} = 1 + (1-0.85)\left[8 + (1-0.95)\cdot 200\right]$$

$$T_{avg} = 1 + 0.15 \cdot \left[8 + 0.05 \cdot 200\right]$$

$$T_{avg} = 1 + 0.15 \cdot \left[8 + 10\right]$$

$$T_{avg} = 1 + 0.15 \cdot 18$$

$$T_{avg} = 1 + 2.7 = 3.7 \text{ ns}$$

> **AMAT = 3.7 ns** (2 marks)

**Step 3 — Compute speedup**:
No-cache access time = $T_{m2} = 200$ ns.

$$S = \dfrac{200}{3.7} \approx 54.05$$

> **Speedup ≈ 54.05×** (2 marks)

**Step 4 — Recompute AMAT with $h_1 = 0.95$**:

$$T_{avg}^{new} = 1 + (1-0.95)\left[8 + 10\right]$$

$$T_{avg}^{new} = 1 + 0.05 \cdot 18 = 1 + 0.9 = 1.9 \text{ ns}$$

> **New AMAT = 1.9 ns** (3 marks: 2 for substitution, 1 for final answer)

---

### **Part B — Question B (Alternative Choice)** (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **direct-mapped cache organization**. A 32-bit system uses a direct-mapped cache of size **32 KB** with a **block size of 16 bytes**. Determine the number of **tag bits**, **index bits**, and **offset bits**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **NOR Flash and NAND Flash** across the following axes: read mechanism, write/erase mechanism, endurance, density, XIP capability, and typical use case. Compute the total capacity (in MB) of a NAND Flash with **4096 blocks × 64 pages/block × 4 KB/page**. **(7 marks)**

### **Model Solution (a)**:

**Block Diagram (description for the answer script)**:

```
   32-bit Address
  | Tag (T bits) | Index (I bits) | Offset (O bits) |
       |                |                |
       v                v                v
   Tag RAM          Cache Data         Byte
   Compare          RAM Array          Selector
```

In direct-mapped cache, each main memory block maps to **exactly one** cache line, identified by the index bits. The tag bits are stored alongside the data and compared on every access. A match + valid bit = HIT.

**Bit Calculations**:

**Step 1 — Number of blocks**:
$$\text{Blocks} = \dfrac{32 \text{ KB}}{16 \text{ B}} = \dfrac{32 \times 1024}{16} = 2048 \text{ sets}$$

**Step 2 — Offset bits**:
$$b_{offset} = \log_2(16) = 4 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3 — Index bits**:
$$b_{index} = \log_2(2048) = 11 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 4 — Tag bits**:
$$b_{tag} = 32 - 11 - 4 = 17 \text{ bits}$$

> **Answer**: Tag = **17 bits**, Index = **11 bits**, Offset = **4 bits**.
> **Verification**: $2^{17}\cdot 2^{11}\cdot 2^4 = 2^{32}$ ✓ (Marks: 2 for diagram, 2 for block count, 1 each for offset/index/tag)

### **Model Solution (b)**:

**Comparison Table** (1 mark per correct row × 5 rows + 1 for any extra valid point):

| Parameter | NOR Flash | NAND Flash |
|-----------|-----------|------------|
| Read mechanism | Random byte access (XIP) | Sequential page read |
| Write/Erase | Slow byte/word program, slow sector erase | Fast page program (~200 µs), block erase (~1 ms) |
| Endurance | 10⁴ – 10⁵ P/E cycles | 10³ – 10⁴ P/E cycles (lower per cell) |
| Density | Lower (1–256 Mb typical) | Very high (1 Gb – 1 Tb) |
| XIP capability | Yes (execute in place) | No (must copy to RAM) |
| Typical use | Firmware, bootloader, BIOS | Mass data, filesystems, eMMC |

**Capacity Calculation**:

$$\text{Total pages} = 4096 \text{ blocks} \times 64 \text{ pages/block} = 262144 \text{ pages}$$

$$\text{Total bytes} = 262144 \times 4 \text{ KB} = 262144 \times 4096 \text{ B}$$

$$= 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \text{ bytes} = 1024 \text{ MB} = 1 \text{ GB}$$

> **Answer**: **1 GB** total NAND Flash capacity (3 marks: 1 for setup, 1 for multiplication, 1 for unit conversion).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Memory hierarchy** has 5 levels: Registers → L1 → L2 → Main → Storage.
- [ ] **SRAM = 6T, no refresh, fast, low density**; **DRAM = 1T+1C, needs refresh every ~64 ms, high density**.
- [ ] **AMAT (1-level)** = $T_{hit} + (1-h)T_{miss}$. **AMAT (2-level)** = $T_{h1} + (1-h_1)[T_{h2} + (1-h_2)T_{m2}]$.
- [ ] **Cache address split**: Tag + Index + Offset = total address bits.
- [ ] **Offset bits = $\log_2(\text{block size in bytes})$**, **Index bits = $\log_2(\text{number of sets})$**.
- [ ] **NOR Flash = XIP, random read, holds firmware**; **NAND Flash = sequential, holds mass data, no XIP**.
- [ ] **FRAM and MRAM** are modern non-volatile memories combining speed of SRAM with non-volatility.
- [ ] **TCM** in ARM Cortex-M gives deterministic, cache-free access for real-time ISRs.
- [ ] **Wear-leveling** is mandatory in NAND Flash to extend endurance.
- [ ] **Cache coherence protocols** (MESI) are needed in multicore embedded SoCs.
- [ ] **ECC** is required in safety-critical (ASIL-D) memory subsystems.
- [ ] **Hit ratio must be high** (typically ≥ 0.95) for the cache to provide meaningful speedup; otherwise the cache is a net loss.
- [ ] **Speedup = $T_{no\ cache} / T_{avg}$**; always > 1 for a beneficial cache.
- [ ] KTU loves **numerical AMAT problems** with two-level caches — practice at least 5.
- [ ] In **address decoding**, $n$ address lines → $2^n$ uniquely addressable locations.
- [ ] **PSRAM** is widely used in ESP32-style Wi-Fi MCUs to add low-pin-count external RAM.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sensors and Actuators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Actuators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Sensor?

A **sensor** is a physical device, module, or subsystem whose purpose is to detect events, changes in its environment, or physical quantities (such as heat, light, pressure, motion, or any other physical variable) and convert them into a measurable, typically electrical, output signal. In the context of KTU 2024 Advanced Embedded Systems, a sensor is formally defined as the *primary input transducer* of an embedded system that interfaces the physical world with the digital processing domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** A sensor is an electronic device that converts a physical stimulus (input quantity) into an equivalent electrical signal (output quantity) suitable for measurement, recording, or control.

The generalized mathematical representation is:

$$y = f(x)$$

where **x** is the physical input stimulus (measurand), **y** is the electrical output signal, and **f** is the transfer function of the sensor.

## 1.2 What is an Actuator?

An **actuator** is a device that converts an electrical control signal (typically low-power, from a microcontroller or processor) into a physical action such as motion, heat, light, or force. It is the *output transducer* of an embedded system and performs the inverse function of a sensor.

$$x_{\text{physical}} = g(V_{\text{control}})$$

where **g** is the actuator's electro-mechanical conversion function.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition (Board Standard):** An actuator is a component of a machine or system that is responsible for moving or controlling a mechanism or system, by converting electrical energy into physical action (linear or rotary motion, heat, light, etc.).

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy: The Human Body

To make this crystal clear for first-time learners, consider the **human body** as a perfect biological embedded system:

| Human Body Component | Embedded System Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Eyes, Ears, Skin, Nose, Tongue | **Sensors** (e.g., camera, microphone, thermistor) | Detect physical stimuli |
| Brain (decision making) | **Microcontroller / Processor** | Process the sensor data |
| Muscles, Glands, Vocal Cords | **Actuators** (e.g., DC motor, speaker, LED) | Produce physical action |
| Nervous System | **Signal Conditioning + ADC/DAC + Bus** | Carry signals between blocks |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Just as our skin detects temperature and our brain commands our hand to move away from a hot object, an embedded system uses sensors to "feel" the world, a microcontroller to "think," and actuators to "act." Sensors are the **senses** of the system, and actuators are the **muscles**.

## 1.4 Why Sensors and Actuators are the Heart of Embedded Systems

Modern embedded systems (IoT devices, robotics, autonomous vehicles, medical devices, industrial automation) are entirely dependent on the quality of their sensors and actuators. The KTU 2024 syllabus treats these as the foundational "edge" components of any cyber-physical system. Without high-fidelity sensors, the system has no situational awareness, and without reliable actuators, the system cannot influence its environment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** In the KTU 2024 scheme, *Sensors and Actuators* in Module 1 covers classification, working principles, performance characteristics, interfacing techniques, and signal conditioning — all foundational for later modules on real-time operating systems, communication protocols, and embedded control.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Sensors

Sensors can be classified along several orthogonal axes. The KTU board consistently expects students to know the following four classifications:

### 2.1.1 Based on Power Requirement

- **Active Sensors (Self-generating):** These generate their own electrical output directly from the physical stimulus and do **not** require an external power source. Examples: **thermocouple, piezoelectric sensor, photovoltaic cell, tachogenerator**.
- **Passive Sensors (Externally powered):** These require an external excitation (voltage or current) to produce an output. The measurand modulates the external excitation. Examples: **thermistor, RTD, LDR, strain gauge, potentiometer**.

### 2.1.2 Based on Output Signal Type

- **Analog Sensors:** Output is a continuous voltage or current proportional to the measurand. Examples: **LM35 temperature sensor, MPX pressure sensor, potentiometer**.
- **Digital Sensors:** Output is a discrete (binary) signal, pulse train, or a digital word over a serial bus (I²C, SPI). Examples: **DS18B20 temperature sensor, MPU6050 IMU, DHT11 humidity sensor, rotary encoder**.

### 2.1.3 Based on Physical Phenomenon

- **Thermal:** Thermistor, RTD, Thermocouple, Bimetallic strip
- **Mechanical:** Strain gauge, Piezoelectric, LVDT, Accelerometer
- **Optical:** LDR, Photodiode, Phototransistor, Photovoltaic cell
- **Magnetic:** Hall effect sensor, Magnetoresistive sensor
- **Chemical:** Gas sensor (MQ series), pH sensor, Humidity sensor
- **Biological/Proximity:** PIR, Ultrasonic, IR proximity

### 2.1.4 Based on Detection Mode

- **Contact Sensors:** Require physical contact (e.g., tactile sensor, RTD)
- **Non-contact Sensors:** Detect without physical contact (e.g., PIR, ultrasonic, IR)

## 2.2 Classification of Actuators

Actuators are classified based on the **type of energy conversion** they perform:

| Energy Domain | Actuator Examples | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|
| Electrical → Mechanical (Rotary) | DC motor, Stepper motor, Servo motor, BLDC motor | Robotics, CNC, Drones |
| Electrical → Mechanical (Linear) | Solenoid, Voice coil, Linear actuator | Valves, Door locks, Printers |
| Electrical → Thermal | Resistive heater, Peltier cooler | 3D printer bed, Incubators |
| Electrical → Optical | LED, Laser diode, LCD, OLED | Displays, Indicators, Optical comm. |
| Electrical → Acoustic | Speaker, Buzzer, Piezo buzzer | Alarms, HMI feedback |
| Electrical → Hydraulic/Pneumatic | Solenoid valve, Pump | Industrial automation |
| Electrical → Magnetic | Relay, Electromagnet | Switching, Solenoid valves |

## 2.3 Static Performance Characteristics of Sensors (KTU High-Yield)

The KTU 2024 board repeatedly tests the following static characteristics. Mastering them guarantees marks.

- **Accuracy:** Closeness of the measured value to the true value. Expressed as a percentage of Full Scale (FS) or Full-Scale Output (FSO).
- **Precision (Repeatability):** Ability of the sensor to produce the same output for the same input under repeated identical conditions. A precise sensor may still be inaccurate due to systematic error.
- **Resolution:** The smallest change in the measurand that produces a detectable change in the output signal. For digital sensors, it is the least significant bit (LSB).
- **Sensitivity:** The ratio of the change in output to the change in input. Mathematically, the slope of the calibration curve.
- **Linearity (Non-linearity):** The degree to which the actual calibration curve deviates from a straight line. Expressed as % of FSO.
- **Hysteresis:** The maximum difference in output for the same input, depending on whether the input is increasing or decreasing. Quantified as % of FSO.
- **Range / Span:** The difference between the minimum and maximum input values the sensor can measure.
- **Drift:** The change of sensor output with time, even when the input is held constant. Sub-categorized as thermal drift and time drift.
- **Dead Band / Threshold:** The range of input for which there is no change in output.
- **Bandwidth:** The frequency range over which the sensor can faithfully respond to input variations (dynamic characteristic).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. KTU board questions frequently involve numerical substitution into one or more of these formulas.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output unit / Input unit | Slope of transfer function |
| 2 | Transfer Function (Linear) | $y = m \cdot x + c$ | — | $m$ is sensitivity, $c$ is offset |
| 3 | Non-linearity ($\%NL$) | $\%NL = \dfrac{\Delta y_{\max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | $\Delta y_{\max}$ is max deviation from ideal line |
| 4 | Hysteresis ($\%H$) | $\%H = \dfrac{\Delta y_{H,\max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | $\Delta y_{H,\max}$ is max up-down difference |
| 5 | Repeatability ($\%R$) | $\%R = \dfrac{\Delta y_{R,\max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Max spread on repeated measurements |
| 6 | Accuracy ($\%A$) | $\%A = \dfrac{\Delta y_{A,\max}}{Y_{FS}} \times 100$ | $\%$ | Includes non-linearity, hysteresis, etc. |
| 7 | ADC Digital Output | $D = \left\lfloor \dfrac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor \times 2^{n}$ | Counts (LSB) | $n$ is ADC resolution in bits |
| 8 | ADC Resolution (Voltage) | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$ | Volts per LSB | Smallest detectable voltage step |
| 9 | Sensor Resolution (Input) | $\Delta x_{\min} = \dfrac{\text{Range}}{S \cdot (2^{n} - 1)}$ | Input units | Combined system resolution |
| 10 | First-Order Step Response | $y(t) = Y_{ss} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$ | Output units | $\tau$ is time constant |
| 11 | Rise Time (10–90%) | $t_{r} \approx 2.2 \, \tau$ | Seconds | For first-order system |
| 12 | DC Motor Back EMF | $E_{b} = K_{e} \cdot \omega$ | Volts | $K_{e}$ in V·s/rad |
| 13 | DC Motor Torque | $T = K_{t} \cdot I_{a}$ | N·m | $K_{t}$ in N·m/A |
| 14 | DC Motor Armature Equation | $V = E_{b} + I_{a} R_{a}$ | Volts | KVL around armature |
| 15 | DC Motor No-Load Speed | $\omega_{0} = \dfrac{V}{K_{e}}$ | rad/s | At zero load torque |
| 16 | DC Motor Stall Torque | $T_{\text{stall}} = \dfrac{K_{t} \cdot V}{R_{a}}$ | N·m | At zero speed |
| 17 | Stepper Step Angle | $\beta = \dfrac{360^{\circ}}{N_{s}}$ | Degrees | $N_{s}$ is steps per revolution |
| 18 | Servo Pulse-to-Angle | $\theta = \dfrac{(PW - 1\,\text{ms})}{1\,\text{ms}} \times 90^{\circ}$ | Degrees | For 0°–180° range |
| 19 | Mechanical Power | $P = T \cdot \omega$ | Watts | $T$ in N·m, $\omega$ in rad/s |
| 20 | Energy Stored in Solenoid | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} L I^{2}$ | Joules | $L$ is inductance, $I$ is current |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pro Tip for KTU Valuation:** Examiners award **1 mark per correct formula** and **1 mark per correct substitution**, with the final 1 mark for the numerical answer. Always write the formula first, then substitute, then compute.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In modern engineering, sensors and actuators form the **cyber-physical interface** in:

- **Automotive:** Crankshaft position sensor (Hall effect), Oxygen sensor (lambda), ABS wheel speed sensor, fuel injector actuator.
- **Healthcare:** Pulse oximeter (PPG sensor), insulin pump (peristaltic pump actuator), ECG electrodes.
- **Industry 4.0:** Vibration sensor on motors, piezoelectric pressure sensor in pipelines, pneumatic actuators in assembly lines.
- **Consumer Electronics:** Smartphone IMU (accelerometer + gyroscope), fingerprint sensor, haptic actuator.
- **Aerospace & Drones:** MEMS gyroscope, barometer, GPS receiver (sensor), ESC + BLDC motor (actuator).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A well-designed embedded system always **matches the sensor bandwidth, resolution, and range to the application**, and **matches the actuator torque, speed, and power rating to the mechanical load**. Mismatched components cause failure modes that are the most common sources of design error in industry.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sensitivity and Transfer Function of a Linear Sensor

### 3.1.1 Statement of the Problem

A linear temperature sensor produces an output voltage $V_{out}$ in response to an input temperature $T$ (in °C). Two calibration points are recorded:

- At $T_{1} = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$, the output is $V_{out,1} = 0.5\,\text{V}$
- At $T_{2} = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$, the output is $V_{out,2} = 4.5\,\text{V}$

Derive the **transfer function** of the sensor, and determine the **sensitivity**, **range**, and **span**.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Recall the generalized linear transfer function.**

A linear sensor obeys the equation of a straight line:

$$V_{out} = m \cdot T + c$$

Here, $m$ is the **sensitivity** (in V/°C) and $c$ is the **offset voltage** (in V), i.e., the output when the input is zero.

**Step 2 — Form two equations by substituting the two calibration points.**

Substituting the first point $(T_{1} = 0,\ V_{out,1} = 0.5)$:

$$0.5 = m \cdot 0 + c \quad \Longrightarrow \quad c = 0.5\,\text{V}$$

Substituting the second point $(T_{2} = 100,\ V_{out,2} = 4.5)$:

$$4.5 = m \cdot 100 + 0.5$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the sensitivity $m$.**

$$m \cdot 100 = 4.5 - 0.5 = 4.0$$

$$m = \frac{4.0}{100} = 0.04\,\text{V/}^{\circ}\text{C}$$

**Step 4 — Write the final transfer function.**

$$\boxed{V_{out} = 0.04 \cdot T + 0.5}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the static characteristics from the transfer function.**

- **Sensitivity:** $S = 0.04\,\text{V/}^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Offset:** $c = 0.5\,\text{V}$
- **Range of input:** $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $100^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Span of input:** $\Delta T = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Output span:** $\Delta V_{out} = 4.5 - 0.5 = 4.0\,\text{V}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Check:** Sensitivity is the slope — how many volts of output change per °C of input. Here, a 1°C rise produces a 0.04 V (40 mV) rise at the output. This matches our expectation for a typical silicon temperature sensor.

## 3.2 Derivation: ADC Resolution and System Input Resolution

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

The above temperature sensor (output range 0.5 V to 4.5 V) is connected to a 10-bit ADC with a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.0\,\text{V}$. Calculate:

1. The voltage resolution (in V/LSB) of the ADC.
2. The temperature resolution (in °C/LSB) at the input of the overall system.
3. The digital code when $T = 75^{\circ}\text{C}$.

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Compute the voltage resolution of the ADC.**

For an $n$-bit ADC, the voltage resolution (size of one LSB) is given by:

$$\Delta V = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1}$$

With $n = 10$ and $V_{ref} = 5.0\,\text{V}$:

$$\Delta V = \frac{5.0}{2^{10} - 1} = \frac{5.0}{1023}$$

$$\Delta V \approx 4.888 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V/LSB} \approx 4.888\,\text{mV/LSB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the temperature resolution at the input.**

The input temperature change $\Delta T$ that produces one LSB of output change is found using the sensitivity:

$$\Delta T_{\min} = \frac{\Delta V}{S} = \frac{4.888 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{V/LSB}}{0.04\,\text{V/}^{\circ}\text{C}}$$

$$\Delta T_{\min} \approx 0.1222^{\circ}\text{C/LSB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the digital code for $T = 75^{\circ}\text{C}$.**

First, find the sensor output voltage at $T = 75^{\circ}\text{C}$ using the transfer function:

$$V_{out}(75) = 0.04 \times 75 + 0.5 = 3.0 + 0.5 = 3.5\,\text{V}$$

Next, apply the ADC conversion equation:

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{V_{in}}{V_{ref}} \right\rfloor \times 2^{n}$$

$$D = \left\lfloor \frac{3.5}{5.0} \right\rfloor \times 1024 = 0.7 \times 1024 = 716.8$$

The 10-bit ADC therefore outputs the integer digital word:

$$\boxed{D = 717 \quad \text{(or 0b1011001101)}}$$

**Step 4 — Reverse check (optional, for full marks).**

If $D = 717$, the equivalent analog voltage is:

$$V_{in,eq} = D \times \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{n} - 1} = 717 \times \frac{5.0}{1023} \approx 3.504\,\text{V}$$

$$T_{eq} = \frac{3.504 - 0.5}{0.04} = \frac{3.004}{0.04} \approx 75.1^{\circ}\text{C} \ \checkmark$$

The small residual error ($\sim 0.1^{\circ}\text{C}$) is the **quantization error** of the ADC, which is fundamental to any digital measurement system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Insight:** Quantization error can never be eliminated, but it can be **halved** (in voltage) by adding one extra bit to the ADC. This is why 12-bit and 16-bit ADCs are preferred in precision applications.

## 3.3 Derivation: First-Order Step Response of a Thermal Sensor

Many physical sensors (thermistors, RTDs, thermocouples) behave as **first-order systems** because their response is dominated by a single thermal time constant. The KTU board frequently tests the time-constant behaviour.

### 3.3.1 Model and Governing Equation

Consider a thermistor with thermal capacitance $C$ (J/K) and thermal resistance $R$ (K/W), in contact with a medium whose temperature suddenly jumps from $T_{0}$ to $T_{\infty}$ at $t = 0$. The energy balance is:

$$C \frac{dT_{s}}{dt} = \frac{T_{\infty} - T_{s}}{R}$$

where $T_{s}(t)$ is the instantaneous sensor temperature.

### 3.3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Rearrange the differential equation into standard form.**

$$\frac{dT_{s}}{dt} + \frac{T_{s}}{RC} = \frac{T_{\infty}}{RC}$$

Define the **thermal time constant** $\tau = RC$ (in seconds). The equation becomes:

$$\frac{dT_{s}}{dt} + \frac{T_{s}}{\tau} = \frac{T_{\infty}}{\tau}$$

**Step 2 — Solve the linear first-order ODE using the integrating-factor method.**

Multiply both sides by the integrating factor $e^{t/\tau}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\left[ T_{s} \cdot e^{t/\tau} \right] = \frac{T_{\infty}}{\tau} \cdot e^{t/\tau}$$

Integrate from $0$ to $t$:
