$$T_{s}(t) \cdot e^{t/\tau} - T_{s}(0) = \frac{T_{\infty}}{\tau} \cdot \tau \cdot \left[ e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right]$$

$$T_{s}(t) \cdot e^{t/\tau} = T_{s}(0) + T_{\infty} \left( e^{t/\tau} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 3 — Apply the initial condition $T_{s}(0) = T_{0}$.**

$$T_{s}(t) = T_{0} \cdot e^{-t/\tau} + T_{\infty} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

$$\boxed{T_{s}(t) = T_{\infty} + (T_{0} - T_{\infty}) \cdot e^{-t/\tau}}$$

**Step 4 — Special case: $T_{0} = 0$, $T_{\infty} = Y_{ss}$.**

The canonical first-order step response is recovered:

$$y(t) = Y_{ss} \left( 1 - e^{-t/\tau} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the rise time (10% to 90%).**

Setting $y(t_{1}) = 0.1 \cdot Y_{ss}$ and $y(t_{2}) = 0.9 \cdot Y_{ss}$:

$$0.1 = 1 - e^{-t_{1}/\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{1} = -\tau \ln(0.9) \approx 0.1054 \, \tau$$

$$0.9 = 1 - e^{-t_{2}/\tau} \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_{2} = -\tau \ln(0.1) \approx 2.3026 \, \tau$$

$$t_{r} = t_{2} - t_{1} = 2.1972 \, \tau \approx 2.2 \, \tau$$

This confirms the formula in the cheat sheet.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Common Sensors (KTU High-Yield Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following comparison matrix is heavily tested in the KTU 2024 board exam. Students are expected to know at least one defining feature, one advantage, and one limitation of each.

| Sensor Type | Measurand | Active/Passive | Output | Sensitivity Range | Key Advantage | Key Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thermocouple | Temperature | Active | mV (μV/°C) | 10–80 μV/°C | Wide range (−200 to 2000°C), rugged | Cold-junction compensation required, low sensitivity |
| RTD (Pt100) | Temperature | Passive | Resistance (Ω) | ~0.385 Ω/°C | High accuracy, linear, stable | Self-heating, expensive, lead-wire error |
| Thermistor (NTC) | Temperature | Passive | Resistance (Ω) | −3 to −5 %/°C | High sensitivity, fast, cheap | Highly non-linear, narrow range |
| LM35 (IC sensor) | Temperature | Active | Voltage (mV/°C) | 10 mV/°C | Calibrated, linear, low cost | Limited range (0–150°C) |
| Strain Gauge | Force / Strain | Passive | ΔR/R | Gauge factor 2–5 | Mature technology, accurate | Sensitive to temperature, requires bridge |
| LVDT | Displacement | Active | AC voltage | Varies with stroke | Frictionless, infinite resolution | Limited to linear displacement |
| Piezoelectric | Pressure / Vibration | Active | Charge (pC) | High at high freq. | Excellent dynamic response | Not suitable for static measurements |
| LDR | Light intensity | Passive | Resistance (Ω) | Highly non-linear | Cheap, simple | Slow response, non-linear |
| Photodiode | Light intensity | Active | Current (μA) | Linear with light | Fast, linear | Requires transimpedance amplifier |
| Hall Effect | Magnetic field | Active | Voltage (mV) | 5–50 mV/G | Solid-state, no contacts | Sensitive to temperature drift |
| Potentiometer | Position | Passive | Voltage (V) | $V_{cc} / \text{stroke}$ | Simple, cheap, absolute | Friction, wear, limited life |
| Rotary Encoder | Angular position | Active | Pulses / Code | High (10–24 bit) | High resolution, digital output | Need debouncing; absolute types expensive |
| Accelerometer (MEMS) | Acceleration | Active | Voltage / I²C / SPI | 0.1–10 V/g | Small, low power, integrated | Limited bandwidth, noise |
| PIR | Body heat / Motion | Active | Digital pulse | On/off | No contact, low power | Limited range and directionality |

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Study of Common Actuators

| Actuator Type | Output Motion | Control Signal | Key Advantage | Key Limitation | Typical Embedded Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| DC Motor (Brushed) | Continuous rotation | PWM (voltage) | Simple, cheap, high speed | Brush wear, EMI | Small fans, toys, pumps |
| Brushless DC (BLDC) | Continuous rotation | 3-phase PWM (ESC) | High efficiency, long life | Needs ESC, complex control | Drones, EVs, CNC spindles |
| Stepper Motor | Discrete step rotation | Pulse train (open loop) | Precise open-loop position | Resonance, low efficiency at high speed | 3D printers, CNC axes, camera gimbals |
| Servo Motor (RC) | Angular position | PWM pulse (1–2 ms) | Easy position control, integrated gear | Limited to ~180° rotation | Robotic arms, steering, pan-tilt |
| Solenoid | Linear push/pull | On/off voltage | Simple, fast, strong force | Limited stroke, on/off only | Door locks, valves, dot-matrix printers |
| Relay | Mechanical switch | Coil voltage | Full galvanic isolation, high current | Slow, mechanical wear, arcing | AC switching, high-power loads |
| Speaker / Buzzer | Sound (acoustical) | PWM / DAC | Audible feedback, simple | Limited to audible range | Alarms, HMI feedback |
| LED | Light | Current (mA) | Fast, efficient, durable | Needs current limiting | Indicators, displays, optical comm. |
| Piezo Buzzer | Sound / Vibration | AC voltage | Small, low power | Low SPL | Alarms, beepers, haptic feedback |
| Peltier (TEC) | Heating / Cooling | DC current | Reversible heat flow | Inefficient, needs heatsink | Portable coolers, incubators |
| Heater (Resistive) | Heat | PWM (current) | Simple, cheap | Slow, hot surfaces | 3D printer beds, soldering stations |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Sensor–Actuator Loop in an Embedded System

The following Mermaid diagram depicts the complete **closed-loop flow** from physical stimulus to physical action, as it would appear in a KTU 2024 board answer.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["PHYSICAL STIMULUS\nTemperature, Pressure, Light, Motion"]
    nodeB["SENSOR\nTransducer Stage"]
    nodeC["SIGNAL CONDITIONING\nAmplifier, Filter, Bridge"]
    nodeD["ADC\nAnalog to Digital Converter"]
    nodeE["MICROCONTROLLER\nDecision and Control Logic"]
    nodeF["DAC or PWM\nDigital to Analog or Pulse Width"]
    nodeG["DRIVER / H BRIDGE\nPower Amplification Stage"]
    nodeH["ACTUATOR\nDC / Stepper / Servo / Solenoid"]
    nodeI["PHYSICAL ACTION\nMotion, Heat, Light, Sound"]
    nodeJ["FEEDBACK SENSOR\nMonitors Output"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeJ -.-> nodeE
```

**How to read this diagram for the exam:**

- The **forward path (solid arrows)** traces the stimulus from the physical world → sensor → ADC → MCU → DAC/PWM → driver → actuator → physical action.
- The **feedback path (dotted arrow)** closes the loop by re-sensing the actuator's output. This is the heart of any **closed-loop control** system (e.g., cruise control, thermostat, robotic arm).
- The **signal conditioning** block is often the most failure-prone block in practice, since raw sensor signals are typically weak, noisy, or non-linear.

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Typical Signal Conditioning Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA["RAW SENSOR OUTPUT\nmV, uA, or high-Z"]
    nodeB["INSTRUMENTATION AMP\nINA128 / AD620"]
    nodeC["LOW PASS FILTER\nRC or Active Filter"]
    nodeD["LEVEL SHIFTER\n0 to Vref for ADC"]
    nodeE["ADC CHANNEL\n12-bit / 16-bit"]
    nodeF["MICROCONTROLLER\nReads, Processes"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Tip:** When asked *"Explain the signal conditioning required for a strain gauge / thermocouple / thermistor"*, draw this block diagram and explain the role of each stage in 2–3 lines. This alone is worth 4–5 marks out of 14.

## 4.5 Interfacing Diagram: DC Motor with Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    mcu["MICROCONTROLLER GPIO\n3.3V Logic, 20 mA Max"]
    pwm["PWM SIGNAL\nUp to 25 kHz"]
    dir["DIRECTION BIT\nHigh or Low"]
    drv["H BRIDGE DRIVER IC\nL293D or L298N"]
    mot["DC MOTOR\nUp to 12V, 1A"]
    fb["OPTICAL ENCODER\nSpeed Feedback"]

    mcu --> pwm
    mcu --> dir
    pwm --> drv
    dir --> drv
    drv --> mot
    mot --> fb
    fb -.-> mcu
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the H-bridge?** A microcontroller GPIO cannot directly supply the current required by a DC motor (typically 100 mA to several amps). The H-bridge is a power amplifier that **isolates the low-power logic from the high-power motor** and also allows **bidirectional control** (forward/reverse) using two direction bits.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between active and passive sensors with two examples each. Mention one application of each type.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor |
|---|---|---|
| Power Source | Self-generating (no external excitation needed) | Requires external excitation (voltage or current) |
| Working Principle | Directly converts physical stimulus to electrical signal | Modulates the external excitation based on stimulus |
| Examples | Thermocouple, Piezoelectric sensor, Photovoltaic cell | Thermistor, LDR, Strain gauge, RTD |
| Application | Industrial furnace temperature monitoring (thermocouple) | Ambient light sensing in streetlights (LDR) |

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for examples, 1 mark for application – KTU standard 3-mark format.)*

### Question A2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "sensitivity" of a sensor. A pressure sensor produces 10 mV at 100 kPa and 30 mV at 300 kPa. Find its sensitivity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Sensitivity of a sensor is the ratio of the change in output signal to the change in the corresponding input stimulus. It is the slope of the calibration curve and is expressed in output units per input unit (e.g., mV/kPa, V/°C).

**Numerical (2 marks):**

$$S = \frac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta P} = \frac{30\,\text{mV} - 10\,\text{mV}}{300\,\text{kPa} - 100\,\text{kPa}}$$

$$S = \frac{20\,\text{mV}}{200\,\text{kPa}} = 0.1\,\text{mV/kPa}$$
