topics.title: Other System Components
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Other System Components

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of "Other System Components" in Embedded Systems

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECPT861 (Advanced Embedded Systems)**, the term **"Other System Components"** refers to all the supporting hardware peripherals and subsystems that surround the central processing unit (CPU) or microcontroller core to form a complete, functional embedded system. While the CPU/MCU acts as the "brain," these other components act as the "senses," "muscles," "memory," and "voice" of the system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:**
> *Other System Components* in an embedded system comprise the auxiliary hardware blocks — including memory subsystems, sensors, actuators, communication interfaces, display units, timers, analog-to-digital/digital-to-analog converters, and interrupt controllers — that interface with the processor core to enable data acquisition, processing, storage, and physical-world interaction in real-time.

### 1.2 The Major Categories

A typical embedded system is an orchestration of the following "other" components working in concert with the processor:

| Category | Function | Real-World Analogy |
|----------|----------|--------------------|
| **Memory** (ROM, RAM, Flash, EEPROM) | Permanent & temporary data storage | The "filing cabinet" of the brain |
| **Sensors** | Convert physical parameters to electrical signals | The "five senses" (eyes, ears, skin) |
| **Actuators** | Convert electrical signals to physical action | The "muscles" that move things |
| **Communication Interfaces** (UART, SPI, I²C, CAN, USB) | Exchange data with other devices | The "voice" and "hearing" |
| **Display Devices** (LCD, LED, OLED, Seven-Segment) | Visual feedback to the user | The "facial expressions" |
| **Timers & Counters** | Precise time measurement and event counting | The "internal clock" |
| **ADC / DAC** | Bridge between analog and digital domains | The "translator" |
| **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** | System reset on hang/fault | The "safety net" |
| **RTC (Real-Time Clock)** | Maintain calendar time | The "wristwatch" |
| **Power Management Unit (PMU)** | Voltage regulation & power efficiency | The "heart" pumping energy |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body

Imagine a human body as an embedded system:
- The **CPU/MCU** is the **brain** — it thinks and makes decisions.
- The **sensors** (temperature sensor, pressure sensor, accelerometer) are the **skin, eyes, and ears** — they collect data from the environment.
- The **actuators** (motors, solenoids, relays) are the **muscles** — they perform physical actions.
- The **memory** is the **memory** — both short-term (RAM) and long-term (ROM/Flash).
- The **communication interfaces** (UART, I²C) are the **nervous system and vocal cords** — they transmit information.
- The **power supply and PMU** are the **heart and blood** — they provide energy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Students must be able to identify, describe, and select appropriate peripheral components for a given embedded application (e.g., automotive, IoT, medical, industrial control) and explain their interfacing mechanisms with the processor bus.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

Key electrical and timing constants used while specifying these components:

- **Logic Voltage Levels:** **TTL — 5 V (V_OH = 2.4 V, V_OL = 0.4 V)**; **CMOS — 3.3 V (V_OH = 2.4 V, V_OL = 0.4 V)**; **LVCMOS — 1.8 V / 1.2 V**.
- **Clock Frequency Range:** **32.768 kHz** (low-power RTC crystal) to **> 1 GHz** (high-performance MCUs).
- **ADC Resolution:** typically **8-bit, 10-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit, 24-bit** (quantization levels = $2^N$).
- **Standard Bus Speeds:** I²C = **100 kbps (Standard), 400 kbps (Fast), 3.4 Mbps (High-Speed)**; SPI = up to **> 50 Mbps**; UART = up to **115.2 kbps** (typical) or higher.
- **Memory Access Time:** typically **ns** (nanoseconds) for SRAM, **µs** for Flash.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Memory Subsystem — The Storage Hierarchy

Memory in an embedded system follows a strict hierarchy based on **speed vs. volatility vs. cost**:

$$
\text{Registers} \; > \; \text{Cache} \; > \; \text{Internal SRAM} \; > \; \text{External SRAM/DRAM} \; > \; \text{Flash/EEPROM} \; > \; \text{Secondary Storage (SD card)}
$$

**Types of Memory:**

- **ROM (Read-Only Memory):** Programmed at the time of manufacture. Used for bootloaders and fixed firmware.
- **PROM (Programmable ROM):** One-time programmable by the user.
- **EPROM (Erasable PROM):** Erasable via UV light through a quartz window.
- **EEPROM (Electrically EPROM):** Byte-wise erasable, electrically — non-volatile.
- **Flash Memory:** Block-wise erasable, high-density, non-volatile. Foundation of modern firmware storage (NOR Flash for code, NAND Flash for data).
- **SRAM (Static RAM):** Uses 6 transistors per bit, fast, volatile, no refresh needed.
- **DRAM (Dynamic RAM):** Uses 1 transistor + 1 capacitor per bit, needs periodic refresh, denser, cheaper.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Questions on memory selection, mapping, and the difference between Harvard vs. von Neumann architecture (which determines whether program & data memory are separate) are recurring in Module 1.

### 2.2 Sensors — The Data Acquisition Front-End

A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a physical quantity (temperature, pressure, light, motion) into an electrical signal (analog voltage, current, or digital code).

**Classification by Output:**
- **Analog Sensors:** LM35 (temperature, 10 mV/°C), TMP36, photodiodes.
- **Digital Sensors:** DHT11/DHT22 (humidity + temperature, single-wire protocol), DS18B20 (1-Wire), MPU6050 (I²C accelerometer + gyroscope), BMP280 (I²C/SPI pressure sensor).

**Key Sensor Specifications:**
- **Range:** minimum and maximum measurable value.
- **Accuracy:** closeness to true value.
- **Precision / Resolution:** smallest detectable change.
- **Sensitivity:** $\Delta \text{output} / \Delta \text{input}$.
- **Linearity:** how linear the response curve is.
- **Response Time:** how quickly the output settles.

### 2.3 Actuators — The Physical Action Output

Actuators convert electrical signals into physical action:

- **DC Motors:** Continuous rotation, speed controlled by PWM.
- **Servo Motors:** Position-controlled (0°–180°), driven by PWM at 50 Hz.
- **Stepper Motors:** Precise angular stepping, no feedback needed.
- **Solenoids:** Linear push/pull motion.
- **Relays:** Electromechanical switches for high-voltage/current.
- **Piezoelectric Buzzers:** Sound generation.
- **LEDs / Laser Diodes:** Light emission.

### 2.4 Communication Interfaces

A bus is a shared communication pathway. Key serial protocols:

| Protocol | Wires | Speed | Topology | Full-Duplex? |
|----------|-------|-------|----------|--------------|
| **UART** | TX, RX (2) | Up to ~1 Mbps | Point-to-point | Yes |
| **SPI** | SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS (4) | 10–50+ Mbps | Master-Slave | Yes |
| **I²C** | SDA, SCL (2) | 100k / 400k / 3.4M bps | Multi-master, multi-slave | Half-duplex |
| **CAN** | CANH, CANL (2) | 1 Mbps | Multi-master bus | Half-duplex |
| **1-Wire** | DQ (1) + GND | ~16 kbps | Single master | Half-duplex |
| **USB** | D+, D- (2) | 12 Mbps (FS) to 10 Gbps | Host-Device | Yes |

### 2.5 ADC and DAC — The Analog-Digital Bridge

**ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Samples a continuous analog voltage and quantizes it into a digital number.

- **Resolution:** $N$ bits → $2^N$ discrete levels.
- **Step Size (LSB Voltage):**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF_{high}} - V_{REF_{low}}}{2^N}
$$

- **Quantization Error:** Maximum error is $\pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2}$.
- **SNR (Signal-to-Noise Ratio):**

$$
\text{SNR (dB)} \approx 6.02N + 1.76
$$

**DAC (Digital-to-Analog Converter):** Converts digital codes into analog voltage.
- Output: $V_{out} = V_{REF} \times \frac{D}{2^N - 1}$, where $D$ is the digital input code.

### 2.6 Timers, Counters, and Watchdog Timer

- **Timer:** Counts clock pulses to measure time intervals.
- **Counter:** Counts external events on a pin.
- **Watchdog Timer (WDT):** A separate hardware timer that resets the MCU if not "kicked" (refreshed) periodically by software — protects against software lockups.

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE — Memorize for KTU ESE.**

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Unit / Notes |
|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| ADC Step Size | $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF_{high}} - V_{REF_{low}}}{2^N}$ | Volts (V) |
| ADC Quantization Error | $\varepsilon_q = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$ | Volts (V) |
| Theoretical SNR of ADC | $\text{SNR} = 6.02N + 1.76$ | Decibels (dB) |
| Sampling Theorem (Nyquist) | $f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}$ | Hertz (Hz) |
| Memory Capacity | $C = 2^N \times M$ where $N = \text{address bits}$, $M = \text{data width}$ | bits |
| Baud Rate (UART) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{f_{clk}}{\text{Divisor}}$ | bits per second |
| I²C Pull-up Resistance | $R_{pull-up} \approx \dfrac{V_{DD} - V_{OL}}{I_{OL}}$ | Ohms (Ω) |
| Power Dissipation (CMOS) | $P = C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f$ | Watts (W) |
| PWM Duty Cycle | $D\% = \dfrac{T_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100$ | Percentage (%) |
| Sensor Sensitivity | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V_{out}}{\Delta \text{Physical Input}}$ | V/unit |
| Capacitor Energy (PMU) | $E = \dfrac{1}{2}CV^2$ | Joules (J) |

**Real-World Engineering Utility:**
- **ADC + Sensor:** Foundational in IoT weather stations, biomedical ECG/EEG machines, industrial process control.
- **CAN bus:** Standard in automobiles (OBD-II, ECUs) due to noise immunity.
- **Watchdog Timer:** Mandatory in ISO 26262 (automotive functional safety) compliant systems.
- **RTC + Low-Power MCU:** Core of wearable fitness bands that run for months on a coin cell.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ADC Step Size (LSB Voltage) and Quantization Error

**Problem Statement:**
A 12-bit ADC in an embedded system uses a reference voltage $V_{REF_{high}} = 3.3\,\text{V}$ and $V_{REF_{low}} = 0\,\text{V}$. Determine (a) the step size $V_{LSB}$, and (b) the maximum quantization error.

**Step 1 — Identify the total number of quantization levels.**

For an $N$-bit ADC, the total number of discrete levels is:

$$
L = 2^N
$$

Substituting $N = 12$:

$$
L = 2^{12} = 4096 \text{ levels}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the step-size formula.**

The step size (also called the LSB voltage) is the voltage difference between two adjacent digital codes:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF_{high}} - V_{REF_{low}}}{2^N}
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3\,\text{V} - 0\,\text{V}}{4096}
$$

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{3.3}{4096}\,\text{V}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerical value.**

$$
V_{LSB} = 0.0008057\,\text{V} \approx 0.806\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the maximum quantization error.**

The maximum quantization error is half of the LSB voltage (since any analog value can lie anywhere within ±½ LSB of the nearest digital code):

$$
\varepsilon_{q_{max}} = \pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2}
$$

$$
\varepsilon_{q_{max}} = \pm \frac{0.806\,\text{mV}}{2} = \pm 0.403\,\text{mV}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (engineering insight).**

A 12-bit ADC with a 3.3 V reference can resolve voltage changes as small as **0.806 mV**. This is why a 12-bit ADC is used in precision applications like digital multimeters and audio codecs, while an 8-bit ADC ($V_{LSB} = 12.89\,\text{mV}$) suffices for simple joystick readings.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR) of an Ideal ADC

**Step 1 — Define the theoretical SNR formula.**

For an ideal $N$-bit ADC where quantization is the only source of noise, the signal-to-noise ratio in decibels is given by:

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02N + 1.76
$$

**Step 2 — Verify for a 16-bit ADC (common in audio).**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 16 + 1.76
$$

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 96.32 + 1.76 = 98.08\,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify for a 12-bit ADC.**

$$
\text{SNR}_{dB} = 6.02 \times 12 + 1.76 = 72.24 + 1.76 = 74.0\,\text{dB}
$$

**Step 4 — Real-world meaning.**

A 12-bit ADC theoretically yields a 74 dB dynamic range — equivalent to roughly **12.3-bit effective resolution** in practical terms (since real-world noise, nonlinearity, and jitter reduce SNR by 1–2 bits).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Memory Capacity Calculation

**Problem Statement:**
An embedded system uses a memory chip with 16 address lines ($A_0$ to $A_{15}$) and a data bus of 8 bits. Calculate the total memory capacity in bytes.

**Step 1 — Compute the total number of addressable locations.**

$$
\text{Locations} = 2^{A} = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ locations}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by the data width.**

$$
\text{Capacity (in bits)} = 65{,}536 \times 8 = 524{,}288 \text{ bits}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert to bytes.**

$$
\text{Capacity (in Bytes)} = \frac{524{,}288}{8} = 65{,}536 \text{ Bytes} = 64\,\text{KB}
$$

**Step 4 — Generalization.**

$$
\text{Capacity (Bytes)} = \frac{2^A \times M}{8}
$$

where $A$ = number of address lines, $M$ = data bus width in bits.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling Theorem Applied to Sensor Data

**Problem Statement:**
A temperature sensor in a smart wearable outputs a signal whose highest frequency component is 50 Hz. Determine the minimum sampling rate for an ADC to accurately reconstruct the signal.

**Step 1 — Apply the Nyquist criterion.**

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times f_{max}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$
f_s \geq 2 \times 50\,\text{Hz} = 100\,\text{Hz}
$$

**Step 3 — Practical rule.**

Engineers typically sample at **5× to 10×** the maximum frequency to allow for anti-aliasing filter roll-off. Hence:

$$
f_{s_{practical}} = 500\,\text{Hz to } 1000\,\text{Hz}
$$

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Communication Protocols (For KTU Long-Answer)

| Parameter | UART | SPI | I²C |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|
| **Signal Lines** | TX, RX, GND (asynchronous) | SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS | SDA, SCL (open-drain) |
| **Clock** | No shared clock | Shared clock (SCLK) | Shared clock (SCL) |
| **Speed** | Up to 1 Mbps | 10–50+ Mbps | 100k/400k/3.4M bps |
| **Devices on Bus** | Point-to-point | Multi-slave (one SS per slave) | Up to 127 (7-bit addr) |
| **Duplex** | Full | Full | Half |
| **Hardware Complexity** | Low | Medium | Low (needs pull-ups) |
| **Use Case** | Debug console, GPS, Bluetooth | SD cards, displays, fast sensors | EEPROM, RTC, low-speed sensors |

---

### 3.6 Worked Example: PWM Duty Cycle for Servo Motor Control

**Problem Statement:**
A hobby servo motor requires a 50 Hz PWM signal (20 ms period). To position at 0°, the pulse must be 1 ms wide. At 180°, the pulse must be 2 ms wide. Find the duty cycle for 90°.

**Step 1 — Determine the pulse width at 90°.**

The pulse width varies linearly from 1 ms (0°) to 2 ms (180°). For 90° (midpoint):

$$
t_{on} = 1\,\text{ms} + \frac{(2\,\text{ms} - 1\,\text{ms})}{2} = 1.5\,\text{ms}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the duty cycle formula.**

$$
D\% = \frac{t_{on}}{T_{period}} \times 100
$$

$$
D\% = \frac{1.5\,\text{ms}}{20\,\text{ms}} \times 100 = 7.5\%
$$

**Step 3 — Engineering takeaway.**

A servo is controlled **not by varying frequency, but by varying the duty cycle of a fixed-frequency PWM**. The pulse width encodes the desired angle.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — A Typical Embedded System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Power Supply and PMU] --> B[CPU or MCU Core]
    B --> C[Memory Subsystem]
    C --> C1[ROM and Flash]
    C --> C2[SRAM and DRAM]
    C --> C3[EEPROM]
    B --> D[Peripheral Interfaces]
    D --> D1[ADC and DAC]
    D --> D2[Timers and WDT]
    D --> D3[Communication UART SPI I2C CAN]
    D --> D4[Interrupt Controller]
    B --> E[External World Interface]
    E --> E1[Sensors]
    E --> E2[Actuators]
    E --> E3[Display Devices]
    E --> E4[User Input Keys]
    F[External Devices] --> D3
    D3 --> F
```

**Description of the flow:**

1. The **Power Supply / PMU** energizes the entire system.
2. The **CPU/MCU Core** fetches program code from **ROM/Flash** and uses **SRAM/DRAM** for runtime data.
3. **EEPROM** stores configuration parameters that survive power cycles.
4. The CPU drives **Peripheral Interfaces** (ADC/DAC, Timers, Communication blocks, Interrupt Controller).
5. These peripherals in turn interact with the **External World** via sensors, actuators, displays, and keypads.
6. **Communication blocks** (UART/SPI/I²C/CAN) link the system to other external devices.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Memory Subsystem Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    M0[CPU Registers fastest smallest] --> M1[Cache L1 L2]
    M1 --> M2[Internal SRAM few KB to few hundred KB]
    M2 --> M3[External SRAM DRAM MB range]
    M3 --> M4[Flash Memory code and data]
    M4 --> M5[EEPROM configuration data]
    M5 --> M6[Secondary Storage SD card eMMC]
```

**Reading direction:** The further down the hierarchy, the **slower**, **larger**, and **cheaper** (per byte) the memory becomes.

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — ADC/DAC Signal Conditioning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Physical Quantity Temperature Pressure Light] --> P2[Sensor Transduction]
    P2 --> P3[Analog Signal Conditioning Amplification and Filtering]
    P3 --> P4[Sample and Hold Circuit]
    P4 --> P5[ADC Quantization and Encoding]
    P5 --> P6[Digital Processing in CPU]
    P6 --> P7[DAC Reconstruction]
    P7 --> P8[Analog Output to Actuator]
```

**Notes on each block:**

- **Sensor Transduction:** Converts physical quantity to raw analog voltage.
- **Signal Conditioning:** Amplifies (e.g., with op-amp) and low-pass filters the signal.
- **Sample and Hold:** Freezes the analog voltage during conversion.
- **ADC:** Converts to N-bit digital code.
- **DAC (in reverse path):** Reconstructs analog waveform from digital data (e.g., audio output).

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Watchdog Timer Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[System Start] --> S1[Initialize Hardware and Peripherals]
    S1 --> S2[Application Main Loop Begins]
    S2 --> S3[Execute Critical Tasks]
    S3 --> S4{Kick Watchdog Timer}
    S4 -- Yes kicked in time --> S2
    S4 -- No timeout occurred --> S5[Watchdog Resets MCU]
    S5 --> S1
```

**Working principle:** If the main loop hangs (e.g., infinite deadlock), the **kick** (write to WDT register) does not occur within the timeout window. The WDT overflows and forces a **hardware reset**, bringing the system back to a known good state.

---

### 4.5 Block Diagram — SPI Master-Slave Interconnection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MASTER[SPI Master MCU] -->|SCLK| S1
    MASTER -->|MOSI| S2
    MASTER -->|SS1| S1
    MASTER -->|SS2| S2
    S1[SPI Slave 1 e.g. Flash] -->|MISO| MASTER
    S2[SPI Slave 2 e.g. Sensor] -->|MISO| MASTER
    S3[SPI Slave 3 e.g. Display] -->|MISO| MASTER
    MASTER -->|SS3| S3
```

**Description:** A single SPI master controls multiple slaves via dedicated **Slave Select (SS)** lines. The master drives SCLK and MOSI; selected slave drives MISO.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*List and briefly explain any three types of memory used in embedded systems. Differentiate between volatile and non-volatile memory with one example each. (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The three common memory types in embedded systems are:

1. **ROM (Read-Only Memory):** Non-volatile memory used to store permanent firmware/boot code. Cannot be rewritten by the CPU during normal operation.
2. **SRAM (Static RAM):** Volatile, high-speed memory used as working data memory (stack, heap, variables). Built using 6-transistor flip-flops; does not require periodic refresh.
3. **Flash Memory:** Non-volatile, electrically erasable, block-wise memory used to store the application firmware. Modern MCUs (e.g., STM32, ESP32) use NOR Flash for program storage.

**Volatile vs Non-Volatile:**

- **Volatile memory** loses its contents when power is removed. *Example:* SRAM.
- **Non-volatile memory** retains its contents even without power. *Example:* Flash memory.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
*Explain the working of a Watchdog Timer (WDT) in an embedded system. Why is it essential in safety-critical applications? (3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Watchdog Timer (WDT)** is a hardware timer that runs independently of the main CPU clock. The application software must periodically "kick" or "feed" the WDT (write a specific value to its register) before the timer expires.

- If the software is healthy, the WDT is reset before timeout → system continues normally.
- If the software hangs (e.g., infinite loop, deadlock, EMI-induced glitch), the WDT is **not** kicked → it overflows and triggers a **hardware reset** of the MCU, restoring the system to a known state.

**Importance in safety-critical systems:** WDTs are mandatory in **automotive ECUs** (compliant with ISO 26262), **medical devices** (pacemakers, ventilators), and **avionics** to ensure recovery from software faults without human intervention.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **confuse memory types** (e.g., stating EEPROM is volatile — INCORRECT). Always specify "non-volatile" for Flash/EEPROM/ROM and "volatile" for SRAM/DRAM.
> - In **ADC problems**, students forget to use the correct formula $V_{LSB} = V_{REF} / 2^N$ (not $V_{REF} / N$). Marks are deducted for this.
> - For **protocol comparisons**, students list protocols but do **not specify the number of signal lines, speed, or topology** — partial credit only.
> - Do **not** omit diagrams in long-answer questions. Even a rough block diagram can earn 1–2 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the major "other system components" (peripherals) found in a typical embedded system. Classify them into at least four functional categories and give one example component for each category. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of an ADC-based data acquisition system. An 8-bit ADC has a reference voltage of 5 V. Calculate (i) the LSB voltage (step size) and (ii) the maximum quantization error. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

The major peripheral components in an embedded system are grouped into the following functional categories:

1. **Memory Components:** Store program and data.
   - *Example:* Flash memory (program storage) + SRAM (runtime data).
2. **Sensors (Input Transducers):** Convert physical quantities into electrical signals.
   - *Example:* LM35 temperature sensor, MPU6050 accelerometer.
3. **Actuators (Output Transducers):** Convert electrical signals into physical action.
   - *Example:* DC motor (via H-bridge driver like L298N), servo motor.
4. **Communication Interfaces:** Enable data exchange with other devices.
   - *Example:* UART (debug console), I²C (inter-IC communication), SPI (display, SD card), CAN (automotive).
5. **Display Devices:** Provide visual feedback.
   - *Example:* 16×2 LCD (HD44780), 7-segment LED display, OLED.
6. **Timers / Counters & Watchdog:** Time-critical functions and fault recovery.
   - *Example:* Hardware Timer module, independent WDT.
7. **Power Management:** Voltage regulation and battery management.
   - *Example:* LDO regulators (LM7805), buck converters (TPS5430).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Block Diagram (refer to SECTION 4.3 — ADC pipeline).**

**Given:** ADC resolution $N = 8\,\text{bit}$, $V_{REF_{high}} = 5\,\text{V}$, $V_{REF_{low}} = 0\,\text{V}$.

**(i) LSB Voltage (Step Size):**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{REF_{high}} - V_{REF_{low}}}{2^N} = \frac{5 - 0}{2^8} = \frac{5}{256}
$$

$$
V_{LSB} = 0.01953\,\text{V} \approx 19.53\,\text{mV}
$$

**(ii) Maximum Quantization Error:**

$$
\varepsilon_{q_{max}} = \pm \frac{V_{LSB}}{2} = \pm \frac{19.53\,\text{mV}}{2} = \pm 9.77\,\text{mV}
$$

**Conclusion:** The 8-bit ADC can resolve voltage changes of about **19.53 mV**, with a worst-case quantization error of **±9.77 mV**. This is suitable for applications like potentiometer-based position sensing but inadequate for precision instrumentation (where 12-bit or 16-bit ADCs are preferred).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare the four major serial communication protocols used in embedded systems — UART, SPI, I²C, and CAN — based on the following parameters: number of signal lines, speed, topology, duplex mode, and a typical application. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Describe the working of a Watchdog Timer with a state diagram. A real-time embedded system uses a Watchdog Timer with a 2-second timeout. The main loop of the application must execute every 500 ms. Explain how the WDT prevents system lockup, and what happens if the application crashes for 3 seconds. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | UART | SPI | I²C | CAN |
|-----------|------|-----|-----|-----|
| Signal Lines | TX, RX (2) | SCLK, MOSI, MISO, SS (4) | SDA, SCL (2) | CANH, CANL (2) |
| Speed | Up to 1 Mbps | 10–50+ Mbps | 100k/400k/3.4M bps | 125k–1 Mbps |
| Topology | Point-to-point | Master-Multi-Slave | Multi-Master, Multi-Slave (up to 127) | Multi-Master Bus |
| Duplex | Full | Full | Half | Half |
| Typical App | Debug console, GPS module, Bluetooth | SD card, TFT display, high-speed sensors | RTC (DS3231), EEPROM (AT24Cxx), low-speed sensors | Automotive ECU networks (OBD-II), industrial automation |

**Key Engineering Insight:** SPI offers the highest speed and is used for data-intensive peripherals. I²C minimizes pin usage (2 wires for many devices) at the cost of speed. CAN is preferred in noise-immune applications like vehicles.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Working of WDT (refer to SECTION 4.4 for state diagram):**

- The WDT is a hardware counter that decrements (or increments) at a fixed rate derived from an internal low-speed oscillator.
- The application must "kick" or "refresh" the WDT (write a specific key sequence to the WDT register) **before** it reaches zero.
- In a healthy system, the main loop executes every **500 ms**, and the WDT is kicked at the start of each iteration, well within the **2-second** timeout.
- **Healthy cycle:** Kick @ 0 ms → 500 ms → 1000 ms → 1500 ms → 2000 ms (timeout never reached).

**Scenario: Application crashes for 3 seconds:**

- The CPU halts (infinite loop, null pointer crash, or hardware fault).
- The WDT is **not** kicked for 3 seconds.
- At **t = 2000 ms**, the WDT counter underflows → triggers a **system reset**.
- The MCU restarts from the reset vector, re-initializes hardware, and resumes normal operation.
- The 3-second crash window is **capped at 2 seconds** by the WDT — total downtime is bounded.

**Conclusion:** WDTs enforce a **maximum fault recovery time** and are essential for unattended, safety-critical embedded systems.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Must Memorize Before KTU ESE**

✅ **Other System Components** = everything in the embedded system *except* the CPU/MCU core.

✅ **Memory Hierarchy (fastest → slowest):** Registers → Cache → SRAM → DRAM → Flash → EEPROM → SD/eMMC.

✅ **Volatile** = loses data on power-off (SRAM, DRAM). **Non-volatile** = retains data (Flash, EEPROM, ROM).

✅ **Harvard Architecture** (AVR, PIC) = separate program & data memory buses. **von Neumann** (8051, ARM7 traditional) = shared bus.

✅ **Sensors** = input (physical → electrical). **Actuators** = output (electrical → physical).

✅ **ADC Formula (HIGH-YIELD):**
  - $V_{LSB} = \dfrac{V_{REF}}{2^N}$
  - $\varepsilon_q = \pm \dfrac{V_{LSB}}{2}$
  - $\text{SNR (dB)} = 6.02N + 1.76$

✅ **Nyquist Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (sample at least twice the highest frequency).

✅ **Communication Protocols — Quick Memory Aid:**
  - **UART** = Asynchronous, TX/RX, point-to-point.
  - **SPI** = 4-wire, fastest, master-slave with SS lines.
  - **I²C** = 2-wire, addressable, up to 127 devices, needs pull-ups.
  - **CAN** = 2-wire differential, automotive-grade noise immunity.

✅ **Watchdog Timer (WDT):** Independent hardware timer → triggers reset if not "kicked" within timeout. Mandatory in **safety-critical** systems (ISO 26262, IEC 61508).

✅ **PWM Servo Control:** Fixed 50 Hz frequency, variable duty cycle (1 ms = 0°, 1.5 ms = 90°, 2 ms = 180°).

✅ **Bus Arbitration** is needed in multi-master protocols (I²C, CAN) to prevent data collision.

✅ **Pull-up resistors (typically 4.7 kΩ for I²C)** are mandatory on SDA/SCL lines because both are open-drain.

✅ **RTC (DS3231, PCF8563)** uses a **32.768 kHz** crystal — the $2^{15}$ division yields a precise 1-second tick for timekeeping.

✅ **PMU (Power Management Unit)** typically includes LDO regulators, DC-DC converters, and battery charging circuits (e.g., BQ24075 for Li-Ion).

✅ **Display Rule of Thumb:** 16×2 LCD (HD44780) uses 4-bit/8-bit parallel; OLED (SSD1306) uses I²C or SPI.

✅ **Common Pitfall in KTU exams:** Students confuse **Harvard vs. von Neumann** when listing memory components — clarify whether the architecture uses unified or separate address spaces.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics and Quality Attributes of Embedded Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Characteristics and Quality Attributes of Embedded Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Embedded System (KTU 2024 Definition):** An embedded system is a **microprocessor/microcontroller-based, software-driven, reliable, real-time control system** that is **dedicated to performing a specific function**, is **constrained by physical hardware resources**, and operates as a **self-contained component** embedded within a larger mechanical or electrical system.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus framework, every embedded system is fundamentally characterized by **two major pillars**:

1. **Characteristics** → The **intrinsic, behavioral, and structural features** that define *what an embedded system is* and *how it behaves* (functional/architectural traits).
2. **Quality Attributes** → The **non-functional, measurable properties** that define *how well* the embedded system performs its dedicated task (performance, power, reliability, etc.).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Distinction (Frequently Tested):** **Characteristics** describe the *nature* of the system (e.g., single-functioned, reactive, real-time), whereas **Quality Attributes** are the *engineering metrics* used to evaluate it (e.g., power, cost, latency, MTBF). Do not interchange these terms in your exam answers — a 2-mark deduction is common when students mix them up.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **smartwatch**:

- It is **not** a general-purpose computer — you cannot install arbitrary software on it the way you would on a laptop. It is **designed only** to track heart rate, count steps, and show notifications. → This is a **Characteristic (Single-functioned / Dedicated)**.
- It must respond to your finger tap **within milliseconds**; if it lags, it is "broken." → This is a **Characteristic (Real-time / Reactive)**.
- It must run for **days** on a tiny 300 mAh battery, fit in a 40 mm case, and cost under ₹3,000. → These are **Quality Attributes (Power, Size, Cost)**.
- If it crashes on your wrist, you may miss an important alert. → This is a **Quality Attribute (Reliability / Safety)**.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for Exams:** *Characteristics = "What it IS"*, *Quality Attributes = "How GOOD it is"*. Use this phrasing in definitions to score full marks.

---

## 1.3 Characteristics of Embedded Systems (Complete List)

An embedded system exhibits the following defining features:

| # | Characteristic | Engineering Meaning |
|---|----------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Single-functioned / Single-purpose** | Executes a **predefined, dedicated task** repeatedly (e.g., washing machine timer). |
| 2 | **Tightly constrained** | Severely limited by **power, memory, CPU speed, and cost**. |
| 3 | **Reactive** | Continuously **responds to changes in the environment** via sensors/actuators. |
| 4 | **Real-time operation** | Must produce output **within a strict, deterministic deadline**. |
| 5 | **Processor/MCU-based** | Built around a **microcontroller, microprocessor, or SoC** with on-chip peripherals. |
| 6 | **Hardware-Software co-design** | Hardware (ASIC/FPGA) and firmware are **jointly optimized**. |
| 7 | **Minimal User Interface** | Often has **no keyboard, mouse, or display** — uses LEDs, switches, or simple LCDs. |
| 8 | **High Reliability & Stability** | Must operate **without failure for years** in unattended environments. |
| 9 | **Fault-tolerant / Robust** | Must **gracefully handle sensor failures or power glitches**. |
| 10 | **Low Power Operation** | Often **battery-powered**, requiring sleep modes and clock gating. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Style Categorization Tip:** Group the 10 characteristics into 3 clusters in your exam answers:
> - **Behavioral** → Reactive, Real-time, Single-functioned
> - **Structural** → Processor-based, HW-SW co-design, Minimal UI
> - **Operational** → Tightly constrained, Reliable, Robust, Low-power

---

## 1.4 Quality Attributes of Embedded Systems (Non-Functional Metrics)

Quality Attributes (also called **non-functional requirements** or **-ilities**) are the *measurable yardsticks* by which an embedded design is judged.

| # | Quality Attribute | Unit / Metric | Engineering Goal |
|---|-------------------|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | **Performance** | MIPS, DMIPS, Latency (ms) | Fast task execution and quick I/O response. |
| 2 | **Power Consumption** | mW, µW (active/sleep) | Maximize battery life. |
| 3 | **Cost** | USD, INR (BOM) | Minimize Bill of Materials for mass production. |
| 4 | **Size / Form Factor** | mm³, PCB area | Fit in compact enclosures (wearables, IoT). |
| 5 | **Reliability** | MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures, hours) | Operate failure-free for the mission lifetime. |
| 6 | **Maintainability / Serviceability** | MTTR (Mean Time To Repair, min) | Easy to diagnose, update firmware, replace parts. |
| 7 | **Safety** | SIL (Safety Integrity Level 1–4) | Prevent harm to humans (ISO 26262, IEC 61508). |
| 8 | **Security** | Attack Resistance Score | Prevent unauthorized access, firmware tampering. |
| 9 | **Flexibility / Upgradability** | API count, OTA capability | Adapt to new requirements via firmware updates. |
| 10 | **Time-to-Market** | Months from concept to release | Competitive advantage — first-mover benefit. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Note:** Quality attributes are often in **direct conflict (trade-offs)** with each other. For example: *Reducing cost* may force a slower CPU, which *reduces performance*. Designers must balance these using **optimization theory** and **Pareto analysis**. This trade-off theme is a **favorite 7-mark question**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analytical Decomposition of Characteristics

### A. Single-Functioned Operation

- An embedded system runs a **single application** repeatedly, often in an **infinite loop** (`while(1) { … }`).
- Contrast with a **general-purpose computer (PC)** which can run spreadsheets, browsers, games, etc., concurrently.
- **Implication:** The OS (if any) is typically an **RTOS (Real-Time Operating System)** — small, deterministic, and tailored to one workload.

> **Example:** A traffic light controller executes *only* the state machine `RED → GREEN → YELLOW`. It never runs a browser.

---

### B. Tightly-Constrained Design

The "**tightness**" of constraints is what differentiates embedded design from general-purpose design.

$$\text{Constraint Tightness} = \frac{\text{Available Resource}}{\text{Demanded Resource}} \approx 1.0$$

When this ratio is **≤ 1.2**, the system is said to be *tightly constrained* (less than 20% headroom).

The four cardinal constraints are:

| Constraint | Typical Embedded Value | General-Purpose PC |
|------------|------------------------|--------------------|
| RAM | 4 KB – 512 KB | 8 GB – 64 GB |
| ROM/Flash | 32 KB – 4 MB | 256 GB – 2 TB |
| CPU Clock | 8 MHz – 200 MHz | 2 GHz – 5 GHz |
| Power Budget | 1 mW – 500 mW | 65 W – 250 W |

---

### C. Reactive & Real-Time Behavior

- **Reactive:** A continuous **sense → compute → actuate** loop driven by **interrupts** and **polled inputs**.
- **Real-time:** The *correctness* of the output depends not only on its **logical value** but also on **the time at which it is delivered**.

Mathematically, a real-time task $T_i$ is characterized by a **4-tuple**:

$$T_i = (\phi_i, \; p_i, \; e_i, \; d_i)$$

Where:

- $\phi_i$ = **Release time** (when the task becomes ready)
- $p_i$ = **Period** (inter-arrival time)
- $e_i$ = **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)**
- $d_i$ = **Relative deadline** (latest allowable completion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition to Memorize:** A **Hard Real-Time System** is one in which missing a deadline is a **system failure** (e.g., airbag, anti-lock braking). A **Soft Real-Time System** allows occasional missed deadlines with **graceful degradation** (e.g., video streaming, GPS). This distinction is **mandatory** in any real-time question.

---

### D. Hardware-Software Co-Design

- Hardware and software are **co-designed and co-optimized**.
- Decisions like *"Should this be done in C code on the MCU, or in a hardware accelerator (FPGA/ASIC)?"* define the co-design process.
- The **performance–cost–power triangle** is the heart of co-design:

$$\text{Performance} \;\uparrow \quad \text{Cost} \;\uparrow \quad \text{Power} \;\uparrow$$

A co-designer's job is to find the **Pareto-optimal point** on this surface.

---

## 2.2 Analytical Decomposition of Quality Attributes

### A. Performance Metrics

**1. Throughput (Throughput Metric)**

$$\text{Throughput} = \frac{\text{Number of tasks completed}}{\text{Unit time}} \;\;[\text{tasks/sec}]$$

**2. Latency (Response Time)**

$$\text{Latency} = T_{\text{start}} - T_{\text{trigger}} \;\;[\mu s, ms]$$

**3. MIPS Rating (Million Instructions Per Second)**

$$\text{MIPS} = \frac{f_{\text{clk}}}{CPI \times 10^6}$$

Where $f_{\text{clk}}$ is the clock frequency (Hz) and $CPI$ is the average cycles per instruction.

**4. Utilization**

$$U = \frac{\text{Busy time}}{\text{Total time}} \times 100\%$$

---

### B. Power Consumption Model

Total power in a CMOS-based embedded system:

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{\text{dynamic}} + P_{\text{static}}$$

$$P_{\text{dynamic}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$$

$$P_{\text{static}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$$

Where:

- $\alpha$ = switching activity factor
- $C$ = load capacitance
- $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage
- $f_{\text{clk}}$ = clock frequency
- $I_{\text{leak}}$ = leakage current

> [!TIP]
> **Voltage Scaling Insight:** Since $P \propto V^2$, **dropping $V_{DD}$ by half cuts power to ¼** — the foundational principle behind **DVFS (Dynamic Voltage and Frequency Scaling)** used in modern MCUs like STM32 and ESP32.

**Effective battery life (in hours):**

$$T_{\text{battery}} = \frac{C_{\text{battery}} \cdot V_{\text{bat}}}{P_{\text{avg}}}$$

---

### C. Reliability — MTBF Calculation

For a system with components in **series reliability block diagram**:

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{system}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$

Where $\lambda_i$ is the failure rate of the $i$-th component (failures/hour).

**Availability:**

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

---

### D. The Cost–Performance Trade-off Curve

For any embedded system, the following holds:

$$\text{Performance} \propto f_{\text{clk}} \propto \text{Cost}$$

Doubling the clock frequency roughly **doubles performance but increases cost non-linearly** (more expensive MCU, more cooling, more PCB layers).

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Used For |
|---|---------|----------------|---------------|----------|
| 1 | $P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha C V_{DD}^2 f$ | Switching power | Watts (W) | Power optimization |
| 2 | $P_{\text{static}} = V_{DD} \cdot I_{\text{leak}}$ | Leakage power | Watts (W) | Sleep mode design |
| 3 | $\text{MIPS} = f_{\text{clk}} / (CPI \times 10^6)$ | Million Instructions Per Second | MIPS | Performance benchmarking |
| 4 | $T_{\text{bat}} = C_{\text{bat}} V_{\text{bat}} / P_{\text{avg}}$ | Battery life | Hours | Battery sizing |
| 5 | $\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} = 1 / \sum \lambda_i$ | Series system reliability | Hours | Reliability analysis |
| 6 | $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ | Availability | Dimensionless (0–1) | Uptime analysis |
| 7 | $U = T_{\text{busy}} / T_{\text{total}}$ | CPU utilization | Percentage (%) | RTOS scheduling |
| 8 | $J = \max(L_i - d_i, 0)$ | Tardiness/Jitter | ms | Real-time deadline check |
| 9 | $T_i = (\phi_i, p_i, e_i, d_i)$ | Real-time task tuple | Mixed | RTOS task definition |
| 10 | $S = K \cdot A \cdot e^{-E_a / kT}$ | Arrhenius reliability model | Failures/h | Accelerated life testing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Strategy:** Memorize **at least formulas 1, 3, 5, 6, and 7** — these account for >80% of the 7-mark analytical questions in KTU ESE.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Dominant Quality Attribute | Why |
|-------------|----------------------------|-----|
| **Automotive Airbag** | Real-time + Safety + Reliability | Must deploy in <10 ms; failure = death. |
| **Smartwatch** | Power + Size + Cost | 7-day battery in a 40 mm case. |
| **Drone Flight Controller** | Performance + Real-time | 1 kHz PID loop, must be deterministic. |
| **Industrial PLC** | Reliability + Maintainability | Operates 24×7 for 20 years in factories. |
| **Smart Card** | Security + Cost | Tamper-resistant, <$1 BOM. |
| **Satellite Avionics** | Reliability + Power | No maintenance possible; solar-powered. |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Power Optimization via Voltage Scaling

**Problem:** An embedded MCU runs at $V_{DD} = 3.3 \text{ V}$ with $f = 72 \text{ MHz}$, $C = 10 \text{ pF}$, $\alpha = 0.2$. Compute the dynamic power and find the new power when voltage is scaled to $1.8 \text{ V}$ at half frequency.

### Step 1 — Identify the governing equation

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = \alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{DD}^2 \cdot f_{\text{clk}}$$

This is the standard CMOS dynamic power model where:
- $\alpha$ represents the average fraction of gates switching per clock,
- $C$ is the total node capacitance,
- $V_{DD}^2$ captures charging energy per cycle,
- $f_{\text{clk}}$ is the switching rate.

### Step 2 — Substitute the given values

We plug in $\alpha = 0.2$, $C = 10 \times 10^{-12}$ F, $V_{DD} = 3.3$ V, $f = 72 \times 10^6$ Hz.

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 0.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (3.3)^2 \times 72 \times 10^6$$

### Step 3 — Square the voltage

$$(3.3)^2 = 10.89 \text{ V}^2$$

### Step 4 — Multiply the constants

$$0.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} = 2 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$2 \times 10^{-12} \times 10.89 = 2.178 \times 10^{-11}$$

$$2.178 \times 10^{-11} \times 72 \times 10^6 = 2.178 \times 72 \times 10^{-5}$$

$$2.178 \times 72 = 156.816$$

$$P_{\text{dyn}} = 156.816 \times 10^{-5} = 1.568 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W}$$

### Step 5 — Final answer (original)

$$P_{\text{dyn,orig}} \approx 1.57 \text{ mW}$$

### Step 6 — Repeat for scaled values

New values: $V_{DD}' = 1.8 \text{ V}$, $f' = 36 \text{ MHz}$.

$$P_{\text{dyn,new}} = 0.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times (1.8)^2 \times 36 \times 10^6$$

$(1.8)^2 = 3.24$

$$0.2 \times 10 \times 10^{-12} \times 3.24 = 6.48 \times 10^{-12}$$

$$6.48 \times 10^{-12} \times 36 \times 10^6 = 233.28 \times 10^{-6} = 2.33 \times 10^{-4} \text{ W}$$

### Step 7 — Compute reduction

$$P_{\text{dyn,new}} \approx 0.233 \text{ mW}$$

$$\text{Reduction factor} = \frac{1.568}{0.233} \approx 6.73 \times$$

$$\text{Power saving} = \left(1 - \frac{0.233}{1.568}\right) \times 100\% \approx 85.1\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** Halving the voltage to 1.8 V and frequency to 36 MHz yields an **85% power reduction** — demonstrating the **DVFS principle** used in nearly every low-power IoT MCU (STM32L, MSP430, nRF52).

---

## 3.2 Derivation: MTBF of a Series System

**Problem:** An embedded board has 3 ICs with failure rates $\lambda_1 = 50 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $\lambda_2 = 80 \times 10^{-6}$/h, $\lambda_3 = 30 \times 10^{-6}$/h. Find MTBF and availability if MTTR = 4 hours.

### Step 1 — Series system formula

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_1 + \lambda_2 + \lambda_3}$$

### Step 2 — Sum the failure rates

$$\sum \lambda_i = (50 + 80 + 30) \times 10^{-6} = 160 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

### Step 3 — Invert

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} = \frac{1}{160 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{10^6}{160} = 6250 \text{ hours}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} \approx 6250 \text{ h} \approx 260 \text{ days}$$

### Step 4 — Compute availability

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{6250}{6250 + 4} = \frac{6250}{6254}$$

$$A \approx 0.99936 = 99.936\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** This MTBF (6250 h) is typical for **consumer-grade** electronics. **Automotive** electronics require MTBF in the order of 100,000+ hours (≈ 11 years), achieved via **redundancy and component derating**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Real-Time Deadline Feasibility Check

**Problem:** Three periodic tasks have parameters as below. Check if the system is **schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS).

| Task | Period $p_i$ (ms) | WCET $e_i$ (ms) |
|------|-------------------|------------------|
| T1 | 10 | 2 |
| T2 | 20 | 5 |
| T3 | 50 | 8 |

### Step 1 — Apply Liu & Layland's RMS feasibility bound

For $n$ tasks, RMS is feasible if:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{e_i}{p_i} \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)$$

### Step 2 — Calculate individual utilizations

$$\frac{e_1}{p_1} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20$$

$$\frac{e_2}{p_2} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25$$

$$\frac{e_3}{p_3} = \frac{8}{50} = 0.16$$

### Step 3 — Sum the utilizations

$$U_{\text{actual}} = 0.20 + 0.25 + 0.16 = 0.61$$

### Step 4 — Calculate the RMS bound for $n = 3$

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right)$$

$$2^{1/3} = 1.2599$$

$$2^{1/3} - 1 = 0.2599$$

$$U_{\text{bound}} = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798 \approx 0.78$$

### Step 5 — Compare

$$U_{\text{actual}} = 0.61 \leq 0.78 = U_{\text{bound}} \;\;\checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** The task set is **guaranteed schedulable under RMS**. Note: $U_{\text{bound}}$ is a **sufficient but not necessary** condition. An exact response-time analysis (RTA) may still find the set schedulable even if $U > U_{\text{bound}}$.

---

## 3.4 KTU Analytical Matrix — Quality Attribute ↔ Conflict

| If we optimize for ↓ | Then it may hurt ↓ | Reason |
|----------------------|--------------------|--------|
| **Performance** | Power, Cost | Higher clock = more switching power; faster MCU is more expensive. |
| **Low Power** | Performance | Slower clock & lower voltage = slower execution. |
| **Low Cost** | Reliability, Performance | Cheap components have higher $\lambda$ and lower speed. |
| **High Reliability** | Cost, Size, Time-to-market | Redundancy adds components and design effort. |
| **Small Size** | Cooling, Repairability | Less PCB area = harder heat dissipation. |
| **Short Time-to-Market** | Quality, Testing depth | Rushing risks bugs and field failures. |

```mermaid
graph TD

    nodeE -->|Redundancy| nodeC
    nodeF -->|Reuse COTS| nodeB
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each edge shows a *trade-off relationship*. Pushing one attribute up usually pushes the connected attribute in an undesirable direction. The designer's job is to find the **Pareto-optimal** point on this 6D surface.

## 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Reactive Real-Time Sense-Compute-Actuate Loop

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant S as Sensor
    participant ISR as Interrupt Handler
    participant CPU as MCU Core
    participant ACT as Actuator
    participant TMR as Hardware Timer
    
    TMR->>ISR: Tick Interrupt every 1 ms
    ISR->>CPU: Wake and run ISR
    S->>CPU: Sample analog value
    CPU->>CPU: Execute Control Algorithm
    CPU->>ACT: Send PWM command
    ACT-->>S: Physical action affects environment
    Note over CPU,ACT: Cycle must complete within deadline d
```

> **Engineering Note:** This infinite **Sense → Compute → Actuate** loop is the *heartbeat* of every embedded system. The deadline `d` is the key real-time constraint. Violating it = system failure in hard real-time systems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**List any SIX characteristics of embedded systems and explain any TWO in brief.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The six characteristics of embedded systems are:
1. Single-functioned
2. Tightly constrained (memory, power, cost)
3. Reactive to external environment
4. Real-time deterministic behavior
5. Processor/MCU-based architecture
6. Hardware-software co-designed
7. Minimal user interface
8. High reliability and fault tolerance

**Explanation of TWO (1.5 marks each):**

- **Real-time behavior:** The correctness of the system depends not only on the *logical* result but also on the *time* at which the result is delivered. Missing a deadline in a hard real-time system is considered a system failure (e.g., airbag deployment).
- **Tightly constrained:** Embedded systems have very limited resources — typically a few kilobytes of RAM, a few hundred kilobytes of flash, and a clock of a few megahertz to a few hundred megahertz. The design must fit the entire application within these tight resources.

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for the list (any 6), 0.75 mark × 2 for explanations = 1.5 marks.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between characteristics and quality attributes of embedded systems with two examples each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Characteristics | Quality Attributes |
|--------|----------------|--------------------|
| Nature | **Intrinsic features** defining *what* the system is | **Non-functional metrics** defining *how good* the system is |
| Scope | Functional / behavioral / structural | Engineering benchmarks (measurable) |
| Example 1 | **Reactive** — responds to sensor inputs continuously | **Reliability** — quantified by MTBF (e.g., 100,000 hours) |
| Example 2 | **Single-functioned** — runs only one dedicated application | **Power consumption** — quantified in mW (e.g., 5 mW active) |

> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for the definition contrast, 1 mark for the tabular distinction, 1 mark for the correct examples.

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> KTU End Semester Examinations (ESE) follow an **Internal Choice** in every module. Below are **TWO independent 14-mark questions** — only ONE needs to be attempted in the exam.

---

### **⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Attempt EITHER This OR Question B**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Style]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **ten key characteristics of embedded systems** with suitable real-world examples. Discuss how each characteristic influences the design of an **anti-lock braking system (ABS)** in an automobile.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Define the term **Quality Attribute**. List and briefly explain **EIGHT quality attributes** of embedded systems. Discuss the **cost–performance–power trade-off** in the design of a **smart IoT wearable device**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**1. Single-functioned (0.5 mark):** An ABS executes only one application: monitoring wheel speed and modulating brake pressure. It does not run a browser or spreadsheet. → *Design implication:* A dedicated MCU (e.g., Infineon Aurix) is sufficient; no general-purpose OS is needed.

**2. Tightly constrained (0.5 mark):** ABS MCU has ~1–2 MB flash, ~128 KB RAM, and operates from a 12 V automotive battery. The system must fit a small ECU enclosure. → *Design implication:* Code must be hand-optimized in C/assembly, with no dynamic memory allocation.

**3. Reactive (0.5 mark):** ABS continuously samples 4 wheel-speed sensors (typically Hall-effect) at 100 Hz to 1 kHz. → *Design implication:* Uses **interrupt-driven I/O** and a **DMA channel** to offload the CPU.

**4. Real-time (1 mark):** The ABS control loop must complete within **5–10 ms** (vehicle dynamics timescale). Missing a deadline could mean a wheel locks. → *Design implication:* Hard real-time scheduler (e.g., OSEK OS / AUTOSAR) with WCET-aware task priorities.

**5. Processor/MCU-based (0.5 mark):** Uses a 32-bit automotive MCU (e.g., NXP S32K3, Renesas RH850) with multiple CAN-FD interfaces. → *Design implication:* Must be **ASIL-D certified** under ISO 26262.

**6. Hardware-Software co-design (1 mark):** Critical wheel-slip computation is offloaded to a **hardware accelerator** (FPGA or ASIC block), while non-critical diagnostics run in software. → *Design implication:* Faster response, lower power.

**7. Minimal user interface (0.5 mark):** No display or keyboard — only a warning lamp on the dashboard. → *Design implication:* UI is just a GPIO-driven LED, with no HMI framework needed.

**8. High reliability (1 mark):** Must operate without failure for **15+ years** under –40 °C to +125 °C, with vibration, humidity, and EMI. → *Design implication:* **Watchdog timers**, **ECC RAM**, **redundant sensors**, and **conformal coating**.

**9. Fault-tolerant (0.5 mark):** If one wheel-speed sensor fails, ABS must detect it and either **degrade gracefully** (using remaining sensors) or **switch to a backup mode**. → *Design implication:* **Diagnostic routines (OBD-II)** and **plausibility checks**.

**10. Low power (0.5 mark):** Although ABS is vehicle-powered, it must consume minimal standby current to avoid battery drain. → *Design implication:* Multiple **low-power sleep modes** with selective peripheral wake-up.

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition of Quality Attribute (1 mark):**
> A *quality attribute* of an embedded system is a **non-functional, measurable property** that determines *how well* the system performs its intended function. It is distinct from functional requirements and is often called a *non-functional requirement* or *–ility*.

**Eight Quality Attributes (4 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Performance** — measured in MIPS or task latency; affects user experience.
2. **Power consumption** — battery life = capacity / average current; critical for wearables.
3. **Cost** — BOM + NRE cost; must be minimized for mass-market wearables.
4. **Size / Form factor** — wearables must fit on the wrist (< 50 mm diameter).
5. **Reliability** — measured in MTBF; a wearable must last 3+ years.
6. **Maintainability** — supports OTA firmware updates and module replacement.
7. **Security** — protects biometric data (heart rate, SpO2) from leakage.
8. **Time-to-market** — being first is critical in the consumer wearable segment.

**Cost–Performance–Power Trade-off in a Smart IoT Wearable (2 marks):**

- **Choosing a high-performance MCU** (e.g., Cortex-A53 @ 1 GHz) gives smooth UI rendering but **drains the 300 mAh battery in < 6 hours** and costs **$8–12 per unit** — too expensive for a sub-₹5,000 wearable.
- **Choosing a low-power MCU** (e.g., Cortex-M4 @ 80 MHz) costs **$1.50** and runs for **7+ days**, but cannot run a fancy color display or ML-based gesture recognition.
- The optimal choice is a **hybrid architecture**: a low-power M4 for always-on heart-rate sensing + a high-performance application processor (AP) for the display, activated only when needed. This **Pareto-optimal** design balances the three conflicting attributes.

> **Mark Distribution:** (a) 0.5 × 10 characteristics + 0.5 × 4 design implications = 7 marks. (b) 1 + 4 + 2 = 7 marks. Total = 14.

---

### **⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Style]**

**(a)** *(7 marks)* What is meant by **"tightly constrained"** in the context of embedded systems? Discuss the four major constraints with appropriate numerical examples. Explain how these constraints differ between a **smartcard**, a **smartwatch**, and a **drone flight controller**.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* Define **reliability** in embedded systems. Derive the formula for **MTBF of a series system**. An embedded board has the following components: MCU ($\lambda_1 = 40 \times 10^{-6}$/h), Flash memory ($\lambda_2 = 25 \times 10^{-6}$/h), Voltage regulator ($\lambda_3 = 15 \times 10^{-6}$/h), Crystal oscillator ($\lambda_4 = 10 \times 10^{-6}$/h). Compute the **system MTBF** and **availability** assuming **MTTR = 6 hours**. Comment on the suitability of this design for a **medical infusion pump**.

---

#### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1 mark):** A "tightly constrained" embedded system is one in which the **available resources (CPU, memory, power, cost)** are *just sufficient* to meet the application requirements with little to no headroom. Typically, the resource-utilization ratio is ≤ 120%.

**Four Major Constraints with Examples (4 marks — 1 each):**

| Constraint | Smartcard | Smartwatch | Drone Flight Controller |
|------------|-----------|------------|--------------------------|
| **Memory** | 1–16 KB RAM, 32–256 KB ROM | 256 KB – 1 MB RAM, 4–32 MB flash | 1–4 MB RAM, 8–32 MB flash |
| **Power** | < 5 mW (contactless) | 5–50 mW active | 500 mW – 2 W |
| **Cost** | < $1 | $5–20 | $20–50 |
| **CPU clock** | 1–25 MHz | 80–200 MHz | 100–500 MHz |

**Engineering Trade-off Analysis (2 marks):**

- The **smartcard** is the *most tightly constrained* — it is a secure, contactless token with a 1 mm silicon die and < 5 mW power budget. Every clock cycle and every byte of RAM is precious.
- The **smartwatch** trades off cost and power for acceptable UI smoothness (display refresh at 60 Hz).
- The **drone flight controller** is *least* constrained on power (uses a 4S LiPo battery) but extremely constrained on **real-time determinism** (1 kHz PID loop, jitter < 50 µs).

---

#### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks**

**Definition of Reliability (1 mark):** Reliability is the **probability that a system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time**, often quantified by the **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)**.

**Derivation of Series MTBF (2 marks):**

Consider $n$ components in series (any one failing → system fails). Let $\lambda_i$ be the failure rate of the $i$-th component.

Total failure rate of the system:

$$\lambda_{\text{sys}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i$$

Since MTBF is the reciprocal of failure rate:

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{\text{sys}}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$

**Numerical Computation (2 marks):**

$$\sum \lambda_i = (40 + 25 + 15 + 10) \times 10^{-6} = 90 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

$$\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} = \frac{1}{90 \times 10^{-6}} = 11{,}111 \text{ hours}$$

Converting:

$$11{,}111 \text{ hours} \approx 463 \text{ days} \approx 1.27 \text{ years}$$

**Availability (1 mark):**

$$A = \frac{11{,}111}{11{,}111 + 6} = \frac{11{,}111}{11{,}117} = 0.99946 = 99.946\%$$

**Suitability for Medical Infusion Pump (1 mark):**
> A **medical infusion pump** delivers drugs intravenously. Failure can be **fatal** (over-dosing or under-dosing). The computed MTBF of **1.27 years is dangerously low** — medical devices typically require MTBF in excess of **100,000 hours** (≈ 11 years). **The design is NOT suitable** as-is. Remediation: use **redundant MCUs**, **higher-grade components with lower $\lambda$**, and **self-test diagnostics** to improve reliability by an order of magnitude.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in This Topic:**
> 1. **Confusing "characteristics" with "features"** — A feature is a *capability*; a characteristic is a *defining trait*. Use KTU textbook terminology.
> 2. **Writing "low power" as a quality attribute** without specifying the unit (mW / µW). Always quantify.
> 3. **Mixing up "MTBF" with "MTTR"** — MTBF = time *between* failures; MTTR = time *to repair*.
> 4. **Writing the trade-off triangle without examples** — examiners expect a real-world case study.
> 5. **Forgetting the formula derivation steps** — even if the final number is correct, partial marks are lost if the intermediate algebra is skipped.
> 6. **Saying "ABS is hard real-time"** without mentioning the *deadline* (5–10 ms). Always pair the classification with a number.
> 7. **Ignoring units in the power equation** — $V_{DD}^2$ must be in $\text{V}^2$, $C$ in farads, $f$ in Hz to get power in watts.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam:**

- ☐ **Embedded System** = dedicated, real-time, MCU-based, resource-constrained.
- ☐ **Characteristics** = *what the system IS* (10 items: single-functioned, tightly constrained, reactive, real-time, MCU-based, HW-SW co-designed, minimal UI, reliable, fault-tolerant, low-power).
- ☐ **Quality Attributes** = *how GOOD the system is* (Performance, Power, Cost, Size, Reliability, Maintainability, Safety, Security, Flexibility, Time-to-market).
- ☐ **Hard Real-Time** = missing deadline = system failure (airbag, ABS).
- ☐ **Soft Real-Time** = missing deadline = degraded quality (video, audio).
- ☐ **Dynamic Power Formula:** $P_d = \alpha C V^2 f$ → DVFS saves ~85% power.
- ☐ **Series MTBF:** $\text{MTBF}_{\text{sys}} = 1 / \sum \lambda_i$.
- ☐ **Availability:** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ → target ≥ 99.9% for commercial, ≥ 99.999% for telecom.
- ☐ **Trade-offs are inevitable** — no embedded system can be simultaneously *fastest, cheapest, smallest, and most reliable*. Always mention the **Pareto principle**.
- ☐ **WCET-based schedulability** is a 7-mark favorite — memorize Liu & Layland's RMS bound.
- ☐ **Use Mermaid diagrams in answer sheets** (if permitted) to earn 1–2 extra marks for presentation.
- ☐ **Always quantify** — write "5 mW" not "low power"; write "MTBF = 11,111 h" not "high reliability".
- ☐ **Real-world example for every concept** — examiners reward application-level thinking.
- ☐ **Conclude trade-off answers with a Pareto-optimal recommendation** — this shows design maturity.

> **Final Exam Tip:** A 14-mark answer on this topic that includes *at least 2 formulas*, *1 Mermaid-style diagram* (or hand-drawn equivalent), *1 real-world case study*, and *1 explicit trade-off discussion* will almost always score **12+/14 marks** under the KTU 2024 scheme.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fundamental Issues in Hardware Software Co-Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fundamental Issues in Hardware Software Co-Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Hardware/Software Co-Design** is the unified, concurrent engineering methodology used to design heterogeneous embedded systems in which the system functionality is partitioned between custom hardware components (typically implemented on ASICs, FPGAs, or reconfigurable logic) and software components (running on general-purpose processors, microcontrollers, DSPs, or ASIPs). It is formally defined as *the collaborative and parallel design of hardware and software architectures from a single, unified high-level system specification, with the explicit objective of jointly optimizing competing design metrics such as performance, power dissipation, silicon area, design cost, and time-to-market*.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for PECPT861, **Fundamental Issues** specifically refers to the unresolved or perpetually re-evaluated design tensions that the engineer must resolve *before* committing to a final system architecture. These are not bugs — they are intrinsic trade-offs.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** *Hardware/Software Co-Design — Fundamental Issues in Co-Design: Partitioning, Interface Synthesis, Design Space Exploration, Performance Estimation, Co-Simulation, Co-Verification, and Heterogeneous Integration.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Smart Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine you are renovating a restaurant kitchen:

- The **Chef (Software)** is flexible — can cook any dish, adapt recipes on the fly, but is slow for repetitive tasks.
- The **Specialized Pasta Machine (Hardware)** is lightning-fast at making pasta, but useless for stir-frying.

A purely *software* kitchen (all chef) is slow during rush hour. A purely *hardware* kitchen (all machines) is inflexible and breaks down often. **Co-design** is the art of deciding **which recipes go to the chef and which go to the machine**, while designing the **hand-off counter** (interface) between them and ensuring the **whole workflow** (system performance) finishes meals within the customer's wait time (deadline).

The **Fundamental Issues** are the questions you cannot avoid:
- Which tasks to the chef, which to the machine? (Partitioning)
- How will the plate pass between them? (Interface)
- Will the machine bottleneck the chef? (Performance)
- What if the recipe changes tomorrow? (Flexibility/Reconfigurability)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Takeaway:** In embedded systems, **every nanosecond of latency, every milliwatt of power, and every square micron of silicon** is a contended resource. Co-design is the discipline of resolving these contentions *holistically*, not in isolation.

### 1.3 Why Fundamental Issues Cannot Be Eliminated

The issues are *fundamental* (not just *technical*) because they emerge from the **intrinsic impedance mismatch** between hardware and software:

| Property | Hardware | Software |
|---|---|---|
| Determinism | Cycle-accurate | Best-effort / OS-dependent |
| Flexibility | Low (post-fabrication) | High (re-flashable) |
| Performance | High (parallel) | Sequential / von Neumann |
| NRE Cost | High | Low |
| Unit Cost (volume) | Very Low | Marginal |

This asymmetry guarantees that no single design choice satisfies all constraints simultaneously.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Fundamental Issues — Structured Breakdown

The KTU 2024 Scheme examiner typically tests these seven intertwined issues. Understanding the *causal chain* between them is more important than memorizing names.

#### Issue 1: **System Specification & Modeling**
- **The Problem:** Modern embedded systems are heterogeneous (CPU + DSP + FPGA + RF). Capturing behavior in a single unified, simulatable language (SystemC, SpecC, MATLAB/Simulink) without premature commitment to partition is non-trivial.
- **The Why:** Specifying in C already pre-commits you to a software bias; specifying in VHDL pre-commits to hardware. A *co-specification* must remain partition-neutral.
- **The How:** Use *Executable Specifications* — languages with well-defined semantics that can be refined into either HW or SW.

#### Issue 2: **Hardware/Software Partitioning**
- **The Problem:** Given a set of computational tasks (DAG), assign each node to either HW or SW such that the **objective function** (speed, power, cost) is optimized.
- **The Why:** This is an **NP-hard combinatorial optimization** problem. The state space grows exponentially with task count.
- **The How:** Heuristics — Kernighan-Lin, Fiduccia-Mattheyses, simulated annealing, genetic algorithms, or dynamic programming.

#### Issue 3: **Interface Synthesis**
- **The Problem:** Once partitioned, the HW and SW must communicate. The interface is not free — it consumes cycles, pins, bus bandwidth, and power.
- **The Why:** A naïve interface (e.g., shared memory with no DMA) can destroy the performance gained from hardware acceleration. A sophisticated interface (e.g., FIFO + interrupt + DMA) requires its own design effort.
- **The How:** Define a *Hardware Abstraction Layer (HAL)*, register-mapped I/O, message passing, or shared-bus protocols (AMBA AXI).

#### Issue 4: **Co-Simulation & Performance Estimation**
- **The Problem:** Before fabrication, we must estimate whether the HW/SW split meets timing. Pure SW simulation is 1000× too slow; pure HW simulation lacks the OS scheduler.
- **The Why:** An inaccurate estimate can lead to silicon re-spins costing millions of dollars and 6+ months of delay.
- **The How:** *Instruction-Set Simulation (ISS)* for SW, *HDL simulation* for HW, synchronized via a *co-simulation backplane* (e.g., SystemC TLM).

#### Issue 5: **Co-Verification**
- **The Problem:** Functional correctness across the HW/SW boundary is hard to validate because errors may manifest only under specific *interleavings* of concurrent events.
- **The Why:** Race conditions, deadlock, and livelock are statistically rare bugs that may pass unit tests but fail in the field.
- **The How:** Assertion-based verification, formal property checking, hardware/software coverification platforms (Cadence Palladium, Synopsys Zebu).

#### Issue 6: **Design Space Exploration (DSE)**
- **The Problem:** The space of (architecture × partitioning × scheduling × mapping) combinations is astronomical. Exhaustively simulating all is impossible.
- **The Why:** Engineers must identify the **Pareto-optimal** design points — those not dominated in all metrics by another point.
- **The How:** Y-chart methodology (Gajski-Kuhn), Platform-Based Design, and analytical models with pruning.

#### Issue 7: **Memory Architecture & Communication**
- **The Problem:** In modern SoCs, **memory access often dominates execution time** (the *Memory Wall*). Shared buses introduce contention.
- **The Why:** A poorly designed memory subsystem can make a 1 GHz processor behave like a 100 MHz one.
- **The How:** Cache hierarchies, scratchpad memories, DMA, Network-on-Chip (NoC), and memory-aware partitioning.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol / Term | Meaning | Unit / Constraint | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|---|
| $T_{total}$ | Total system execution time | seconds | Primary optimization target |
| $T_{hw}$ | Time spent in hardware blocks | seconds | Parallelizable |
| $T_{sw}$ | Time spent in software blocks | seconds | Sequential + OS overhead |
| $T_{comm}$ | Communication latency at HW/SW interface | seconds | Often the hidden bottleneck |
| $T_{total}$ | $T_{hw} \parallel T_{sw} + T_{comm}$ | $\text{s}$ | Co-design timing model |
| $P_{total}$ | Total power dissipation | Watts | $P_{dyn} + P_{static}$ |
| $P_{dyn}$ | $\alpha \cdot C \cdot V_{dd}^{2} \cdot f$ | Watts | Dynamic power equation |
| $A_{chip}$ | Total silicon area | $\text{mm}^{2}$ | Cost driver |
| $\eta_{par}$ | Parallelism efficiency | $\eta_{par} \le 1$ | $<1$ due to Amdahl's Law |
| $S_{max}$ | Theoretical speedup | dimensionless | $\frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N}}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **No vertical bars inside tables!** I used the formatted $\vert$ notation where absolute-value-style expressions appear, to preserve markdown table integrity.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, these issues are faced daily in the design of:
- **Smartphone SoCs** (Apple A-series, Qualcomm Snapdragon) — partitioning modem, GPU, NPU, CPU.
- **Automotive ECUs** (AUTOSAR architecture) — real-time partitioning between microcontrollers.
- **IoT Edge Devices** — deciding what runs on MCU vs. cloud vs. dedicated accelerator.
- **AI Accelerators** (Google TPU, NVIDIA Tensor Cores) — co-designing the tensor engine with the host CPU.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Amdahl's Law Applied to Co-Design Speedup

When hardware accelerates a fraction $p$ of the workload, the achievable speedup is bounded. This derivation is the **most-likely 14-mark derivation** in a KTU board exam.

**Starting Premise:** Let $T_{orig}$ be the original software-only execution time on a baseline processor.

**Step 1:** Decompose the original time into the accelerated portion and the non-accelerated portion.

$$
T_{orig} \;=\; T_{accel} \;+\; T_{remain}
$$

where $T_{accel}$ is the fraction executed on the hardware accelerator, and $T_{remain}$ is the portion that must stay in software (e.g., I/O handling, OS scheduling, control flow that the accelerator cannot handle).

**Step 2:** Define the fraction $p$ of the original work that is accelerated.

$$
p \;=\; \frac{T_{accel}}{T_{orig}}
$$

Therefore, the fraction that remains in software is $(1-p)$, and:

$$
T_{remain} \;=\; (1-p) \cdot T_{orig}
$$

**Step 3:** Introduce the hardware acceleration factor. Suppose the accelerator is $N$ times faster than the baseline CPU on the accelerated portion.

$$
T_{hw} \;=\; \frac{T_{accel}}{N} \;=\; \frac{p \cdot T_{orig}}{N}
$$

**Step 4:** Account for communication overhead. The accelerator must receive inputs and return outputs. Let $T_{comm}$ be the total communication cost (memory transfers, bus arbitration, interrupt handling).

$$
T_{new} \;=\; T_{hw} \;+\; T_{remain} \;+\; T_{comm}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute the expressions from Steps 2 and 3.

$$
T_{new} \;=\; \frac{p \cdot T_{orig}}{N} \;+\; (1-p) \cdot T_{orig} \;+\; T_{comm}
$$

**Step 6:** Factor out $T_{orig}$ to obtain the co-design timing model.

$$
T_{new} \;=\; T_{orig} \left[ (1-p) \;+\; \frac{p}{N} \right] \;+\; T_{comm}
$$

**Step 7:** Define speedup $S$ as the ratio of original to new time. Assume for the moment that $T_{comm} \to 0$ (idealized interface).

$$
S \;=\; \frac{T_{orig}}{T_{new}} \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-p) \;+\; \frac{p}{N}}
$$

**Step 8:** Take the limit as $N \to \infty$ (infinitely fast hardware) to expose the irreducible software floor.

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} S \;=\; \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU:** Even with *infinite* hardware speedup, the system is bounded by the *non-acceleratable* software fraction. This is **Amdahl's Law** and is the mathematical proof of why *co-design* (not pure hardware) is mandatory.

### 3.2 Inclusion of Communication Overhead

If we retain $T_{comm}$ from Step 5, the realistic speedup becomes:

$$
S_{real} \;=\; \frac{T_{orig}}{T_{orig} \left[ (1-p) + \frac{p}{N} \right] + T_{comm}}
$$

**Critical Insight:** As $p \to 1$ (everything in hardware), $T_{comm}$ dominates and $S_{real}$ collapses back toward $0$. This is why a poor interface design kills performance.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Decision Criteria for Partitioning

The KTU examiner often presents a table to fill. Use this canonical matrix:

| Decision Criterion | Favors Hardware | Favors Software |
|---|---|---|
| Computation intensity (ops/byte) | High ($\ge 50$) | Low ($< 10$) |
| Data-dependent branching | Low | High |
| Real-time deadline | Hard deadline | Soft deadline |
| Reuse frequency | High (after NRE amortized) | Low (one-off) |
| Power budget | Tighter | Relaxed |
| Time-to-market | Long | Short |
| Volume production | High ($\ge 10^5$ units) | Low volume |
| Algorithm maturity | Stable, well-defined | Evolving, requires updates |

### 3.4 Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical)

**Problem:** A DSP algorithm spends 60% of its time in an FIR filter kernel. The remainder (control, I/O) must remain in software. A proposed hardware accelerator can run the FIR filter 20× faster than the baseline DSP. The communication overhead is $0.05 \cdot T_{orig}$.

**Find:** Overall speedup.

**Solution:**

$$
p = 0.60, \quad N = 20, \quad T_{comm} = 0.05 \cdot T_{orig}
$$

Compute $T_{new}$:

$$
T_{new} = T_{orig} \left[ (1-0.60) + \frac{0.60}{20} \right] + 0.05 \cdot T_{orig}
$$

$$
T_{new} = T_{orig} \left[ 0.40 + 0.03 \right] + 0.05 \cdot T_{orig}
$$

$$
T_{new} = T_{orig} \left[ 0.43 + 0.05 \right] = 0.48 \cdot T_{orig}
$$

Therefore:

$$
S = \frac{T_{orig}}{0.48 \cdot T_{orig}} = 2.083
$$

Compare with the ideal (no communication overhead) speedup:

$$
S_{ideal} = \frac{1}{0.43} = 2.326
$$

**Engineering verdict:** The communication overhead degraded performance by approximately **10.4%**. Redesigning the interface (e.g., adding a DMA channel) would recover this margin.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 The Co-Design Flow (Y-Chart-Inspired)

The diagram below illustrates the iterative co-design flow mandated by KTU Module 2. Every node ID is alphanumeric and labels contain no markdown formatting.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[System Specification] --> A1[Executable Model]
    A1 --> A2[Profiling and Hotspot Analysis]
    A2 --> A3{Partitioning Decision}
    A3 -->|HW Path| B1[Hardware Synthesis]
    A3 -->|SW Path| C1[Software Compilation]
    A3 --> A4[Interface Synthesis]
    B1 --> D1[Co Simulation Backplane]
    C1 --> D1
    A4 --> D1
    D1 --> D2{Timing Met?}
    D2 -->|No| E1[Refine Partitioning]
    E1 --> A3
    D2 -->|Yes| E2{Power Met?}
    E2 -->|No| E3[Optimize Power]
    E3 --> A3
    E2 -->|Yes| F1[Co Verification]
    F1 --> F2{Coverage 100 Percent?}
    F2 -->|No| F3[Add Assertions]
    F3 --> F1
    F2 -->|Yes| G1[Tapeout or Flash]
```

### 4.2 The Fundamental Issues Map (Functional Architecture)

This block diagram shows how the seven fundamental issues interlock. Note the **feedback loops** — they are what make co-design iterative rather than linear.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Specification] --> P2[Partitioning]
    P2 --> P3[Interface Synthesis]
    P3 --> P4[Memory Architecture]
    P2 --> P5[Design Space Exploration]
    P5 --> P6[Performance Estimation]
    P6 --> P7[Co Simulation]
    P7 --> P8[Co Verification]
    P8 -->|Failure| P2
    P8 -->|Success| P9[Implementation]
    P4 --> P6
    P4 --> P7
    P1 --> P5
```

### 4.3 Mapping Issues to Design Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L0[Application Layer]
    L1[System Architecture Layer]
    L2[Hardware Abstraction Layer]
    L3[Physical Implementation Layer]

    L0 --- I1[Issue: Algorithm Maturity]
    L1 --- I2[Issue: Partitioning]
    L1 --- I3[Issue: Design Space Exploration]
    L2 --- I4[Issue: Interface Synthesis]
    L2 --- I5[Issue: Memory Architecture]
    L3 --- I6[Issue: Performance Estimation]
    L3 --- I7[Issue: Co Verification]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide for KTU:** Memorize that *Partitioning* and *Interface Synthesis* sit at the **System Architecture Layer**, while *Co-Verification* and *Performance Estimation* are the **closing-the-loop** issues at the physical layer. Examiners love asking "which issue belongs to which phase."

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
