## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define Hardware/Software Co-Design. List any four fundamental issues addressed during co-design of an embedded system.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation: 1 for definition, 2 for the list):**

Hardware/Software Co-Design is a unified design methodology in which the hardware and software components of an embedded system are designed concurrently from a single executable specification, with the objective of jointly optimizing performance, power, area, and time-to-market.

Four fundamental issues are:
1. **Hardware/Software Partitioning** — deciding which tasks execute in HW vs. SW.
2. **Interface Synthesis** — designing the communication mechanism between HW and SW.
3. **Co-Simulation and Co-Verification** — validating correctness across the boundary.
4. **Design Space Exploration** — navigating the architectural trade-off space.

*(Any four from: Partitioning, Interface Synthesis, Co-Simulation, Co-Verification, Performance Estimation, Memory Architecture, Design Space Exploration.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Sessional]**
*What is the role of an Instruction Set Simulator (ISS) in hardware/software co-design?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An **Instruction Set Simulator (ISS)** is a software model of a target processor that emulates instruction execution cycle-by-cycle (or functionally) on a host machine. In co-design, the ISS serves two roles:
1. It executes the software portion of the system specification on a virtual processor *before* the real silicon is available.
2. It acts as the **timing reference** for the software side during **co-simulation**, allowing the hardware HDL simulator to synchronize with software execution and accurately predict overall system performance.

It thereby enables early **performance estimation** without fabricating silicon.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question has **internal choice**. You must answer **either** Option A **or** Option B. Each carries 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Expected Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **Hardware/Software Partitioning** problem in embedded system design. Discuss the criteria used to decide which functionality should be implemented in hardware and which in software. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Amdahl's Law** based speedup equation for a co-designed system and explain why a pure hardware migration is suboptimal. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** *Partitioning* is the process of assigning each computational task in a system specification to either a hardware implementation (ASIC/FPGA) or a software implementation (running on a CPU/DSP).

**Criteria for partition decision:**

1. **Computational Intensity** — Tasks with high operations-per-memory-byte ratio (compute-bound kernels like FFT, encryption) belong in hardware.
2. **Real-Time Constraints** — Tasks with hard deadlines (motor control loop, packet processing) often need hardware determinism.
3. **Data-Dependent Control Flow** — Heavily branching code is cheaper in software due to flexible program counter.
4. **Reuse Volume vs. NRE Cost** — High-volume production amortizes the high NRE of hardware.
5. **Flexibility / Upgrade Path** — Tasks likely to evolve should remain in software (re-flashable).
6. **Power Budget** — Hardware can be optimized for low voltage; software benefits from clock gating.

**Partitioning Algorithms:** Kernighan-Lin, Fiduccia-Mattheyses (heuristic graph partitioning), simulated annealing, genetic algorithms.

**(b)** Refer to the derivation in Section 3.1. The final equation is:

$$
S \;=\; \frac{1}{(1-p) \;+\; \frac{p}{N}}
$$

As $N \to \infty$:

$$
S_{max} \;=\; \frac{1}{1-p}
$$

Pure hardware migration is suboptimal because:
- The non-accelerated software fraction $(1-p)$ remains a **hard floor**.
- Communication overhead $T_{comm}$ grows as $p \to 1$, eroding gains.
- Power and area cost rises super-linearly with $p$.

Hence co-design — not pure hardware — is the engineering optimum.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified Pattern]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **co-simulation** environment for a hardware/software system. Explain how an **ISS** is synchronized with an **HDL simulator**. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An embedded image-processing algorithm spends 70% of its runtime in a pixel-wise convolution kernel. A proposed FPGA accelerator can execute this kernel 15× faster than the baseline ARM processor. Communication overhead is 4% of the original execution time. Calculate:
   1. The overall speedup including communication overhead. **(4 Marks)**
   2. The maximum achievable speedup if communication overhead were eliminated. **(3 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** **Co-Simulation Block Diagram (text-rendered for clarity):**

The co-simulation environment consists of three major components connected via a **backplane**:
- **Software Simulator (ISS)** running on host — executes C/C++ code of SW partition.
- **HDL Simulator** (e.g., ModelSim, Vivado XSim) — executes VHDL/Verilog of HW partition.
- **Co-Simulation Backplane / Bus Functional Model** — synchronizes the two simulators using a master–slave clock and exchanges signals (transactions, interrupts, bus events).

**Synchronization:** The HDL simulator is typically the **master** (cycle-accurate). At every HDL clock edge, it calls the ISS via the **Procedure Call Interface (PCI)** or **socket-based RPC**. The ISS advances the simulated CPU by an equivalent number of instructions (or cycles) and returns to the HDL simulator. Lockstep or message-passing protocols ensure deterministic event ordering.

**(b)** Given: $p = 0.70$, $N = 15$, $T_{comm} = 0.04 \cdot T_{orig}$.

**Part (b)(1):** Total new time:

$$
T_{new} = T_{orig}\left[(1-0.70) + \frac{0.70}{15}\right] + 0.04 \cdot T_{orig}
$$

$$
T_{new} = T_{orig}\left[0.30 + 0.0467\right] + 0.04 \cdot T_{orig}
$$

$$
T_{new} = T_{orig}\left[0.3467 + 0.04\right] = 0.3867 \cdot T_{orig}
$$

$$
S_{real} = \frac{1}{0.3867} \approx 2.586
$$

**Part (b)(2):** With $T_{comm} = 0$:

$$
S_{ideal} = \frac{1}{0.3467} \approx 2.885
$$

**Engineering insight:** Communication overhead costs $\approx 0.30$ of speedup units (~10.4% degradation). Engineers should invest in **DMA + FIFO interface** to recover this margin.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks on this topic:**
> 1. **Muddling "co-simulation" with "co-verification"** — Co-simulation answers *how fast*, co-verification answers *is it correct*. Examiners deduct 2 marks for this confusion.
> 2. **Forgetting $T_{comm}$ in the speedup formula** — The idealized Amdahl form is not acceptable unless the question explicitly states "ignore communication." Always include $T_{comm}$ in full-form answers.
> 3. **Vague partitioning criteria** — Writing "speed" alone is too generic. Use specific quantitative thresholds (ops/byte ratio, deadline in $\mu$s, etc.).
> 4. **No mention of NP-hardness** — When asked about partitioning complexity, students omit that it is NP-hard and therefore heuristic-driven. Examiners expect this.
> 5. **Skipping the diagram in Question A(a)** — The question explicitly says "with neat block diagram." A missing diagram costs 2 marks even if the text is perfect.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Hardware/Software Co-Design** = unified, concurrent design of HW + SW from a single specification.
- The **seven fundamental issues** are: Specification, Partitioning, Interface Synthesis, Co-Simulation, Co-Verification, Design Space Exploration, and Memory Architecture.
- **Partitioning is NP-hard** — solved by heuristics (KL, FM, SA, GA).
- **Amdahl's Law** governs the theoretical speedup: $S = \frac{1}{(1-p) + \frac{p}{N} + \text{comm overhead}}$.
- **Communication overhead** is the silent killer of hardware acceleration — always include $T_{comm}$.
- **Co-simulation** uses an **ISS + HDL simulator** synchronized over a **backplane** (PCI/RPC).
- **Y-chart methodology** is the canonical DSE framework (Gajski-Kuhn).
- **Memory wall**: memory access, not compute, often dominates modern embedded performance.
- **Pareto-optimal** design points are the output of DSE — never compromise all metrics for one.
- For KTU 14-mark answers, **always draw the block diagram** even if not explicitly asked — examiners reward visualization.
- **Distinguish "Co-Design" (methodology) from "Co-Simulation" (verification technique)** — this is a favorite 2-mark trick question.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Computational Models in Embedded Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Computational Models in Embedded Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computational Model (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition):**
> A *computational model* in embedded system design is an abstract, mathematically rigorous description of the system's behaviour, structure, and data manipulation that allows the designer to reason about correctness, performance, power, and concurrency **before** committing to hardware or software implementation.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, a computational model is the **bridge between specification and implementation**. It abstracts away the underlying silicon and instead exposes the *intent* of the system: what computations occur, in what order, under what conditions, and on what data.

The major families of computational models covered under the PECPT861 syllabus are:

1. **Sequential / Imperative Models** — Von Neumann and Harvard execution models.
2. **State-Oriented Models** — Finite State Machines (FSM), Mealy, Moore, and Statecharts.
3. **Data-Flow Models** — Data Flow Graphs (DFG), Synchronous Data Flow (SDF).
4. **Concurrent / Reactive Models** — Petri Nets, Process Networks, Discrete Event Systems.
5. **Communication-Oriented Models** — CSP, Actor Model, Message-Passing.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen":**
> Think of an embedded system as a **restaurant kitchen**.
> - The **computational model** is the *recipe book format* the head chef (designer) uses.
> - A **sequential model** is a step-by-step recipe card (do A, then B, then C).
> - An **FSM** is a flowchart of cooking stations (boil → fry → plate → serve) that switches based on the dish ordered.
> - A **data-flow model** is a conveyor belt where each ingredient moves from one station to the next the moment it is ready, regardless of order.
> - A **Petri net** is a set of trays and tokens: each tray holds a fixed number of plates, and a chef fires up a station only when enough tokens are present.
> - A **process network** is a fully parallel brigade where each station cooks independently and passes dishes through a window.
>
> Just as a kitchen must pick a *style of recipe* that suits the cuisine, an embedded engineer must pick a *computational model* that suits the application's concurrency, timing, and reactivity needs.

## 1.3 Why Computational Models Are Mandatory in KTU 2024 Embedded Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2 of PECPT861):**
> Modern embedded systems (autosar ECUs, IoT nodes, SoCs, RTOS-based controllers) are *heterogeneous, concurrent, and reactive*. Without a formal computational model, the designer cannot:
> 1. **Verify** timing constraints (worst-case execution time, deadline analysis).
> 2. **Detect** deadlocks, race conditions, and starvation.
> 3. **Synthesize** hardware/software partitions deterministically.
> 4. **Communicate** intent unambiguously across design teams.

The choice of model directly influences:
- **Performance** (latency, throughput)
- **Power consumption** (active vs idle states)
- **Determinism** (predictability of behaviour)
- **Scalability** (ease of adding features)

Standard metrics that students **must** remember in **bold**:

- **Throughput** — tasks completed per unit time (typically *operations/second*).
- **Latency** — time from input to corresponding output.
- **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** — upper bound on execution time, denoted $\text{WCET}$.
- **State Space Size** — number of distinct system states, denoted $\vert S \vert$.
- **Concurrency Degree** — maximum number of simultaneously active computational units.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Computational Models

The computational models used in embedded design are organised along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Question It Answers | Example Models |
|------|--------------------|----------------|
| **Time** | Is time continuous, discrete, or event-driven? | Discrete Event, Synchronous, Asynchronous |
| **State** | Does the system carry state? How much? | FSM, SDF, Petri Net |
| **Concurrency** | Can computations overlap? | Sequential, Kahn PN, CSP, Actors |

## 2.2 Detailed Analysis of Each Model Family

### 2.2.1 Finite State Machine (FSM) — The Workhorse

- **Definition:** A 6-tuple $\mathcal{M} = (S, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta, \lambda, s_0)$ where
  - $S$ = finite set of states
  - $\Sigma$ = input alphabet
  - $\Delta$ = output alphabet
  - $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$ = transition function
  - $\lambda : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Delta$ (Mealy) **or** $\lambda : S \rightarrow \Delta$ (Moore)
  - $s_0 \in S$ = initial state.
- **Why it matters:** Almost every embedded controller (washing machine, ECU, traffic light) is implemented as an FSM. It is *finite* → analysable → verifiable.
- **How it works:** The machine is in exactly one state at a time. On each input event, $\delta$ moves it to the next state, and $\lambda$ produces an output.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mealy vs Moore (KTU High-Yield Distinction):**
> - **Mealy:** output depends on *current state AND current input* → $\lambda(s, x)$.
> - **Moore:** output depends *only on current state* → $\lambda(s)$.
> - Moore machines are **one clock cycle slower** but are safer in asynchronous designs because outputs are glitch-free.

### 2.2.2 Data Flow Graph (DFG)

- **Definition:** A directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where each vertex $v \in V$ is a *computational actor* and each edge $e \in E$ carries data tokens.
- **Why it matters:** Maps directly onto DSP, streaming, and signal-processing pipelines.
- **How it works:** An actor *fires* (executes) when all its input tokens are available. Output tokens are produced and pushed along outgoing edges.

### 2.2.3 Synchronous Data Flow (SDF)

- **Extension of DFG** where each actor consumes and produces a **fixed, statically known** number of tokens per firing.
- **Why it matters:** Enables *static scheduling* — the entire execution order can be computed at compile time, eliminating runtime overhead. Used heavily in DSP processors (e.g., TI C6000, Qualcomm Hexagon).

### 2.2.4 Petri Net

- **Definition:** A 5-tuple $\mathcal{P} = (P, T, F, W, M_0)$ where
  - $P$ = places (conditions)
  - $T$ = transitions (events)
  - $F \subseteq (P \times T) \cup (T \times P)$ = flow arcs
  - $W : F \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ = weights
  - $M_0 : P \rightarrow \mathbb{N}$ = initial marking.
- **Why it matters:** Naturally models **concurrency, synchronisation, and resource contention** — critical in multiprocessor embedded SoCs.
- **How it works:** A transition *fires* when all its input places have at least the required number of tokens; tokens are removed from inputs and added to outputs.

### 2.2.5 Kahn Process Network (KPN)

- **Definition:** A set of sequential processes communicating via **unbounded FIFO channels**, where `send` is non-blocking and `receive` is blocking.
- **Why it matters:** Deterministic, deadlock-free model for streaming applications (video codecs, baseband processing).
- **How it works:** Each process runs autonomously; communication is governed purely by data availability on channels.

### 2.2.6 Discrete Event (DE) Model

- **Definition:** A system whose state changes only at discrete, time-ordered events.
- **Why it matters:** Foundation of SystemC TLM and many hardware simulators.
- **How it works:** Maintains a global event queue sorted by time stamp; processes the next earliest event on each simulation step.

### 2.2.7 Statecharts (Hierarchical FSM)

- **Definition:** Extension of FSMs with **hierarchy, orthogonality (parallel states), and broadcast communication**.
- **Why it matters:** Designed by David Harel; used in UML state diagrams and frameworks like Qt State Machine Framework.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **board-exam essential**. Memorise the units and the meaning of every symbol.

| # | Formula / Definition | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|----------------------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | $\vert S \vert = $ number of states | State space size | dimensionless |
| 2 | $\text{WCET} = \max_{p \in \text{paths}} \sum_{b \in p} t_b$ | Worst-case execution time | seconds (s) |
| 3 | $T_{\text{throughput}} = \dfrac{1}{\text{cycle time}}$ | Pipeline throughput | ops/s (Hertz equivalent) |
| 4 | $L_{\text{latency}} = t_{\text{out}} - t_{\text{in}}$ | End-to-end latency | seconds (s) |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{util}} = \dfrac{T_{\text{busy}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | Resource utilisation | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 6 | $\delta : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow S$ | FSM transition function | — |
| 7 | $\lambda_{\text{Mealy}} : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Delta$ | Mealy output | — |
| 8 | $\lambda_{\text{Moore}} : S \rightarrow \Delta$ | Moore output | — |
| 9 | $\text{SDF balance} : \forall e \in E,\; q_e \cdot \text{prod}(a, e) = q_e \cdot \text{cons}(a, e)$ | SDF balance equation | dimensionless |
| 10 | $M : P \rightarrow \mathbb{N}_0$ | Petri net marking | tokens |
| 11 | $R_{\text{reach}} = \{M_0, M_1, M_2, \dots\}$ | Reachability set | — |
| 12 | $\text{CPU bound} : T_{\text{req}} \leq \text{WCET} + J$ | Schedulability (J = jitter) | seconds (s) |
| 13 | $D_{\text{deadline}} \geq \text{WCET} + \text{preemption overhead}$ | Deadline condition | seconds (s) |
| 14 | $\rho = \dfrac{U}{1}$ (single core) | CPU load (must be $< 1$ for steady state) | dimensionless |
| 15 | $N_{\text{iterations}}^{\text{SDF}} = \text{lcm}(\vec{q})$ | Minimum SDF iterations | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse the SDF balance equation with the reachability of a Petri net. The SDF balance is a *static* property computed from the production/consumption vectors, while reachability is a *dynamic* property of the marking graph.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECUs (AUTOSAR):** Use a layered model combining FSMs (for BSW modes) with runnables scheduled by an OS (Discrete Event).
- **Smartphone SoCs:** Heterogeneous cores (CPU + DSP + NPU) are coordinated by SDF graphs scheduled at compile time.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Often modelled as Reactive (event-driven) FSMs with power states.
- **Aerospace / Avionics:** Petri nets are used for *DO-178C* level-A software verification because they expose deadlocks formally.
- **Medical Devices (FDA Class III):** Statecharts allow hierarchical breakdown of modes (Normal → Caution → Alarm → Service).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mealy-to-Moore Machine Conversion

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **KTU favourite** — it appears in nearly every Dec/Dec supplementary paper.

**Problem statement:** Given a Mealy machine $\mathcal{M}_{\text{Mealy}} = (S, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta_M, \lambda_M, s_0)$, derive an equivalent Moore machine $\mathcal{M}_{\text{Moore}}$.

### Step 1 — Identify Mealy transitions and outputs
For every transition $\delta_M(s, x) = s'$ producing output $\lambda_M(s, x) = y$, record the tuple $(s, x, s', y)$.

### Step 2 — Split each Mealy state into one Moore state per *distinct output*
For each Mealy state $s$, define the set of outputs produced on its outgoing transitions:
$$
\text{Out}(s) = \{\, \lambda_M(s, x) \mid x \in \Sigma \,\}
$$

For every output $y \in \text{Out}(s)$, create a new Moore state $s_y$ whose output is $\lambda_{\text{Moore}}(s_y) = y$.

### Step 3 — Re-route transitions
For each original transition $(s, x, s', y)$ in the Mealy machine, in the Moore machine add:
$$
\delta_{\text{Moore}}(s_y, x) = s'_y
$$
where $y$ is the output associated with the *outgoing* edge from $s$ on input $x$, and $y'$ is the output associated with the *outgoing* edge from $s'$.

### Step 4 — Identify the new initial state
The new initial Moore state is $s_{0, y_0}$ where $y_0 = \lambda_M(s_0, x_0)$ for the *first* input $x_0$. If $s_0$ has multiple distinct outputs, multiple initial states exist (one per possible first output).

### Step 5 — State count bound
If the Mealy machine has $\vert S \vert$ states and at most $k$ distinct outputs per state, then the equivalent Moore machine has at most:
$$
\vert S_{\text{Moore}} \vert \leq \vert S \vert \cdot k
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked numerical example (KTU 2022 style):**
> Mealy: $A \xrightarrow{0/0} B$, $B \xrightarrow{0/1} C$, $C \xrightarrow{0/0} A$, $A \xrightarrow{1/0} C$.
> Out$(A) = \{0\}$ → single Moore state $A_0$.
> Out$(B) = \{1\}$ → single Moore state $B_1$.
> Out$(C) = \{0\}$ → single Moore state $C_0$.
> Moore transitions: $\delta(A_0, 0) = B_1$, $\delta(B_1, 0) = C_0$, $\delta(C_0, 0) = A_0$, $\delta(A_0, 1) = C_0$.
> Output function: $\lambda_{\text{Moore}}(A_0) = 0$, $\lambda_{\text{Moore}}(B_1) = 1$, $\lambda_{\text{Moore}}(C_0) = 0$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — SDF Minimum Iteration Count

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The repetition vector $\vec{q}$ of an SDF graph is required for *any* static schedule derivation.

### Step 1 — Construct the topology matrix $\Gamma$
For an SDF graph with $n$ actors, define $\Gamma \in \mathbb{Z}^{n \times n}$ as:
$$
\Gamma_{ij} = \text{consumption of actor } i \text{ from edge } j - \text{production of actor } i \text{ to edge } j
$$
More commonly, edges and actors are aligned so that $\Gamma$ has:
$$
\Gamma_{ij} = \text{prod}(j \to i) - \text{cons}(j \to i)
$$

### Step 2 — Solve the balance equation
The smallest positive integer vector $\vec{q} \in \mathbb{N}^n$ satisfying:
$$
\Gamma \cdot \vec{q} = \vec{0}
$$

### Step 3 — Minimum number of firings per actor
The component $q_i$ of $\vec{q}$ tells how many times actor $i$ must fire in one *period* of the SDF schedule. The minimum iterations to return to the initial state is:
$$
N_{\text{iterations}} = \text{lcm}(q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n)
$$

### Worked Example
Consider a 2-actor SDF: Actor A produces 2 tokens on edge $e$, Actor B consumes 3 tokens from $e$ per firing.
$\Gamma = \begin{pmatrix} 2 & -3 \end{pmatrix}^{\top}$ (for the edge $e$).
Balance: $2 q_A - 3 q_B = 0$ → smallest positive solution $q_A = 3$, $q_B = 2$.
Therefore A fires **3 times** and B fires **2 times** in one schedule period, and $N = \text{lcm}(3,2) = 6$ total firings.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — State Minimisation of a Fully-Specified FSM

### Step 1 — Partition table initialisation
Create partition $P_0$ grouping states by their *output behaviour* (Moore) or *(state, output)* pair (Mealy).

### Step 2 — Iterative refinement
For each block $B \in P_k$, check if all states in $B$ transition to states in the *same* blocks of $P_k$ on every input. If not, split $B$.

### Step 3 — Termination
When $P_{k+1} = P_k$, the partition is stable. Each remaining block becomes a single state in the minimal machine.

### Step 4 — Number-of-states bound
For a machine with original $\vert S \vert$ states and $m$ input symbols, the worst-case number of partition steps is bounded by $\vert S \vert - 1$.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Model | Time Abstraction | Concurrency | State | Verification | Typical Use |
|-------|------------------|-------------|-------|--------------|-------------|
| **FSM (Mealy/Moore)** | Discrete, synchronous | Low (one state) | Explicit | Exhaustive state exploration | Controllers, protocol handlers |
| **Statechart** | Discrete | High (orthogonal regions) | Hierarchical | Model checking (SPIN, UPPAAL) | UI flows, avionics, medical |
| **DFG** | Continuous or discrete | Medium (data-driven) | Implicit (data tokens) | Cycle detection, liveness | DSP, image processing |
| **SDF** | Discrete, static | High (compile-time scheduled) | Implicit | Balance equation, throughput | Streaming codecs, radio baseband |
| **Petri Net** | Discrete, asynchronous | High (places/transitions) | Marking $M$ | Reachability, invariant analysis | Multiprocessor sync, manufacturing |
| **KPN** | Continuous, asynchronous | High (processes + FIFOs) | Per-process | Kahn property, deadlock detection | Streaming on heterogeneous SoCs |
| **Discrete Event** | Discrete event list | High (processes) | Event queue | DES simulators (SystemC) | Hardware/software cosimulation |
| **CSP / Actor** | Discrete message-passing | Very high (channels) | Mailbox | FDR model checker, pi-calculus | Distributed IoT, sensor networks |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchy of Computational Models (Master Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootCM[Computational Models in Embedded Design]
    rootCM --> seqM[Sequential Imperative Models]
    rootCM --> stateM[State Oriented Models]
    rootCM --> dataM[Data Flow Models]
    rootCM --> concM[Concurrent Reactive Models]
    rootCM --> commM[Communication Oriented Models]

    seqM --> vonN[Von Neumann Model]
    seqM --> harv[Harvard Model]

    stateM --> fsm[Finite State Machine]
    fsm --> mealy[Mealy Machine]
    fsm --> moore[Moore Machine]
    fsm --> statech[Statechart Harel]

    dataM --> dfg[Data Flow Graph]
    dataM --> sdf[Synchronous Data Flow]

    concM --> petri[Petri Net]
    concM --> kpn[Kahn Process Network]
    concM --> de[Discrete Event System]

    commM --> csp[CSP Hoare]
    commM --> actor[Actor Model]

    vonN --> app1[General Purpose MCU]
    harv --> app2[DSP Microcontroller]
    mealy --> app3[Traffic Light Controller]
    moore --> app4[Elevator Controller]
    statech --> app5[Mobile Phone UI Modes]
    dfg --> app6[Audio Equalizer]
    sdf --> app7[MP3 Decoder on DSP]
    petri --> app8[Multiprocessor Resource Lock]
    kpn --> app9[H264 Video Pipeline]
    de --> app10[SystemC TLM Simulation]
    csp --> app11[Go based IoT Firmware]
    actor --> app12[AKKA based Edge Node]
```

## 4.2 FSM State Diagram (Mealy vs Moore Comparison)

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Idle

    Idle: S0 Idle
    Running: S1 Running
    Fault: S2 Fault
    Service: S3 Service

    Idle --> Running : ev1 Start and out1 equals Motor ON
    Running --> Idle : ev2 Stop and out2 equals Motor OFF
    Running --> Fault : ev3 Overheat and out3 equals Alarm HIGH
    Fault --> Service : ev4 Reset and out4 equals Alarm LOW
    Service --> Idle : ev5 Done and out5 equals Ready
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The labels follow the `event / output` Mealy convention. For an equivalent Moore machine, the *output* moves from the transition label to the *target* state's annotation.

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Generic Embedded System

```mermaid
graph LR
    inSensor[Input Sensor Bank] --> digFront[Digital Front End Filter]
    inUser[User Interface Buttons] --> digFront
    digFront --> ctrlCore[Control Core FSM]
    ctrlCore --> actBank[Actuator Driver Bank]
    ctrlCore --> diagMod[Diagnostic and Logger Module]
    actBank --> outMotor[DC Motor Output]
    actBank --> outRelay[Relay Output]
    diagMod --> uart[UART Telemetry Link]
    uart --> hostPC[Host PC]
    ctrlCore --> wdt[Watchdog Timer]
    wdt --> ctrlCore
    pwrMgr[Power Manager State Machine] --> ctrlCore
    pwrMgr --> digFront
    pwrMgr --> actBank
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block interpretation:** The *Control Core FSM* sits at the centre. The *Power Manager* is itself a smaller FSM in a **hierarchical Statechart** relationship with the core. The *Watchdog* provides a safety reset transition back to *Idle*. The *Diagnostic Module* runs as an orthogonal (concurrent) state region in Statechart terminology.

## 4.4 Data Flow Graph for a Simple FIR Filter

```mermaid
graph LR
    src[Source Sample x n] --> mulA[Multiply by h0]
    mulA --> sumA[Summation]
    src --> delay1[Unit Delay z inv]
    delay1 --> mulB[Multiply by h1]
    mulB --> sumA
    delay1 --> delay2[Unit Delay z inv]
    delay2 --> mulC[Multiply by h2]
    mulC --> sumA
    sumA --> out[Output y n]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the DFG:** Each multiplication actor is a vertex, each edge carries one sample token. The two `Unit Delay` blocks are stateful actors, the rest are stateless. This graph is a **homogeneous SDF** because every edge produces and consumes exactly one token per firing.

## 4.5 Petri Net of a Producer–Consumer System

```mermaid
graph LR
    p1((Place P1 Buffer Full)) -->|token consumed| t1{Transition T1 Produce}
    t1 -->|token produced| p2((Place P2 Item Available))
    p2 -->|token consumed| t2{Transition T2 Consume}
    t2 -->|token produced| p1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Petri net interpretation:** Places $P_1$ and $P_2$ hold tokens. The initial marking $M_0$ decides how many items are in the buffer. $T_1$ fires when $P_1$ has a token (i.e., there is free space), and $T_2$ fires when $P_2$ has a token (i.e., an item is available). This mutual exclusion is what makes Petri nets ideal for resource-locking verification.

## 4.6 Kahn Process Network (KPN) for a Streaming Pipeline

```mermaid
graph LR
    srcP[Process SRC Capture] -->|FIFO ch1| procA[Process A Encode]
    procA -->|FIFO ch2| procB[Process B Encrypt]
    procB -->|FIFO ch3| sinkP[Process SNK Transmit]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KPN rule:** Every FIFO is *unbounded* in theory, bounded in practice. `send` is **non-blocking**; `receive` is **blocking**. The model guarantees *determinism* — the output is independent of process scheduling order.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between Mealy and Moore machines with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mealy Machine | Moore Machine |
|--------|---------------|---------------|
| Output dependency | Output depends on **current state and current input** | Output depends **only on current state** |
| Output function | $\lambda : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Delta$ | $\lambda : S \rightarrow \Delta$ |
| Latency | Faster (output on transition) | Slower by one clock (output on state entry) |
| Glitch sensitivity | Sensitive to input glitches | Glitch-free |
| Typical use | Serial data receivers, edge-triggered controllers | Counter-driven displays, asynchronous resets |

**Example:** A 2-bit up-counter controlling a 7-segment display is best modelled as a **Moore** machine (output tied to the count), while a 1-bit pattern detector on a serial line is a **Mealy** machine (output on the detected edge).

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List and briefly explain any three properties of a Petri net.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Concurrency:** Multiple transitions can fire simultaneously if their input places are marked — directly models parallelism in embedded multiprocessors.
2. **Synchronisation:** A transition can require tokens from *several* input places before firing — models barrier-style coordination.
3. **Asynchrony:** There is no global clock; firing order depends only on token availability — models event-driven embedded firmware.
4. (Bonus) **Resource conflict / mutual exclusion:** Identical tokens competing for a transition model critical sections naturally.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern (Module Internal Choice):** Each 14-mark question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Both sub-parts must be answered from the chosen option.

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Von Neumann and Harvard computational models** used in embedded processors. Compare them on at least five parameters. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Design a **Moore machine** that detects the sequence **"1011"** on a single-bit input stream with overlap allowed. Draw the state transition diagram, the state table, and explain the VHDL/Verilog implementation strategy. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a)

**Von Neumann Model:**
- Single shared memory holds both *program instructions* and *data*.
- Single bus for instruction fetch and data read/write → **Von Neumann bottleneck**.
- Examples: Intel 8051, ARM Cortex-M0 (in single-bus configurations).

**Harvard Model:**
- **Separate** program memory and data memory with **independent buses**.
- Allows simultaneous instruction fetch and data access.
- Examples: Atmel AVR, TI MSP430, most DSP cores.

**Five-point comparison:**

| Parameter | Von Neumann | Harvard |
|-----------|-------------|---------|
| Memory | Unified | Separate I and D |
| Bus | One | Two |
| Fetch + Execute | Sequential | Parallel possible |
| Cost / Complexity | Lower | Higher |
| Power consumption | Lower | Higher (more buses) |
| Determinism of fetch | Lower (prefetch may stall) | Higher |
| Example | 8051 | AVR, DSPs |

**Diagram description:** Draw two boxes labelled "PM" and "DM" in Harvard, with two distinct arrows to the CPU; for Von Neumann show one combined "Memory" box with one arrow.

#### Model Answer for (b)

**State Design (Moore, sequence "1011", overlap allowed):**

| State | Meaning | Output (z) |
|-------|---------|-----------|
| S0 | Initial / last input not '1' | 0 |
| S1 | Saw '1' (1 in a row) | 0 |
| S2 | Saw "10" | 0 |
| S3 | Saw "101" | 0 |
| S4 | Saw "1011" | **1** |

**State Table:**

| Present State | Input x=0 | Input x=1 | Output z |
|---------------|-----------|-----------|----------|
| S0 | S0 | S1 | 0 |
| S1 | S2 | S1 | 0 |
| S2 | S0 | S3 | 0 |
| S3 | S2 | S4 | 0 |
| S4 | S2 | S1 | 1 |

**Overlap rule:** From S4 on input 1, go to S1 (the last 1 may start a new "1011"), not S0. From S4 on input 0, go to S2 (the last 0 is a valid suffix of "10").

**Implementation strategy:** Encode states in 3 bits using binary or one-hot. Use a `case` statement in VHDL/Verilog or a lookup ROM. Reset the FSM to S0. Register the output `z` directly from the present state (Moore property).

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **Synchronous Data Flow (SDF) computational model** with a suitable example. Derive the **balance equation** and compute the **minimum iteration count** for the example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Petri net model** of a **mutual exclusion** problem (two processes competing for one shared resource). Identify all reachable markings and prove that the model is **deadlock-free** and **starvation-free** under fairness. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for (a)

**SDF Model Definition:** An SDF graph is a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ where each actor $a \in V$ consumes $\text{cons}(a, e)$ tokens from each input edge $e$ and produces $\text{prod}(a, e)$ tokens on each output edge $e$ per firing. The production and consumption numbers are *constants known at compile time*.

**Example — 3-actor audio mixer:**
- Actor A: produces 2 samples on edge $e_1$, produces 1 sample on edge $e_2$.
- Actor B: consumes 1 sample on $e_1$, produces 1 sample on $e_3$.
- Actor C: consumes 1 sample on $e_2$, consumes 1 sample on $e_3$.

**Balance equation derivation:** Edge $e_1$: $2 q_A = 1 q_B$. Edge $e_2$: $1 q_A = 1 q_C$. Edge $e_3$: $1 q_B = 1 q_C$. Solving: $q_A = 1$, $q_B = 2$, $q_C = 1$ is *not* consistent. Re-scaling by $\text{lcm}$: multiply by 2 → $q_A = 2$, $q_B = 4$, $q_C = 2$.

**Minimum iterations:** $N = \text{lcm}(2, 4, 2) = 4$ firings per period.

**Schedule within one period (one valid order):** A, A, B, C (note that A fires twice consecutively because its outputs are buffered).

#### Model Answer for (b)

**Petri net for mutual exclusion of two processes P1 and P2 accessing one shared resource R:**

- Places: $p_1$ (P1 wants access), $p_2$ (P2 wants access), $r$ (resource free).
- Transitions: $t_1$ (P1 takes resource), $t_2$ (P2 takes resource), $u_1$ (P1 releases), $u_2$ (P2 releases).
- Initial marking $M_0 = (p_1, p_2, r) = (1, 1, 1)$.

**Reachability analysis** (state = $(p_1, p_2, r)$ tokens):

- $M_0 = (1, 1, 1)$ — both processes idle, resource free.
- $M_1 = (0, 1, 1)$ — P1 took resource, P2 still waiting.
- $M_2 = (1, 0, 1)$ — P2 took resource, P1 still waiting.
- $M_3 = (0, 1, 0)$ — P1 holding, P2 waiting, resource not free.
- $M_4 = (1, 0, 0)$ — P2 holding, P1 waiting, resource not free.
- $M_5 = (1, 1, 1) = M_0$ — releases return to initial.

All markings have at least one enabled transition → **deadlock-free**.
Under *fairness* (each enabled transition eventually fires), both $t_1$ and $t_2$ fire infinitely often → **starvation-free**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 2):**
> 1. **Confusing Mealy and Moore output notation** — examiners deduct 1–2 marks if the output function is written incorrectly (must be $\lambda : S \times \Sigma \rightarrow \Delta$ for Mealy and $\lambda : S \rightarrow \Delta$ for Moore).
> 2. **Skipping the balance equation derivation in SDF** — students often quote $\vec{q}$ without showing $\Gamma \cdot \vec{q} = 0$. This costs up to 3 marks.
> 3. **Forgetting the overlap rule in sequence detectors** — Moore sequence detector for "1011" must be drawn with the **S4 → S1** transition on input 1; otherwise it is wrong.
> 4. **Petri net reachability missing cases** — listing only $M_0$ and $M_1$ loses half the marks; you must enumerate *all* reachable markings from $M_0$.
> 5. **Mixing Von Neumann and Harvard** — calling Harvard "faster Von Neumann" is a common mistake; Harvard has *separate buses*, not just separate memory.
> 6. **Confusing SDF with general DFG** — DFG is dynamic; SDF is static. Do not interchange them in definitions.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Module 2 — Computational Models in Embedded Design:**

- ☐ A **computational model** is an abstraction used to analyse and design embedded systems *before* implementation.
- ☐ Three classification axes: **time abstraction**, **state representation**, **concurrency**.
- ☐ **Von Neumann**: single bus, single memory, cheaper, lower determinism. **Harvard**: dual bus, dual memory, faster, more expensive.
- ☐ **FSM 6-tuple**: $(S, \Sigma, \Delta, \delta, \lambda, s_0)$ — must be written correctly.
- ☐ **Mealy** output = $\lambda(s, x)$; **Moore** output = $\lambda(s)$.
- ☐ Moore → Mealy conversion keeps state count; Mealy → Moore may **increase** state count.
- ☐ **Statecharts** add *hierarchy* and *orthogonality* (parallel states) to FSMs.
- ☐ **DFG** is data-driven; an actor fires when all inputs are available.
- ☐ **SDF** is a *static* DFG — production/consumption numbers are compile-time constants. Balance equation: $\Gamma \vec{q} = \vec{0}$.
- ☐ Minimum SDF firings = $\text{lcm}(q_1, q_2, \dots, q_n)$.
- ☐ **Petri net 5-tuple**: $(P, T, F, W, M_0)$.
- ☐ A Petri net transition fires when *all* input places have the required number of tokens.
- ☐ **KPN** uses *unbounded* FIFOs; `send` is non-blocking, `receive` is blocking; *deterministic*.
- ☐ **Discrete Event** model uses a time-ordered event queue (used in SystemC TLM).
- ☐ **CSP / Actor** models focus on *message passing* between independent processes.
- ☐ Model choice affects: throughput, latency, power, determinism, scalability.
- ☐ Automotive: AUTOSAR uses FSM + OS scheduling. Aerospace: Petri nets (formal verification). IoT: Reactive FSM + Statecharts.
- ☐ Always check schedulability: $T_{\text{req}} \leq \text{WCET} + J$ and $D_{\text{deadline}} \geq \text{WCET} + \text{preemption overhead}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Unified Modelling Language
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Introduction to Unified Modelling Language # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of UML ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Unified Modeling Language (UML)** is a **standardized, general-purpose, visual modeling language** in the field of **object-oriented software engineering** and **embedded systems design**. It is maintained and standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. UML provides a **set of graphical notation techniques** to create abstract models of systems, capturing both **structural** and **behavioral** aspects of software-intensive systems through 14 standardized diagram types. The current version widely referenced in industry and academia is **UML 2.5.1** (released December 2017), with **UML 2.5** being the standard baseline taught in KTU 2024 scheme curriculum. > [!NOTE] > **OMG (Object Management Group)** — An international, open membership, not-for-profit computer industry standards consortium that maintains UML as ISO/IEC 19505 standard. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain English Intuition Imagine you are going to build a **complex house** (your embedded system). Before laying a single brick, an architect draws: - A **floor plan** (showing rooms, doors, walls) — this is a *Structural Diagram* - An **electrical wiring plan** (showing how power flows) — this is a *Behavioral Diagram* - A **sequence of activities** during construction (foundation → walls → roof) — this is a *Behavioral/Interaction Diagram* UML does **exactly the same thing** for software and embedded systems. Instead of bricks and cement, UML helps engineers draw **blueprints** of software components, their relationships, their lifecycles, and how they interact over time — *before* a single line of code is written. > [!TIP] > **Think of UML as the "Engineering Blueprint" of the software world.** Just as no civil engineer builds a skyscraper without drawings, no serious embedded system should be built without UML models. ## 1.3 Historical Evolution — Why UML Exists Before UML, the **"Method Wars"** of the 1990s produced dozens of competing object-oriented modeling notations: | Year | Event | Key Contributor | |------|-------|-----------------| | **1991–1994** | OMT (Object Modeling Technique) developed | James Rumbaugh | | **1991** | Booch Method developed | Grady Booch | | **1987–1993** | OOSE (Object-Oriented Software Engineering) | Ivar Jacobson | | **1994** | Rumbaugh joins Booch at Rational Software | Merger begins | | **1995** | "Unified Method 0.8" released | Booch & Rumbaugh | | **1996** | Jacobson joins; renamed to **UML** | The "Three Amigos" | | **1997** | UML **1.0** submitted to OMG | Adopted as standard | | **2005** | UML **2.0** major revision (14 diagrams) | OMG | | **2017** | UML **2.5.1** (current) | OMG/ISO | > [!NOTE] > The **"Three Amigos"** — Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, and Ivar Jacobson — are credited as the original creators of UML at Rational Software (later acquired by IBM). ## 1.4 Purpose of UML in Embedded Systems For an **Advanced Embedded Systems** course, UML serves four critical engineering purposes: 1. **Specification** — Precisely define system requirements and behavior. 2. **Visualization** — Communicate architecture between hardware and software teams. 3. **Documentation** — Provide standardized, language-independent system blueprints. 4. **Code Generation** — Model-Driven Architecture (MDA) tools can auto-generate C/C++ code from UML state machines (e.g., in **Enterprise Architect**, **Rhapsody**, **Astah**). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Map ## 2.1 The 14 UML 2.5 Diagrams — Complete Classification UML organizes its diagrams into **two major categories**, further divided into sub-categories: ### Category A: Structure Diagrams (Static View — "What is in the system?") > [!IMPORTANT] > **Structure diagrams** show the **static architecture** of the system — its components, classes, packages, and their relationships. They do **NOT** depict dynamic behavior. | # | Diagram | Purpose | KTU Relevance | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 1 | **Class Diagram** | Shows classes, attributes, operations, and relationships | ★★★★★ | | 2 | **Object Diagram** | Snapshot of instances at a moment in time | ★★ | | 3 | **Component Diagram** | Organization of physical software components | ★★★★ | | 4 | **Composite Structure Diagram** | Internal structure of a class | ★★★ | | 5 | **Package Diagram** | Grouping of model elements into packages | ★★ | | 6 | **Deployment Diagram** | Physical hardware nodes and software allocation | ★★★★★ | ### Category B: Behavior Diagrams (Dynamic View — "What happens over time?") > [!IMPORTANT] > **Behavior diagrams** show the **dynamic behavior** of the system — how objects interact, change state, and process activities over time. | # | Diagram | Purpose | KTU Relevance | |---|---------|---------|---------------| | 7 | **Use Case Diagram** | Functional requirements from actor perspective | ★★★★★ | | 8 | **Activity Diagram** | Workflows, business processes, parallel actions | ★★★★ | | 9 | **State Machine Diagram** | Lifecycle states of an object (huge in embedded!) | ★★★★★ | | 10 | **Sequence Diagram** | Time-ordered message exchange | ★★★★ | | 11 | **Communication Diagram** | Message exchange focused on object links | ★★ | | 12 | **Timing Diagram** | State changes over time with time axis | ★★★★ | | 13 | **Interaction Overview Diagram** | Activity diagram of interactions | ★★ | | 14 | **Profile Diagram** | Extending UML for domain-specific needs | ★★ | ## 2.2 The Four Common Mechanisms (Reusable Constructs) Every UML diagram is built using **four common mechanisms** that apply universally: 1. **Specifications** — Textual description of semantics behind graphical elements (e.g., what a class symbol *means*). 2. **Adornments** — Additional graphical details on standard symbols (e.g., `{readOnly}` next to an attribute). 3. **Common Divisions** — Two ways to view any model: - **Type/Instance** (Class vs. Object) - **Static/Dynamic** (Snapshot vs. Behavior) 4. **Extensibility Mechanisms**: - **Stereotype** — `<>`, `<>` — extends vocabulary - **Tagged Value** — adds properties: `{author = "John"}` - **Constraint** — `{ XOR }`, `{ ordered }` — adds semantic rules > [!NOTE] > **Stereotypes** are written in `<>` and are the most frequently examined extensibility mechanism in KTU exams. ## 2.3 "Views" of a System — The 4+1 Architectural View Model UML was originally designed around the **"4+1 View Model"** by Philippe Kruchten (1995): | View | Purpose | Typical Diagrams | |------|---------|------------------| | **Logical View** | Functional requirements (end-user) | Class, Object, Composite Structure | | **Process View** | Concurrency, performance | Activity, State Machine | | **Physical View** | Hardware topology, deployment | Deployment, Component | | **Development View** | Software modules in dev environment | Component, Package | | **+1 Use Case View** | Scenarios tying all views together | Use Case | > [!TIP] > For embedded systems, the **+1 Use Case View** acts as the "glue" that validates all four other views are consistent and complete. ## 2.4 UML Building Blocks — The Fundamental Elements Every UML diagram is composed of **three categories** of building blocks: ### A. Things (Structural, Behavioral, Grouping, Annotational) | Type | Things | Description | |------|--------|-------------| | **Structural** | Class, Interface, Collaboration, Use Case, Active Class, Component, Node, Artifact | Nouns of the model | | **Behavioral** | Interaction, State Machine, Activity | Verbs of the model | | **Grouping** | Package | Organizing mechanism | | **Annotational** | Note (Comment) | Documentation | ### B. Relationships (The "Glue" Between Things) | Relationship | Notation | Meaning | |--------------|----------|---------| | **Association** | Solid line | "uses-a" / structural connection | | **Directed Association** | Line with arrow | One-way usage | | **Aggregation** | Hollow diamond ◇ | Weak "has-a" (whole–part) | | **Composition** | Filled diamond ◆ | Strong "owns-a" (lifecycle-bound) | | **Generalization** | Hollow triangle △ | "is-a" (inheritance) | | **Realization** | Dashed line + △ | "implements" an interface | | **Dependency** | Dashed arrow --> | "depends on" (transient) | ### C. Diagrams (The 14 visualizations listed above) ## 2.5 UML Extensibility — The Three Mechanisms $$\text{UML Extensibility} = \underbrace{\text{Stereotypes}}_{\text{new types}} + \underbrace{\text{Tagged Values}}_{\text{new properties}} + \underbrace{\text{Constraints}}_{\text{new semantics}}$$ - **Stereotype** $\rightarrow$ extends the **vocabulary** (e.g., `<>` for a real-time task in embedded systems) - **Tagged Value** $\rightarrow$ extends the **properties** (e.g., `{priority = 1}`) - **Constraint** $\rightarrow$ extends the **semantics** (e.g., `{deadline < 10ms}`) ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility | Industry | UML Application | |----------|-----------------| | **Automotive (AUTOSAR)** | State machines for ECU behavior | | **Avionics (DO-178C)** | Model-Based Development with UML | | **Medical Devices (FDA)** | Class & State diagrams for safety-critical firmware | | **IoT/Embedded RTOS** | Task scheduling activity diagrams | | **Telecommunications** | Sequence diagrams for protocol stack interactions | # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Walkthrough of UML Concepts ## 3.1 Worked Example: From Problem Statement to UML Use Case Diagram **Problem:** *"Design a system for an Automatic Washing Machine with microcontroller."* ### Step 1: Identify Actors Actors are **external entities** (human or another system) that interact with the system. - **User (Person)** — presses buttons, loads clothes - **Water Level Sensor** — provides water level feedback - **Door Sensor** — detects if door is closed - **Temperature Sensor** — provides water temperature ### Step 2: Identify Use Cases Use cases are **system functions** that provide value to actors. - `LoadClothes` - `SelectWashCycle` - `FillWater` - `HeatWater` - `Wash` - `Rinse` - `Spin` - `DrainWater` - `BuzzerAlert` - `DisplayStatus` ### Step 3: Identify Relationships - `<>` — mandatory sub-function (e.g., `Wash` `<>` `FillWater`) - `<>` — optional extension (e.g., `BuzzerAlert` `<>` `Wash`) - **Generalization** — child use case inherits parent (e.g., `QuickWash` is-a `Wash`) ### Step 4: Construct the Diagram (Verbal Mermaid Description) ``` Actor "User" -- UC1 "Load Clothes" Actor "User" -- UC2 "Select Wash Cycle" US1 "Washing Machine" -- UC2 US1 -- UC3 "Fill Water" US1 -- UC4 "Heat Water" US1 -- UC5 "Wash" US1 -- UC6 "Rinse" US1 -- UC7 "Spin" US1 -- UC8 "Drain Water" US1 -- UC9 "Buzzer Alert" UC5 -.include.-> UC3 UC5 -.extend.-> UC9 UC6 -.include.-> UC3 ``` ## 3.2 Worked Example: Class Diagram for an Embedded Sensor System **Entities to model:** | Class | Attributes | Operations | |-------|-----------|------------| | `Microcontroller` | -model: String, -clockMHz: int | +readSensor(), +actuatorCtrl() | | `Sensor` | -type: String, -pin: int | +read(): float | | `Actuator` | -type: String, -pin: int | +activate(), +deactivate() | | `UART` | -baudRate: int | +send(String), +receive(): String | ### Deriving the Relationships (Logic Flow) 1. The `Microcontroller` *has-a* `Sensor` → **Aggregation** (sensor can exist independently) 2. The `Microcontroller` *owns-a* `Actuator` → **Composition** (actuator dies with the MCU board) 3. The `Microcontroller` *uses* `UART` → **Association** 4. `UART` *implements* `CommunicationInterface` → **Realization** ### Resulting Multiplicities $$1 \text{ Microcontroller} \xrightarrow{\text{aggregation}} 1..* \text{ Sensors}$$ $$1 \text{ Microcontroller} \xrightarrow{\text{composition}} 0..* \text{ Actuators}$$ ## 3.3 Analytical Derivation: Why UML 2.5 Has Exactly 14 Diagrams The 14-diagram count emerges from a systematic enumeration: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Diagrams} &= \text{Structure} + \text{Behavior} \\ &= 6 + 8 \\ &= 14 \end{aligned}$$ Where: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Structure} &= \text{Class} + \text{Object} + \text{Component} \\ &\quad + \text{Composite} + \text{Package} + \text{Deployment} = 6 \end{aligned}$$ $$\begin{aligned} \text{Behavior} &= \text{UseCase} + \text{Activity} + \text{StateMachine} \\ &\quad + \text{Sequence} + \text{Communication} + \text{Timing} \\ &\quad + \text{InteractionOverview} + \text{Profile} = 8 \end{aligned}$$ ## 3.4 Algorithmic-Style Decision: When to Use Which Diagram (Embedded Context) ``` IF modeling hardware topology → DEPLOYMENT DIAGRAM IF modeling software modules → COMPONENT DIAGRAM IF modeling user interactions → USE CASE DIAGRAM IF modeling object lifecycles → STATE MACHINE DIAGRAM IF modeling workflows/algorithms → ACTIVITY DIAGRAM IF modeling message protocols → SEQUENCE DIAGRAM IF modeling class structures → CLASS DIAGRAM IF modeling real-time constraints → TIMING DIAGRAM ``` > [!TIP] > For **RTOS-based embedded systems**, the most frequently used UML diagrams are: **State Machine**, **Sequence**, **Deployment**, and **Activity** — these should be your KTU exam focus. ## 3.5 Comparative Analysis Table: UML vs. Other Modeling Languages | Feature | UML | SysML | BPMN | Flowcharts | |---------|-----|-------|------|------------| | **Domain** | Software/Embedded | Systems Engineering | Business Process | Algorithms | | **Standard** | OMG/ISO | OMG | OMG | Informal | | **Diagram Count** | 14 | 9 | 5 | 1 | | **Object-Oriented** | Yes | Yes | No | No | | **Hardware Modeling** | Limited | Excellent | No | No | | **Used in Embedded** | Yes | Yes | Rarely | Basic | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Master Classification Flowchart of UML 2.5 Diagrams ```mermaid flowchart TD A[UML 2.5 - 14 Diagrams] --> B[Structure Diagrams - 6] A --> C[Behavior Diagrams - 8] B --> B1[Class Diagram] B --> B2[Object Diagram] B --> B3[Component Diagram] B --> B4[Composite Structure Diagram] B --> B5[Package Diagram] B --> B6[Deployment Diagram] C --> C1[Use Case Diagram] C --> C2[Activity Diagram] C --> C3[State Machine Diagram] C --> D[Interaction Diagrams - 4] D --> D1[Sequence Diagram] D --> D2[Communication Diagram] D --> D3[Timing Diagram] D --> D4[Interaction Overview Diagram] C --> C4[Profile Diagram] ``` ## 4.2 UML 4+1 Architectural View Model ```mermaid flowchart LR UC[Plus 1 - Use Case View
Scenarios glue views] --> LV[Logical View] UC --> PV[Process View] UC --> PView[Physical View] UC --> DV[Development View] LV --> D1[Class Diagram] LV --> D2[Object Diagram] PV --> D3[Activity Diagram] PV --> D4[State Machine Diagram] PView --> D5[Deployment Diagram] DV --> D6[Component Diagram] ``` ## 4.3 Relationship Notation Reference Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Class A] ---|Association| B1[Class B] A2[Class A] -->|Directed Association| B2[Class B] A3[Whole] o--|Aggregation - hollow diamond| B3[Part] A4[Whole] x--|Composition - filled diamond| B4[Part] A5[Child] --o|Generalization - hollow triangle| B5[Parent] A6[Class] ..> |Realization - dashed| B6[Interface] A7[Client] ..>|Dependency - dashed arrow| B7[Supplier] ``` ## 4.4 UML Stereotype and Extensibility Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD EXT[UML Extensibility Mechanisms] --> S[Stereotypes - new types] EXT --> TV[Tagged Values - new properties] EXT --> CN[Constraints - new semantics] S --> S1[double bracket notation] S --> S2[example: interface] S --> S3[example: RTtask for embedded] TV --> TV1[curly braces] TV --> TV2[example: priority equals 1] CN --> CN1[curly braces] CN --> CN2[example: deadline less than 10ms] ``` ## 4.5 Embedded System UML Modeling Architecture (Functional Block View) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph HARDWARE[Physical Layer - Hardware Nodes] MCU[Microcontroller Node] SENS[Sensor Node] ACT[Actuator Node] DISP[Display Node] end subgraph SOFTWARE[Software Layer - Components] DRV[Device Drivers] RTOS[RTOS Scheduler] APP[Application Logic] COMM[Communication Stack] end subgraph BEHAVIOR[Behavioral Models] SM[State Machine - System Lifecycle] ACT2[Activity - Task Workflow] SEQ[Sequence - Inter-process Messages] end MCU --> DRV SENS --> DRV DRV --> RTOS RTOS --> APP APP --> ACT APP --> COMM APP --> DISP SM -.governs.-> APP ACT2 -.models.-> RTOS SEQ -.models.-> COMM ``` ## 4.6 Use Case Diagram — Conceptual Template ```mermaid flowchart LR User[Actor: User] --- UC1((Load Clothes)) User --- UC2((Select Wash Cycle)) System([Washing Machine System]) --- UC2 System --- UC3((Fill Water)) System --- UC4((Wash)) System --- UC5((Rinse)) System --- UC6((Spin)) UC4 -.include.-> UC3 UC5 -.include.-> UC3 UC4 -.extend.-> UC7((Buzzer Alert)) ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: Define Unified Modeling Language (UML). List any four structural diagrams of UML.** **Model Answer:** **UML Definition:** UML (Unified Modeling Language) is a **standardized, general-purpose visual modeling language** standardized by the **Object Management Group (OMG)**. It is used to specify, visualize, construct, and document the artifacts of software-intensive systems, especially those built using object-oriented methodologies. The current version is **UML 2.5.1**, adopted as **ISO/IEC 19505**. **Four Structural Diagrams:** 1. **Class Diagram** — depicts classes, attributes, operations, and their relationships. 2. **Component Diagram** — shows organization of physical software components. 3. **Deployment Diagram** — models physical hardware nodes and software artifacts deployed on them. 4. **Package Diagram** — shows the grouping of model elements into logical packages. 5. *(Alternative)* Object Diagram, Composite Structure Diagram. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often confuse "Class Diagram" with "Object Diagram." A **Class Diagram** shows *types/templates*, while an **Object Diagram** shows *specific instances* (e.g., `myCar:Toyota`). --- ### **Question A2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: What are stereotypes in UML? Give two examples of stereotypes used in embedded systems.** **Model Answer:** **Definition:** A **stereotype** is one of the three **UML extensibility mechanisms** that allows engineers to extend the vocabulary of UML by creating new kinds of model elements derived from existing ones, but specific to a particular problem domain. Stereotypes are denoted within `<>`. **Two Examples for Embedded Systems:** 1. **`<>`** — Used to model a real-time task in an RTOS-based system. 2. **`<>`** — Used to model a hardware/software interface (e.g., I2C, SPI bus interface). 3. **`<>`** — Used to model an Interrupt Service Routine. 4. **`<>`** — Used to model physical sensor entities. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Do **NOT** use **bold** (`**`) or HTML tags inside the double quotes of a node label in Mermaid diagrams. Use plain text like `<>`. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice ### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q: (a)** Explain the different categories of UML diagrams with a neat sketch. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** UML 2.5 defines **14 diagrams** organized into **two main categories**: **1. Structure Diagrams (Static View) — 6 diagrams:** These depict the static structure of the system — its components, classes, packages, and their relationships. | Diagram | Purpose | |---------|---------| | Class | Classes, attributes, methods, relationships | | Object | Instances at runtime snapshot | | Component | Physical software modules | | Composite Structure | Internal structure of a class | | Package | Logical grouping | | Deployment | Hardware topology | **2. Behavior Diagrams (Dynamic View) — 8 diagrams:** These depict the dynamic behavior — how the system behaves over time. | Sub-Category | Diagrams | |--------------|----------| | **Use Case** | Use Case Diagram | | **State-Based** | State Machine Diagram | | **Activity-Based** | Activity Diagram | | **Interaction** (4) | Sequence, Communication, Timing, Interaction Overview | | **Extension** | Profile Diagram | ``` UML 2.5 ├── Structure (6) │ ├── Class, Object │ ├── Component, Composite Structure │ └── Package, Deployment └── Behavior (8) ├── Use Case ├── State Machine ├── Activity ├── Interaction (4): Sequence, Communication, Timing, IO └── Profile ``` **Use case for embedded systems:** A **Deployment Diagram** shows which software runs on which MCU/hardware node, while a **State Machine Diagram** models lifecycle states (e.g., `Idle → Sensing → Transmitting → Sleep`). --- **Q: (b)** With a suitable example, explain the concept of generalization, association, aggregation, and composition relationships in UML class diagrams. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** Consider a **Smart Home Embedded System** with classes: `SmartHomeController`, `Room`, `Sensor`, `Alarm`. **1. Association** (Solid Line) — *"uses-a"* - The `SmartHomeController` is associated with `Room`. - A controller manages 1..* rooms; each room belongs to 1 controller. - Notation: `SmartHomeController —— Room` with multiplicity `1` and `1..*`. **2. Aggregation** (Hollow Diamond ◇) — *Weak "has-a"* - A `Room` *aggregates* `Sensor`(s). - A sensor can exist without the room (it can be moved/replaced). - Notation: `Room ◇—— Sensor` — hollow diamond on the Room side. **3. Composition** (Filled Diamond ◆) — *Strong "owns-a"* - A `Room` *composes* an `Alarm` (e.g., a built-in siren). - The alarm's lifecycle is bound to the room; if the room is destroyed, the alarm ceases to exist. - Notation: `Room ◆—— Alarm` — filled diamond on the Room side. **4. Generalization** (Hollow Triangle △) — *"is-a" (Inheritance)* - `TemperatureSensor` and `MotionSensor` are *specializations* of `Sensor`. - They inherit common attributes and add their own. - Notation: `TemperatureSensor ──▷ Sensor`. **Summary Table:** | Relationship | Symbol | Strength | Lifetime Coupling | |--------------|--------|----------|-------------------| | Association | —— | Weakest | Independent | | Aggregation | ◇—— | Weak | Independent | | Composition | ◆—— | Strong | Coupled | | Generalization | ——▷ | N/A (inheritance) | Inherited | --- ### **Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative) — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q: (a)** What is a use case diagram? Identify the actors, use cases, and relationships for an **Automatic Traffic Light Control System** with microcontroller. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Use Case Diagram Definition:** A **Use Case Diagram** is a UML behavior diagram that depicts a set of **actions** (use cases) that a system performs in collaboration with **external actors** to deliver observable value. **Automatic Traffic Light Control System Analysis:** **Actors Identified:** - `Driver` (primary actor — interacts via road) - `Pedestrian` (uses crosswalk button) - `Timer` (internal trigger) - `Vehicle Sensor` (provides traffic density) - `Microcontroller` (internal system) **Use Cases Identified:** 1. `DetectVehicles` 2. `GreenSignalOn` 3. `RedSignalOn` 4. `YellowSignalOn` 5. `PedestrianCrossRequest` 6. `EmergencyOverride` *(ambulance priority)* 7. `NightMode` **Relationships:** - `GreenSignalOn` `<>` `DetectVehicles` — vehicle detection is mandatory - `PedestrianCrossRequest` `<>` `RedSignalOn` — optional extension - `EmergencyOverride` `<>` `GreenSignalOn` — extends the normal flow - `NightMode` is a **generalization** of the regular signal flow **Sketch (Verbal):** ``` Driver ----> [GreenSignalOn] Pedestrian ---> [PedestrianCrossRequest] Timer ----> [NormalCycle] VehicleSensor ---> [DetectVehicles] [GreenSignalOn] -.include.-> [DetectVehicles] [PedestrianCrossRequest] -.extend.-> [RedSignalOn] [EmergencyOverride] -.extend.-> [GreenSignalOn] [NormalCycle] --▷ [NightMode] ``` --- **Q: (b)** Explain the 4+1 architectural view model of UML. State its significance in embedded system design. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** The **4+1 View Model**, proposed by **Philippe Kruchten (1995)**, is a way of organizing UML diagrams into different perspectives of a system, where each view addresses a separate set of stakeholder concerns. **The Four Primary Views:** 1. **Logical View** — Addresses the *functional requirements*. - Diagrams used: **Class Diagram, Object Diagram, Composite Structure Diagram**. - Concern: What does the system provide to end-users? 2. **Process View** — Addresses *concurrency, performance, scalability*. - Diagrams used: **Activity Diagram, State Machine Diagram**. - Concern: How does the system behave at runtime? (Tasks, threads, states) 3. **Physical (Deployment) View** — Addresses *hardware topology*. - Diagrams used: **Deployment Diagram**. - Concern: On which hardware does the software run? (MCUs, sensors, buses) 4. **Development (Implementation) View** — Addresses *software module organization*. - Diagrams used: **Component Diagram, Package Diagram**. - Concern: How is the software organized for development? (libraries, files) **The +1 View:** 5. **Use Case View (+1)** — Contains the use cases that *glue* the other four views together. Use cases drive the requirements captured in all other views. **Significance in Embedded System Design:** | View | Embedded System Use | |------|---------------------| | Logical | Define classes for sensors, actuators, controllers | | Process | Model RTOS tasks, interrupts, concurrency | | Physical | Map software to MCUs, buses (I2C, SPI, CAN) | | Development | Organize firmware into HAL, drivers, middleware, application | | Use Case (+1) | Validate all views via scenarios | > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **"+1"** use case view. Always mention all **FIVE** views in your answer to get full 7 marks. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Mark-Loss Pitfalls in UML Exam Questions:** > 1. **Confusing Aggregation vs. Composition** — Remember: filled diamond = strong ownership; hollow = weak "has-a". > 2. **Forgetting the +1 in 4+1** — Always list **5 views**, not 4. > 3. **Stereotype notation** — Must be in `<>`, not single. > 4. **Structure vs. Behavior count** — 6 + 8 = **14 diagrams** (UML 2.x). Older UML 1.x had 9. > 5. **Drawing mistakes** — Use real UML notation symbols; don't invent arrows. Generalization uses a **hollow triangle** (not arrowhead). --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 UML Module 2 Exam:** - ✅ **UML** = Unified Modeling Language, standardized by **OMG**, current version **2.5.1 (ISO/IEC 19505)** - ✅ **Three Amigos**: Grady Booch, James Rumbaugh, Ivar Jacobson (created UML at Rational Software) - ✅ **Total diagrams in UML 2.5 = 14** (6 Structure + 8 Behavior) - ✅ **Structure diagrams (6)**: Class, Object, Component, Composite Structure, Package, Deployment - ✅ **Behavior diagrams (8)**: Use Case, Activity, State Machine, Sequence, Communication, Timing, Interaction Overview, Profile - ✅ **4+1 View Model** = Logical + Process + Physical + Development + **(+1) Use Case** - ✅ **Four Building Blocks**: Things, Relationships, Diagrams, Extensibility Mechanisms - ✅ **Three Extensibility Mechanisms**: Stereotypes (`<<...>>`), Tagged Values (`{...}`), Constraints (`{...}`) - ✅ **Four Relationship Types** (most examined): - **Association** (line) - **Aggregation** (hollow diamond ◇) — *weak has-a* - **Composition** (filled diamond ◆) — *strong owns-a* - **Generalization** (hollow triangle △) — *is-a / inheritance* - **Realization** (dashed + △) — *implements interface* - **Dependency** (dashed arrow) — *uses temporarily* - ✅ **Most-used diagrams in embedded systems**: **State Machine, Sequence, Deployment, Class, Use Case** - ✅ **Real-world tools**: Enterprise Architect, IBM Rhapsody, Astah, Visual Paradigm, PlantUML - ✅ **Common embedded stereotypes**: `<>`, `<>`, `<>`, `<>`, `<>` - ✅ **Class Diagram** shows *types*; **Object Diagram** shows *instances* - ✅ **Sequence Diagram** emphasizes *time*; **Communication Diagram** emphasizes *links* - ✅ **Deployment Diagram** models **hardware nodes** (essential for embedded!)

topics.title: Hardware Software Trade-offs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hardware Software Trade-offs ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) In the design of any **Embedded System**, the functional specification can be realized either by executing instructions on a general-purpose processor (software) or by synthesizing dedicated logic gates, ASICs, or FPGA fabrics (hardware). The disciplined, analytical process of deciding *which portion* of a given system functionality should be migrated from a software implementation to a custom hardware implementation (and vice versa) — while balancing competing design metrics — is termed **Hardware–Software Trade-off** (often called **HW/SW Partitioning** or **HW/SW Co-design**). The trade-off is fundamentally a **multi-objective optimization problem**, where the design variables are the *implementation medium* for each computational block, and the objective functions are vectors containing **performance (latency / throughput)**, **power dissipation**, **unit cost**, **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) cost**, **flexibility / upgradability**, **time-to-market**, and **reliability**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The KTU syllabus frames Hardware–Software Co-design as the *single most critical design decision* in advanced embedded systems because it irreversibly commits the system to a particular cost, power, and performance envelope. Errors here *cannot* be fully recovered later in the V-model. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition Imagine you are the **head chef of a restaurant** designing the kitchen workflow to prepare 500 meals per hour. - **Pure Software Approach (General Cook + Written Recipe):** You hire one very smart cook, give him a thick recipe book, and let him interpret every step using a single stove. He is **flexible** (change the recipe tomorrow easily) and the **initial setup cost is low** (just one stove + one recipe). But he gets **tired (power/heat)** and is **slow (latency)**. - **Pure Hardware Approach (Custom Robotic Assembly Line):** You build a custom robotic machine that physically *hard-wires* each cooking motion (chopping, stirring, frying) into gears and belts. The machine is **blazingly fast** and **energy-efficient per dish**, but it is **inflexible** (cannot make a new recipe without rebuilding it) and the **initial fabrication cost is astronomical**. - **Hardware–Software Trade-off (The Smart Compromise):** You keep the smart cook (software on a CPU) for creative, changeable tasks like *garnishing and plating* (decision-heavy, low-volume logic). For the heavy lifting — *chopping 5,000 onions per hour* (repetitive, throughput-critical, well-defined tasks) — you install an **automatic onion-chopping machine** (custom hardware block / ASIC). You have **partitioned** the workload. The chef orchestrates; the machine accelerates the bottleneck. *This is exactly what HW/SW co-design does in an embedded SoC.* > [!NOTE] > **Plain-English Takeaway:** Software is **flexible but slow** (sequential, interpreted by a CPU). Hardware is **fast and efficient but rigid** (parallel, but fixed at fabrication). The trade-off decides the *optimal boundary* between the two. ### 1.3 The Fundamental Asymmetry (Why a Trade-off Exists at All) The very existence of a trade-off is rooted in the **execution model asymmetry** between the two media: | Property | Software (on µP / µC / DSP) | Hardware (ASIC / FPGA logic) | |----------|----------------------------|------------------------------| | Execution Nature | Sequential / Fetch-Decode-Execute | Parallel / Spatial / Combinational | | Clock Cycles per Task | High (10s – 1000s) | Low (often 1 cycle or pipelined) | | Flexibility | High (re-programmable) | Low (post-fabrication fixed) | | Per-Unit Cost | Low (commodity processor) | Medium-High (silicon area) | | NRE Cost | Zero | Very High (mask / fab) | | Power per Operation | High (instruction overhead) | Low (direct logic) | | Determinism | Predictable but slow | Ultra-predictable (sub-cycle) | Explicitly stated standard metric: **Design metrics commonly traded** — **Performance (MIPS/MOPS)**, **Power (mW / µW)**, **Area (gate equivalent / mm²)**, **Cost ($)**, and **Time-to-Market (weeks)**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Pillars of HW/SW Trade-off (The Decision Vector) Every partitioning decision in a KTU-level embedded design is evaluated against these five canonical pillars. A designer maximizes a *weighted sum* (or satisfies a *Pareto front*) of these metrics. **(a) PERFORMANCE / SPEED** - Software implementations execute tasks serially under a CPU clock $f_{CPU}$. A loop of $N$ instructions takes $\frac{N}{f_{CPU}}$ seconds. - Hardware implementations exploit **spatial parallelism** — multiple operations execute simultaneously across dedicated logic paths. - A function taking **1000 cycles in software** may take **10 cycles in dedicated hardware** ⇒ **100× speedup**. **(b) COST** - **NRE (Non-Recurring Engineering) Cost** $C_{NRE}$: one-time cost of masks, synthesis, layout. Borne once, amortized over volume. - **Per-Unit Cost** $C_{unit}$: silicon area cost of hardware block vs. royalty cost of pre-built IP. - Total cost of a design: $$C_{total}(V) = C_{NRE} + V \cdot C_{unit}$$ where $V$ is the production volume. Hardware is justified only when $V$ exceeds the **breakeven volume**. **(c) POWER DISSIPATION** - CMOS power has **dynamic** and **static** components: $$P_{total} = P_{dynamic} + P_{static} = \alpha \cdot C_{L} \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f + I_{leak} \cdot V_{DD}$$ where $\alpha$ = switching activity, $C_{L}$ = load capacitance, $V_{DD}$ = supply voltage, $f$ = clock frequency. - Custom hardware often operates at *lower* $V_{DD}$ and *lower* $f$ ⇒ drastically lower power. **(d) FLEXIBILITY / UPGRADABILITY** - Software: Updatable in the field via firmware flash. Critical for evolving standards (e.g., 5G codecs). - Hardware: Fixed at tape-out. *Bug-fix requires re-spin* (multi-million dollar mistake). - Modern **FPGAs** blur this boundary — they are *hardware that can be re-configured like software*. **(e) TIME-TO-MARKET (TTM)** - Pure software on an off-the-shelf CPU has the **shortest TTM** (weeks). - Custom ASIC has the **longest TTM** (12–18 months for fabrication). - FPGAs and modern SoCs (with CPU + FPGA fabric, e.g., Xilinx Zynq, Intel Cyclone V SoC) sit in the middle. ### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Exam-Ready) > [!NOTE] > All formulas below are **high-yield for KTU ESE**. Memorize the symbols and the units. | # | Formula / Rule | Symbol Legend | Application | |---|----------------|---------------|-------------| | 1 | $\text{Speedup} = \dfrac{T_{sw}}{T_{hw}}$ | $T_{sw}$ = software execution time, $T_{hw}$ = hardware execution time | Direct performance comparison | | 2 | Amdahl's Law: $\displaystyle S(n) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$ | $f$ = parallelizable fraction, $n$ = hardware speedup factor of that fraction | Upper bound on hardware acceleration | | 3 | Breakeven Volume: $\displaystyle V_{BE} = \frac{C_{NRE,hw} - C_{NRE,sw}}{C_{unit,sw} - C_{unit,hw}}$ | $C_{NRE}$ = one-time cost, $C_{unit}$ = recurring cost | Volume above which custom HW is cheaper | | 4 | CMOS Dynamic Power: $P_d = \alpha \, C_L \, V_{DD}^2 \, f$ | $\alpha$ = activity, $C_L$ = capacitance, $V_{DD}$ = voltage, $f$ = frequency | Power estimation per gate | | 5 | Total Cost: $C_{total}(V) = C_{NRE} + V \cdot C_{unit}$ | $V$ = production volume | Lifecycle cost analysis | | 6 | Energy per Operation: $E = P \cdot t$ | $P$ = power, $t$ = execution time | Battery-life estimation | | 7 | Silicon Area Approx: $A \approx k \cdot N_{gates}$ | $N_{gates}$ = gate count, $k$ = process constant (e.g., $k \approx 5 \mu m^2$/gate at 180nm) | Cost-from-area estimation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Notation Rule:** In KTU answer sheets, always show units in brackets. e.g., $P_d = 12.5$ $\mu$W, not just $12.5$. ### 2.3 The Partitioning Decision Matrix (Qualitative) A KTU-favorite topic. The following matrix guides which functions typically migrate to hardware: | System Function | Typical Implementation | Justification | |-----------------|------------------------|---------------| | Control loops, state machines | **Software** | Logic-heavy, low-data, changeable | | Cryptographic kernels (AES, SHA) | **Hardware (ASIC/FPGA)** | Bit-serial ops, throughput-critical | | Pixel processing, FIR filters | **Hardware (DSP/ASIC)** | Massive data parallelism | | Network protocol stacks (TCP/IP) | **Software** | Evolving standards, complex branching | | Sensor I/O polling | **Software** | Low-speed, flexibility needed | | Video encode/decode | **Hardware** | Gbps throughput required | | Decision-heavy AI inference | **Hardware NPU** | Matrix multiply dominates | ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility This trade-off discipline directly enables every modern SoC you have ever used: the **smartphone SoC** (Qualcomm Snapdragon, Apple A-series) contains a **CPU cluster** (for the OS, apps, branching code), a **GPU** (parallel graphics), an **NPU** (neural matrix math), dedicated **ISP hardware** (camera pipelines), and **DSP cores** (audio/voice). *None of these decisions were arbitrary* — they were the result of rigorous hardware–software partitioning analysis driven by power, area, and latency budgets. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Derivation 1:** Amdahl's Law applied to HW/SW Partitioning. > **Mandatory Derivation 2:** Breakeven Volume Analysis. ### 3.1 Derivation of Amdahl's Law for Hardware Acceleration Let the total execution time of a software-only baseline be $T_{total}$.
