Let a fraction $f$ of that workload be migrated to a hardware accelerator that provides a speedup factor of $n$ over the original software implementation. **Step 1 — Decompose the total time into parallelizable and non-parallelizable portions:** $$T_{total} = T_{serial} + T_{parallel}$$ where $T_{parallel} = f \cdot T_{total}$ and $T_{serial} = (1-f) \cdot T_{total}$. **Step 2 — Apply the hardware speedup $n$ to only the parallelizable portion:** After hardware acceleration, the new execution time becomes: $$T_{accelerated} = T_{serial} + \frac{T_{parallel}}{n}$$ $$T_{accelerated} = (1-f) \cdot T_{total} + \frac{f \cdot T_{total}}{n}$$ **Step 3 — Factor out $T_{total}$:** $$T_{accelerated} = T_{total} \left[ (1-f) + \frac{f}{n} \right]$$ **Step 4 — Define overall speedup $S(n) = T_{total} / T_{accelerated}$:** $$S(n) = \frac{T_{total}}{T_{total} \left[(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}\right]}$$ $$\boxed{\,S(n) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Examine limiting behavior (essential for KTU 8-mark questions):** - As $n \to \infty$ (perfect hardware): $\displaystyle \lim_{n \to \infty} S(n) = \frac{1}{1-f}$ - As $f \to 1$ (fully parallelizable): $S(n) \to n$ - Even with $f = 0.95$ and $n = 100$: $S = \dfrac{1}{0.05 + 0.0095} \approx 16.95$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight:** This is the *Killer Argument* in KTU valuation — even if you build infinitely fast hardware for 95% of the code, the remaining 5% serial portion caps your system-wide speedup at 20×. This is the foundational argument against "just throw hardware at it." --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Breakeven Volume for Custom Hardware A design team must decide between two implementation alternatives: - **Option SW:** Use a pre-built IP core (license fee model). NRE = $C_{NRE,sw}$, per-unit royalty = $C_{unit,sw}$. - **Option HW:** Design a custom ASIC block. NRE = $C_{NRE,hw}$, per-unit = $C_{unit,hw}$ (typically much lower than $C_{unit,sw}$). **Step 1 — Total cost of Option SW:** $$C_{SW}(V) = C_{NRE,sw} + V \cdot C_{unit,sw}$$ **Step 2 — Total cost of Option HW:** $$C_{HW}(V) = C_{NRE,hw} + V \cdot C_{unit,hw}$$ where $V$ = production volume (units), and typically $C_{NRE,hw} \gg C_{NRE,sw}$ and $C_{unit,hw} < C_{unit,sw}$. **Step 3 — Set the two cost curves equal to find breakeven volume $V_{BE}$:** $$C_{NRE,sw} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{unit,sw} = C_{NRE,hw} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{unit,hw}$$ **Step 4 — Rearrange to isolate $V_{BE}$:** $$V_{BE} \cdot (C_{unit,sw} - C_{unit,hw}) = C_{NRE,hw} - C_{NRE,sw}$$ $$\boxed{\,V_{BE} = \frac{C_{NRE,hw} - C_{NRE,sw}}{C_{unit,sw} - C_{unit,hw}}\,}$$ **Step 5 — Decision rule:** - If $V > V_{BE}$ ⇒ **Hardware is more economical** (amortized NRE is recovered). - If $V < V_{BE}$ ⇒ **Software / pre-built IP is more economical.** > [!NOTE] > **Numerical Worked Example (KTU-favorite style):** > Given $C_{NRE,hw} = \$1{,}000{,}000$, $C_{NRE,sw} = \$50{,}000$, $C_{unit,sw} = \$15$, $C_{unit,hw} = \$3$: > $V_{BE} = (1{,}000{,}000 - 50{,}000) / (15 - 3) = 950{,}000 / 12 \approx 79{,}167$ units. > **Interpretation:** For volume below ~79k units, licensing the IP (software path) is cheaper. For volume above ~79k units, the custom ASIC pays off. ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Speedup vs. Cost vs. Power Trade Space The KTU examiner often expects a *comparative table* style answer. The trade space for the *same* algorithm implemented across three media is: | Metric | Pure Software (on ARM Cortex-M4 @ 168 MHz) | HW on FPGA (Xilinx Artix-7) | Custom ASIC (28 nm) | |--------|---------------------------------------------|------------------------------|---------------------| | Throughput | $X_1$ (baseline) | $k_1 \cdot X_1$ (typically $10$–$100 \times$) | $k_2 \cdot X_1$ (typically $50$–$500 \times$) | | Power (mW) | $P_1$ (high, CPU always on) | $P_2 \approx 0.1 \, P_1$ | $P_3 \approx 0.01 \, P_1$ | | Unit Cost ($) | $C_1$ (low, commodity µC) | $C_2 \approx 3 \times C_1$ | $C_3 \approx 1.5 \times C_1$ (at high V) | | NRE ($) | $\approx 0$ | $\approx 100k$ (one-time) | $\approx 1M+$ (masks) | | Flexibility | Highest (C code) | Medium (re-flash bitstream) | Lowest (no field update) | ### 3.4 Analytical Note: Energy-Delay Product (EDP) A common KTU higher-order metric that *combines* both speed and power is the **Energy-Delay Product**: $$EDP = E \cdot t_{exec} = P \cdot t_{exec}^2$$ Custom hardware typically reduces both $P$ and $t_{exec}$ simultaneously, leading to orders-of-magnitude improvement in EDP — which is *the* metric for battery-powered embedded IoT nodes. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 The Hardware–Software Co-Design Flow (KTU Diagram 1) The following Mermaid diagram represents the canonical *Y-chart* (Gajski-Kuhn) view of HW/SW co-design as taught in KTU Module 2. It maps the three abstraction domains (Behavioral, Structural, Geometric) onto the three implementation axes (System, CPU, Hardware). ```mermaid flowchart TD A["System Specification
Functional Requirements"] --> B["Partitioning Decision
HW or SW per Block"] B --> C["Hardware Domain
RTL Design and FPGA ASIC"] B --> D["Software Domain
C Code on RTOS or Bare Metal"] C --> E["Synthesis and Place Route"] D --> F["Compilation and Linking"] E --> G["Co Simulation and Verification"] F --> G G --> H{"Metrics Satisfied
Power Cost Timing Area"} H -- Yes --> I["Final Embedded Product"] H -- No --> J["Re Partition Iterate"] J --> B ``` **Reading the Diagram:** - The **Partitioning Decision** node is the *heart* of the trade-off — it consumes the spec and emits two parallel implementation streams. - The **Co-Simulation** node validates that hardware and software, modeled separately, still *functionally interact correctly* at the bus / interrupt boundary. - The **Re-Partition Iterate** loop is what makes co-design iterative — the first partition is almost never optimal. ### 4.2 Partitioning Decision Logic (KTU Diagram 2) The following Mermaid flowchart codifies the *decision algorithm* a KTU student should follow when asked "Should this function be in HW or SW?" ```mermaid flowchart TD S["Function Under
Partitioning Analysis"] --> Q1{"Throughput
Critical"} Q1 -- Yes --> H1["Candidate Hardware"] Q1 -- No --> Q2{"Algorithm Subject to
Frequent Change"} Q2 -- Yes --> SW1["Candidate Software"] Q2 -- No --> Q3{"Production Volume
Above Breakeven"} Q3 -- Yes --> H2["Candidate Hardware"] Q3 -- No --> SW2["Candidate Software"] H1 --> Q4{"Power Budget
Tight"} Q4 -- Yes --> HW["FINAL HW"] Q4 -- No --> SW3["FINAL SW"] SW1 --> FW["FINAL SW"] SW2 --> FW H2 --> HW ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Tip for KTU Answers:** When you draw this kind of decision tree in an exam, *always* label the decision criteria in the diamond nodes. A diamond without a label fetches zero marks. ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture (System View) The following Mermaid block diagram represents a typical partitioned embedded SoC — the **physical manifestation** of a HW/SW co-design decision for, say, a smart-camera IoT node: ```mermaid flowchart LR CPU["CPU Core
ARM Cortex
Software Domain"] --> BUS["Shared Bus
AXI or AMBA"] DSP["DSP Core
Software Domain"] --> BUS ASIC1["AES Hardware Accelerator
Hardware Domain"] --> BUS ASIC2["FIR Filter ASIC Block
Hardware Domain"] --> BUS ASIC3["Pixel Pipeline ISP
Hardware Domain"] --> BUS MEM["Shared Memory
DDR or SRAM"] --> BUS BUS --> IO["GPIO UART SPI I2C
Peripheral Interface"] IO --> EXT["External Sensors and Actuators"] ``` **Block Interpretation Table:** | Block | Domain | Justification for Partition | |-------|--------|------------------------------| | CPU Core | Software | OS, control flow, branching logic | | DSP Core | Software | Audio codec (evolving standards) | | AES Accelerator | Hardware | Bit-level ops, latency-critical security | | FIR Filter | Hardware | Streaming data, throughput-bound | | Pixel Pipeline | Hardware | Massive pixel parallelism, real-time | | Memory & I/O | Shared | Both domains access via bus | ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > *Define Hardware–Software Trade-off in embedded system design. List any four design metrics that influence this trade-off.* **Model Answer:** Hardware–Software Trade-off is the engineering process of deciding whether a given functionality of an embedded system should be implemented in **software** (running on a general-purpose processor via instructions) or in **hardware** (as dedicated logic in ASIC/FPGA). The decision is made to optimally balance competing design constraints. The four key design metrics are: 1. **Performance / Throughput** (execution time, latency) 2. **Power dissipation** (battery life) 3. **Cost** (NRE + per-unit) 4. **Flexibility / Upgradability** (ability to change post-deployment) *(Any 4 of: cost, performance, power, flexibility, time-to-market, reliability, area.)* --- > **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > *State Amdahl's Law. Explain its significance in the context of hardware–software partitioning.* **Model Answer:** Amdahl's Law gives the theoretical maximum overall speedup $S$ achievable when a fraction $f$ of a task is accelerated by a factor of $n$: $$S(n) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \dfrac{f}{n}}$$ **Significance in HW/SW Partitioning:** Even if we build infinitely fast custom hardware ($n \to \infty$) for the parallelizable fraction, the overall system speedup is bounded by $\dfrac{1}{1-f}$. This means that **a small non-accelerated serial portion (e.g., 5%) caps the maximum system speedup at 20×**. The law tells designers that simply throwing hardware at a problem is wasteful unless the *entire critical path* is parallelizable — motivating careful identification of the *true bottleneck* before committing to hardware. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]** > > **(a)** [7 Marks] *Explain the five primary factors that drive hardware–software trade-off decisions in modern embedded system design. For each factor, give one example of when hardware is preferred and one when software is preferred.* > > **(b)** [7 Marks] *Derive the breakeven volume formula for selecting between a custom ASIC and a licensed software IP core. A design team is evaluating two options for an AES encryption block:* > - *Option 1 (License IP): NRE = $50{,}000$, royalty = $12$ per unit.* > - *Option 2 (Custom ASIC): NRE = $800{,}000$, unit cost = $2$.* > > *Calculate the breakeven volume. If the company plans to ship $100{,}000$ units, which option is cheaper and by how much?* **Model Answer for Part (a):** The five primary factors driving HW/SW trade-offs are: **(1) Performance / Speed:** - *HW preferred:* AES encryption engine — bit-level ops done in parallel cycles. - *SW preferred:* Decision-tree traversal in user-interface logic — unpredictable branches. **(2) Cost (NRE + per-unit):** - *HW preferred:* High-volume consumer product (millions of units) amortizes mask cost. - *SW preferred:* Low-volume industrial controller — NRE of ASIC is unjustifiable. **(3) Power Dissipation:** - *HW preferred:* Wearable heart-rate monitor ASIC — 10× lower mW than software CPU. - *SW preferred:* Wall-powered smart home hub — power budget not tight. **(4) Flexibility / Upgradability:** - *SW preferred:* Cellular modem firmware — protocol updates without hardware recall. - *HW preferred:* Cryptocurrency SHA-256 miner — algorithm is frozen, no upgrades needed. **(5) Time-to-Market:** - *SW preferred:* Startup MVP — needs prototype in 8 weeks. - *HW preferred:* Mature high-volume product where the 12-month ASIC timeline is acceptable. *(Each factor explained with example ≈ 1.4 marks; structure & clarity ≈ remaining.)* --- **Model Answer for Part (b):** **Derivation:** The total lifecycle cost of an implementation alternative as a function of volume $V$ is: $$C(V) = C_{NRE} + V \cdot C_{unit}$$ For the breakeven condition $C_{SW}(V_{BE}) = C_{HW}(V_{BE})$: $$C_{NRE,sw} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{unit,sw} = C_{NRE,hw} + V_{BE} \cdot C_{unit,hw}$$ Solving for $V_{BE}$: $$V_{BE} \cdot (C_{unit,sw} - C_{unit,hw}) = C_{NRE,hw} - C_{NRE,sw}$$ $$\boxed{\,V_{BE} = \frac{C_{NRE,hw} - C_{NRE,sw}}{C_{unit,sw} - C_{unit,hw}}\,}$$ **Numerical Calculation (Step-by-step):** Substituting the given values: $$V_{BE} = \frac{800{,}000 - 50{,}000}{12 - 2} = \frac{750{,}000}{10} = 75{,}000 \text{ units}$$ **Decision at $V = 100{,}000$ units (since $V > V_{BE}$, hardware is cheaper):** Cost of Option 1 (Software IP): $$C_{SW}(100{,}000) = 50{,}000 + 100{,}000 \times 12 = 50{,}000 + 1{,}200{,}000 = \$1{,}250{,}000$$ Cost of Option 2 (Custom ASIC): $$C_{HW}(100{,}000) = 800{,}000 + 100{,}000 \times 2 = 800{,}000 + 200{,}000 = \$1{,}000{,}000$$ **Savings with ASIC:** $$\Delta C = C_{SW} - C_{HW} = 1{,}250{,}000 - 1{,}000{,}000 = \$250{,}000$$ **Conclusion:** Since the planned volume (100,000 units) exceeds $V_{BE}$ (75,000 units), the **custom ASIC** is more economical by **\$250,000** over the product lifetime. --- > **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]** > > **(a)** [7 Marks] *With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the Hardware–Software Co-design flow (Y-chart approach). Explain the role of partitioning and co-simulation in this flow.* > > **(b)** [7 Marks] *A real-time image-processing function takes 800 ms on a 200 MHz ARM Cortex processor. The critical inner loop constitutes 70% of this time and can be accelerated 50× by moving it to a custom hardware block, while the remaining 30% stays in software.* > - *(i) Calculate the new total execution time after hardware acceleration.* > - *(ii) Calculate the overall speedup achieved.* > - *(iii) What is the theoretical maximum speedup possible if the hardware could be made infinitely fast?* **Model Answer for Part (a):** The Hardware–Software Co-design flow is a *concurrent* design methodology in which hardware and software are designed in parallel from a common specification, rather than sequentially. The Y-chart (Gajski-Kuhn) representation views the system along three axes: **Behavioral** (what it does), **Structural** (how it is built), and **Geometric** (physical layout), and three abstraction levels: **System**, **CPU (Register-Transfer)**, and **Gate/Transistor**. **Block Diagram Description:** ``` Specification | Partitioning (HW vs SW) / \ / \ HW SW RTL C-Code | | Synth Compile \ / Co-Simulation | Verification (timing, power, cost) | Iterate if metrics not met --> back to Partitioning ``` **Role of Partitioning:** Partitioning is the *heart* of co-design. It examines each computational block in the specification and assigns it to either hardware (ASIC/FPGA) or software (CPU instruction stream) based on performance, cost, and power budgets. The first partition is rarely optimal. **Role of Co-Simulation:** Because hardware and software are designed concurrently, their *interaction* must be validated before fabrication. Co-simulation runs the software (often on an instruction-set simulator like QEMU) and the hardware (on an HDL simulator like ModelSim) *together*, synchronizing at the bus-transaction level (e.g., AXI). This catches integration bugs (wrong bus protocol, race conditions) before tape-out — saving millions of dollars. --- **Model Answer for Part (b):** **Given:** - Original execution time $T_{sw} = 800$ ms - Parallelizable fraction $f = 0.70$ - Hardware speedup factor $n = 50$ - Serial fraction $= 1 - f = 0.30$ **(i) New execution time after hardware acceleration:** By Amdahl's Law, the new total time is: $$T_{new} = T_{sw} \left[ (1-f) + \frac{f}{n} \right]$$ $$T_{new} = 800 \left[ 0.30 + \frac{0.70}{50} \right]$$ $$T_{new} = 800 \left[ 0.30 + 0.014 \right]$$ $$T_{new} = 800 \times 0.314 = 251.2 \text{ ms}$$ **(ii) Overall speedup achieved:** $$S = \frac{T_{sw}}{T_{new}} = \frac{800}{251.2} \approx 3.184 \times$$ **(iii) Theoretical maximum speedup (as $n \to \infty$):** $$S_{max} = \frac{1}{1 - f} = \frac{1}{0.30} \approx 3.333 \times$$ > [!NOTE] > **Insight (Valuable for 14-mark question):** Notice that even with a 50× speedup on the inner loop, the *system-wide* speedup is only ~3.18× — and *no amount* of hardware acceleration on the 70% portion can ever exceed 3.33× because of the Amdahl ceiling imposed by the remaining 30% serial code. This is exactly why experienced embedded architects identify and *eliminate* serial bottlenecks before designing more hardware. --- ### 5.3 ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU students typically lose marks on this topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting the units in the final answer** — KTU examiners explicitly deduct for missing units ($V_{BE} = 75{,}000$ *what?* Units!). Always write "**75,000 units**" or "**75k units**". > > 2. **Confusing NRE with per-unit cost** — NRE is a *one-time* cost. Students often subtract per-unit costs as if they were NRE. Read the question carefully. > > 3. **Plugging into Amdahl's formula without explaining the meaning of $f$ and $n$** — Always state: "$f$ = fraction parallelized = 0.70; $n$ = speedup factor = 50." > > 4. **Drawing a Y-chart diagram without labeling the three axes** — A box with no labels is worth zero. Label: *Behavioral / Structural / Geometric* and *System / RT / Gate*. > > 5. **Ignoring the *iteration loop* in co-design flow** — Many students describe it as a linear pipeline. KTU expects you to acknowledge that partitioning is iterative (re-partition if metrics fail). > > 6. **Writing "hardware is faster" without quantitative support** — Always quantify: "*hardware provides a 10×–100× speedup due to spatial parallelism*". > > 7. **Using the wrong base for percentage calculation in breakeven** — Always express the saving relative to the chosen baseline (in our example, saving = $C_{SW} - C_{HW} = \$250{,}000$). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final High-Density Revision Block — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.** - **Definition:** HW/SW trade-off = analytical decision of *which blocks* go to hardware and *which* to software, optimizing a vector of (performance, power, cost, flexibility, time-to-market). - **Hardware is fast & efficient but rigid & NRE-expensive.** Software is flexible & cheap but slow & power-hungry. **Trade-off is the boundary optimization.** - **The Five Pillars:** *Performance, Power, Cost, Flexibility, Time-to-Market.* Memorize one real example per pillar per side (HW preferred vs SW preferred). - **Amdahl's Law** is non-negotiable: $$S(n) = \frac{1}{(1-f) + \frac{f}{n}}$$ Limiting case as $n \to \infty$ gives $S_{max} = \frac{1}{1-f}$. Always compute $S_{max}$ when the problem mentions "infinite hardware" or "perfect accelerator". - **Breakeven Volume** is the **economic** counterpart to Amdahl's **performance** law: $$V_{BE} = \frac{C_{NRE,hw} - C_{NRE,sw}}{C_{unit,sw} - C_{unit,hw}}$$ Always state the decision rule: $V > V_{BE} \Rightarrow$ custom HW; $V < V_{BE} \Rightarrow$ licensed IP / software. - **Co-design flow** is *iterative* — specification → partitioning → parallel HW & SW design → co-simulation → verification → (iterate if metrics fail) → fabrication. - **Standard partition examples to memorize:** - Cryptography, FIR filters, pixel pipelines → **Hardware** - Protocol stacks, OS kernels, control loops → **Software** - **Modern SoCs** (smartphone, IoT edge) are *physical evidence* of partitioning: CPU + DSP + NPU + ISP + GPU on one die, each owning its optimal workload. - **Energy-Delay Product (EDP)** is the unified battery-life metric: $EDP = P \cdot t^2$. - **CMOS Dynamic Power:** $P_d = \alpha C_L V_{DD}^2 f$. The $V_{DD}^2$ dependence makes voltage scaling the most effective power-saving lever. - **Always show the unit** in your final numerical answer. Always **state the assumption** (e.g., "assuming $f = 0.7$ as parallelizable").

topics.title: Operating System Basics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Operating System Basics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **Operating System (OS)** is a collection of system software that acts as an interface between the user/application layer and the underlying hardware of a computing system. It is responsible for **resource management, process scheduling, memory management, file system handling, and providing a consistent execution environment** for application programs.

In the specific context of **Advanced Embedded Systems**, the OS is a stripped-down, deterministic, and resource-constrained piece of firmware that runs directly on the microcontroller/processor. For KTU 2024 Scheme, we broadly classify embedded operating environments into three categories:

1. **Bare-Metal (No OS):** A simple super-loop or foreground/background architecture where the firmware directly polls hardware registers.
2. **General-Purpose OS (GPOS):** Linux, Windows — used in high-end embedded boards (Raspberry Pi, BeagleBone). Non-deterministic.
3. **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS):** FreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS, QNX. Deterministic, designed for hard/soft real-time constraints.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A *Real-Time Operating System* is *not necessarily fast* — it is **deterministic**. The key performance metric is *worst-case latency*, not average throughput. This distinction is the single most important conceptual differentiator for university exam answers.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The OS as an Air Traffic Controller

Imagine the **CPU** as a single runway at Cochin International Airport. Multiple flights (processes/tasks) want to land and take off. Without a controller, planes would collide. The **Operating System** is the **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** that:

| Airport Analogy | OS Concept | Description |
|---|---|---|
| Runway | CPU | Single (or few) execution resource |
| Flights | Processes/Threads | Independent units of work requesting the CPU |
| ATC scheduling desk | Scheduler | Decides which flight uses the runway next |
| Holding pattern | Ready Queue | Tasks waiting for CPU access |
| Landing time | Burst Time | Duration the task needs the CPU |
| Gate/Stand | I/O Wait / Blocked State | Task is waiting for I/O, freeing the CPU |
| Flight Priority | Task Priority | Critical flights get runway access first |
| Diversion deadline | Hard Deadline | Task must land by this time, or system fails |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** The OS does not *create* work — it **organises** the work that already exists, ensuring fairness, safety, and efficient use of limited resources.

---

## 1.3 Core Functions of an Embedded OS

The OS performs six classical functions, each critical in the embedded domain:

1. **Process/Task Management** — Creating, scheduling, and terminating tasks.
2. **Memory Management** — Allocating SRAM, stack, heap, and managing MMU/MPU.
3. **Interrupt Handling** — Prioritising and dispatching hardware interrupts.
4. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** — Semaphores, queues, mailboxes, mutexes.
5. **I/O Device Management** — Abstracting drivers (UART, SPI, I²C, ADC).
6. **Time Management** — System tick, software timers, delays, time-slicing.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Embedded OS Principle:** Unlike desktop OS, an embedded OS is **static** — the set of tasks, memory pools, and priorities is determined at compile time. There is no dynamic loading of programs at runtime in most RTOS deployments.

---

## 1.4 Kernel vs User Space vs Hardware

A *kernel* is the **privileged core** of the OS that runs in supervisor/CPU privileged mode. All other code (drivers, applications) runs in user mode. This separation provides **protection** and **stability**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical OS Architecture

Modern embedded OS architectures are organised in a strict layered model:

| Layer | Component | Mode | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 5 | Application Code | User | Sensor processing loop, motor control |
| 4 | Middleware / Libraries | User | TCP/IP stack, FATFS, GUI |
| 3 | Device Drivers | Kernel/Supervisor | UART driver, GPIO driver |
| 2 | OS Kernel (Scheduler, IPC) | Supervisor | FreeRTOS kernel, µC/OS-II |
| 1 | HAL / BSP | Supervisor | STM32 HAL, CMSIS |
| 0 | Hardware | — | ARM Cortex-M4, AVR, RISC-V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why layering matters in KTU exams:** Examiners frequently ask *"Where do device drivers sit in the OS architecture?"* — the correct answer is **Layer 3, inside the kernel mode**, since they require privileged access to memory-mapped hardware registers.

---

## 2.2 Classification of OS Kernels

### A. Monolithic Kernel
The entire OS (scheduler, drivers, FS, networking) runs in a single supervisor-mode address space.

- **Pros:** High performance, low context-switch overhead.
- **Cons:** A bug in any driver crashes the entire system. Hard to maintain.
- **Example:** Traditional Linux kernel, µC/OS-II.

### B. Microkernel
Only the absolute essentials (IPC, basic scheduling, address space) are in the kernel. Drivers and FS run in user space.

- **Pros:** Fault isolation, security, modularity.
- **Cons:** High IPC overhead, lower throughput.
- **Example:** QNX, MINIX, L4 microkernel family.

### C. Hybrid Kernel
Combines aspects of both — microkernel structure with some monolithic performance optimisations.

- **Example:** Windows NT, XNU (macOS), modern RTOS variants.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** Most embedded RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) are *technically monolithic* in design philosophy. Always state the kernel type explicitly when describing an embedded OS in 14-mark answers.

---

## 2.3 Process vs Thread — A Critical Embedded Distinction

| Property | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Address Space | Independent, isolated | Shared within parent process |
| Creation Cost | High (full context + memory map) | Low (just stack + TCB) |
| Communication | IPC mechanisms (pipes, sockets) | Shared variables + synchronisation |
| Failure Impact | One crash does not affect others | Thread crash can kill entire process |
| Embedded Example | Two separate apps in Linux | Two tasks in FreeRTOS sharing globals |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** A **Thread** is the *smallest unit of CPU execution* that has its own program counter, stack, and register set, but **shares** the heap, global data, and file descriptors with sibling threads of the same process.

---

## 2.4 Process States and Transitions

A process/thread in any OS moves through a finite set of states. The state diagram is one of the **most frequently asked 14-mark questions** in KTU exams.

### The Five Canonical States

1. **New** — Process is being created (TCB initialised, stack allocated).
2. **Ready** — Process is loaded in memory and waiting for CPU allocation.
3. **Running** — Process is currently executing on the CPU.
4. **Blocked (Waiting)** — Process cannot proceed until an event occurs (I/O, semaphore, sleep).
5. **Terminated (Exit)** — Process has finished execution; resources are released.

### State Transition Triggers

| Transition | Trigger |
|---|---|
| New $\rightarrow$ Ready | Admission control accepts the process |
| Ready $\rightarrow$ Running | Scheduler dispatches the process |
| Running $\rightarrow$ Ready | Preemption (higher priority task ready, time slice expired) |
| Running $\rightarrow$ Blocked | I/O request, sleep, semaphore wait, event flag |
| Blocked $\rightarrow$ Ready | I/O complete, semaphore signalled, timeout expiry |
| Running $\rightarrow$ Terminated | Task finishes, calls `exit()`, exception/abort |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hard Real-Time Insight:** In a hard RTOS, the scheduler must guarantee that the time spent in *Ready* state plus the time spent in *Running* state must never exceed the **deadline**. This is the basis of Rate Monotonic Analysis (RMA).

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet — Essential OS Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **mandatory** for any scheduling problem in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE). Memorise the variable definitions precisely.

| Metric | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Turnaround Time (TAT)** | $TAT = CT - AT$ | Completion Time minus Arrival Time | time units |
| **Waiting Time (WT)** | $WT = TAT - BT$ | TAT minus Burst Time | time units |
| **Response Time (RT)** | $RT = ST - AT$ | First CPU allocation minus Arrival Time | time units |
| **Throughput** | $\text{Throughput} = \dfrac{N}{\text{Total Time}}$ | Processes completed per unit time | tasks $\div$ time |
| **CPU Utilisation** | $U = \dfrac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100$ | Percentage of time CPU is not idle | percent |
| **Deadline** | $d_i$ | Time by which task $i$ must finish | time units |
| **Slack Time** | $S = d_i - (\text{Ready} + \text{Remaining})$ | Time available before missing deadline | time units |
| **Context Switch Overhead** | $T_{cs} = N_{cs} \times t_{cs}$ | Total time lost to context switching | time units |
| **Rate Monotonic Bound** | $U_{max} = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ | Schedulability bound for N periodic tasks | dimensionless |

Where:
- $CT$ = Completion Time, $AT$ = Arrival Time, $BT$ = Burst Time, $ST$ = Start Time (first CPU allocation)
- $N$ = Number of processes, $N_{cs}$ = Number of context switches, $t_{cs}$ = Time per context switch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Avoidance:** Do **NOT** confuse *Response Time* with *Waiting Time*. Response Time uses *Start Time* (first CPU entry). Waiting Time uses *Burst Time* (total CPU duration). This is a common 3-mark conceptual trap.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, OS metrics are used as follows:

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** TAT and deadline miss rate determine ECU safety certification (ISO 26262).
- **Medical Devices (Pacemakers):** Response time for critical interrupts must be < 10 ms.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Worst-case execution time (WCET) is bounded for every task.
- **IoT Edge Devices:** Throughput and energy per instruction determine battery life.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A 100% theoretical exam question on scheduling can still be worth 14 marks. KTU examiners expect students to **connect formulas to real embedded constraints** like jitter, latency, and power budget.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — FCFS Scheduling with Full Derivations

This is the **most likely 14-mark scheduling problem** in the KTU 2024 ESE. We solve it exhaustively with every algebraic step.

### Problem Statement
Four processes arrive in the ready queue. Calculate Average Waiting Time (AWT), Average Turnaround Time (ATAT), and CPU Utilisation.

| Process | Arrival Time (AT) | Burst Time (BT) |
|---|---|---|
| P1 | 0 ms | 8 ms |
| P2 | 1 ms | 4 ms |
| P3 | 2 ms | 9 ms |
| P4 | 3 ms | 5 ms |

The OS uses **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)** scheduling, non-preemptive.

---

### Step 1 — Construct the Gantt Chart

FCFS executes tasks in the order they arrive in the ready queue, without preemption.

```
Time:   0    1    2   3       8       12      13         21
        |----|----|---|--------|--------|-------|----------|
       P1 (start)  P2          P3              P4 (end)
```

**Detailed reasoning:**

1. At $t = 0$, the only process in the queue is P1, so the dispatcher selects P1.
2. P1 begins execution at $t = 0$ and continues for $BT_1 = 8$ ms.
3. P1 completes at $t = 0 + 8 = 8$ ms.
4. By $t = 8$, all other processes (P2, P3, P4) have arrived (since their $AT \le 3$ ms).
5. The queue now contains [P2, P3, P4] in arrival order. P2 is selected.
6. P2 executes from $t = 8$ to $t = 8 + 4 = 12$ ms.
7. P3 executes from $t = 12$ to $t = 12 + 9 = 21$ ms.
8. P4 executes from $t = 21$ to $t = 21 + 5 = 26$ ms.

### Step 2 — Determine Start, End, and Completion Times

From the Gantt chart:

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P1} &= 8 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P2} &= 12 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P3} &= 21 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P4} &= 26 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since FCFS is non-preemptive, $ST = AT$ for the first job, and $ST$ for subsequent jobs equals the $CT$ of the previous job.

$$
\begin{aligned}
ST_{P1} &= AT_1 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
ST_{P2} &= CT_{P1} = 8 \text{ ms} \\
ST_{P3} &= CT_{P2} = 12 \text{ ms} \\
ST_{P4} &= CT_{P3} = 21 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the TAT Formula $TAT_i = CT_i - AT_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
TAT_{P1} &= CT_1 - AT_1 = 8 - 0 = 8 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P2} &= CT_2 - AT_2 = 12 - 1 = 11 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P3} &= CT_3 - AT_3 = 21 - 2 = 19 \text{ ms} \\
TAT_{P4} &= CT_4 - AT_4 = 26 - 3 = 23 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the WT Formula $WT_i = TAT_i - BT_i$

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P1} &= TAT_1 - BT_1 = 8 - 8 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P2} &= TAT_2 - BT_2 = 11 - 4 = 7 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P3} &= TAT_3 - BT_3 = 19 - 9 = 10 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P4} &= TAT_4 - BT_4 = 23 - 5 = 18 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Compute the Averages

$$
\begin{aligned}
ATAT &= \frac{TAT_{P1} + TAT_{P2} + TAT_{P3} + TAT_{P4}}{N} = \frac{8 + 11 + 19 + 23}{4} = \frac{61}{4} = 15.25 \text{ ms} \\
AWT  &= \frac{WT_{P1} + WT_{P2} + WT_{P3} + WT_{P4}}{N} = \frac{0 + 7 + 10 + 18}{4} = \frac{35}{4} = 8.75 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute CPU Utilisation and Throughput

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CPU Utilisation} &= \frac{\text{Busy Time}}{\text{Total Time}} \times 100 = \frac{8 + 4 + 9 + 5}{26} \times 100 = \frac{26}{26} \times 100 = 100\% \\
\text{Throughput} &= \frac{N}{\text{Total Time}} = \frac{4 \text{ processes}}{26 \text{ ms}} = 0.154 \text{ processes/ms} \approx 154 \text{ processes/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observation:** FCFS gives 100% CPU utilisation when there are no idle gaps in the Gantt chart, but suffers from the **convoy effect** — short jobs (P2) are blocked behind long jobs (P3).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) Bound

Three periodic real-time tasks with periods $T_1, T_2, T_3$ and execution times $C_1, C_2, C_3$. Determine if they are schedulable using the RMS sufficient condition.

### Problem Data

| Task | Period $T_i$ | Execution Time $C_i$ | Priority (by RMS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 20 ms | 5 ms | Highest (shortest period) |
| $\tau_2$ | 40 ms | 10 ms | Middle |
| $\tau_3$ | 80 ms | 15 ms | Lowest (longest period) |

### Step 1 — Compute the Utilisation of Each Task

RMS scheduling assigns priority based on period: shorter period $\Rightarrow$ higher priority. The utilisation per task is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{5}{20} = 0.25 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{10}{40} = 0.25 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{15}{80} = 0.1875
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Compute the Total Processor Utilisation

$$
U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.25 + 0.25 + 0.1875 = 0.6875 = 68.75\%
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the RMS Sufficient Bound (Liu & Layland)

The Liu & Layland sufficient condition states that for $N$ periodic independent tasks, schedulability is guaranteed if:

$$
U_{total} \le N(2^{1/N} - 1)
$$

For $N = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound} &= 3 \times (2^{1/3} - 1) \\
&= 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) \\
&= 3 \times 0.2599 \\
&= 0.7797 = 77.97\%
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Compare and Conclude

$$
U_{total} = 0.6875 \le U_{bound} = 0.7797
$$

Since the total utilisation is **less than or equal to** the bound, the task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on necessity:** The RMS bound is *sufficient but not necessary*. A task set with $U_{total} > 0.7797$ may still be schedulable — exact analysis requires the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** recurrence, which is beyond KTU scope but worth mentioning in 14-mark answers for extra credit.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Scheduling Algorithms

| Algorithm | Type | Preemption | Starvation | Overhead | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **FCFS** | Non-preemptive | No | No | Very low | Batch systems, simple embedded loops |
| **SJF / SRTF** | Non-preemptive / Preemptive | Optional | Yes (long jobs) | Low to medium | Throughput-optimised systems |
| **Round Robin** | Preemptive | Yes (time quantum) | No | Medium (context switches) | Time-sharing GPOS |
| **Priority** | Both | Optional | Yes (low priority) | Low | Real-time, control systems |
| **Rate Monotonic (RMS)** | Preemptive, fixed priority | Yes (preempt by higher period) | No (periodic only) | Medium | Hard real-time periodic tasks |
| **EDF (Earliest Deadline First)** | Preemptive, dynamic priority | Yes | No | Higher | Dynamic real-time, aperiodic + periodic |
| **Multilevel Queue** | Hybrid | Yes | Possible (lower queues) | High | Mixed real-time and best-effort |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Comparison:** RMS is *static* (priority decided at design time), EDF is *dynamic* (priority changes at runtime). EDF has a 100% theoretical utilisation bound, but RMS is preferred in industry because of **simpler implementation** and **predictable worst-case behaviour** — both critical for safety certification.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level OS Architecture in an Embedded System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph USpace["USER SPACE / APPLICATION LAYER"]
        APP1["Application Task 1\nSensor Acquisition"]
        APP2["Application Task 2\nMotor Control Loop"]
        APP3["Application Task 3\nCommunication Handler"]
    end

    subgraph KSpace["KERNEL SPACE / SUPERVISOR MODE"]
        SCHED["Scheduler\nPriority + Round Robin"]
        DISP["Dispatcher\nContext Switch Engine"]
        IPC["Inter-Process Communication\nQueues, Semaphores, Mutexes"]
        MM["Memory Manager\nHeap, Stack, Pool Allocation"]
        TMR["Timer Manager\nSysTick, Software Timers"]
        ISR["Interrupt Service Routines\nNVIC Priority Levels"]
    end

    subgraph DRV["DEVICE DRIVER LAYER"]
        DRV1["UART Driver"]
        DRV2["SPI Driver"]
        DRV3["I2C Driver"]
        DRV4["ADC Driver"]
        DRV5["GPIO Driver"]
    end

    subgraph HW["HARDWARE LAYER"]
        CPU["ARM Cortex-M CPU Core"]
        MEM["SRAM / Flash Memory"]
        PER["Peripherals\nUART, SPI, ADC, GPIO, Timers"]
    end

    APP1 --> SCHED
    APP2 --> SCHED
    APP3 --> SCHED
    SCHED --> DISP
    DISP --> IPC
    DISP --> MM
    DISP --> TMR
    ISR --> SCHED
    SCHED --> DRV1
    SCHED --> DRV2
    SCHED --> DRV3
    SCHED --> DRV4
    SCHED --> DRV5
    DRV1 --> PER
    DRV2 --> PER
    DRV3 --> PER
    DRV4 --> PER
    DRV5 --> PER
    MM --> MEM
    DISP --> CPU
```

---

## 4.2 Process State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> New : Task Created
    New --> Ready : Initialisation Complete
    Ready --> Running : Scheduler Dispatch
    Running --> Ready : Preemption\nTime Slice Expired\nHigher Priority Ready
    Running --> Blocked : I/O Request\nSemaphore Wait\nSleep / Delay
    Blocked --> Ready : I/O Complete\nSemaphore Signalled\nTimeout Expired
    Running --> Terminated : Task Exit\nException / Abort
    Terminated --> [*] : Resources Released
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** When drawing this diagram in a 14-mark answer, label **every transition arrow** with its triggering event. Examiners specifically award 2–3 marks for clearly labelled transition triggers (preemption, I/O completion, etc.).

---

## 4.3 Scheduling Flow — How the Kernel Selects the Next Task

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["Tick Interrupt or Event Occurs"] --> B["Save Current Task Context\nto TCB on Stack"]
    B --> C["Update Task States\nCheck Blocked List Timeouts"]
    C --> D{"Ready Queue\nEmpty?"}
    D -- Yes --> E["Idle Task Runs\nCPU in Low Power Mode"]
    D -- No --> F["Run Scheduler Algorithm\nSelect Highest Priority Ready Task"]
    F --> G{"New Task\nPriority >\nCurrent Task?"}
    G -- No --> H["Restore Current Task\nContinue Execution"]
    G -- Yes --> I["Context Switch\nRestore New Task Context"]
    I --> J["New Task Begins Execution"]
    H --> K["Continue Current Task"]
    J --> K
    K --> A
    E --> A
```

---

## 4.4 RTOS vs GPOS — Decision Tree for Embedded Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    START["Choosing an OS for\nEmbedded Product"] --> Q1{"Is deterministic\nresponse required?"}
    Q1 -- "Yes (Hard Deadline)" --> Q2{"Periodic or\nAperiodic Tasks?"}
    Q1 -- "No (Best Effort)" --> Q3["General Purpose OS\nLinux, Windows IoT, Android"]
    Q2 -- "Periodic Dominant" --> Q4["Static Priority RTOS\nFreeRTOS, VxWorks, RTEMS\nUse Rate Monotonic Scheduling"]
    Q2 -- "Mixed / Dynamic" --> Q5["Dynamic Priority RTOS\nUse EDF Scheduling\nQNX, ThreadX"]
    Q3 --> Q6{"Resource Budget\n> 32 MB RAM, > 100 MHz?"}
    Q6 -- Yes --> Q7["Full Linux Build\nYocto, Buildroot"]
    Q6 -- No --> Q8["Embedded Linux Variant\nuClinux, NuttX"]
    Q4 --> END["Final Selection\nCertified for Safety Domain"]
    Q5 --> END
    Q7 --> END
    Q8 --> END
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1: Define an Operating System. List four essential functions of an OS in an embedded system.
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

An **Operating System** is system software that manages hardware resources and provides services to application programs through a defined set of abstractions and system calls.

**Four essential functions in an embedded system:**

1. **Task Scheduling** — Decides which task executes on the CPU at any given time using a deterministic algorithm (priority-based, RMS, EDF).
2. **Memory Management** — Allocates and deallocates SRAM for stack, heap, and memory pools; enforces memory protection boundaries.
3. **Interrupt Handling** — Prioritises and dispatches hardware interrupts from peripherals (UART, timers, GPIO) to the correct ISR.
4. **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** — Provides synchronisation primitives like semaphores, mutexes, message queues, and event flags to allow safe communication between concurrent tasks.

---

### Question 2: Distinguish between a Hard Real-Time System and a Soft Real-Time System. Give one example of each.
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Hard Real-Time System | Soft Real-Time System |
|---|---|---|
| Deadline Consequence | Missing deadline = **system failure** (catastrophic) | Missing deadline = **degraded performance** (tolerable) |
| Determinism | Strict, bounded worst-case latency | Best-effort, statistical guarantees |
| Design Focus | Worst-case execution time analysis | Average-case performance |
| Example | Anti-lock Braking System (ABS), Pacemaker, Flight Control | Video streaming, Online gaming, Smart TV UI |
| OS Example | VxWorks, QNX, FreeRTOS (configured for hard RT) | Standard Linux, Windows, Android |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Phrase to Use:** "Hard real-time systems must *guarantee* completion before the deadline; soft real-time systems *aim* to complete before the deadline."

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Scheduling and OS Metrics
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the FCFS and Round Robin scheduling algorithms with suitable diagrams. Compare their advantages and disadvantages in an embedded RTOS context. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Consider the following set of five processes arriving at time 0 in a system using **Shortest Job First (SJF)** non-preemptive scheduling. Calculate the **Average Waiting Time (AWT)**, **Average Turnaround Time (ATAT)**, and draw the **Gantt chart**. **(7 marks)**

| Process | Burst Time (ms) |
|---|---|
| P1 | 6 |
| P2 | 2 |
| P3 | 8 |
| P4 | 3 |
| P5 | 4 |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(a) — FCFS vs Round Robin (7 marks)

**FCFS (First-Come-First-Served):**
- The process that arrives first in the ready queue is allocated the CPU.
- It runs to completion (non-preemptive) before the next process is dispatched.
- **Implementation:** A simple FIFO queue.

**Gantt Chart Example (FCFS with P1, P2, P3 arriving at 0):**
```
| P1 | P2 | P3 |
0    6   10  18
```

**Advantages:**
- Simplest possible algorithm — no complex data structures.
- Zero starvation — every process eventually runs.
- No context switch overhead during execution.

**Disadvantages:**
- Suffers from the **convoy effect** — short jobs wait behind long jobs.
- Non-deterministic response time — poor for real-time systems.
- Average waiting time is often worse than other algorithms.

**Round Robin (RR):**
- Each process gets a fixed time slice called a **time quantum** $q$.
- After $q$ expires, the process is preempted and placed at the back of the ready queue.
- The scheduler then dispatches the next process in the queue.

**Gantt Chart Example (RR, q = 4 ms, P1=6, P2=2, P3=8):**
```
| P1 (4) | P2 (2) | P3 (4) | P1 (2) | P3 (4) |
0        4        6        10       12      16
```

**Advantages:**
- Fair allocation of CPU time to all processes.
- Excellent response time for short jobs.
- No starvation.

**Disadvantages:**
- Performance is highly sensitive to the time quantum size.
- Too small $q$ leads to excessive context switch overhead.
- Too large $q$ degenerates into FCFS.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RTOS Context:** Round Robin is used in *soft real-time* RTOS (e.g., embOS with time slices). For *hard real-time*, fixed-priority preemption (RMS) is preferred because RR cannot guarantee deadline bounds in the worst case.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-A(b) — SJF Calculation (7 marks)

**Step 1:** Since all processes arrive at time 0, SJF simply sorts them by burst time ascending.

Execution order: **P2 (2), P4 (3), P5 (4), P1 (6), P3 (8)**

**Step 2 — Construct the Gantt chart:**

```
| P2 | P4 | P5 | P1 | P3 |
0    2    5    9   15   23
```

**Step 3 — Compute Completion Time and TAT for each process:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CT_{P2} &= 2, \quad TAT_{P2} = 2 - 0 = 2 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P4} &= 5, \quad TAT_{P4} = 5 - 0 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P5} &= 9, \quad TAT_{P5} = 9 - 0 = 9 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P1} &= 15, \quad TAT_{P1} = 15 - 0 = 15 \text{ ms} \\
CT_{P3} &= 23, \quad TAT_{P3} = 23 - 0 = 23 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Waiting Time $WT = TAT - BT$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
WT_{P2} &= 2 - 2 = 0 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P4} &= 5 - 3 = 2 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P5} &= 9 - 4 = 5 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P1} &= 15 - 6 = 9 \text{ ms} \\
WT_{P3} &= 23 - 8 = 15 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Averages:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AWT &= \frac{0 + 2 + 5 + 9 + 15}{5} = \frac{31}{5} = 6.2 \text{ ms} \\
ATAT &= \frac{2 + 5 + 9 + 15 + 23}{5} = \frac{54}{5} = 10.8 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often incorrectly compute TAT as $CT - BT$ instead of $CT - AT$. Since all arrivals are at $t=0$ in this problem, both give the same result — **do not generalise this shortcut to other problems** where arrival times differ. Always write the full formula $TAT = CT - AT$ explicitly.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — RTOS Architecture and Real-Time Scheduling
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **kernel architecture** of a typical embedded RTOS. Differentiate between **monolithic** and **microkernel** designs. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Three periodic real-time tasks execute on an RTOS using **Rate Monotonic Scheduling**. Determine if the task set is schedulable using the Liu & Layland sufficient condition. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 25 | 5 |
| $\tau_3$ | 50 | 8 |

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(a) — RTOS Kernel Architecture (7 marks)

**Typical RTOS Kernel Architecture Diagram:**

```
+-----------------------------------------------+
|              APPLICATION TASKS                 |
+-----------------------------------------------+
|         MIDDLEWARE (TCP/IP, File System)       |
+-----------------------------------------------+
|  Scheduler  |  IPC  |  Timer  |  Memory Mgr   |   <-- KERNEL
+-----------------------------------------------+
|           DEVICE DRIVERS (UART, SPI, I/O)      |
+-----------------------------------------------+
|               HARDWARE ABSTRACTION             |
+-----------------------------------------------+
|                HARDWARE (MCU)                  |
+-----------------------------------------------+
```

**Monolithic Kernel:**
- All OS services (scheduler, drivers, FS, network stack) run in a **single privileged address space**.
- Function calls between modules are direct, giving **very low overhead**.
- However, a bug in a driver can crash the entire system.
- Examples: Traditional Linux, µC/OS-II, FreeRTOS.

**Microkernel:**
- The kernel contains only **minimal essential services**: scheduling, IPC, and basic address space management.
- All other services (drivers, FS, network) run in **user space** as separate processes.
- Communication happens via **message passing** (IPC), which adds overhead.
- **Fault isolation:** A driver crash does not bring down the kernel.
- Examples: QNX, L4, MINIX.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Monolithic Kernel | Microkernel |
|---|---|---|
| Performance | Higher (direct calls) | Lower (IPC overhead) |
| Reliability | Lower (single address space) | Higher (isolated modules) |
| Modularity | Difficult to extend | Highly modular |
| Code Size | Smaller | Larger |
| Embedded Use | Most RTOS (FreeRTOS, VxWorks) | High-reliability systems (QNX in cars) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight (for extra marks):** Modern automotive ECUs use **AUTOSAR** with a microkernel-like separation between the Basic Software (BSW) and Application Layer, even when the underlying OS is monolithic — this is achieved through the Runtime Environment (RTE) abstraction layer.

---

#### Model Solution to Q-B(b) — RMS Schedulability (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Compute Per-Task Utilisation $U_i = C_i / T_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_1 &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} = \frac{2}{10} = 0.20 \\
U_2 &= \frac{C_2}{T_2} = \frac{5}{25} = 0.20 \\
U_3 &= \frac{C_3}{T_3} = \frac{8}{50} = 0.16
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute Total Utilisation:**

$$
U_{total} = U_1 + U_2 + U_3 = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.16 = 0.56
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Liu & Layland Bound for $N = 3$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{bound} &= N(2^{1/N} - 1) \\
&= 3 \times (2^{1/3} - 1) \\
&= 3 \times (1.2599 - 1) \\
&= 3 \times 0.2599 \\
&= 0.7797
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Decision:**

$$
U_{total} = 0.56 \le U_{bound} = 0.7797
$$

The total utilisation (56%) is **less than** the RMS bound (77.97%), therefore the task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Trap:** Students sometimes write $2^{1/N}$ as $2^{N}$ — this is wrong. The exponent is $\frac{1}{N}$, not $N$. Also remember to use **parentheses** carefully: $2^{(1/N)}$ not $2^1 / N$. KTU examiners specifically deduct 1 mark for this careless notation error.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Operating System Basics (Module 3)**

### Core Concepts to Memorise

- [ ] **OS Definition:** System software acting as a hardware-user interface, managing resources and providing abstractions.
- [ ] **Three OS Categories:** Bare-metal, GPOS, RTOS — distinguish by determinism, not by speed.
- [ ] **Hard vs Soft Real-Time:** Hard = deadline miss causes failure; Soft = deadline miss causes degradation.
- [ ] **Five Process States:** New, Ready, Running, Blocked, Terminated — know all transition triggers.
- [ ] **Process vs Thread:** Process has its own address space; thread shares with siblings.
- [ ] **Three Kernel Types:** Monolithic, Microkernel, Hybrid — know pros/cons and examples.

### Formulas You Must Remember Cold

- [ ] $TAT = CT - AT$
- [ ] $WT = TAT - BT$
- [ ] $RT = ST - AT$ (start time, not completion)
- [ ] $U = \sum (C_i / T_i)$ (RMS)
- [ ] $U_{bound} = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$ (Liu & Layland)
- [ ] $\text{CPU Utilisation} = (\text{Busy Time} / \text{Total Time}) \times 100\%$
- [ ] $\text{Throughput} = N / \text{Total Time}$

### Diagrams You Must Be Able to Draw From Memory

- [ ] Process state transition diagram (with all transition labels)
- [ ] OS layered architecture (Application → Middleware → Kernel → Driver → HAL → Hardware)
- [ ] Gantt chart for FCFS, SJF, RR, and Priority scheduling

### Common KTU Pitfalls to Avoid

- [ ] Do NOT confuse **Response Time** with **Waiting Time** — they use different variables.
- [ ] Do NOT assume FCFS gives minimum AWT — it usually gives the worst.
- [ ] Do NOT write $2^N$ instead of $2^{1/N}$ in the RMS bound.
- [ ] Do NOT call an RTOS "fast" — it is **deterministic**.
- [ ] Do NOT skip the **Gantt chart** in scheduling problems — it is the foundation of every calculation.

### Key Names and Concepts (for 1-mark bonus recall)

- [ ] **Liu & Layland Theorem** (1973) — Foundation of RMS analysis.
- [ ] **Convoy Effect** — FCFS drawback with mixed job lengths.
- [ ] **Priority Inversion** — Lower priority task blocks higher priority task (Mariner 1, 1997 case study).
- [ ] **Priority Inheritance Protocol** — Solution to priority inversion (used in VxWorks, FreeRTOS).
- [ ] **AUTOSAR / OSEK** — Automotive OS standards.
- [ ] **POSIX** — IEEE 1003 standard for portable OS APIs.

### Real-Time OS Design Checklist (Industry Relevant)

- [ ] Static task set, static priorities (avoid dynamic allocation).
- [ ] Bounded interrupt latency, bounded context switch time.
- [ ] Stack overflow detection enabled.
- [ ] Watchdog timer for system health.
- [ ] Memory pools pre-allocated at startup (no malloc in ISRs).
- [ ] WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time) measured for every task.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of OS
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of OS

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Advanced Embedded Systems** under the KTU 2024 Scheme, an **Operating System (OS)** is a privileged software layer (the *kernel* and its associated system services) that abstracts underlying heterogeneous hardware resources — **CPU cores, timers, memory, I/O peripherals, buses, and network interfaces** — and presents them as deterministic, schedulable services to application software. The *type* of an OS is dictated by its **timing guarantees, scheduling discipline, footprint, and interaction model** with the physical world.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition**
> An embedded OS is a software platform designed specifically for resource-constrained, dedicated-function devices. Unlike a *General Purpose OS (GPOS)* used in desktops, an embedded OS must satisfy **strict determinism, low latency, small footprint, and real-time responsiveness** to external stimuli.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller"

Imagine the **CPU** as a single **runway** at a busy international airport. Hundreds of **aircraft (tasks/processes)** are queued up, each needing to land or take off within a strict time window. The **Operating System** is the **Air Traffic Controller (ATC)** who decides:

- **Which aircraft gets the runway next?** → *Scheduling Policy*
- **What if an emergency flight arrives?** → *Preemption / Priority*
- **How do we avoid collisions?** → *Resource Synchronization*
- **What if a flight misses its slot?** → *Deadline Management (Hard/Soft Real-Time)*

A *Hard Real-Time OS* is like a **military ATC** — if a fighter jet is told to land by 12:00:00.000, it **MUST** land by that microsecond, or lives are lost. A *Soft Real-Time OS* is like a **commercial ATC** — a 5-minute delay is annoying but not catastrophic. A *GPOS* is like an **uncontrolled open field** — fair use, no guarantees.

## 1.3 The KTU Classification Tree

Embedded OS types are broadly classified along three orthogonal axes:

| Axis | Dimension | Categories |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Timing Determinism** | When *must* work happen? | Hard / Firm / Soft Real-Time, Non-Real-Time |
| **User Interaction** | How many users/tasks? | Single-User / Multi-User |
| **Execution Concurrency** | How many programs run together? | Single-Task / Multi-Task / Multi-Processor / Distributed |

> [!NOTE]
> In Module 3 of PECPT861, the primary focus is on **timing-based classification** because that is what distinguishes an *embedded* OS from a desktop/server OS.

## 1.4 Quick-Reference Type Matrix

| OS Type | Timing Guarantee | Typical Use-Case | Examples |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Hard Real-Time** | **Absolute** deadline; **zero** tolerance | Airbag, Pacemaker, Anti-lock Braking | VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS, FreeRTOS |
| **Firm Real-Time** | Late result has **no value** (useless) | Video frame rendering, Stock trading | Some multimedia OS |
| **Soft Real-Time** | Late result **degrades** quality gracefully | Audio streaming, VoIP | Windows CE, Embedded Linux (PREEMPT_RT) |
| **GPOS (Non-RT)** | **No timing guarantee** | Desktop, servers | Windows, Linux, macOS |
| **Mobile Embedded** | Soft real-time + touch UI | Smartphones, Tablets | Android, iOS, Tizen |
| **Network OS** | Soft real-time + packet priority | Routers, Switches | Cisco IOS, Junos |
| **Distributed OS** | Cluster-wide coordination | IoT edge, Cloud | ROS 2, Azure RTOS |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key KTU Terminology**: The terms **deterministic** and **preemptive** are *not* synonyms. Determinism means *bounded, predictable response time*. Preemption means *the kernel can forcibly take the CPU from a running task*. A Hard RTOS must be **both deterministic AND preemptive**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) — The Heart of Embedded OS

An **RTOS** is an OS designed to serve real-time applications that process data as it comes in, typically without buffering delays. The RTOS kernel manages:

1. **Task Scheduling** (Rate-Monotonic, EDF, Priority-based)
2. **Interrupt Handling** (bounded ISR latency)
3. **Inter-Task Communication** (Queues, Semaphores, Mailboxes)
4. **Memory Management** (often static / pool-based)
5. **Time Management** (system ticks, high-resolution timers)

### 2.1.1 Hard Real-Time Operating System (HRTOS)

- **Definition**: A system where missing an absolute deadline is considered a **total system failure** — potentially causing loss of life, physical damage, or financial catastrophe.
- **Latency Bound**: Interrupt latency **≤ 10 µs** (typical), Jitter **≤ 1 µs**.
- **Examples**: VxWorks (used in Mars rovers, Boeing 787), QNX (in BlackBerry, automotive infotainment), RTEMS (in space probes).

### 2.1.2 Soft Real-Time Operating System (SRTOS)

- **Definition**: A system where deadline misses cause **performance degradation** but **not system failure**. The utility of a late result decays smoothly with delay.
- **Latency Bound**: Soft deadlines, typically in milliseconds; performance degrades if missed.
- **Examples**: Embedded Linux with PREEMPT_RT patches, Windows IoT.

### 2.1.3 Firm Real-Time Operating System

- **Definition**: A system where missing a deadline renders the result **worthless** (utility drops to zero), but does not cause catastrophic failure. The task is simply discarded.
- **Examples**: Video frame decoding (a missed frame is dropped, no replay), stock ticker updates.

## 2.2 General-Purpose Operating System (GPOS)

A GPOS is optimized for **average-case throughput**, **fairness**, and **user convenience**. It uses sophisticated resource management (virtual memory, complex file systems, GUI), but its interrupt latency and scheduling jitter are **unbounded** from the application's perspective.

| Property | RTOS | GPOS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Goal** | Meet deadlines | Maximize throughput |
| **Scheduling** | Priority / Preemptive | Time-sharing / Fair-share |
| **Interrupt Latency** | Bounded (µs) | Unbounded (ms–s) |
| **Kernel Size** | Tiny (KB to few MB) | Large (hundreds of MB) |
| **Response Time** | Deterministic | Best-effort |
| **Cost** | High licensing / Certification | Low / Free (Linux) |

## 2.3 Other Specialized OS Types in Embedded Context

### 2.3.1 Single-Task / Super-Loop OS
The simplest "OS" — a `while(1)` loop. No multitasking. Found in **8-bit microcontrollers** (8051, ATmega328 in Arduino UNO).

### 2.3.2 Multi-Tasking OS with Cooperative Scheduling
Tasks voluntarily yield the CPU. Used in early **TinyOS** (wireless sensor nodes).

### 2.3.3 Multi-Tasking OS with Preemptive Scheduling
The scheduler can forcibly evict a running task. Standard for **FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX**.

### 2.3.4 Mobile Embedded OS
Optimized for **touch UI, power management, and connectivity**. Android uses a modified Linux kernel; iOS uses XNU (hybrid Mach + BSD).

### 2.3.5 Network OS
Specialized for **packet processing**. Runs on routers/switches. Examples: Cisco IOS, Junos, VyOS.

### 2.3.6 Distributed OS / IoT OS
Coordinates computation across multiple networked embedded nodes. Examples: **Contiki-NG**, **Zephyr**, **RIOT OS**, **Azure RTOS (ThreadX)**.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates every formula, metric, and bound you must memorize for PECPT861 examination on "Types of OS".

| Symbol / Formula | Meaning | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | **CPU Utilization** under Rate-Monotonic Scheduling | Dimensionless, $0 \leq U \leq 1$ |
| $U_{RMS} \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | **Liu & Layland Bound** for RMS schedulability | As $n \to \infty$, $U_{RMS} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ |
| $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | **Response Time** of task $i$ (fixed-point iteration) | $R_i \leq T_i$ for schedulability |
| $T_{latency} = T_{detect} + T_{dispatch} + T_{ISR} + T_{schedule}$ | **End-to-End Interrupt Latency** | Microseconds (µs) for HRTOS |
| $Jitter = \max(t_{response}) - \min(t_{response})$ | **Timing Jitter** for periodic task | Lower is better; HRTOS: µs-scale |
| $Worst\ Case\ Execution\ Time (WCET) = C_i$ | Upper-bound execution time | Critical for schedulability tests |
| $\text{Priority Inversion Time} \leq (n-1) \cdot C_{max}$ | **Priority Inheritance Protocol bound** | Prevents unbounded inversion |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility**
> In the automotive industry (AUTOSAR standard), a vehicle's **Electronic Control Unit (ECU)** runs an HRTOS. An airbag must deploy within **5 ms** of crash detection — a GPOS with its millisecond-scale jitter would be **unacceptable**. In medical devices (FDA Class III), the OS must be **certified** (IEC 62304) as deterministic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Liu & Layland's Utilization Bound for Rate-Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU examinations on RTOS scheduling.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Given a set of $n$ periodic tasks $\{\tau_1, \tau_2, \dots, \tau_n\}$ where each task $\tau_i$ has:
- **Period** $T_i$ (and deadline $D_i = T_i$)
- **Worst-Case Execution Time** $C_i$
- **Priority** $p_i$ assigned inversely with $T_i$ (shorter period → higher priority)

**Question**: What is the maximum CPU utilization $U$ such that **all deadlines are guaranteed to be met**?

### 3.1.2 The Critical Instant Argument

The **worst-case response time** for any task occurs at its **critical instant** — the time when all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with the task under analysis.

At a critical instant, the processor demand on task $\tau_i$ during interval $[0, t]$ is:

$$
W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

where the summation is over all tasks with **higher priority** than $\tau_i$.

### 3.1.3 The Fixed-Point Iteration

The response time $R_i$ of $\tau_i$ is the **smallest fixed-point** of $W_i(t) = t$:

$$
R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

**Termination Condition**: $R_i^{(k+1)} = R_i^{(k)}$ or $R_i^{(k+1)} > T_i$ (deadline miss).

**Schedulability Condition**: $\tau_i$ is schedulable if and only if $R_i \leq T_i$.

### 3.1.4 The Closed-Form Bound (Liu & Layland, 1973)

For $n$ tasks with periods $T_i$ and utilizations $u_i = C_i / T_i$, the **least upper bound** of total utilization that guarantees schedulability under RMS is:

$$
U_{bound}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

**Step-by-step expansion**:

$$
U_{bound}(n) = n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

As $n \to \infty$, using the limit $\lim_{x \to 0} 2^x = 1 + x \ln 2 + O(x^2)$:

$$
U_{bound}(n) \approx n \left( \frac{\ln 2}{n} \right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931
$$

**Numerical values (memorize these for KTU):**

$$
U_{bound}(1) = 1.000, \quad U_{bound}(2) \approx 0.828, \quad U_{bound}(3) \approx 0.779, \quad U_{bound}(\infty) \approx 0.693
$$

## 3.2 Derivative Worked Example: Response Time Calculation

### 3.2.1 Given Task Set

Consider three tasks scheduled under RMS on an RTOS:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Utilization $u_i$ |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\tau_1$ | 6 | 2 | 0.333 |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 3 | 0.300 |
| $\tau_3$ | 15 | 3 | 0.200 |

Total Utilization: $U = 0.333 + 0.300 + 0.200 = 0.833$

Liu & Layland bound for $n=3$: $U_{bound}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.779$

**Observation**: $U = 0.833 > 0.779$, but this does **not necessarily** mean unschedulable — the bound is *sufficient but not necessary*. We must check **exact response times**.

### 3.2.2 Iterative Response-Time Analysis

**Step 1**: Compute $R_1$ for $\tau_1$ (highest priority, no preemption by others):

$$
R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

Since there are no higher-priority tasks, $R_1 = 2$ ms $\leq T_1 = 6$ ms. **Schedulable.**

**Step 2**: Compute $R_2$ for $\tau_2$ (one higher-priority task $\tau_1$):

$$
R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 = 3 + 1 \cdot 2 = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{6} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 = 3 + 1 \cdot 2 = 5 \text{ ms}
$$

Fixed point reached: $R_2 = 5$ ms $\leq T_2 = 10$ ms. **Schedulable.**

**Step 3**: Compute $R_3$ for $\tau_3$ (higher-priority tasks $\tau_1, \tau_2$):

$$
R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 3 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{10} \right\rceil C_2 = 3 + 1(2) + 1(3) = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{6} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{8}{10} \right\rceil (3) = 3 + 2(2) + 1(3) = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{10}{10} \right\rceil (3) = 3 + 2(2) + 1(3) = 10 \text{ ms}
$$

Fixed point reached: $R_3 = 10$ ms $\leq T_3 = 15$ ms. **Schedulable.**

**Conclusion**: Even though utilization **exceeds** the Liu & Layland bound, all tasks meet their deadlines. This is the *sufficiency vs necessity* nuance examiners love to test.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Hard vs Firm vs Soft Real-Time — The "Utility Function"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Utility** = value of a task's result as a function of its completion time $t_c$.

$$
U_{hard}(t_c) = \begin{cases} 1 & t_c \leq D \\ -\infty & t_c > D \end{cases}
$$

$$
U_{firm}(t_c) = \begin{cases} 1 & t_c \leq D \\ 0 & t_c > D \end{cases}
$$

$$
U_{soft}(t_c) = \begin{cases} 1 & t_c \leq D \\ 1 - \frac{t_c - D}{\alpha} & t_c > D \end{cases}
$$

where $\alpha$ is a decay constant. This **utility-function framework** is a frequent KTU short-answer question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Hierarchical Classification of Embedded OS Types

The following Mermaid block renders the **complete taxonomy of OS types** as covered in Module 3 of PECPT861.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[OPERATING SYSTEMS] --> B[Real-Time OS RTOS]
    A --> C[General Purpose OS GPOS]
    A --> D[Specialized Embedded OS]

    B --> B1[Hard Real-Time]
    B --> B2[Firm Real-Time]
    B --> B3[Soft Real-Time]

    B1 --> B1A["VxWorks"]
    B1 --> B1B["QNX Neutrino"]
    B1 --> B1C["RTEMS"]
    B1 --> B1D["FreeRTOS with config"]

    B2 --> B2A[Video Frame Renderers]
    B2 --> B2B[Stock Trading Tickers]

    B3 --> B3A["Embedded Linux PREEMPT RT"]
    B3 --> B3B["Windows IoT Core"]
    B3 --> B3C["Android User Space"]

    C --> C1[Desktop OS]
    C --> C2[Server OS]
    C1 --> C1A["Windows"]
    C1 --> C1B["Ubuntu Desktop"]
    C2 --> C2A["Red Hat Enterprise Linux"]
    C2 --> C2B["Windows Server"]

    D --> D1[Mobile Embedded OS]
    D --> D2[Network OS]
    D --> D3[Distributed IoT OS]
    D --> D4[Sensor Node OS]

    D1 --> D1A["Android"]
    D1 --> D1B["iOS"]
    D1 --> D1C["Tizen"]

    D2 --> D2A["Cisco IOS"]
    D2 --> D2B["Junos"]
    D2 --> D2C["VyOS"]

    D3 --> D3A["Zephyr"]
    D3 --> D3B["Azure RTOS ThreadX"]
    D3 --> D3C["NuttX"]

    D4 --> D4A["TinyOS"]
    D4 --> D4B["Contiki NG"]
    D4 --> D4C["RIOT OS"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Interrupt-to-Task Response Pipeline

This diagram illustrates the **end-to-end flow** from hardware interrupt to task execution in an RTOS, mapping where each timing metric is measured.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HWINT["HARDWARE INTERRUPT Signal Edge"] --> LAT1["Interrupt Detection Time Tdetect"]
    LAT1 --> LAT2["Vector Lookup and ISR Entry Tdispatch"]
    LAT2 --> LAT3["ISR Execution Time Tisr"]
    LAT3 --> LAT4["Scheduler Decision Tschedule"]
    LAT4 --> TASK["High Priority Task Activated"]
    TASK --> EXEC["Task Execution Time Cexec"]
    EXEC --> DONE["Deadline Check Tdeadline"]
    DONE --> OUT1["Deadline Met Hard RT"]
    DONE --> OUT2["Deadline Missed Failure"]
```

## 4.3 Real-Time Scheduling Decision Flow (RMS / EDF Selection)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START["New Task Arrival"] --> Q1{"Are Deadlines Absolute and Critical"}
    Q1 -->|"Yes"| Q1A["Use Hard RTOS HRTOS"]
    Q1 -->|"No"| Q2{"Is Task Set Periodic with Fixed Periods"}
    Q2 -->|"Yes"| Q2A["Apply Rate Monotonic RMS"]
    Q2 -->|"No"| Q3{"Do Deadlines Differ from Periods"}
    Q3 -->|"Yes"| Q3A["Apply Earliest Deadline First EDF"]
    Q3 -->|"No"| Q4["Apply Round Robin or Time Sharing"]
```

## 4.4 Priority Inversion Scenario (Classic KTU Problem)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant L as Low Priority Task L
    participant H as High Priority Task H
    participant M as Medium Priority Task M
    participant K as Kernel Scheduler

    Note over L: Acquires shared lock LOCK_X
    L->>K: Running
    K->>H: Activate H preempt L
    Note over H: Tries to acquire LOCK_X BLOCKED
    K->>M: Preempt L with M
    Note over M: Runs to completion
    K->>L: Resume L
    L->>K: Releases LOCK_X
    K->>H: Now unblocked runs
    Note over H: Deadline possibly MISSED
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall Visualized**: In the diagram above, Task $H$ (highest priority) is forced to wait until $L$ releases the lock — but $L$ is preempted by $M$. This is the **unbounded priority inversion problem**. Solutions: **Priority Inheritance Protocol** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol**.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Q1]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between a **Hard Real-Time OS** and a **Soft Real-Time OS**. Give **two examples** of each.

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

| Aspect | Hard Real-Time OS | Soft Real-Time OS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Deadline** | Strict, absolute — missing it causes system failure | Flexible — missing it degrades performance |
| **Response Time** | Strictly bounded (µs scale) | Best-effort (ms scale) |
| **Examples** | VxWorks (Mars Rovers), QNX (Automotive ECUs) | Android, Windows IoT |

*(Award 1 mark for clear distinction, 1 mark for correct examples, 1 mark for appropriate engineering context.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Q2]**
> **Q2.** Define **Firm Real-Time System**. Explain with an example how a missed deadline is handled.

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A **Firm Real-Time System** is one in which a late result has **zero utility** (is discarded), but missing the deadline does not cause catastrophic failure. The task is simply **aborted or dropped** upon deadline expiry.

**Example**: In a digital video decoder, if a frame is not decoded by its display deadline (say 33 ms for 30 fps video), the frame is **skipped** and the next frame is decoded. The viewer may perceive a minor stutter, but the system continues functioning.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for "discard on miss", 1 mark for valid example with numerical context.)*

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3, Question A]**
> **Q3 (a).** With a neat diagram, explain the **classification of Operating Systems** used in embedded systems. Discuss the salient features of **Hard Real-Time, Soft Real-Time, and Firm Real-Time** systems. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Part (a) — Classification Diagram**:

Refer to **Section 4.1** of these notes for the complete hierarchical taxonomy. The classification is along three axes:

1. **Timing Determinism** (Hard / Firm / Soft / Non-Real-Time)
2. **User Concurrency** (Single-User / Multi-User)
3. **Task Concurrency** (Single-Task / Multi-Task / Multi-Processor / Distributed)

**Hard Real-Time OS**: A system where missing an absolute deadline constitutes **total system failure**. Response time is strictly bounded in microseconds. Used in safety-critical applications: anti-lock braking systems (ABS), pacemakers, aircraft flight control. Examples: **VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS**.

**Soft Real-Time OS**: A system where missing a deadline causes **graceful degradation**. Examples: **Android, Embedded Linux (PREEMPT_RT), Windows IoT Core**.

**Firm Real-Time OS**: A system where a late result has **no value** (utility = 0) but does not cause system failure. Example: a video frame renderer drops late frames rather than displaying them late.

*(Award 2 marks for diagram, 3 marks for hard RT discussion, 2 marks for soft & firm distinction.)*

---

> **Q3 (b).** Explain the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm. Derive the **Liu & Layland utilization bound** for $n$ tasks. Verify the schedulability of the following task set using **exact response-time analysis**:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) |
| :---: | :---: | :---: |
| $\tau_1$ | 5 | 2 |
| $\tau_2$ | 10 | 4 |

**(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Algorithm**: Rate Monotonic Scheduling assigns priorities **inversely proportional to task periods**. The shortest-period task gets the highest priority. It is **static** (priorities assigned at design time) and **preemptive**.

**Derivation Sketch**:

For $n$ periodic tasks with $u_i = C_i / T_i$, Liu & Layland (1973) proved the sufficient schedulability condition:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} u_i \leq n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right) = U_{bound}(n)
$$

**Proof Outline**: At the critical instant, all tasks are released simultaneously. The worst-case completion time $W_i(t)$ for task $\tau_i$ satisfies:

$$
W_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

Setting $W_i(t) = t$ and analyzing worst-case ratios yields the closed-form bound.

**Numerical Verification for Given Task Set**:

Step 1: Compute utilizations:
$$
u_1 = 2/5 = 0.400, \quad u_2 = 4/10 = 0.400
$$
$$
U_{total} = 0.800
$$

Step 2: Liu & Layland bound for $n=2$:
$$
U_{bound}(2) = 2(2^{1/2} - 1) = 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \approx 2(0.4142) \approx 0.8284
$$

Step 3: Compare: $U_{total} = 0.800 \leq 0.8284$ → **Sufficient condition met**, likely schedulable.

Step 4: Exact response-time verification:

$$
R_1^{(0)} = C_1 = 2 \text{ ms}
$$

Fixed point: $R_1 = 2 \leq T_1 = 5$ ✓ **Schedulable**.

$$
R_2^{(0)} = C_2 = 4 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_2^{(1)} = C_2 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{5} \right\rceil \cdot C_1 = 4 + 1(2) = 6 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{5} \right\rceil (2) = 4 + 2(2) = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

$$
R_2^{(3)} = 4 + \left\lceil \frac{8}{5} \right\rceil (2) = 4 + 2(2) = 8 \text{ ms}
$$

Fixed point: $R_2 = 8$ ms $\leq T_2 = 10$ ms ✓ **Schedulable**.

**Conclusion**: Both tasks meet their deadlines under RMS.

*(Award 2 marks for algorithm explanation, 2 marks for Liu & Layland derivation, 2 marks for exact numerical analysis, 1 mark for final conclusion.)*

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024, Module 3, Question B (Alternative Choice)]**
> **Q4 (a).** Compare **RTOS** and **GPOS** in detail. Why is a **GPOS like Linux unsuitable** for a hard real-time automotive braking system? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

| Parameter | RTOS | GPOS |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Primary Objective** | Predictability, deadline adherence | Throughput, fairness |
| **Scheduling** | Priority-based preemptive | Time-sharing / fair-share |
| **Interrupt Latency** | Bounded (µs) | Unbounded (ms–s) |
| **Kernel Footprint** | 5 KB – 2 MB | 50 MB – GB scale |
| **Memory Mgmt** | Often static / pool-based | Virtual memory, paging, swap |
| **Examples** | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, QNX | Windows, Desktop Linux |

**Why GPOS is unsuitable for automotive braking**:

1. **Unbounded interrupt latency**: Linux can disable interrupts for *milliseconds* during page faults, IRQ balancing, or kernel preemption-disabled sections.
2. **Non-deterministic scheduler**: CFS (Completely Fair Scheduler) optimizes for fairness, not deadlines.
3. **Garbage collection / page faults**: User-space processes may suffer *seconds* of latency due to demand paging.
4. **Braking requirement**: ABS must respond within **5 ms** of wheel-lock detection; a GPOS cannot guarantee this.
5. **Safety certification**: ISO 26262 ASIL-D certification is essentially **impossible** for an unmodified GPOS kernel.

**Workaround**: Some systems use **PREEMPT_RT** patches, dual-kernel approaches (e.g., Xenomai), or hardware-assisted virtualization, but pure GPOS remains unsuitable.

*(Award 3 marks for table comparison, 4 marks for automotive-specific justification.)*

---

> **Q4 (b).** Explain the **Priority Inversion Problem** in real-time systems. Describe the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** and **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** as solutions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution**:

**Priority Inversion**: Occurs when a high-priority task is **indirectly preempted** by a lower-priority task because the low-priority task holds a shared resource (mutex/lock) needed by the high-priority task. A medium-priority task may further delay the low-priority task, causing **unbounded blocking**.

**Classic Example — Mars Pathfinder (1997)**: A priority inversion bug caused system resets on the Mars rover. Solution: enable priority inheritance in VxWorks.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)**:

When a high-priority task $H$ blocks on a lock held by low-priority task $L$:
1. $L$ **temporarily inherits** the priority of $H$.
2. $L$ runs at this elevated priority until it releases the lock.
3. $L$ then reverts to its original priority.

**Bound on blocking time**: A task can be blocked by **at most one** lower-priority task per lock acquisition, and transitively by at most $n$ tasks total.

**Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**:
