Each mutex/semaphore is assigned a **priority ceiling** equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it.

1. A task may acquire a lock only if its priority is **strictly higher** than the priority ceilings of all locks currently held by other tasks.
2. This **prevents deadlocks** and ensures a task is blocked by at most **one** lower-priority task.

**Comparison**: PCP provides stronger guarantees (deadlock-free, bounded blocking) than PIP, at the cost of more complex runtime checks.

*(Award 2 marks for problem definition, 2 marks for PIP, 2 marks for PCP, 1 mark for Mars Pathfinder / real-world example.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Ways Students Lose Marks in Module 3 OS-Type Questions**
>
> 1. **Conflating "real-time" with "fast"**: An RTOS is not necessarily faster than a GPOS — it is **more predictable**. Writing "RTOS is faster" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing preemption with determinism**: Preemption is a *mechanism*; determinism is a *property*. An OS can be preemptive but non-deterministic (e.g., preemptive Linux without PREEMPT_RT).
> 3. **Wrong bound application**: Using Liu & Layland bound as a *necessary* condition. It is **sufficient, not necessary**. Always perform exact response-time analysis if utilization exceeds the bound.
> 4. **Skipping fixed-point iteration steps**: Examiners want every iteration shown. Stopping after one iteration when fixed point is not yet reached loses marks.
> 5. **Wrong priority for PIP/PCP comparison**: PIP allows deadlock but bounds blocking. PCP prevents deadlock AND bounds blocking. Mix-up = 2-mark penalty.
> 6. **Example mistakes**: Naming Android as a "Hard Real-Time OS" — Android is Soft Real-Time. Naming VxWorks as a GPOS — VxWorks is Hard Real-Time.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Types of OS" (Module 3, PECPT861)**

- [x] **OS in embedded context** = software layer abstracting hardware for deterministic application execution.
- [x] **Three orthogonal classification axes**: timing determinism, user concurrency, task concurrency.
- [x] **Hard RTOS**: absolute deadlines, µs-scale jitter, failure on miss. Examples: VxWorks, QNX, RTEMS.
- [x] **Soft RTOS**: graceful degradation on deadline miss. Examples: Embedded Linux (PREEMPT_RT), Android.
- [x] **Firm RTOS**: late result discarded, no system failure. Example: video frame decoder.
- [x] **GPOS**: no timing guarantee, optimized for throughput. Examples: Windows, Desktop Linux.
- [x] **Specialized Embedded OS**: Mobile (Android/iOS), Network (Cisco IOS), IoT (Zephyr, Contiki, RIOT).
- [x] **Liu & Layland Bound**: $U_{bound}(n) = n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, limit $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$. Memorize: $n=1:1.000$, $n=2:0.828$, $n=3:0.779$.
- [x] **Response Time Iteration**: $R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^{(k)} / T_j \rceil C_j$; stop when fixed-point reached.
- [x] **Priority Inversion**: solved by **PIP** (priority inheritance) or **PCP** (priority ceiling). Mars Pathfinder 1997 is the canonical example.
- [x] **Interrupt Latency**: bounded in RTOS (µs), unbounded in GPOS (ms–s).
- [x] **End-to-End pipeline**: $T_{detect} \to T_{dispatch} \to T_{ISR} \to T_{schedule} \to T_{exec}$.
- [x] **Certification context**: ISO 26262 (automotive), IEC 62304 (medical), DO-178C (avionics) — only HRTOS-grade OSes qualify for ASIL-D / Class C.

> **Final Note for KTU 2024 Candidates**: Module 3 carries significant weight in Part B questions. Master the **Liu & Layland derivation** and the **response-time iteration algorithm** — these appear in nearly every KTU end-semester exam for PECPT861.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Tasks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of an **Advanced Embedded System** executing a **Real-Time Operating System (RTOS)**, a **Task** (also termed a *thread of execution* or *lightweight process*) is defined as the fundamental, independently schedulable unit of work recognized by the kernel. Each task is a sequential program segment that owns its own private **stack**, an assigned **priority level**, an **execution context** (register snapshot), and a unique **Task Control Block (TCB)** maintained by the kernel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECPT861 – Module 3):**
> A *task* is a semi-independent program segment whose execution is managed cooperatively or preemptively by the RTOS scheduler. A task is NOT the same as a function call — it possesses its own **context**, **priority**, and **lifecycle states**, and can be suspended and resumed independently by the scheduler.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Specialist Worker" Model

Imagine a busy **hospital emergency department** with several **specialist doctors** (Cardiologist, Neurologist, Orthopedic). Each doctor:

- Has a **specialization** (analogous to **task priority**)
- Maintains a **personal patient list and clipboard** (analogous to the **Task Control Block / Stack**)
- Can be **called in, paused, and resumed** mid-surgery (analogous to **state transitions: Ready → Running → Blocked → Ready**)
- May need to **wait for lab results** before proceeding (analogous to a task entering the **Blocked** state on a Semaphore or Message Queue)
- The **head nurse (scheduler)** constantly checks who is most critical to attend to next and reassigns doctors accordingly

> [!NOTE]
> Just as doctors share the same hospital infrastructure but maintain independent workflows, multiple **tasks share the same CPU and memory** but each maintains an isolated execution context, allowing deterministic, concurrent behavior on a single-core embedded processor.

### 1.3 Why Tasks Matter in Embedded Systems

| Aspect | Benefit in Embedded Design |
|---|---|
| **Modularity** | Sensor polling, motor control, and communication can be developed as independent tasks |
| **Determinism** | Preemptive scheduling with priority ensures critical work meets deadlines |
| **Concurrency Illusion** | Multiple I/O operations appear to happen simultaneously on single-core hardware |
| **Maintainability** | Functions can be upgraded without rewriting the entire firmware |
| **Resource Isolation** | Stack corruption in one task does not crash the entire system (with MPU support) |

### 1.4 Task Control Block (TCB) — The Task's "Identity Card"

The kernel allocates a **Task Control Block** (sometimes called *Process Control Block* in desktop OS literature) for every created task. It is a static or dynamically allocated data structure that persists for the lifetime of the task.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard TCB Fields in uC/OS-II / FreeRTOS-style RTOS:**
> - `TaskID` / `TaskName` — unique identifier
> - `TaskPriority` — static or dynamic priority value
> - `TaskState` — current lifecycle state
> - `StackPointer` (SP) — top of the task's private stack
> - `StackBase` / `StackSize` — base address and depth of stack region
> - `StackOverflowMarker` — for stack-limit checking
> - `DelayTicks` — for `vTaskDelay()` / `OSTimeDly()` operations
> - `EventListPtr` — pointer to events (semaphores, queues) the task is blocked on
> - `TaskFunction` / `EntryPoint` — pointer to the task's C function

**Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU High-Yield):**

- **Stack Word Size** (32-bit architecture): **4 bytes**
- **Typical RTOS Tick Rate**: **10 ms** (configurable, 1 ms – 100 ms)
- **Minimum Number of Tasks in an Embedded Application**: **≥ 2** (Idle task is mandatory in most RTOS)
- **Maximum Priority Levels** (uC/OS-II): **64**; (FreeRTOS): **unlimited (configMAX_PRIORITIES)**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Task Lifecycle — The Five Canonical States

A task, from creation to termination, traverses a well-defined **Finite State Machine (FSM)**. Although the exact state names differ between RTOS implementations, the KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following five states:

1. **Dormant (or Deleted) State**
   - The task has been created and its TCB exists, but the task is not yet activated. Equivalent to "task code present in memory but not part of the ready queue." Some kernels use this state for tasks that have called `OSTaskDel()` / `vTaskDelete()`.

2. **Ready State**
   - The task is eligible to run, and the scheduler may select it at the next context switch. It is placed in the **Ready Queue** indexed by priority.

3. **Running State**
   - Exactly **one task per CPU core** can be in this state at any instant. The task currently owns the CPU and its instructions are being executed.

4. **Blocked (or Waiting) State**
   - The task voluntarily relinquishes the CPU because it is waiting on a kernel event — a semaphore, mutex, message queue, or a timeout (`vTaskDelay()`). Blocked tasks are NOT in the ready queue.

5. **Suspended State (optional in some kernels)**
   - The task is forcibly taken out of scheduling by an explicit call (`vTaskSuspend()`). It will NOT be scheduled even if its priority is highest — explicit `vTaskResume()` is required.

### 2.2 Why Are There So Many States? The Engineering Rationale

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind state partitioning:**
> - Keeping a task **Ready** costs scheduler overhead during every tick.
> - If a task is waiting for slow I/O (e.g., ADC conversion, UART byte arrival), placing it in the **Blocked** state frees the CPU for higher-priority work — this is the **core principle of preemptive RTOS efficiency**.
> - **Suspended** is used for **graceful degradation**: a faulty sensor task can be suspended without deleting it, allowing field-recovery without reboot.

### 2.3 Task Creation — The Operational Sequence

The generic call pattern in C is:

```c
TaskHandle_t xHandle;
xTaskCreate( vTaskFunction,       // Task entry point (function pointer)
             "NAME",              // Human-readable name
             STACK_DEPTH,         // Stack size in words
             pvParameters,        // Optional argument
             PRIORITY,            // Priority (0 = lowest)
             &xHandle );          // Optional handle return
```

**Step-by-step internal operations performed by the kernel:**

1. **Allocate TCB** from the task pool or heap.
2. **Allocate stack** of size `STACK_DEPTH × word_size`.
3. **Initialize the stack** to look as if the task has been preempted immediately after a context-restore — i.e., the stack is *pre-loaded* with a dummy interrupt frame (PC, LR, PSR, R0–R12, etc.).
4. **Set priority** in the TCB.
5. **Set state** to *Ready*.
6. **Insert into the Ready List** (typically an array of bit-mapped doubly-linked lists indexed by priority).
7. **Trigger a scheduler pass** — if newly created task's priority > current task's priority, an immediate context switch occurs (preemptive).

### 2.4 Task Termination / Deletion

A task self-terminates or is terminated by another task. The kernel performs:
- Removal from Ready/Blocked list.
- TCB and stack memory freed (if dynamic) or marked invalid.
- Scheduler re-evaluates highest-priority Ready task.

> [!WARNING]
> In hard real-time systems, a task **must not** self-delete silently, because other tasks may hold pointers to its TCB or queue items. KTU examiners will penalize unsafe deletion patterns.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Variables & Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization (U)** | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $C_i$ = Worst-case execution time (WCET) in µs; $T_i$ = task period in µs |
| 2 | **Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound** | $U_{bound} = n\left(2^{\,1/n} - 1\right)$ | $n$ = number of tasks. Limit ≈ **69.3 %** as $n \to \infty$ |
| 3 | **Liu & Layland Bound (Hard RT)** | Schedulable if $U \le n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Applies to **independent, preemptive** tasks with $D_i = T_i$ |
| 4 | **Response Time Equation** | $R_i = C_i + I_i$ where $I_i = \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | $I_i$ = interference from higher-priority tasks |
| 5 | **Hard Deadline Constraint** | $D_i \le T_i$ (most RTOS assumption) | $D_i$ = relative deadline; $T_i$ = period |
| 6 | **Hard Real-Time Schedulability (exact)** | $R_i \le D_i$ for all $i$ | Recursive iteration: start $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ |
| 7 | **Context Switch Time** | $T_{cs} \approx 2 \cdot t_{save} + t_{restore} + t_{scheduler}$ | Typically **5 µs – 50 µs** in commercial RTOS |
| 8 | **Jitter (Task Release)** | $J_i = R_i^{max} - R_i^{min}$ | Ideally **0** for deterministic tasks |
| 9 | **Throughput** | $\Theta = \dfrac{\text{useful work}}{\text{total time}}$ | Bound by context switch overhead |
| 10 | **Priority Inversion Time (worst case)** | $T_{pi} = T_{cs} \cdot k$ | $k$ = number of lower-priority tasks holding resource |

> **Note on Notation (KTU Convention):** $hp(i)$ denotes the set of tasks with priority *higher* than task $i$. The ceiling function $\lceil \cdot \rceil$ rounds up to the nearest integer since a higher-priority task can preempt multiple times.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU (Engine Control Unit)**: Crank-shaft position sensing task has period 1 ms with the highest priority; dashboard update task has period 100 ms with lower priority.
- **Medical Ventilator**: Pressure sensor task must complete within 5 ms (hard deadline) — Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) provides mathematical guarantees.
- **Drone Flight Controller**: IMU read task @ 1 kHz, GPS read task @ 10 Hz, telemetry TX task @ 50 Hz — all scheduled by a preemptive priority RTOS such as FreeRTOS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Utilization Bound (Liu & Layland, 1973)

**Statement to prove:** For $n$ independent, periodic tasks scheduled by fixed-priority Rate Monotonic Scheduling (shorter period = higher priority), a sufficient condition for schedulability is:

$$U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \;\le\; n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

**Step 1 — Worst-Case Instantaneous Utilization**

Consider the critical instant where all tasks are released simultaneously at $t = 0$. In this scenario, the processor demand is maximized.

At time $t = 0$, every task $i$ demands $C_i$ units of computation before its first deadline $T_i$.

**Step 2 — Construct the Critical Time Point**

For task $n$ (the lowest-priority task under RMS), the maximum completion time is bounded by the sum of all higher-priority tasks' worst-case interferences.

$$R_n = C_n + \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_n}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$

**Step 3 — Transform to Continuous Approximation**

For a sufficient (not exact) bound, the ceiling function is relaxed to the real-valued case:

$$R_n \;\le\; C_n + \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \left(\frac{R_n}{T_j}\right) C_j$$

Rearranging:

$$R_n - R_n \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \;\le\; C_n$$

$$R_n \left(1 - \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \frac{C_j}{T_j}\right) \;\le\; C_n$$

$$R_n \;\le\; \frac{C_n}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} (C_j / T_j)}$$

**Step 4 — Apply Hard Deadline Constraint**

For task $n$ to be schedulable: $R_n \le T_n$.

$$\frac{C_n}{1 - \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} (C_j / T_j)} \;\le\; T_n$$

$$\frac{C_n}{T_n} \;\le\; 1 - \sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \frac{C_j}{T_j}$$

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n-1} \frac{C_j}{T_j} + \frac{C_n}{T_n} \;\le\; 1$$

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \;\le\; 1$$

**Step 5 — Minimize Over All Priority Assignments**

Liu & Layland proved that the **worst-case** priority assignment minimizing the upper bound is RMS (shorter period = higher priority). The least upper bound of $U$ for RMS is obtained by:

$$U_{RMS}^{bound}(n) = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$$

**Step 6 — Compute Asymptotic Limit**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) = \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$$

Hence the **theoretical ceiling** for RMS is **69.31 %**, even though actual schedulable utilization often reaches ~88 % empirically.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problem — Response Time Calculation

**Problem:** Consider three tasks scheduled by RMS on a single-core RTOS:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | WCET $C_i$ (ms) | Priority (RMS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| $\tau_1$ | 10 | 2 | High |
| $\tau_2$ | 30 | 4 | Mid |
| $\tau_3$ | 40 | 10 | Low |

Determine the worst-case response time of $\tau_3$.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify higher-priority set**

$$hp(3) = \{1, 2\}$$

**Step 2 — Initialize response time iteration**

$$R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 10 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 3 — Iteration 1**

$$R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^{(0)}}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{10}{30} \right\rceil (4)$$

$$R_3^{(1)} = 10 + (1)(2) + (1)(4) = 16 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 4 — Iteration 2**

$$R_3^{(2)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{16}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{16}{30} \right\rceil (4)$$

$$R_3^{(2)} = 10 + (2)(2) + (1)(4) = 18 \text{ ms}$$

**Step 5 — Iteration 3**

$$R_3^{(3)} = 10 + \left\lceil \frac{18}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{18}{30} \right\rceil (4)$$

$$R_3^{(3)} = 10 + (2)(2) + (1)(4) = 18 \text{ ms}$$

**Convergence reached.** $R_3 = 18$ ms.

**Step 6 — Schedulability Check**

$$R_3 = 18 \text{ ms} \;\le\; D_3 = T_3 = 40 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

**Total Utilization:**

$$U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{30} + \frac{10}{40} = 0.2 + 0.1333 + 0.25 = 0.5833 \;=\; 58.33\%$$

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \approx 0.7797 \;=\; 77.97\%$$

Since $0.5833 < 0.7797$, the task set is schedulable by RMS sufficient condition. $\blacksquare$

### 3.3 Context Switch Time Derivation

**Step 1 — Trigger Event**
When the scheduler decides to preempt the currently running task $A$ in favor of higher-priority task $B$, a **context switch** is initiated.

**Step 2 — Save Context of A**

The hardware/CPU pushes general-purpose registers, program counter, and link register onto task A's stack. The stack pointer of A is saved into A's TCB.

**Step 3 — Restore Context of B**

The kernel reads the stack pointer from B's TCB, restores all registers, and jumps to the saved PC of B.

**Step 4 — Total Cost**

$$T_{cs} = T_{save} + T_{scheduler} + T_{restore}$$

For an ARM Cortex-M3 @ 72 MHz, typical values:
- $T_{save} \approx 12$ cycles
- $T_{scheduler} \approx 30$ cycles
- $T_{restore} \approx 12$ cycles
- $T_{cs} \approx 54$ cycles $\approx 0.75$ µs

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU numerical problems, use $T_{cs}$ values from the problem statement. Do not assume a single universal value.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Task States vs. Kernel Operations

| Operation | Dormant → Ready | Ready → Running | Running → Ready | Running → Blocked | Blocked → Ready |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Trigger** | `xTaskCreate()` | Scheduler pick | Preemption / time-slice expiry | `vTaskDelay()`, `xSemaphoreTake()` (unavailable) | Event signaled / timeout |
| **Stack Effect** | Pre-loaded with dummy frame | SP loaded from TCB | SP saved to TCB | SP saved to TCB | SP remains valid |
| **TCB Update** | State = Ready; Linked into ready list | State = Running | State = Ready; Linked back | State = Blocked; Linked into event list | State = Ready; Removed from event list |
| **Scheduler Pass** | YES (if higher prio) | YES | — | — | YES (re-evaluate) |
| **Context Switch** | Possible | YES | YES | NO (task voluntarily yields) | Possible |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Task State Transition Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> dormantA : xTaskCreate()
    dormantA --> readyA : Task Activated
    readyA --> runningA : Scheduler Dispatch
    runningA --> readyA : Preempted by Higher Priority Task
    runningA --> blockedA : vTaskDelay or xSemaphoreTake unavailable
    runningA --> suspendedA : vTaskSuspend call
    blockedA --> readyA : Event Signaled or Timeout Expired
    suspendedA --> readyA : vTaskResume call
    runningA --> dormantA : vTaskDelete self delete
    blockedA --> dormantA : vTaskDelete from another task
    suspendedA --> dormantA : vTaskDelete from another task
    readyA --> dormantA : vTaskDelete from another task
    runningA --> [*] : System Reset or Scheduler Halt
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- All transitions are governed by kernel API calls or hardware interrupts.
- A task in the `readyA` state is a candidate for execution but currently not selected.
- Only one task can be in `runningA` on a single-core CPU.
- Transitions to `dormantA` from any state imply memory release and TCB invalidation.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Task Control Block (TCB) Internal Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef field fill:#E8F4FD,stroke:#1F4E79,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef ptr fill:#FFF4E6,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    tcbTitle["TASK CONTROL BLOCK TCB"]:::field
    fieldA["TaskName CHAR array 16 bytes"]:::field
    fieldB["TaskPriority UINT8"]:::field
    fieldC["TaskState ENUM Ready Running Blocked Suspended Dormant"]:::field
    fieldD["StackBase VOID pointer"]:::field
    fieldE["StackSize UINT32 in words"]:::field
    fieldF["StackPointer VOID pointer saved SP"]:::field
    fieldG["DelayTicks UINT32 for vTaskDelay"]:::field
    fieldH["EventListPtr pointer to sema or queue"]:::ptr
    fieldI["TaskFunction pointer to task entry function"]:::ptr
    fieldJ["NextPtr pointer to next TCB in list"]:::ptr
    fieldK["PrevPtr pointer to previous TCB in list"]:::ptr
    fieldL["StackOverflowGuard canary word"]:::field

    tcbTitle --> fieldA
    fieldA --> fieldB
    fieldB --> fieldC
    fieldC --> fieldD
    fieldD --> fieldE
    fieldE --> fieldF
    fieldF --> fieldG
    fieldG --> fieldH
    fieldH --> fieldI
    fieldI --> fieldJ
    fieldJ --> fieldK
    fieldK --> fieldL
    fieldL --> outID([End of TCB Structure])

    outID:::field
```

**Interpretation:**
- Pointer-type fields (orange) are stored separately to emphasize that they must be initialized BEFORE the task is inserted into the ready list.
- `StackOverflowGuard` is a debugging feature used to detect memory corruption by checking for a fixed magic word (commonly `0xDEADBEEF` or `0xAA55AA55`) at the bottom of the stack.
- The list pointers (`NextPtr`/`PrevPtr`) form a doubly-linked list, allowing O(1) insertion and removal from the ready/pending/event lists.

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Task Lifecycle in a Preemptive RTOS

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef user fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,color:#000
    classDef kern fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#F1F8E9,stroke:#33691E,color:#000
    classDef io fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,color:#000

    appLayer["Application Layer Task Code"]:::user
    apiLayer["RTOS API Layer xTaskCreate vTaskDelay xQueueReceive"]:::kern
    schLayer["Scheduler Fixed Priority Round Robin EDF"]:::kern
    tcbLayer["TCB Pool Ready List Blocked List Event List"]:::kern
    ctxLayer["Context Switch Engine Save Restore Registers"]:::kern
    hwLayer["CPU Core ARM Cortex M3 M4 M7"]:::hw
    intLayer["SysTick Interrupt Hardware Timer"]:::hw
    ioLayer["UART ADC GPIO I2C SPI Peripherals"]:::io

    appLayer -->|invokes| apiLayer
    apiLayer -->|manipulates| tcbLayer
    tcbLayer -->|consulted by| schLayer
    schLayer -->|triggers| ctxLayer
    ctxLayer -->|loads SP and PC into| hwLayer
    intLayer -->|tick interrupt every 1 to 10 ms| schLayer
    apiLayer -->|blocking calls pause task| ioLayer
    ioLayer -->|ISR signals event| apiLayer
    ctxLayer -.->|saves state to| tcbLayer
```

**Engineering Insight from the Architecture:**
- The **SysTick Interrupt** is the heartbeat of the RTOS — it periodically interrupts the CPU to invoke the scheduler.
- The **Scheduler** never executes application code directly; it only manipulates the **TCB Pool** and triggers the **Context Switch Engine**.
- The **Application Layer** is unaware of hardware context — it only calls API functions.
