## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question 1]**
> **Q: Differentiate between multiprocessing and multitasking in the context of embedded systems. Identify the architectural layer (hardware vs. software) at which each concept primarily operates.**

**Model Answer:**
Multiprocessing is a **hardware-level architectural concept** in which a system contains two or more processing elements (cores, DSPs, FPGAs) that can execute instruction streams in physical parallel. Multitasking is a **software-level operating-system concept** in which a single processing element executes multiple software tasks in a time-interleaved manner via a scheduler. Multiprocessing uses spatial parallelism; multitasking uses temporal concurrency. A real system frequently combines both: multiple cores, each running an OS instance that itself multitasks several threads. (3 marks)

---

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question 2]**
> **Q: What is priority inversion? How does the Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) eliminate it in a real-time RTOS?**

**Model Answer:**
Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly in which a higher-priority task is **indirectly preempted** by a lower-priority task, effectively "inverting" the intended priority order, because the lower-priority task holds a shared resource (mutex) that the higher-priority task needs. The unbounded form, known as the **unbounded priority inversion problem** (famously caused the Mars Pathfinder reset in 1997), is resolved using the Priority Ceiling Protocol. In PCP, every mutex is statically assigned a *ceiling priority* equal to the highest priority of any task that may lock it. A task may lock the mutex only if no other task is currently holding a mutex with a *higher or equal* ceiling priority. This protocol prevents deadlocks and bounds the blocking time of any task $i$ to the duration of at most **one** critical section of any lower-priority task, eliminating chained blocking. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]`

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** algorithm. State its assumptions and derive Liu & Layland's utilization bound. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Consider the following task set for an automotive ECU:
>
> $$\tau_1 = (C_1=2, T_1=5), \quad \tau_2 = (C_2=2, T_2=10), \quad \tau_3 = (C_3=4, T_3=20)$$
>
> (i) Compute total CPU utilization.
> (ii) Test schedulability using Liu & Layland bound.
> (iii) Perform exact response-time analysis.
> (iv) If task $\tau_3$ is replaced with $\tau_3' = (C_3=5, T_3=20)$, does the set remain schedulable? Justify. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — RMS Algorithm and Derivation:**

Rate Monotonic Scheduling is a **fixed-priority preemptive** algorithm. The scheduling rule is: *the task with the shortest period receives the highest priority*. If two tasks share the same period, the tie-breaker is arbitrary (often by ID).

**Assumptions of Liu & Layland (1973):**
1. All tasks are periodic.
2. Deadline equals period: $D_i = T_i$ (implicit deadline).
3. Tasks are independent (no shared resources, no precedence).
4. Worst-case execution time $C_i$ is constant and known.
5. Context switch time is zero.
6. Single processor.

**Derivation Sketch:**
- The critical instant of task $i$ occurs when all higher-priority tasks release a job simultaneously with task $i$.
- The processor demand function is $W_i(t) = \sum_{j=1}^{i} C_j \lceil t/T_j \rceil$.
- For schedulability, the smallest $t$ where $W_i(t) = t$ must satisfy $t \leq T_i$.
- Using the worst-case harmonic period configuration $T_j = T_i / 2^{(i-j)/n}$ and maximizing over the period ratio, one obtains the **Liu & Layland bound**: $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i / T_i \leq n (2^{1/n} - 1)$.
- This bound is **sufficient** (passes ⇒ schedulable) but not necessary (fails ⇒ inconclusive).

For $n=1, 2, 3, \infty$, the bound evaluates to $1.000$, $0.828$, $0.780$, and $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ respectively.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Analysis:**

**Step (i) — Total CPU Utilization:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U &= \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \\
  &= \frac{2}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{20} \\
  &= 0.400 + 0.200 + 0.200 \\
  &= 0.800
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step (ii) — Liu & Layland Bound Test:**

For $n=3$: $U_{bound} = 3 \cdot (2^{1/3} - 1) = 0.7798$.

Since $U = 0.800 > 0.7798$, the **bound test is inconclusive**. We must resort to exact analysis.

**Step (iii) — Exact Response-Time Analysis:**

For $\tau_1$ (no higher-priority interference):

$$
R_1 = C_1 = 2
$$

For $\tau_2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_2^{(0)} &= 2 \\
R_2^{(1)} &= 2 + \lceil 2/5 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 \cdot 2 = 4 \\
R_2^{(2)} &= 2 + \lceil 4/5 \rceil \cdot 2 = 2 + 1 \cdot 2 = 4 \quad (\text{converged})
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_2 = 4 \leq D_2 = 10$ ✓

For $\tau_3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^{(0)} &= 4 \\
R_3^{(1)} &= 4 + \lceil 4/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 4/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 2 + 2 = 8 \\
R_3^{(2)} &= 4 + \lceil 8/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 8/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 4 + 2 = 10 \\
R_3^{(3)} &= 4 + \lceil 10/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 10/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 4 + 4 + 2 = 10 \quad (\text{converged})
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_3 = 10 \leq D_3 = 20$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The task set **is schedulable** under RMS, even though it violates the Liu & Layland sufficient bound.

**Step (iv) — Modified Task Set with $\tau_3' = (5, 20)$:**

New utilization:

$$
U' = \frac{2}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{5}{20} = 0.400 + 0.200 + 0.250 = 0.850
$$

Even higher, bound still inconclusive. We re-run the iteration for $\tau_3'$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3'^{(0)} &= 5 \\
R_3'^{(1)} &= 5 + \lceil 5/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 5/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 5 + 2 + 2 = 9 \\
R_3'^{(2)} &= 5 + \lceil 9/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 9/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 5 + 4 + 2 = 11 \\
R_3'^{(3)} &= 5 + \lceil 11/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 11/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 5 + 6 + 4 = 15 \\
R_3'^{(4)} &= 5 + \lceil 15/5 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 15/10 \rceil \cdot 2 = 5 + 6 + 4 = 15 \quad (\text{converged})
\end{aligned}
$$

$R_3' = 15 \leq D_3 = 20$ ✓

**Conclusion:** The modified task set is **still schedulable** under RMS.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[Alternative Question]`

> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of a Symmetric Multiprocessing (SMP)** system. Discuss cache coherency, the MESI protocol, and inter-processor interrupts (IPI). **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Compare **preemptive** and **cooperative multitasking** in terms of determinism, latency, and CPU overhead. Implement a FreeRTOS-style pseudo-code for two cooperating tasks using a counting semaphore to synchronize a producer-consumer buffer. **(7 marks)**

##### **Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — SMP Architecture:**

In SMP, $N$ identical processor cores share:
- A unified main memory (DDR via a coherent interconnect like ARM CCI or Intel QPI).
- A symmetric I/O subsystem.
- A single OS kernel image and runqueue.

```
   +-------+   +-------+   +-------+
   | CPU 0 |   | CPU 1 | ... | CPU k |
   | L1 D  |   | L1 D  |     | L1 D  |
   | L1 I  |   | L1 I  |     | L1 I  |
   +---+---+   +---+---+     +---+---+
       |           |             |
   +---+---+   +---+---+     +---+---+
   |  L2   |   |  L2   |     |  L2   |
   +---+---+   +---+---+     +---+---+
       \           |             /
        +----------+-------------+
                   |
          +--------+--------+
          |  Coherent Bus    |  (e.g., ARM CCN, Intel QPI)
          |   / Interconnect |
          +--------+--------+
                   |
       +-----------+-----------+
       |                       |
   +---+---+               +---+---+
   |  L3   |               | Main  |
   | Cache |               | Memory|
   +-------+               +-------+
```

**Cache Coherency — MESI Protocol:** Each cache line carries a 2-bit state tag:
- **M**odified: This core has the only valid copy; main memory is stale.
- **E**xclusive: This core has the only valid copy; main memory is identical.
- **S**hared: Multiple cores may hold valid copies; main memory is identical.
- **I**nvalid: This cache line is not valid in this core.

A *snooping* or *directory-based* mechanism broadcasts memory transactions on the coherent bus; cores transition states accordingly (e.g., on a write to a Shared line, all other cores invalidate their copy, transitioning the writer to Modified — a "cache line bounce").

**Inter-Processor Interrupts (IPI):** Special interrupt lines or message-passing registers allow one core to trigger an interrupt on another core. In Linux, IPIs are used for:
- TLB shootdown (forcing all cores to flush stale translations after a page-table update).
- Scheduler load balancing (a rebalance tick).
- CPU hotplug notifications.
- Reschedule IPI (force another core to call the scheduler).

---

**Part (b) — Preemptive vs. Cooperative & Producer-Consumer Pseudo-Code:**

**Comparison Table:**

| Property | Preemptive | Cooperative |
|---|---|---|
| **Determinism** | High (RTOS-controlled ticks) | Low (depends on task cooperation) |
| **Worst-case latency** | Bounded by tick period | Unbounded (rogue task can starve) |
| **CPU overhead** | Higher (context switch) | Lower (no forced switches) |
| **Fault isolation** | Better (kernel can preempt runaway task) | Worse (one stuck task freezes system) |
| **Implementation complexity** | Higher (requires MPU/SysTick) | Lower (bare-metal friendly) |

**Pseudo-code: Producer-Consumer with Counting Semaphore (FreeRTOS Style):**

```c
/* Shared resources */
SemaphoreHandle_t xCountSem;   /* counts filled slots */
SemaphoreHandle_t xSpaceSem;   /* counts free slots */
SemaphoreHandle_t xMutex;      /* guards the buffer */
QueueHandle_t    xBuffer;

/* Producer Task */
void vProducerTask(void *pvParams) {
    int item;
    while (1) {
        item = produce();                 /* generate data */
        xSemaphoreTake(xSpaceSem, portMAX_DELAY); /* wait for free slot */
        xSemaphoreTake(xMutex,    portMAX_DELAY);
        xQueueSend(xBuffer, &item, 0);
        xSemaphoreGive(xMutex);
        xSemaphoreGive(xCountSem);         /* signal consumer */
    }
}

/* Consumer Task */
void vConsumerTask(void *pvParams) {
    int item;
    while (1) {
        xSemaphoreTake(xCountSem, portMAX_DELAY); /* wait for data */
        xSemaphoreTake(xMutex,     portMAX_DELAY);
        xQueueReceive(xBuffer, &item, 0);
        xSemaphoreGive(xMutex);
        xSemaphoreGive(xSpaceSem);          /* signal producer */
        process(item);                      /* consume data */
    }
}

/* Main initialization */
int main(void) {
    xBuffer   = xQueueCreate(BUFFER_SIZE, sizeof(int));
    xCountSem = xSemaphoreCreateCounting(BUFFER_SIZE, 0);
    xSpaceSem = xSemaphoreCreateCounting(BUFFER_SIZE, BUFFER_SIZE);
    xMutex    = xSemaphoreCreateMutex();

    xTaskCreate(vProducerTask, "Prod", 1024, NULL, 2, NULL);
    xTaskCreate(vConsumerTask, "Cons", 1024, NULL, 1, NULL);
    vTaskStartScheduler();
    for (;;);
}
```

This pattern guarantees **no busy waiting**, **mutual exclusion** on the shared buffer, and **deterministic blocking semantics** — the producer blocks if the buffer is full; the consumer blocks if the buffer is empty.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Scheme Valuation:**
>
> 1. **Confusing multiprocessing with multitasking** (1–2 marks lost in any Part A question). Always anchor your answer with the words "hardware-level" vs. "software-level" parallelism.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the implicit deadline assumption** ($D_i = T_i$) when applying the Liu & Layland bound. If the question gives $D_i \neq T_i$, the RMS bound is **not directly applicable** — you must use the generalized response-time analysis.
>
> 3. **In the response-time iteration, students often forget to iterate until convergence**. Marks are awarded only when you show two consecutive equal $R_i$ values. A single iteration that "looks safe" is not acceptable.
>
> 4. **RMS is *not* optimal** for arbitrary task sets. EDF achieves full utilization ($U \leq 1$). Failing to mention this comparative point costs the "discussion" marks in Part B.
>
> 5. **In SMP questions, omitting cache coherency** is a critical gap. The examiner expects at least the MESI acronym and a one-line description.
>
> 6. **For AMP systems**, students frequently forget to mention the **lack of cache coherency** and the resulting need for software-managed shared regions (e.g., RPMsg, mailbox registers).
>
> 7. **Pseudo-code in Part B must be syntactically clean**; sloppy indentation or missing semicolons/mutex-take gives-are flagged.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> 📌 **Rapid Revision Checklist**

- ☐ **Multiprocessing = hardware parallelism** (multiple CPUs/cores). **Multitasking = software concurrency** (one CPU, many tasks).
- ☐ **SMP**: identical cores, single OS, cache-coherent; **AMP**: heterogeneous cores, isolated OS instances, often no HW coherency; **BMP**: hybrid with task pinning.
- ☐ **RMS priority rule**: shorter period $\Rightarrow$ higher priority. **EDF priority rule**: closer absolute deadline $\Rightarrow$ higher priority.
- ☐ **Liu & Layland bound**: $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$. Sufficient but **not necessary**. For large $n$, bound tends to $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- ☐ **EDF utilization bound**: $U \leq 1$ — both necessary and sufficient.
- ☐ **Response-time formula**: $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$. Iterate from $R_i^{(0)} = C_i$ until convergence.
- ☐ **Context switch overhead**: $T_{ctx} = 2T_{save} + 2T_{load} + T_{sched}$. Must be a small fraction of the tick period.
- ☐ **Priority inversion** is resolved by **Priority Inheritance** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.
- ☐ **Counting semaphores** decouple producers and consumers; **mutexes** protect critical sections.
- ☐ **MESI** states: Modified, Exclusive, Shared, Invalid — the foundation of cache coherency in SMP.
- ☐ **IPI** (Inter-Processor Interrupts) enable one core to force a reschedule or TLB shootdown on another core.
- ☐ **Hyperperiod** = $\text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ — useful for full-system simulation up to steady state.
- ☐ **Jitter** = $T_{response}^{max} - T_{response}^{min}$ — critical for control-loop stability and audio/video quality.
- ☐ **Standard exam keywords** to memorize verbatim: "determinism," "bounded blocking," "sporadic tasks," "aperiodic servers," "spinning vs. blocking," "temporal/spatial partitioning," "mixed-criticality."
- ☐ For any KTU 2024 numerical, **show all iteration steps in a table format**; examiners award partial marks for visible convergence logic.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Scheduling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Task Scheduling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Task Scheduling** in real-time embedded systems is defined as the mechanism by which the operating system (or Real-Time Operating System, RTOS) determines the *order of execution* and *allocation of processor time* among a set of concurrent tasks, subject to predefined timing constraints such as **deadlines**, **periods**, and **worst-case execution times (WCET)**.

In the context of the **KTU PECPT861 – Advanced Embedded Systems** syllabus (Module 3), task scheduling is studied under the broader framework of **Real-Time Systems Theory**, where every task $\tau_i$ is characterized by the temporal tuple:

$$
\tau_i = (C_i, \; T_i, \; D_i, \; P_i)
$$

where:
- $C_i$ = **Worst-Case Execution Time (WCET)** of task $\tau_i$
- $T_i$ = **Period** of task $\tau_i$ (inter-arrival time of successive jobs)
- $D_i$ = **Relative Deadline** of task $\tau_i$
- $P_i$ = **Static Priority** assigned to task $\tau_i$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Scheduling is classified by:
> 1. **Timing constraints** → Hard / Firm / Soft real-time
> 2. **Preemptivity** → Preemptive / Non-preemptive
> 3. **Priority assignment** → Static (fixed) / Dynamic (runtime)
> 4. **Processor model** → Uni-processor / Multi-processor scheduling

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Air Traffic Controller" Intuition

Imagine a **single runway** at a busy airport (the **CPU**), and multiple aircraft (the **tasks**) requesting to land. Each aircraft has:

- A **fuel/buffer time** (WCET) — how long it will occupy the runway
- A **scheduled arrival window** (Period) — how frequently it appears
- A **hard landing deadline** (Deadline) — must land before this time, or crash!

The **Air Traffic Controller** is the **Scheduler**. The controller must guarantee (or *try to guarantee*) that **no aircraft misses its landing slot** → analogous to **no task misses its deadline**.

If the controller uses:
- **Strict order based on aircraft size** → **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**
- **Closest deadline gets priority** → **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**
- **FIFO landing** → **First-Come-First-Served (FCFS)**

This analogy makes scheduling *immediately intuitive* for first-time learners.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Real-Time Tasks

> [!NOTE]
> **Periodic Tasks** — release jobs at regular intervals $T_i$ (e.g., sensor sampling every 10 ms)
> **Aperiodic Tasks** — released at irregular, unpredictable times (e.g., emergency interrupts)
> **Sporadic Tasks** — aperiodic tasks with a **minimum inter-arrival time** bound
> **Hard Real-Time** — missing a deadline = system failure (e.g., airbag deployment, anti-lock braking)
> **Soft Real-Time** — missing a deadline = degraded quality (e.g., video streaming)
> **Firm Real-Time** — late results have *zero* value but no catastrophic failure

---

## 1.4 Preemptive vs. Non-Preemptive Scheduling

| Property | Preemptive | Non-Preemptive |
|----------|------------|----------------|
| Interrupt during execution | Allowed (higher-priority task preempts) | Not allowed (task runs to completion) |
| Response time | Better, more predictable | Worse under heavy load |
| Implementation complexity | Higher (context switch overhead) | Lower |
| KTU relevance | **RMS, EDF (preemptive versions)** | Classical FCFS |

> [!WARNING]
> In KTU 2024 examinations, students often lose marks for **not specifying preemptive vs. non-preemptive assumptions** in scheduling analyses. Always declare the model explicitly.

---

## 1.5 Why Task Scheduling is Critical in Advanced Embedded Systems

Modern embedded platforms (AUTOSAR-based ECUs, RTOS-driven IoT nodes, UAV flight controllers) must coordinate **dozens to hundreds of concurrent tasks** on a single processor. Without a rigorous scheduling policy:
- Deadlines are violated → **catastrophic system failure**
- CPU is over- or under-utilized → **energy/time waste**
- No *a priori* guarantee of correctness → **unsafe product**

Thus, task scheduling provides **deterministic, mathematical guarantees** about timing behavior — a foundational requirement of any safety-critical embedded system.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Two Foundational Scheduling Algorithms

The **Liu & Layland (1973) seminal paper** established two canonical algorithms for periodic real-time task scheduling on a **single processor**:

### A. Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)
- **Static priority** policy.
- **Shorter period = higher priority**.
- Optimal among all *static-priority* algorithms.
- **Preemptive**.

### B. Earliest Deadline First (EDF)
- **Dynamic priority** policy.
- Task with the **earliest absolute deadline** gets the CPU.
- **Optimal** among all scheduling algorithms (uniprocessor).
- Can be preemptive or non-preemptive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Both RMS and EDF are *optimal* in their respective classes. RMS is optimal among static-priority algorithms; EDF is optimal among *all* algorithms on a uniprocessor.

---

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic — RMS

For a task set $\Gamma = \{\tau_1, \tau_2, \ldots, \tau_n\}$:

1. **Sort tasks** by non-increasing priority, i.e., non-decreasing period: $T_1 \leq T_2 \leq \ldots \leq T_n$.
2. **Assign priority** $P_i = \frac{1}{T_i}$ (inverse of period).
3. **At any scheduling decision point**:
   - Release the highest-priority ready task.
   - If multiple tasks are ready, choose the one with the shortest period.
4. **Preemption rule**: A newly-released higher-priority task immediately preempts the currently running lower-priority task.

> [!NOTE]
> In RMS, priorities are **fixed at design time** and **never change** at runtime → predictable, low overhead, easier certification.

---

## 2.3 Step-by-Step Operational Logic — EDF

1. **At each scheduling decision point** (task release, completion, or tick):
   - Compute the **absolute deadline** for each ready job: $d_i = r_i + D_i$ (release time + relative deadline).
2. **Select the job with the smallest $d_i$**.
3. **Preempt** the running task if a newly-released task has an earlier deadline.

EDF is **work-conserving** — the CPU is never idle while a ready task exists.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations for the KTU 2024 ESE — they appear in 90% of scheduling problems.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** for task set | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Fraction of processor time consumed |
| 2 | **Liu & Layland RMS Bound** | $U_{RMS} \leq n \left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | Sufficient (not necessary) schedulability test |
| 3 | **Liu & Layland Limit** as $n \to \infty$ | $\lim_{n \to \infty} n(2^{1/n}-1) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$ | Maximum RMS utilization in worst case |
| 4 | **EDF Schedulability** (exact) | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq 1$ | Necessary *and* sufficient on uniprocessor |
| 5 | **Hyperbolic Bound (RMS)** | $\prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(1 + \frac{C_i}{T_i}\right) \leq 2$ | Tighter than Liu-Layland for low $U$ |
| 6 | **Worst-Case Response Time (WCRT)** | $R_i^{k+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^k}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Fixed-point iteration (Joseph & Pandya) |
| 7 | **Schedulability by WCRT** | $R_i \leq D_i \;\forall i$ | Task is schedulable iff WCRT $\leq$ deadline |
| 8 | **Time Demand Function** | $w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Cumulative demand on task $i$ up to time $t$ |
| 9 | **Processor Demand Criterion** | $\forall L \in [0, L^*]: \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i}(L) \leq L$ | EDF exact test over busy period |
| 10 | **Critical Instant (RMS)** | All higher-priority tasks release simultaneously at $t=0$ | Yields worst-case response time |

> [!WARNING]
> **Notation safeguard:** In LaTeX tables above, `\lceil` and `\rceil` are ceiling brackets. Never write them as literal `|` pipes — use `\vert` or `\mid` if absolute-value delimiters are needed in tables.

---

## 2.5 The "Why" and "How" — Intuition Behind Each Formula

### 2.5.1 Why Utilization Matters
If tasks collectively demand more CPU time than 1.0 (i.e., 100%), **no scheduler can satisfy all deadlines** — it is mathematically impossible (no idle time left). So $U \leq 1$ is a *necessary* condition for *any* algorithm.

### 2.5.2 Why RMS Has a Bound Below 1.0
RMS can be **pessimal** when periods are in *harmonic relationships*. For example, 3 tasks with periods $\{1, 2, 4\}$ achieve $U = 1.0$ (perfectly schedulable). But 3 tasks with periods $\{1, \sqrt{2}, 2\}$ may fail even at $U = 0.83$. The bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ captures this worst-case scenario.

### 2.5.3 Why EDF Achieves $U \leq 1$
EDF dynamically re-prioritizes — it can adapt to deadline pressure. Mathematically, **Baruah et al. (2010)** proved EDF is optimal: a feasible schedule exists **iff** $U \leq 1$ on a uniprocessor.

### 2.5.4 WCRT Intuition
The WCRT equation models the **interference** a task suffers from higher-priority tasks. Each iteration $k$ refines the estimate: "If task $i$ must finish by time $R_i^k$, how many jobs of higher-priority task $j$ will preempt it?" The iteration converges to the **smallest fixed point**.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application | Scheduler Used |
|--------|-------------|----------------|
| Automotive ECU (AUTOSAR OS) | Engine control, ABS | OSEK/VDX with fixed priority (RMS-like) |
| Avionics (DO-178C) | Flight control, autopilot | Rate Monotonic / deadline monotonic |
| Industrial PLCs | Real-time control loops | Cyclic executives (deterministic) |
| Multimedia (soft RT) | Audio/video playback | EDF or CBS |
| Spacecraft (NASA CORE) | Telemetry, attitude control | Time-triggered + RMS hybrid |
| Robotics (ROS 2) | Sensor fusion, motor control | Mixed: rate-based + dynamic |

> [!NOTE]
> In **certification-critical** domains (avionics, medical), **static-priority RMS** dominates because of its **analysability and predictability** — auditors need ironclad timing proofs.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Liu & Layland's RMS Utilization Bound

### Statement
For $n$ independent, preemptable periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$ scheduled under RMS on a uniprocessor, a sufficient schedulability condition is:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the critical instant.**  
For task $\tau_k$, the *critical instant* is the time at which the **worst-case response time** occurs. Liu & Layland proved that under RMS, the critical instant is when $\tau_k$ is released **simultaneously with all higher-priority tasks** at time $t = 0$.

*Conversion logic:* Higher-priority tasks release jobs most frequently at $t=0$, maximizing preemption pressure on $\tau_k$.

**Step 2 — Compute the response time demand.**  
The total computation demanded by $\tau_k$ and all higher-priority tasks $\tau_j$ (with $j < k$) in the interval $[0, t]$ is:

$$
W_k(t) = C_k + \sum_{j=1}^{k-1} \left\lceil \frac{t}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

*Conversion logic:* Each $\lceil t/T_j \rceil$ counts the number of jobs of $\tau_j$ that arrive in $[0, t]$.

**Step 3 — Solve for the smallest $t$ such that $W_k(t) \leq t$.**  
The task $\tau_k$ is schedulable if the processor demand does not exceed available time. The worst case occurs when $W_k(t) = t$.

**Step 4 — Use the largest value of $C_j$ in the worst case.**  
Liu & Layland bound $C_j$ by the *minimum* possible period for the highest-priority task, then iteratively use the relation:

$$
\frac{C_j}{T_j} \leq \left(2^{1/k} - 1\right) \quad \text{for the } k\text{-th task}
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply the per-task bound over all $n$ tasks.**  
A geometric product bound yields:

$$
U \leq \prod_{i=1}^{n} \left(2^{1/i} - 1 + 1\right) - 1
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify the product.**  
Telescoping the product $\prod_{i=1}^{n} (2^{1/i})$ via exponential identity:

$$
\prod_{i=1}^{n} 2^{1/i} = 2^{\sum_{i=1}^{n} 1/i}
$$

**Step 7 — Bound the harmonic sum.**  
Using the inequality $\sum_{i=1}^{n} 1/i \leq \ln(2n) + 1$ (with tighter bounding):

$$
U_{max} = n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

**Step 8 — Take the limit as $n \to \infty$.**  

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{n \to \infty} n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) &= \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{2^{1/n} - 1}{1/n}
\end{aligned}
$$

Apply L'Hôpital's rule by setting $x = 1/n \to 0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^{x} - 1}{x} &= \lim_{x \to 0} \frac{2^x \ln 2}{1} = \ln 2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Final result:**

$$
\boxed{U_{RMS} \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right) \quad \xrightarrow{n \to \infty} \quad \ln 2 \approx 0.6931}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Worst-Case Response Time (WCRT) — Joseph & Pandya

### Statement
For a task $\tau_i$ with higher-priority task set $hp(i)$:

$$
R_i^{k+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^k}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

The WCRT $R_i$ is the smallest $R_i^k$ satisfying $R_i^{k+1} = R_i^k$ AND $R_i \leq D_i$.

### Step-by-Step Algorithm (Worked Procedure)

**Step 1 — Initialize.** $R_i^0 = C_i$.

**Step 2 — Iterate.** Compute $R_i^{k+1}$ using the formula above.

**Step 3 — Termination condition.** Stop when either:
- $R_i^{k+1} = R_i^k$ (fixed point reached), OR
- $R_i^{k+1} > D_i$ (deadline missed — task unschedulable).

**Step 4 — Decision.** If $R_i \leq D_i$, $\tau_i$ is schedulable.

---

### Worked Example (Numerical)

**Task set:**

| Task | $C_i$ (ms) | $T_i$ (ms) | $D_i$ (ms) |
|------|-----------|-----------|-----------|
| $\tau_1$ | 1 | 4 | 4 |
| $\tau_2$ | 2 | 6 | 6 |
| $\tau_3$ | 3 | 10 | 10 |

Priority order: $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ (shorter period = higher priority in RMS).

**Compute WCRT for $\tau_3$ (lowest priority):**

Iteration $k = 0$: $R_3^0 = C_3 = 3$ ms.

Iteration $k = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^1 &= C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^0}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^0}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 \\
      &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{3}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{3}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
      &= 3 + (1)(1) + (1)(2) \\
      &= 6 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Iteration $k = 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^2 &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{6}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{6}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
      &= 3 + (2)(1) + (1)(2) \\
      &= 7 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Iteration $k = 3$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^3 &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{7}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{7}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
      &= 3 + (2)(1) + (2)(2) \\
      &= 9 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Iteration $k = 4$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^4 &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{9}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
      &= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) \\
      &= 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

Iteration $k = 5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^5 &= 3 + \left\lceil \frac{10}{4} \right\rceil (1) + \left\lceil \frac{10}{6} \right\rceil (2) \\
      &= 3 + (3)(1) + (2)(2) \\
      &= 10 \text{ ms}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fixed point reached:** $R_3 = 10$ ms $= D_3$. ✅ **Schedulable (just barely).**

**Check utilization:**

$$
U = \frac{1}{4} + \frac{2}{6} + \frac{3}{10} = 0.25 + 0.333 + 0.30 = 0.883
$$

Liu-Layland bound for $n=3$: $3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798$.

$U = 0.883 > 0.7798$ → **Liu-Layland test fails**, but task set is **still schedulable** (proved by WCRT test). This illustrates the **conservatism** of the Liu-Layland bound and the **tightness** of the WCRT test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: EDF Processor Demand Criterion

For a task set with deadlines $D_i \leq T_i$, define the **processor demand** in an interval $[0, L]$:

$$
g(0, L) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left\lfloor \frac{L + T_i - D_i}{T_i} \right\rfloor C_i
$$

A task set is EDF-schedulable **iff** there exists $L \in [0, L^*]$ (where $L^* = \max\{D_1, \ldots, D_n\}$ plus the busy-period bound) such that:

$$
g(0, L) \leq L
$$

*Conversion logic:* The processor must satisfy cumulative demand within any interval $[0, L]$ where $L$ is a deadline of some task.

This is the **necessary and sufficient** condition for EDF on a uniprocessor — making EDF *exactly analysable*, unlike RMS which has only sufficient conditions in general.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Real-Time Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Task Set Gamma] --> A1[Task Arrival / Release Event]
    A1 --> A2[Task Descriptor Table]
    A2 --> A3[Scheduler Core Engine]
    A3 --> A4{RMS or EDF?}
    A4 -->|Static Priority| A5[Priority Table: sorted by Period]
    A4 -->|Dynamic Priority| A6[Deadline Queue: sorted by Deadline]
    A5 --> A7[Dispatch Highest Priority Task]
    A6 --> A7
    A7 --> A8[CPU Execution]
    A8 --> A9{Task Complete or Tick?}
    A9 -->|Complete| A10[Release CPU and Update Ready Queue]
    A9 -->|Tick| A11[Re-evaluate Priorities and Re-dispatch]
    A10 --> A2
    A11 --> A2
    A2 --> B1[Schedulability Analyzer: U and WCRT]
    B1 --> B2[Acceptance Test Result]
    B2 -->|Pass| B3[Task Set Schedulable]
    B2 -->|Fail| B4[Task Set Rejected: Reduce Load]
```

---

## 4.2 RMS Scheduling Timeline — Worked Visualization

**Task set:** $\tau_1$ ($C=1, T=4$), $\tau_2$ ($C=2, T=6$).

```mermaid
gantt
    title Rate Monotonic Scheduling Timeline
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section Task Tau1
    T1 Job1 (Priority High)    :a1, 0, 1
    T1 Job2 (Priority High)    :a2, 4, 1
    T1 Job3 (Priority High)    :a3, 8, 1
    section Task Tau2
    T2 Job1 (Preempted then Resumed)  :b1, 0, 2
    T2 Job1 Completion                  :b2, 6, 2
    T2 Job2 (Preempted then Resumed)  :b3, 6, 2
    T2 Job2 Completion                  :b4, 12, 2
```

*Interpretation:* At $t=0$, both tasks release. $\tau_1$ has higher priority (shorter period), runs first (0–1). Then $\tau_2$ runs (1–3). At $t=4$, $\tau_1$ releases again and preempts $\tau_2$. $\tau_2$ resumes at $t=5$ and completes at $t=6$, exactly on its deadline.

---

## 4.3 EDF Scheduling Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Scheduling Tick] --> D1[Identify All Ready Tasks]
    D1 --> D2[Compute Absolute Deadline for Each: di = ri + Di]
    D2 --> D3[Sort by Earliest Deadline]
    D3 --> D4[Dispatch Task with Min di]
    D4 --> D5{Task Finishes Before New Arrival?}
    D5 -->|Yes| D6[Continue Current Task]
    D5 -->|No| D7[Preempt and Switch to New Task with Earlier Deadline]
    D6 --> D0
    D7 --> D0
```

---

## 4.4 Comparison Matrix: RMS vs. EDF

| Property | RMS (Rate Monotonic) | EDF (Earliest Deadline First) |
|----------|----------------------|-------------------------------|
| Priority type | Static (fixed) | Dynamic (runtime) |
| Implementation overhead | Low (lookup table) | Higher (sorted queue) |
| Schedulability test | Sufficient: $U \leq n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Exact: $U \leq 1$ |
| Optimality | Optimal among static-priority | Optimal among all (uniprocessor) |
| Runtime cost | O(1) selection | O(log n) selection |
| Predictability for certification | Excellent | Good |
| Susceptibility to *transient overload* | Tolerates well | Cascading failures possible |
| KTU 2024 relevance | Core syllabus topic | Core syllabus topic |
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Rate Monotonic Scheduling. State the Liu & Layland sufficient schedulability condition. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) is a *static-priority*, *preemptive* scheduling algorithm for periodic real-time tasks on a uniprocessor. It assigns priorities inversely proportional to task periods: **shorter period ⇒ higher priority**.

For a set of $n$ independent periodic tasks with $D_i = T_i$, RMS is a sufficient (but not necessary) schedulability test:

$$
U = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{T_i} \leq n\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)
$$

If this condition is satisfied, all tasks meet their deadlines. The bound approaches $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ as $n \to \infty$.

---

### Question 2: Differentiate between RMS and EDF. Which is optimal? `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | RMS | EDF |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Priority | Static (function of $T_i$) | Dynamic (function of $d_i$) |
| Schedulability bound | $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ | $U \leq 1$ (exact) |
| Runtime overhead | Low | Higher (deadline queue maintenance) |
| Optimality scope | Optimal among *static-priority* algorithms | Optimal among *all* uniprocessor algorithms |

EDF is **globally optimal** on a uniprocessor. RMS is optimal only within the class of static-priority algorithms.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — RMS Schedulability Analysis** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** A periodic task set consists of three tasks:
- $\tau_1$: $C_1 = 2$, $T_1 = 10$
- $\tau_2$: $C_2 = 3$, $T_2 = 15$
- $\tau_3$: $C_3 = 4$, $T_3 = 25$

All deadlines equal periods. Apply **Liu & Layland's** sufficient condition. Is the task set **guaranteed** to be schedulable under RMS? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the **Worst-Case Response Time (WCRT) iteration** (Joseph & Pandya), determine the exact WCRT of $\tau_3$ and verify whether it meets its deadline. Comment on the **conservatism** of the Liu-Layland bound. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Liu-Layland Test

**Step 1: Compute utilization.**

$$
U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{15} + \frac{4}{25} = 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.16 = 0.56
$$

**Step 2: Compute Liu-Layland bound for $n=3$.**

$$
U_{LL}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798
$$

**Step 3: Compare.**

$$
U = 0.56 \leq 0.7798 = U_{LL}(3) \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under RMS by the sufficient Liu-Layland test.

**Allocation:** 2 marks — utilization; 2 marks — bound calculation; 2 marks — comparison & verdict; 1 mark — interpretation.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — WCRT Iteration

Priority: $P_1 > P_2 > P_3$ (since $T_1 < T_2 < T_3$).

For $\tau_3$, higher-priority tasks: $hp(3) = \{\tau_1, \tau_2\}$.

**Initialize:** $R_3^0 = C_3 = 4$.

**Iteration 1:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^1 &= C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^0}{T_1} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{R_3^0}{T_2} \right\rceil C_2 \\
      &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{4}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{4}{15} \right\rceil (3) \\
      &= 4 + (1)(2) + (1)(3) = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Iteration 2:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_3^2 &= 4 + \left\lceil \frac{9}{10} \right\rceil (2) + \left\lceil \frac{9}{15} \right\rceil (3) \\
      &= 4 + (1)(2) + (1)(3) = 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Fixed point reached:** $R_3 = 9$ ms.

**Deadline check:** $D_3 = T_3 = 25$ ms. $R_3 = 9 \leq 25$ ✅ **Schedulable.**

**Conservatism commentary (2 marks):**  
The Liu-Layland bound $0.7798$ is **sufficient but not necessary**. It is *pessimistic*: it may reject task sets that are actually schedulable. In this example, $U = 0.56$ is well below the bound $0.7798$, leaving $0.2198$ as *headroom*. The WCRT test, in contrast, gives an *exact* answer: $R_3 = 9$ ms with $16$ ms of slack. Hence WCRT is **tighter** and **necessary & sufficient** for fixed-priority systems.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — EDF Schedulability & Comparison** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**(a)** For the same task set as Q.A ($\tau_1, \tau_2, \tau_3$), apply the **EDF schedulability test** based on utilization. Compare with RMS results. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the concept of **priority inversion** and its impact on real-time task scheduling. Discuss how **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** solves it. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — EDF Test

**Step 1: Compute total utilization.**

$$
U = \frac{2}{10} + \frac{3}{15} + \frac{4}{25} = 0.56
$$

**Step 2: Apply EDF test (sufficient & necessary for uniprocessor).**

$$
U = 0.56 \leq 1.0 \;\; \checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Task set is **schedulable under EDF**.

**Comparison with RMS (3 marks):**
- RMS: $U = 0.56$ vs bound $0.7798$ → schedulable, $0.22$ headroom *theoretical*.
- EDF: $U = 0.56$ vs limit $1.00$ → schedulable, $0.44$ headroom *theoretical*.
- **EDF admits 2× more headroom** in this case. RMS may fail task sets with utilization in the range $(0.693, 1.0)$ that EDF can schedule.
- Trade-off: EDF requires dynamic priority handling (more runtime overhead), while RMS has fixed priority tables (cheaper, easier to certify).

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Priority Inversion & PIP

**Priority Inversion** (3 marks):  
A high-priority task $\tau_H$ is **indirectly blocked** by a low-priority task $\tau_L$ because $\tau_L$ holds a shared resource (mutex) that $\tau_H$ needs. During the blocking, a *medium-priority* task $\tau_M$ preempts $\tau_L$, further delaying $\tau_H$. The effective priority of $\tau_H$ is *inverted* below $\tau_M$.

**Classic example:** NASA's Mars Pathfinder (1997) experienced repeated system resets due to priority inversion in its bus management task — a well-documented industrial case.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** (Sha, Rajkumar, Lehoczky — 1990) (4 marks):

- When a high-priority task $\tau_H$ blocks on a resource held by a low-priority task $\tau_L$, the scheduler **temporarily elevates** $\tau_L$'s priority to that of $\tau_H$.
- $\tau_L$ then runs at the inherited (high) priority until it **releases the resource**.
- Upon release, $\tau_L$'s priority **reverts** to its original value.
- This prevents medium-priority tasks from preempting $\tau_L$ and indirectly blocking $\tau_H$.

**Key property of PIP:** Bounded blocking — a task $\tau_H$ can be blocked for at most the duration of **one critical section** of each lower-priority task. This makes PIP **analysable** and predictable — essential for hard real-time systems.

**Variants:** Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) and Stack Resource Policy (SRP) provide *deadlock-free* + *transitive blocking* guarantees, used in AUTOSAR OS and POSIX real-time extensions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU 2024 Scheduling Problems:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting ceiling brackets** in the WCRT formula: writing $\frac{R_i^k}{T_j}$ instead of $\left\lceil \frac{R_i^k}{T_j} \right\rceil$ — costs **2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing WCRT iteration index** ($R_i^{k+1}$ uses $R_i^k$, not $R_i^{k-1}$).
> 3. **Not specifying preemptive vs. non-preemptive** assumptions explicitly — **1 mark penalty**.
> 4. **Mixing up RMS and EDF schedulability bounds** in the answer (RMS bound is $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ asymptotically; EDF bound is $1.0$).
> 5. **Omitting the units (ms / $\mu$s)** in the WCRT numerical answer — partial deduction.
> 6. **In priority-inversion answers**, students forget to mention **transitive blocking** in PIP analysis — loses **1–2 marks**.
> 7. **Not stating the optimality claim scope** ("RMS is optimal *among static-priority* algorithms") — examiners look for the precise qualifier.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this checklist before entering the KTU ESE hall.**

- [ ] A real-time task is characterized by the tuple $(C_i, T_i, D_i, P_i)$: WCET, period, deadline, priority.
- [ ] **Hard real-time**: deadline miss = system failure. **Soft real-time**: deadline miss = degraded quality.
- [ ] **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)** — static priority, shorter period ⇒ higher priority, preemptive.
- [ ] **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** — dynamic priority, earliest absolute deadline wins.
- [ ] **Liu & Layland RMS bound:** $U \leq n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, sufficient but not necessary.
- [ ] **Liu & Layland limit:** $n \to \infty \Rightarrow \ln 2 \approx 0.6931$.
- [ ] **EDF bound (exact):** $U \leq 1$ on a uniprocessor.
- [ ] **WCRT iteration (Joseph & Pandya):** $R_i^{k+1} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i^k / T_j \rceil C_j$.
- [ ] Schedulable iff $R_i \leq D_i$ for all tasks (fixed-priority exact test).
- [ ] **Hyperbolic RMS bound:** $\prod_i (1 + C_i/T_i) \leq 2$ — sometimes tighter than Liu-Layland.
- [ ] **Critical instant** in RMS: all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously at $t = 0$ → worst case.
- [ ] **RMS is optimal within static-priority class**; **EDF is globally optimal** on uniprocessors.
- [ ] **Priority inversion** = high-priority task blocked by low-priority task holding a shared resource.
- [ ] **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** boosts the priority of a resource-holding low-priority task to that of the highest-priority waiter.
- [ ] PIP provides **bounded blocking**: at most one critical section duration per lower-priority task.
- [ ] **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** and **Stack Resource Policy (SRP)** prevent deadlocks and transitive blocking.
- [ ] In **safety-critical** domains (avionics, automotive), static-priority RMS is preferred for **certifiability**.
- [ ] In **dynamic-load multimedia or soft-RT systems**, EDF provides higher utilization.
- [ ] Always **declare assumptions** preemptive vs. non-preemptive, $D_i = T_i$ vs. $D_i < T_i$, independent tasks, etc.
- [ ] A necessary condition for *any* scheduler: $U \leq 1$.
- [ ] **Harmonic periods** (e.g., $\{1, 2, 4, 8\}$) maximize RMS schedulability — utilization can approach 1.0.
- [ ] **Time demand function** $w_i(t) = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil t/T_j \rceil C_j \leq t$ is equivalent to WCRT in continuous form.
- [ ] KTU 2024 frequently tests: utilization calculation, RMS bound application, WCRT iteration, priority-inversion scenarios.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Threads
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Threads

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **THREAD (Lightweight Process)**
> A **thread** is the smallest unit of CPU execution and program sequencing that exists within a process. It is an independent flow of control that shares its code segment, data segment, and operating system resources (open files, signals, environment) with peer threads belonging to the same parent process, but maintains its **own** thread control block (TCB), **thread-local storage (TLS)**, **stack pointer**, **program counter (PC)**, and **register set**.

In the context of **Advanced Embedded Systems**, a thread is typically implemented as a **task** in a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) such as **FreeRTOS**, **VxWorks**, **RTLinux**, or **ThreadX**. The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes threads in terms of their creation, lifecycle, scheduling, and synchronization primitives (mutexes, semaphores, condition variables).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Restaurant Kitchen

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: A Multi-Chef Restaurant Kitchen**
> Imagine a **restaurant kitchen** (this is your **PROCESS**). The kitchen has one shared pantry, one shared refrigerator, one shared set of utensils, and one shared recipe book. These represent the **shared resources** of a process.
> 
> - A **single-chef** kitchen is like a **single-threaded** process. One chef does everything — chopping, cooking, plating. The kitchen is idle when the chef is not working.
> - A **multi-chef** kitchen is like a **multi-threaded** process. Multiple chefs (THREADS) work in the SAME kitchen, share the SAME pantry and utensils (shared memory/heap), but each chef has their **own cutting board and knife** (private stack and registers). The head chef dispatches tasks — that's the **SCHEDULER**.
> 
> If two chefs reach for the same knife at the same time, there is a **race condition** — which is exactly why we need **MUTEXES** (knife access token). The board outside showing "Order #5 ready" is a **SEMAPHORE** (a signaling mechanism).

## 1.3 Process vs. Thread — The Critical Distinction

| Property | Process | Thread |
|---|---|---|
| Memory Space | Independent (own address space) | Shares with peer threads |
| Creation Cost | **Heavy** (full PCB allocation) | **Light** (TCB allocation only) |
| Communication | IPC (pipes, sockets, shared memory) | Shared variables (in same address space) |
| Context Switch | **~1–5 ms** (TLB flush, page tables) | **~1–10 µs** (no address space change) |
| Failure Isolation | One crash does not kill others | One thread crash can kill the whole process |
| Synchronization Need | Weak (separate address space) | **Critical** (race conditions on shared data) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Pearl:**
> "A thread is *not* a process. A process is a *container*; a thread is a *worker inside the container*. A process must contain **at least one** thread (the main thread)."

## 1.4 Why Threads Matter in Embedded Systems

In an embedded SoC (e.g., ARM Cortex-M4 with FreeRTOS), threads are not optional — they are the **primary concurrency mechanism**. Typical applications require:

1. **Sensor acquisition thread** (periodic, deterministic)
2. **Communication thread** (UART/SPI/CAN, event-driven)
3. **Control loop thread** (motor control, hard real-time)
4. **User-interface thread** (HMI, low priority)
5. **Watchdog/Idle thread** (system health)

All of these run *concurrently* on a single CPU core using **preemptive priority-based scheduling**, and the **RTOS scheduler** is what makes it look like true parallelism.

---

## 1.5 Types of Threads in Embedded RTOS Context

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification by Kernel Visibility:**

1. **User-Level Threads (ULT)** — Managed entirely in user space by a thread library (e.g., `pthread` over `Linux`). Kernel sees only the process. Fast to create, but **blocking system call blocks the entire process**.
2. **Kernel-Level Threads (KLT)** — Managed directly by the kernel. Each thread is a schedulable entity. **Blocking system call blocks only that thread**. Standard in RTOS like VxWorks, FreeRTOS (with port layer).
3. **Hybrid Threads (M:N model)** — M user threads mapped to N kernel threads. Example: modern POSIX threads on Linux (NPTL). Rare in resource-constrained embedded MCUs.

| Model | Used In | Concurrency on Multicore? |
|---|---|---|
| **1:1 (KLT)** | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, ThreadX | ✅ Yes (true parallelism) |
| **N:1 (ULT)** | Older Java Green Threads, GNU Pth | ❌ No (kernel sees 1 process) |
| **M:N (Hybrid)** | Go runtime, modern Solaris | ✅ Yes (with N kernels) |

---

## 1.6 The Thread Control Block (TCB) — Heart of Every Thread

Every thread, irrespective of RTOS brand, has an internal data structure called the **Thread Control Block (TCB)** or **Task Control Block**. For a KTU exam, you must know its fields:

| TCB Field | Purpose |
|---|---|
| `Thread ID (TID)` | Unique identifier |
| `Thread State` | READY, RUNNING, BLOCKED, SUSPENDED, TERMINATED |
| `Program Counter (PC)` | Resume point after context switch |
| `Stack Pointer (SP)` | Top of this thread's private stack |
| `Register Set` | Saved CPU registers (R0–R15 on ARM) |
| `Priority` | Static or dynamic priority value |
| `Stack Base / Size` | Bounds checking for stack overflow |
| `Wait Queue Link` | For mutex/semaphore/event blocking |
| `Time Slice Counter` | Round-robin quantum tracker |
| `TLS Pointer` | Thread-Local Storage base address |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Size in FreeRTOS:** `TCB_t` is typically **~70–100 bytes** per task, while a `pthread` TCB in Linux is **~1.5–4 KB** (much heavier).

---

## 1.7 Key Terminology Box (High-Yield for KTU)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glossary of Thread Vocabulary:**
> - **Spawning** — Creating a new thread
> - **Joining** — A parent thread waits for child to terminate
> - **Detaching** — Releasing TCB resources automatically on exit
> - **Yielding** — Voluntarily giving up CPU (`sched_yield()`)
> - **Cancelling** — Requesting another thread to terminate
> - **Daemon Thread** — Background thread, killed when main exits
> - **Cancellation Point** — POSIX function where cancellation request is checked
> - **Race Condition** — Outcome depends on non-deterministic thread interleaving
> - **Critical Section** — Code region accessing shared resources
> - **Reentrancy** — Function safe to call from multiple threads simultaneously

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Thread Lifecycle — Five Canonical States

A thread in any modern RTOS / OS cycles through the following **five states** (sometimes six, with `CREATED`/`NEW` as a pre-READY state):

```
        ┌──────────┐
        │  NEW     │ (Thread created but not yet runnable)
        └─────┬────┘
              │ start()
              ▼
        ┌──────────┐         scheduler dispatch
        │  READY   │ ─────────────────────────┐
        └────┬─────┘                          │
             │                                ▼
             │ ◄──────── preempted / time ─► ┌──────────┐
             │          slice expired         │ RUNNING  │
             │                                └────┬─────┘
             │                                     │
   event     │    ┌──────────────────────┐         │ exit / return
   received   ──► │   BLOCKED / WAITING  │ ◄──────┘
             ◄── │ (sleep, I/O, mutex)  │         │
                  └──────────────────────┘         ▼
                                              ┌──────────┐
                                              │TERMINATED│
                                              └──────────┘
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** On a single-core system, only **ONE** thread can be in the `RUNNING` state at any instant. On an N-core SoC, up to **N** threads can be RUNNING simultaneously (true parallelism). All other runnable threads sit in the `READY` queue.

### State Transition Triggers

| From → To | Trigger | Function/Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| NEW → READY | `pthread_create()` returns | Thread enters scheduler's ready queue |
| READY → RUNNING | Scheduler dispatch | Highest-priority ready thread selected |
| RUNNING → READY | Time-slice expired / preempted by higher-priority | `tick interrupt` → context switch |
| RUNNING → BLOCKED | Wait on mutex/semaphore/queue/sleep | `vTaskDelay()`, `xQueueReceive()`, `sem_wait()` |
| BLOCKED → READY | Event/signaled/mutex released | ISR or peer thread unblocks it |
| RUNNING → TERMINATED | Function returns / `pthread_exit()` | Resources freed (joined or detached) |

---

## 2.2 Thread Creation, Termination, and Joining — POSIX Semantics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **POSIX `pthread` API Cheat Sheet (high-yield for KTU):**

| Function | Purpose | Return Value on Success |
|---|---|---|
| `pthread_create(tid, attr, fn, arg)` | Spawn a new thread | `0` |
| `pthread_exit(retval)` | Terminate calling thread | Never returns |
| `pthread_join(tid, retval)` | Block until peer thread exits | `0` |
| `pthread_detach(tid)` | Mark thread as detached (auto-cleanup) | `0` |
| `pthread_self()` | Get calling thread's TID | `pthread_t` |
| `pthread_equal(t1, t2)` | Compare two TIDs | Non-zero if equal |
| `pthread_cancel(tid)` | Request cancellation of a thread | `0` |
| `pthread_kill(tid, sig)` | Send a signal to a thread | `0` |

### 2.2.1 Detailed Creation Steps

When `pthread_create()` is invoked on a POSIX system, the OS performs:

1. **Allocate TCB** in kernel memory (or user space for NPTL).
2. **Allocate Stack** — default **8 MB** on Linux (configurable via `ulimit -s`), 1 KB–64 KB on embedded RTOS.
3. **Initialize TCB fields** — set SP to top of stack, PC to start function, priority inherited from `attr`.
4. **Set TLS region** — set up thread-local storage pointer.
5. **Insert into scheduler's ready queue** with computed priority.
6. **Return TID** to caller.

### 2.2.2 Join vs. Detach — Memory Ownership

> [!NOTE]
> - **`pthread_join()`** — Caller BLOCKS until target thread terminates. Retrieves return value. **TCB is freed only after join** → prevents zombie threads. Joinable threads MUST be joined or detached, or they leak TCB memory.
> - **`pthread_detach()`** — Tells the kernel: "When this thread exits, clean up its TCB and stack automatically." The thread becomes **unjoinable**. Detached threads are sometimes called **daemon threads** (especially in Java/Python terminology).

---

## 2.3 Thread Scheduling — Embedded RTOS Perspective

### 2.3.1 Scheduling Policies

| Policy | Description | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **FCFS / FIFO** | First-Come, First-Served; non-preemptive | Simple bare-metal, cooperative kernels |
| **Round-Robin (RR)** | Each thread gets equal time quantum `Q` | Linux SCHED_RR, FreeRTOS with same-priority tasks |
| **Fixed Priority Preemptive (FPP)** | Highest-priority READY thread always runs; preempts lower ones | FreeRTOS, VxWorks, most hard-RTOS |
| **Rate Monotonic (RMS)** | Static priority ∝ frequency; optimal for fixed-priority | Hard real-time theory |
| **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** | Dynamic priority by absolute deadline | Linux SCHED_DEADLINE, RTOS theory |
| **Multilevel Feedback Queue (MLFQ)** | Priority adjusted based on CPU usage history | General-purpose OS (Windows, Linux) |

### 2.3.2 Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS) — Liu & Layland (1973)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RMS Utilization Bound for N threads:**
> $$U_{bound}(N) = N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)$$

**Schedulability condition:** If total CPU utilization $U \leq U_{bound}(N)$, then **all deadlines are guaranteed** to be met.

| N (number of threads) | $U_{bound}(N)$ |
|---|---|
| 1 | 1.000 (100%) |
| 2 | 0.828 (82.8%) |
| 3 | 0.779 (77.9%) |
| 4 | 0.756 (75.6%) |
| 5 | 0.743 (74.3%) |
| ∞ | $\ln 2 \approx 0.693$ (69.3%) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pitfall:** This bound is **sufficient but not necessary**. Schedulability can still hold for $U$ up to 100% (e.g., when periods are harmonic). The **exact** test is the **Response Time Analysis (RTA)** iteration:
> $$R_i^{(0)} = C_i$$
> $$R_i^{(k+1)} = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \frac{R_i^{(k)}}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$$
> Fixed point: if $R_i \leq D_i$ (deadline), then task $i$ is schedulable.

---

## 2.4 Thread Stack Sizing Formula (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stack Size Estimation (FreeRTOS / Embedded):**
> $$\text{Stack Size} = \underbrace{C_{main}}_{\text{local vars}} + \underbrace{\sum_{n=1}^{N} C_{call_n}}_{\text{nested function calls}} + \underbrace{N_{int} \cdot C_{ISR}}_{\text{interrupts}} + \underbrace{P_{margin}}_{\text{safety padding}}$$

**Typical empirical rule of thumb:**
$$\text{Stack Size} \approx 4 \times \text{(deepest call depth)} \times \text{(word size)} + \text{largest local array size} + 1\,\text{KB margin}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Stack overflow is the **#1 cause** of embedded system crashes. FreeRTOS provides `configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW` to detect this in two ways: **Method 1** (paint stack with magic value on creation) and **Method 2** (check SP is within bounds on every context switch).

---

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Table)

> [!CAUTION]
> **Note on `|` symbol:** All absolute-value and ceiling-style vertical bars in formulas below use `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table syntax.

| # | Concept | Formula / Definition | Units / Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Thread CPU Utilization | $U_i = \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Dimensionless, $0 \leq U_i \leq 1$ |
| 2 | Total System Utilization | $U = \sum_{i=1}^{N} U_i = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | $\leq 1$ for single core |
| 3 | RMS Schedulability Bound | $U_{bound}(N) = N\,(2^{1/N} - 1)$ | Approaches $\ln 2$ as $N \to \infty$ |
| 4 | Context Switch Time | $T_{cs} = 2 \cdot T_{save} + 2 \cdot T_{load} + T_{sched}$ | Typically 1–10 µs |
| 5 | CPU Throughput | $\Theta = \dfrac{N_{work}}{T_{wall}}$ | work units / second |
| 6 | Thread Creation Cost | $C_{create} = T_{alloc} + T_{init} + T_{queue}$ | ~5–50 µs on RTOS |
| 7 | Worst-Case Stack Depth | $S_{max} = \sum_{call} S_{frame} + S_{ISR}$ | Bytes |
| 8 | Priority Inversion Time | $T_{PI} = T_{critical\,section}$ (worst case) | Unbounded in theory |
| 9 | Deadline Miss Probability | $P_{miss} = P(R_i > D_i)$ | For stochastic analysis |
| 10 | Cache Miss Penalty (per context switch) | $T_{miss} = N_{lines} \cdot T_{mem}$ | ~10–100 ns per line |
| 11 | Priority Inheritance | Effective priority = $\max(P_{current}, P_{waiting})$ | Numeric |
| 12 | Stack Overflow Margin | $M = S_{allocated} - S_{peak}$ | Bytes (must be $\geq 256$) |

---

## 2.6 Thread Synchronization Primitives — At a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Four Pillars of Thread Synchronization (in POSIX & RTOS):**

1. **Mutex (MUT-ual EX-clusion)**
   - Binary lock. Only one thread can hold it at a time.
   - **API:** `pthread_mutex_lock(&mtx)` / `unlock(&mtx)`
   - **Owner concept** — only the locker can unlock. Enables **priority inheritance**.
   - **Risk:** Deadlock if two mutexes locked in opposite order.

2. **Semaphore** (counting)
   - Counter-based signaling. `wait()` decrements; `post()` increments.
   - **Binary semaphore (value = 0/1)**: pure signaling (ISR ↔ task).
   - **Counting semaphore (value = N)**: resource pool of N identical resources.
   - **API:** `sem_wait(&sem)` / `sem_post(&sem)`

3. **Condition Variable**
   - Allows a thread to block until a **predicate** becomes true.
   - **Always used with a mutex.**
   - **API:** `pthread_cond_wait(&cond, &mtx)` / `pthread_cond_signal(&cond)`

4. **Read-Write Lock (RWLock)**
   - Multiple concurrent **readers** OR one exclusive **writer**.
   - Useful for read-mostly data (e.g., config tables).

| Primitive | Owner? | Priority Inheritance? | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mutex | Yes | Yes | Protect critical section |
| Semaphore | No | No (in basic form) | Resource counting, ISR→Task signal |
| Cond Variable | N/A | N/A | Wait for state change |
| RWLock | Yes (writer) | Implementation-dependent | Read-heavy shared data |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where threads are non-negotiable in KTU-aligned embedded systems:**

- **AUTOSAR** in automotive ECU — each Runnable is a thread; BSW tasks are preemptive threads.
- **Drone flight controller (PX4)** — runs on NuttX RTOS with ~30+ threads: attitude control, position control, sensor fusion, telemetry, logger.
- **IoT gateway (ZephyrOS)** — BLE stack thread, MQTT thread, sensor thread, OTA thread.
- **Industrial PLC (IEC 61131-3)** — periodic task = thread, event task = thread.

The choice of **thread count, priority assignment, and stack size** directly determines whether the system meets its **WCET (Worst-Case Execution Time)** budget and is **deadline-compliant**.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — RMS Utilization Bound $U_{bound}(N) = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$

We derive the classical Liu & Layland (1973) bound for Rate Monotonic Scheduling.

**Assumptions:**
- $N$ periodic tasks, priorities assigned inversely to periods (shorter period → higher priority).
- Each task $i$ has computation time $C_i$ and period $T_i$.
- Utilization $U_i = C_i / T_i$.
- All tasks are independent; deadline $D_i = T_i$.

**Step 1 — Critical Instant Definition**

The worst-case response time of a task occurs at a **critical instant** — when all higher-priority tasks release simultaneously with the task of interest. This is a proven theorem (originally by Liu & Layland).

**Step 2 — Worst-Case Response Time for Task $i$**

At the critical instant, task $i$ must wait for:
- Its own computation: $C_i$
- All jobs of every higher-priority task $j$ released during its response window.

Number of jobs of task $j$ released in time interval $[0, R_i]$:

$$N_j(R_i) = \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil$$

**Step 3 — Conservative Approximation for the Bound**

To get a closed-form (sufficient) condition, we relax the ceiling: $\lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \leq R_i / T_j + 1$. This gives:

$$R_i \leq C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left( \frac{R_i}{T_j} + 1 \right) C_j$$

Wait — that introduces $R_i$ on both sides. The standard trick is to set $R_i = T_i$ (the deadline) and solve the resulting inequality:

$$T_i \geq C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left( \frac{T_i}{T_j} + 1 \right) C_j$$

**Step 4 — Divide through by $T_i$:**

$$1 \geq \frac{C_i}{T_i} + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \frac{C_j}{T_j} + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \frac{C_j}{T_i}$$

**Step 5 — The trick of equalizing utilizations**

Liu & Layland showed the bound is **tightest** (most restrictive) when all higher-priority tasks have $C_j / T_j$ equal to each other, and the bound expression is maximized. The derivation then reduces (after a long algebraic chain involving the inequality $T_i \leq T_j$ for $j$ with higher priority — wait, actually $T_j \leq T_i$, so $1/T_j \geq 1/T_i$, hence the worst case is when $C_j = T_j$ for all $j$ — i.e., $U_j = 1$, which is impossible since $j$ must leave time for lower-priority tasks). The full derivation yields:

$$U_{bound}(N) = N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)$$

**Step 6 — Limit as $N \to \infty$:**

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right)$$

Let $x = 1/N$, so $x \to 0$ as $N \to \infty$. Then $2^{1/N} = 2^x = e^{x \ln 2}$, and:

$$N \left( 2^{1/N} - 1 \right) = \frac{2^x - 1}{x} = \frac{e^{x \ln 2} - 1}{x}$$

Using Taylor expansion: $e^{x \ln 2} \approx 1 + x \ln 2 + \frac{(x \ln 2)^2}{2} + \ldots$, so:

$$\frac{e^{x \ln 2} - 1}{x} \approx \ln 2 + O(x)$$

Therefore:

$$\lim_{N \to \infty} U_{bound}(N) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693147$$

**Conclusion:** No matter how many tasks you have under fixed-priority preemptive scheduling, total CPU utilization should not exceed **69.3%** to guarantee all deadlines are met under the Liu-Layland assumptions.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stack Size for an Embedded Thread

Suppose a FreeRTOS thread on a 32-bit ARM Cortex-M4 calls functions in this order:
- `task_main()` → `read_sensor()` → `parse_packet()` → `crc16()`

| Function | Local variables (bytes) | Call depth | Saved regs (ARM) |
|---|---|---|---|
| `task_main` | 64 (struct sensor_data) | 1 | 32 |
| `read_sensor` | 16 (uint8_t buffer[16]) | 2 | 32 |
| `parse_packet` | 256 (uint8_t frame[256]) | 3 | 32 |
| `crc16` | 4 (uint16_t, uint8_t) | 4 | 32 |

**Step 1 — Sum the local variables:**

$$C_{main} = 64 + 16 + 256 + 4 = 340 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the call-frame overheads (4 calls × 32 bytes):**

$$\sum C_{call_n} = 4 \times 32 = 128 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 3 — Interrupt nesting overhead:**

Assume 3 levels of interrupt nesting, each saving ~64 bytes on the ISR stack:

$$N_{int} \cdot C_{ISR} = 3 \times 64 = 192 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 4 — Safety margin (recommended 256 bytes for FreeRTOS):**

$$P_{margin} = 256 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 5 — Total Stack:**

$$\text{Stack Size} = 340 + 128 + 192 + 256 = 916 \text{ bytes}$$

**Step 6 — Round up to power of 2 (RTOS convention):**

$$\text{Allocated Stack Size} = 1024 \text{ bytes} = 1\,\text{KB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Rule:** In production, multiply this by **1.5–2×** to handle compiler optimizations that may grow stack frames unpredictably. So allocate **2 KB** for safety.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Priority Inversion Time Bound

**Scenario:** Three threads — High (H), Medium (M), Low (L). H needs a mutex held by L. M is runnable.

**Timeline without priority inheritance:**

| Time | Event | Running Thread |
|---|---|---|
| $t_0$ | L acquires mutex, enters critical section | L |
| $t_1$ | H becomes ready, preempts L | H |
| $t_2$ | H tries to lock mutex (held by L), blocks | — |
| $t_3$ | M becomes ready, preempts L (L still holds mutex) | M |
| $t_4$ to $t_4 + C_M$ | M runs to completion | M |
| $t_5$ | L resumes critical section | L |
| $t_6$ | L releases mutex, H unblocks | H |
| $t_7$ | H runs to completion | H |

**Inversion time for H:**

$$T_{PI} = C_L + C_M$$

**In the worst case (M, M, M, ... keep preemption L's chance to release):**

If there are $k$ medium-priority threads each with execution time $C_M$:

$$T_{PI}^{worst} = C_L + k \cdot C_M$$

This is **unbounded** in the general case — there is no upper bound on the number of medium-priority threads that can preempt L.

**With Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP):**

When H blocks on L's mutex, L's priority is **boosted to H's priority** for the duration of the critical section. M cannot preempt L. Then:

$$T_{PI}^{PIP} = C_L \quad \text{(bounded!)}$$

This is why **priority inheritance mutexes** are essential in hard real-time systems (e.g., POSIX `pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(... PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT ...)`).

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Thread Operation Cost Comparison

| Operation | Linux NPTL | FreeRTOS (Cortex-M4) | VxWorks | Bare-Metal |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Thread creation | ~30–50 µs | ~1–5 µs | ~5–20 µs | N/A (no thread) |
| Context switch | ~2–10 µs | ~0.5–2 µs | ~1–5 µs | N/A |
| Mutex lock/unlock | ~1 µs (uncontended) | ~50–200 ns | ~200–500 ns | ~20 cycles (DMB) |
| Semaphore post (from ISR) | ~5 µs | ~100–300 ns | ~500 ns | N/A |
| Min stack per thread | 8 MB (default) | 64–256 bytes (idle) | 4 KB | 0 |
| Memory per TCB | ~4 KB | ~70–100 bytes | ~500 bytes | 0 |

---

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Problem — RMS Schedulability

**Problem:** Three periodic tasks in an embedded system:

| Task | Period $T_i$ (ms) | Execution Time $C_i$ (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| T1 (highest priority) | 10 | 2 |
| T2 | 20 | 5 |
| T3 (lowest priority) | 50 | 5 |

**Step 1 — Compute individual utilizations:**

$$U_1 = 2/10 = 0.200$$
$$U_2 = 5/20 = 0.250$$
$$U_3 = 5/50 = 0.100$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization:**

$$U = 0.200 + 0.250 + 0.100 = 0.550$$

**Step 3 — Apply Liu-Layland bound for N=3:**

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3 \left( 2^{1/3} - 1 \right) = 3 \left( 1.2599 - 1 \right) = 3 \times 0.2599 = 0.7798$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$U = 0.550 \leq U_{bound}(3) = 0.7798 \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **guaranteed schedulable** under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. All deadlines will be met.

**Step 5 — Verify with exact Response Time Analysis for T3 (most critical):**

Iteration $k=0$: $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 5$

Iteration $k=1$:
$$R_3^{(1)} = C_3 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{10} \right\rceil C_1 + \left\lceil \frac{5}{20} \right\rceil C_2$$
$$= 5 + 1 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 5 = 12 \text{ ms}$$

Iteration $k=2$:
$$R_3^{(2)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{12}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5$$
$$= 5 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 5 = 5 + 4 + 5 = 14 \text{ ms}$$

Iteration $k=3$:
$$R_3^{(3)} = 5 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{10} \right\rceil \cdot 2 + \left\lceil \frac{14}{20} \right\rceil \cdot 5$$
$$= 5 + 2 \cdot 2 + 1 \cdot 5 = 14 \text{ ms} \quad \text{(converged)}$$

**Step 6 — Check against deadline:**
$$R_3 = 14 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = T_3 = 50 \text{ ms} \quad \checkmark$$

T3 meets its deadline with **36 ms of slack** (72% headroom).

**Reading the Gantt Chart:**
- $0$–$15$ ms: Low-priority thread L runs and acquires the mutex.
- $5$ ms: H becomes ready and preempts L.
- $10$ ms: H tries to lock the mutex (held by L) — **H blocks**.
- $15$ ms: Medium-priority M becomes ready, preempts L.
- $25$ ms: M completes, L resumes.
- $35$ ms: L releases mutex, H unblocks and runs.
- **Total inversion time for H: 30 ms**, although L's critical section was only 15 ms.
