# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Differentiate between user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Mention any two disadvantages of user-level threads."**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | User-Level Threads (ULT) | Kernel-Level Threads (KLT) |
|---|---|---|
| Managed by | Thread library in user space | Kernel scheduler |
| Kernel awareness | Kernel sees only the process | Each thread is a schedulable entity |
| Thread creation | ~10–100× faster (no syscall) | Slower (requires `clone()`/`fork()`) |
| Blocking syscall behavior | **Whole process blocks** | Only the calling thread blocks |
| Parallelism on multicore | **Not possible** | Yes — true parallelism |
| Context switch | Cheap (user space only) | Expensive (kernel space + TLB) |

**Two disadvantages of ULT:**
1. **No true parallelism on multicore systems** — because the kernel schedules the process, only one ULT can run at a time even on a many-core CPU.
2. **Blocking I/O blocks the entire process** — if one ULT invokes a blocking system call, the kernel de-schedules the whole process, stalling all peer ULTs.

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"Explain the role of a Thread Control Block (TCB) in an RTOS. List any four fields stored in a TCB."**

**Model Answer:**

A **Thread Control Block (TCB)** is a kernel data structure that stores all per-thread state information required to **manage, schedule, and resume** a thread. The RTOS scheduler uses the TCB to represent each thread in the system.

**Four essential TCB fields:**
1. **Thread State** — current state (READY, RUNNING, BLOCKED, SUSPENDED, TERMINATED).
2. **Stack Pointer (SP)** — points to the top of this thread's private stack.
3. **Program Counter (PC)** — saved PC for context restoration after a context switch.
4. **Priority** — static or dynamic priority value used by the scheduler.

*(Other valid fields: register set, thread ID, stack base/size, wait queue link, time slice counter.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks. Each question has internal choice. Sub-parts are typically **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**.

---

### **Question B1 (a)** — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**"With a neat state transition diagram, explain the various states of a thread in an RTOS. Describe the events that cause transitions between READY, RUNNING, and BLOCKED states."**

**Model Answer:**

A thread in an RTOS moves through a finite set of states during its lifetime. The five canonical states are:

1. **NEW** — thread created, not yet admitted to scheduler.
2. **READY** — thread is runnable, waiting for CPU allocation.
3. **RUNNING** — thread is currently executing on the CPU (only one per core).
4. **BLOCKED** — thread is waiting for an event (semaphore, mutex, I/O, delay).
5. **TERMINATED** — thread has finished, awaiting cleanup.

**State Transitions (with triggering events):**

| Transition | Triggering Event |
|---|---|
| NEW → READY | `xTaskCreate()` returns successfully; thread enters ready queue |
| READY → RUNNING | Scheduler selects this thread (highest priority / FIFO of ready queue) |
| RUNNING → READY | Time slice expired OR higher-priority thread became ready (preemption) |
| RUNNING → BLOCKED | Calls `vTaskDelay()`, `xQueueReceive()` (empty), `sem_wait()` (unavailable) |
| BLOCKED → READY | Timeout expired OR signaling thread performed `sem_post()` / `xQueueSend()` |
| RUNNING → TERMINATED | Thread function returns OR `vTaskDelete()` called |

**Diagram:** *(see SECTION 4.1 for Mermaid state diagram)*

**Key points for valuation:**
- Mention that on a single-core system, only ONE thread is in RUNNING state at any instant.
- The READY queue is typically ordered by priority (in preemptive priority-based schedulers).
- BLOCKED threads are NOT in the ready queue — they are in per-resource wait lists.

---

### **Question B1 (b)** — 7 Marks

**"Explain priority inversion with a suitable example. How does the Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) solve this problem?"**

**Model Answer:**

**Priority Inversion** is a scheduling anomaly where a **higher-priority thread is indirectly preempted by a lower-priority thread**, effectively "inverting" the relative priorities. This happens when:

1. A low-priority thread $L$ acquires a mutex.
2. A high-priority thread $H$ tries to lock the same mutex → **H blocks**.
3. A medium-priority thread $M$ (which does not need the mutex) becomes runnable and **preempts $L$**, delaying $L$'s release of the mutex.
4. $H$ is now waiting for $L$ to release, but $L$ cannot run because $M$ is hogging the CPU.

**Example (Mars Pathfinder Bug, 1997):**

A high-priority meteorological thread on the Mars Pathfinder spacecraft would occasionally reset the system due to priority inversion. The bus management thread held a shared semaphore; the meteorological thread needed the same semaphore and would block; meanwhile, a medium-priority communication thread would preempt the bus thread. The watchdog would fire and reset the system.

**Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP) solution:**

- When a high-priority thread $H$ blocks on a mutex held by a lower-priority thread $L$, the kernel **temporarily raises $L$'s priority to $H$'s priority** for the duration that $L$ holds the mutex.
- This prevents any medium-priority thread $M$ from preempting $L$.
- When $L$ releases the mutex, its priority reverts to its original value.
- $H$ then immediately acquires the mutex and runs to completion.

**Effect:** The inversion time is bounded by the critical section length of $L$, not by the unbounded arrival of medium-priority threads.

**POSIX API to enable PIP:**
```c
pthread_mutexattr_t attr;
pthread_mutexattr_init(&attr);
pthread_mutexattr_setprotocol(&attr, PTHREAD_PRIO_INHERIT);
pthread_mutex_init(&mtx, &attr);
```

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse PIP with Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP).** PCP sets the lock holder to a *predeclared ceiling priority* at lock acquisition; PIP dynamically raises to the priority of the *highest waiting thread*.
> 2. **PIP is automatic only on some RTOS** — FreeRTOS requires `configUSE_MUTEXES = 1` and `configUSE_PIP = 1`; POSIX requires the attribute call shown above. Bare-metal default mutex does NOT do PIP.
> 3. **PIP does NOT prevent deadlock** — only reduces inversion time. Two mutexes locked in opposite order still deadlock.

---

### **Question B2 (a)** — 7 Marks [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**"Explain the Rate Monotonic Scheduling algorithm. Three periodic tasks have periods and execution times as given below. Determine whether the task set is schedulable under RMS.**

| Task | Period (ms) | Execution Time (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| T1 | 8 | 2 |
| T2 | 12 | 3 |
| T3 | 24 | 4 |"

**Model Answer:**

**Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS):**
- Static-priority, preemptive scheduling algorithm.
- **Priority inversely proportional to period**: shorter period → higher priority.
- Optimal among fixed-priority algorithms (Liu & Layland, 1973).
- Schedulability bound: $U \leq N(2^{1/N} - 1)$.

**Step 1 — Compute individual utilizations:**

$$U_1 = 2/8 = 0.250$$
$$U_2 = 3/12 = 0.250$$
$$U_3 = 4/24 = 0.167$$

**Step 2 — Total utilization:**

$$U = 0.250 + 0.250 + 0.167 = 0.667$$

**Step 3 — Bound for N = 3:**

$$U_{bound}(3) = 3(2^{1/3} - 1) = 3(1.2599 - 1) = 0.7798$$

**Step 4 — Compare:**

$$U = 0.667 \leq 0.7798 = U_{bound}(3) \quad \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:** The task set is **schedulable under RMS**. All deadlines will be met.

**Step 5 — Exact Response Time Analysis (for full marks):**

For T3 (lowest priority):
- $R_3^{(0)} = C_3 = 4$
- $R_3^{(1)} = 4 + \lceil 4/8 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 4/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 1\cdot2 + 1\cdot3 = 9$
- $R_3^{(2)} = 4 + \lceil 9/8 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 9/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2\cdot2 + 1\cdot3 = 11$
- $R_3^{(3)} = 4 + \lceil 11/8 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 11/12 \rceil \cdot 3 = 4 + 2\cdot2 + 1\cdot3 = 11$ (converged)
- $R_3 = 11 \text{ ms} \leq D_3 = 24 \text{ ms}$ ✓

**Worst-case response times for all tasks:**
- T1: $R_1 = C_1 = 2$ ms (no higher priority) → meets $D_1 = 8$ ms ✓
- T2: $R_2 = 3 + \lceil 3/8 \rceil \cdot 2 = 3 + 1\cdot 2 = 5$ ms → meets $D_2 = 12$ ms ✓
- T3: 11 ms → meets $D_3 = 24$ ms ✓

---

### **Question B2 (b)** — 7 Marks

**"Explain the need for thread synchronization in embedded systems. Compare mutexes and semaphores with suitable examples."**

**Model Answer:**

**Need for Synchronization:**

When multiple threads access shared resources (global variables, peripheral registers, buffers), **race conditions** occur because the order of thread execution is non-deterministic. This leads to:
- **Data corruption** — inconsistent reads/writes.
- **Deadlocks** — circular wait on resources.
- **Priority inversion** — discussed in Q1(b).
- **Non-deterministic behavior** — system becomes hard to test.

**Comparison: Mutex vs. Semaphore**

| Feature | Mutex | Semaphore |
|---|---|---|
| Value range | Binary (0 or 1) | Counting (0 to N) |
| Ownership | Yes — only owner can unlock | No ownership |
| Use case | Mutual exclusion | Signaling / resource pool |
| Priority inheritance | Yes (typically) | Not usually |
| Initial state | Unlocked | N (for N resources) |

**Example 1 — Mutex (protecting UART):**

```c
pthread_mutex_t uart_mtx = PTHREAD_MUTEX_INITIALIZER;

void send_uart(char c) {
    pthread_mutex_lock(&uart_mtx);
    UART_DR = c;                    // write to shared UART data register
    while (!(UART_SR & TX_COMPLETE));
    pthread_mutex_unlock(&uart_mtx);
}
```

Two threads calling `send_uart('A')` and `send_uart('B')` simultaneously will produce `'AB'` or `'BA'`, never interleaved bytes (no torn writes).

**Example 2 — Counting Semaphore (managing a 5-slot buffer pool):**

```c
sem_t buf_pool;
sem_init(&buf_pool, 0, 5);          // 5 buffers available

void *worker(void *arg) {
    sem_wait(&buf_pool);            // acquire a buffer (decrement count)
    use_buffer();
    release_buffer();
    sem_post(&buf_pool);            // return buffer (increment count)
}
```

Up to 5 worker threads can hold buffers simultaneously. The 6th thread calling `sem_wait()` will block until a buffer is released.

**Example 3 — Binary Semaphore (ISR-to-Task signaling):**

```c
sem_t data_ready;
void ISR_UART_RX(void) {
    sem_post(&data_ready);          // signal from ISR
}
void *task(void *arg) {
    while (1) {
        sem_wait(&data_ready);      // block until ISR signals
        process_received_byte();
    }
}
```

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout for B2(b):**
> 1. **Never use a semaphore where a mutex is required** — semaphores have no ownership, so unlocking from a non-acquirer thread is allowed. This breaks mutual exclusion and is the source of subtle bugs.
> 2. **Do not call `sem_wait()` inside an ISR** — it can block, which is illegal in interrupt context. Use `sem_trywait()` or signal FROM the ISR.
> 3. **Mutex destruction while held** — undefined behavior in POSIX. Always unlock before destroying.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam (print this):**

### **Core Concepts**
- ☐ Thread = lightweight execution unit inside a process. Shares address space with peer threads.
- ☐ Process = container; Thread = worker. Minimum one thread per process (main thread).
- ☐ TCB holds: state, PC, SP, registers, priority, stack bounds, wait-queue link, time slice.

### **States**
- ☐ NEW → READY → RUNNING ⇄ BLOCKED → TERMINATED.
- ☐ Only ONE thread RUNNING per core.
- ☐ BLOCKED threads are NOT in the ready queue.

### **Synchronization**
- ☐ Mutex = ownership + binary + priority inheritance (PIP).
- ☐ Semaphore = no ownership + counting + signaling.
- ☐ Always pair condition variables with a mutex.
- ☐ `pthread_join()` reaps TCB; `pthread_detach()` auto-cleans.

### **Scheduling**
- ☐ RMS: priority ∝ 1/period. Sufficient bound: $U_{bound}(N) = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$.
- ☐ As $N \to \infty$, $U_{bound} \to \ln 2 \approx 0.693$.
- ☐ RTA exact test: $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil \cdot C_j$.

### **Embedded-Specific**
- ☐ FreeRTOS stack overflow: `configCHECK_FOR_STACK_OVERFLOW = 1 or 2`.
- ☐ PIP is enabled by attribute in POSIX and by config in FreeRTOS.
- ☐ `configUSE_TIME_SLICING = 1` enables Round-Robin among equal-priority tasks.
- ☐ Idle task has priority 0 (lowest) — runs when no other task is ready.

### **Numerical Formulas to Memorize**
- ☐ $U_i = C_i / T_i$
- ☐ $U = \sum_i C_i / T_i$
- ☐ $U_{bound}(N) = N(2^{1/N} - 1)$
- ☐ $\lim_{N \to \infty} U_{bound}(N) = \ln 2 \approx 0.693$
- ☐ Stack size = locals + call frames + ISR nesting + margin

### **Common Exam Traps**
- ☐ "Process" and "thread" used interchangeably in informal questions — read carefully.
- ☐ Blocked vs Suspended — Suspended is not in any queue, only resumed by another thread.
- ☐ Deadlock vs Priority Inversion — both involve blocking, but PIP only fixes the latter.
- ☐ POSIX `pthread` ≠ Linux kernel thread — they are related via NPTL, but distinct.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Processes and Scheduling: Putting them altogether
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Processes and Scheduling: Putting them altogether

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Advanced Embedded Systems**, *Processes and Scheduling: Putting them altogether* refers to the **systematic integration of the four fundamental mechanisms** of a Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) kernel — **Process Control Blocks (PCBs), Process States, Schedulers, and Context Switching** — into a single, deterministic, and unified execution model.

A *process* (or *task*, in RTOS parlance) is an independent, sequentially executed program flow that the kernel manages through a **Process/Task Control Block (TCB)**, while *scheduling* is the kernel's policy of deciding **which ready process gets the CPU at any given instant**, based on a chosen algorithm (e.g., **Rate Monotonic Scheduling (RMS)**, **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)**, or **Round-Robin**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (CO Mapped):**
> "Putting together processes and scheduling" = the engineering act of *binding* the **Process Control Block (PCB) data structure** to the **Scheduler (decision logic)**, the **Dispatcher (execution logic)**, and the **Context Switcher (state preservation logic)** so that a multi-tasking embedded application can be designed, analyzed for *schedulability*, and deployed on a deterministic target.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Air Traffic Control Tower

Imagine a **single-runway airport** with **five aircraft** (your embedded tasks) circling overhead, each holding a different **urgency level** (deadline), a different **flight time on runway** (execution time `C`), and a different **arrival periodicity** (period `T`).

| Airport Entity | Embedded RTOS Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Runway | **CPU** (single execution core) |
| Aircraft circling | **Ready Queue** of processes |
| Air Traffic Controller (ATC) | **Scheduler** (decision-maker) |
| ATC's microphone instructions | **Dispatcher** (action-taker) |
| Pilot handing over the controls to next pilot | **Context Switch** |
| Aircraft flight log (fuel, route, passengers) | **Process Control Block (PCB/TCB)** |
| Pre-departure checklist / Post-landing | **Save / Restore Context** |

**Why the analogy works for KTU students:**
- Only **one aircraft lands at a time** → only one process runs on the CPU.
- The ATC must decide **which plane lands first** based on priority/urgency → the scheduler decides which TCB gets the CPU.
- The pilot must hand over the runway (CPU) to the next aircraft, preserving all flight state → the dispatcher performs a **context switch**, saving registers of the outgoing process into its PCB and loading the registers of the incoming process.

> [!NOTE]
> **"Putting it altogether"** is *not* a separate algorithm — it is the **architectural glue** that connects PCB ↔ Scheduler ↔ Dispatcher ↔ Context Switcher. Without all four working in unison, a multi-tasking kernel cannot exist.

## 1.3 Why This Topic is *High-Yield* in KTU 2024 ESE

This topic is **frequently examined** because it tests **all four Course Outcomes (COs)** of Module 3 simultaneously:

- **CO3** — Design task structures for concurrent embedded applications.
- **CO4** — Analyze schedulability of real-time task sets.
- **CO5** — Evaluate the impact of context switches and overhead on real-time guarantees.
- **CO6** — Justify the choice of scheduling algorithm for a given embedded workload.

A single 14-mark question on this topic can therefore assess the full **Bloom's L4–L6 cognitive range** (Analyze, Evaluate, Create), which is exactly the KTU 2024 Scheme's ESE (End Semester Evaluation) philosophy under NEP 2020.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Process Scheduling

### Pillar 1: Process Control Block (PCB / TCB)

A **Process Control Block** is a kernel-allocated data structure (usually in RAM) that stores *everything the kernel needs to know* about a process so it can be stopped and later resumed *transparently*. It is the **"identity card"** of the process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical PCB Fields (Embedded Cortex-M / FreeRTOS Style):**
> - `task_id` — unique process identifier
> - `state` — current state (Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended)
> - `priority` — static or dynamic priority value
> - `stack_pointer (SP)` — saved top-of-stack address
> - `program_counter (PC)` — saved return address / next instruction
> - `general_purpose_registers` — R0–R12 (Cortex-M)
> - `xPSR` — program status register (flags, interrupt state)
> - `period T_i` — task period
> - `execution_time C_i` — worst-case execution time (WCET)
> - `deadline D_i` — relative or absolute deadline
> - `next_release_time` — when task will next become ready
> - `event_flags` / `semaphore_handles` — synchronization primitives

### Pillar 2: Process States & Transitions

A process in an embedded RTOS exists in one of **four canonical states**:

```
   ┌──────────┐   create   ┌──────────┐  dispatch  ┌──────────┐
   │  (none)  │ ─────────► │  READY   │ ─────────► │ RUNNING  │
   └──────────┘            └──────────┘            └──────────┘
                                   ▲                     │
                                   │ preemption /        │
                                   │ time-slice expiry  ▼
                                ┌──────────┐
                                │ BLOCKED  │  ◄── I/O, semaphore wait
                                └──────────┘
                                       │
                                       │ event satisfied
                                       ▼
                                   (back to READY)
```

- **Ready** — eligible to run, waiting for CPU.
- **Running** — currently executing on the CPU (only ONE process at a time on a single core).
- **Blocked** — waiting for an event (I/O completion, semaphore, timer expiry).
- **Suspended** (optional) — explicitly paused by another process or kernel API.

### Pillar 3: The Scheduler (Decision-Maker)

The **scheduler** is a pure function `Scheduler() → task_id` that examines the ready queue and the current time, then returns the ID of the next process to run.

**Two broad families used in KTU-relevant embedded systems:**

| Family | Examples | Used In |
|---|---|---|
| **Static-priority** | Rate Monotonic, Deadline Monotonic | Hard real-time (RMS is the KTU classic) |
| **Dynamic-priority** | Earliest Deadline First (EDF), Least Laxity First (LLF) | Dynamic real-time systems |
| **Time-sharing** | Round-Robin, Weighted Round-Robin | Embedded Linux, soft RT |

### Pillar 4: The Dispatcher & Context Switch

The **dispatcher** is the *mechanism* that physically performs the chosen schedule. Its work is the **context switch**:

1. Save CPU registers of the **outgoing** process into its PCB (on the process's stack).
2. Update the outgoing process's PCB `state` field from `Running` → `Ready` (or `Blocked`).
3. Load CPU registers of the **incoming** process from its PCB.
4. Update the incoming process's PCB `state` field from `Ready` → `Running`.
5. Return-from-interrupt / branch to incoming process.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Context switch time `T_cs` is pure OVERHEAD.** It is **not useful work**. A well-designed embedded kernel guarantees `T_cs < 1%` of the shortest task period to avoid deadline misses.

## 2.2 Putting It All Together — The Kernel Execution Loop

The unified flow in an embedded RTOS is:

```
while (system_powered) {
    current_time = hardware_timer_read();
    update_delayed_tasks(current_time);          // unblock tasks whose events have fired
    next_task = Scheduler(ready_queue, current_time);  // DECIDE
    Dispatcher(next_task);                        // ACT
    // ↳ context switch happens here, then
    // ↳ next_task runs until preempted / blocked / completed
}
```

This is the **"putting them altogether"** moment: PCB provides the *state*, Scheduler provides the *policy*, Dispatcher provides the *mechanism*, and the timer interrupt provides the *trigger*.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL: KTU examiners expect students to MEMORIZE and APPLY the formulas below without derivation prompts.** Reproduce them exactly with the same symbols in your answer scripts.

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **CPU Utilization** | $U \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \dfrac{C_i}{T_i}$ | Fraction of CPU used by all `n` tasks. Must be `≤ 1` for feasibility. |
| 2 | **RMS Schedulability Test (Liu & Layland)** | $U \;\le\; n\!\left(2^{1/n} - 1\right)$ | Sufficient (not necessary) condition for RMS-feasibility. Bound → ln 2 ≈ **0.693** as `n → ∞`. |
| 3 | **RMS Priority Rule** | Shorter $T_i$ → Higher priority | Assign priorities by **period length**, not by execution time or importance. |
| 4 | **EDF Feasibility** | $U \le 1.0$ | Necessary *and* sufficient (single processor). |
| 5 | **Response Time Equation** | $R_i \;=\; C_i \;+\; \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \left\lceil \dfrac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j$ | Fixed-point iteration. Task `i` is schedulable iff `R_i ≤ D_i`. |
| 6 | **Hyperperiod** | $H \;=\; \mathrm{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ | Repetition interval of the schedule. All analysis done on `[0, H)`. |
| 7 | **Effective CPU Utilization (with overhead)** | $U_{\text{eff}} \;=\; \sum \dfrac{C_i + T_{cs}}{T_i}$ | Real-world schedulability, accounts for context-switch cost. |
| 8 | **Stack Size Rule of Thumb** | $\text{Stack}_{\min} \ge C_{\max} \cdot s_{\text{reg}} \cdot n_{\text{nest}} + 64$ | `C_max`=max call depth, `s_reg`=stack frame size, +64B for ISR margin. |
| 9 | **Maximum Context-Switch Overhead Budget** | $T_{cs} \;<\; 0.01 \cdot T_{\min}$ | Keep CS time < 1% of shortest task period. |
| 10 | **Priority Inversion Bound (Priority Ceiling Protocol)** | Blocking ≤ 1 critical-section execution | PCP prevents unbounded inversion. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** These formulas are used *daily* by embedded firmware engineers designing ECUs for **automotive (AUTOSAR)**, **avionics (ARINC 653)**, **medical devices (FDA IEC 62304)**, and **industrial PLC** systems. A misapplied RMS bound can cause **field recalls costing millions of dollars**.

## 2.4 Why the "Putting it altogether" Design is Critical

In a **bare-metal** microcontroller, the firmware is a single `while(1)` loop — no scheduler, no PCB, no context switch. The moment a system needs:

- more than one **logical activity** (e.g., sensor read + motor control + UI update),
- **deterministic timing guarantees**, or
- **independent software modules** developed by different teams,

…then a **scheduled multi-process model becomes mandatory**. "Putting it altogether" is the engineering act of *constructing* that model from the four pillars so the embedded system becomes **modular, analyzable, and certifiable**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION_3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Rate Monotonic Schedulability Bound

We derive the **RMS upper bound** $n(2^{1/n} - 1)$ from first principles to satisfy KTU's L4 (Analyze) requirement.

### 3.1.1 Setup

Consider `n` periodic, independent, preemptive tasks with:
- Period $T_i$ (constant, no jitter)
- Worst-case execution time $C_i$
- Deadline $D_i = T_i$ (constrained deadline)
- Static priority: priority of task `i` > task `j` if and only if $T_i < T_j$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Assumption for the Liu & Layland proof:** The *critical instant* (worst-case response time) for any task `i` occurs when **all higher-priority tasks are released simultaneously with task `i` at `t = 0`**.

### 3.1.2 Worst-Case Response Time at the Critical Instant

At the critical instant `t = 0`, task `i` must complete its work before being preempted `k` times by each higher-priority task `j`, where `k` is the number of times task `j` releases during `[0, R_i]`:

$$
k_{j}(R_i) \;=\; \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil
$$

So the total work that preempts task `i` during `[0, R_i]` is:

$$
W_{i}(R_i) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

The **response time** $R_i$ is the smallest fixed point satisfying:

$$
R_i \;=\; C_i \;+\; W_i(R_i) \;=\; C_i \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left\lceil \frac{R_i}{T_j} \right\rceil C_j
$$

### 3.1.3 Relaxation of the Ceiling Function

For an *upper bound* we can drop the ceiling (because $\lceil x \rceil \le x + 1$):

$$
R_i \;\le\; C_i \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \left( \frac{R_i}{T_j} + 1 \right) C_j
$$

We are looking for the **boundary utilization** at which $R_i = T_i$ (task just meets its deadline). Setting $R_i = T_i$ and dividing by $T_i$:

$$
1 \;\le\; \frac{C_i}{T_i} \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \;+\; \sum_{j=1}^{i-1} \frac{C_j}{T_i}
$$

Since $T_j \ge T_i$ for all $j < i$ (RMS priority rule), we have $C_j / T_i \le C_j / T_j$. Dropping the term for *task i itself* gives the **classical RMS bound**:

$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} \frac{C_j}{T_j} \;\le\; n \left( 2^{1/n} - 1 \right)
$$

### 3.1.4 Numerical Values (MUST memorize for KTU)

| n | Bound $n(2^{1/n}-1)$ | Decimal |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $1(2^1 - 1)$ | **1.0000** |
| 2 | $2(\sqrt{2} - 1)$ | **0.8284** |
| 3 | $3(\sqrt[3]{2} - 1)$ | **0.7798** |
| 4 | $4(\sqrt[4]{2} - 1)$ | **0.7568** |
| $\infty$ | $\ln 2$ | **0.6931** |

## 3.2 Worked Example: Schedulability of a 3-Task Set

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3 — adapted]**

**Task Set:**

| Task | Period `T` (ms) | Execution `C` (ms) | Priority (RMS) |
|---|---|---|---|
| τ1 | 5 | 1 | Highest |
| τ2 | 10 | 2 | Middle |
| τ3 | 20 | 4 | Lowest |

### Step 1: Compute CPU Utilization

$$
U \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{3} \frac{C_i}{T_i}
$$

$$
U \;=\; \frac{1}{5} + \frac{2}{10} + \frac{4}{20}
$$

$$
U \;=\; 0.2000 + 0.2000 + 0.2000
$$

$$
U \;=\; 0.6000
$$

### Step 2: Apply Liu & Layland Bound for `n = 3`

$$
\text{Bound} \;=\; 3\left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) \;=\; 3\left(\sqrt[3]{2} - 1\right) \;\approx\; 0.7798
$$

### Step 3: Compare

$$
U \;=\; 0.6000 \;\le\; 0.7798 \;\checkmark
$$

**Conclusion:** Task set is **RMS-feasible** by the sufficient test.

### Step 4: Verify with Exact Response-Time Analysis (RTA)

**For τ1** (highest priority, no preemption):

$$
R_1 \;=\; C_1 \;=\; 1.0 \text{ ms} \;\le\; D_1 = 5 \text{ ms} \;\checkmark
$$

**For τ2:** iterate $R_2^{(k+1)} = C_2 + \lceil R_2^{(k)}/T_1 \rceil C_1$

$$
R_2^{(0)} = 2.0
$$

$$
R_2^{(1)} = 2.0 + \lceil 2.0/5 \rceil \cdot 1.0 = 2.0 + 1 \cdot 1.0 = 3.0
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 2.0 + \lceil 3.0/5 \rceil \cdot 1.0 = 2.0 + 1 \cdot 1.0 = 3.0 \quad (\text{fixed point})
$$

$$
R_2 = 3.0 \text{ ms} \;\le\; D_2 = 10 \text{ ms} \;\checkmark
$$

**For τ3:** iterate $R_3^{(k+1)} = C_3 + \lceil R_3^{(k)}/T_1 \rceil C_1 + \lceil R_3^{(k)}/T_2 \rceil C_2$

$$
R_3^{(0)} = 4.0
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 4.0 + \lceil 4.0/5 \rceil \cdot 1.0 + \lceil 4.0/10 \rceil \cdot 2.0 = 4.0 + 1 \cdot 1.0 + 1 \cdot 2.0 = 7.0
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 4.0 + \lceil 7.0/5 \rceil \cdot 1.0 + \lceil 7.0/10 \rceil \cdot 2.0 = 4.0 + 2 \cdot 1.0 + 1 \cdot 2.0 = 8.0
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 4.0 + \lceil 8.0/5 \rceil \cdot 1.0 + \lceil 8.0/10 \rceil \cdot 2.0 = 4.0 + 2 \cdot 1.0 + 1 \cdot 2.0 = 8.0 \quad (\text{fixed point})
$$

$$
R_3 = 8.0 \text{ ms} \;\le\; D_3 = 20 \text{ ms} \;\checkmark
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All three tasks meet their deadlines.** Both the sufficient test (Liu & Layland) and the exact RTA confirm schedulability. The RTA is *necessary and sufficient*; the Liu-Layland bound is only *sufficient* (i.e., it may reject feasible task sets).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Property | Rate Monotonic (RMS) | Earliest Deadline First (EDF) | Round-Robin (RR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Priority assignment | Static (by period) | Dynamic (by absolute deadline) | Time-quantum circular |
| Optimal for | Fixed priorities | Uniprocessor (any task set `U < 1`) | Time-sharing fairness |
| Schedulability bound | $n(2^{1/n}-1) \le 0.693$ | $U \le 1.0$ (exact) | None (best-effort) |
| Overhead at runtime | Low (priority comparison) | Higher (deadline updates) | Highest (timer ticks) |
| Predictability | Excellent | Excellent (when feasible) | Poor for hard RT |
| KTU relevance | ★★★★★ | ★★★★ | ★★ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 rules, all node IDs below are **purely alphanumeric** (e.g., `node1`, `stateA`, `schedA`) and **no markdown formatting** appears inside the double-quoted labels.

## 4.2 Diagram 1 — Process State Transition Diagram

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> stateNew : create
    stateNew --> stateReady : initialize TCB
    stateReady --> stateRunning : dispatcher grant
    stateRunning --> stateReady : preempted OR time slice expired
    stateRunning --> stateBlocked : wait event OR I/O OR semaphore
    stateBlocked --> stateReady : event fired OR I/O complete
    stateRunning --> [*] : terminate
    stateBlocked --> stateSuspended : suspend call
    stateSuspended --> stateReady : resume call
    stateReady --> stateSuspended : suspend call
```

## 4.3 Diagram 2 — Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Scheduler

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HW Timer Interrupt Tick] --> B[Kernel Tick Handler]
    B --> C[Update Delayed Task List]
    C --> D[Move Expired Tasks to Ready Queue]
    D --> E[Scheduler Decision Function]
    E --> F{Ready Queue Empty?}
    F -- yes --> G[Run Idle Task]
    F -- no --> H[Pick Highest Priority TCB]
    H --> I[Outgoing PCB Save Registers]
    I --> J[Update Outgoing State to Ready]
    J --> K[Incoming PCB Restore Registers]
    K --> L[Update Incoming State to Running]
    L --> M[Branch to Incoming Task]
    G --> N[Wait Next Tick]
    M --> N
    N --> A
```

## 4.4 Diagram 3 — PCB / TCB Memory Layout (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Top of Stack] --> B[Saved R4 to R11]
    B --> C[Saved R12]
    C --> D[Saved LR Link Register]
    E[TCB Structure Header] --- F[task_id field]
    E --- G[state field]
    E --- H[priority field]
    E --- I[stack pointer field]
    E --- J[period T_i field]
    E --- K[execution C_i field]
    E --> L[pointer to top of stack]
    L --> A
```

## 4.5 Diagram 4 — Putting-it-All-Together Sequence

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant HWTimer as Hardware Timer
    participant Kernel as Kernel Tick Handler
    participant Sched as Scheduler
    participant Disp as Dispatcher
    participant TaskA as Task A Running
    participant TaskB as Task B Ready

    HWTimer->>Kernel: Tick at t=0
    Kernel->>Kernel: Unblock expired tasks
    Kernel->>Sched: schedule ready_queue
    Sched-->>Kernel: choose TaskA (higher priority)
    Kernel->>Disp: dispatch TaskA
    Note over TaskA: Already running, no switch
    HWTimer->>Kernel: Tick at t=2 ms
    Kernel->>Sched: schedule ready_queue
    Sched-->>Kernel: choose TaskB (newly ready, higher priority)
    Kernel->>Disp: dispatch TaskB
    Disp->>TaskA: save TaskA context to TCB_A
    TaskA-->>Disp: TCB_A saved
    Disp->>TaskB: restore TaskB context from TCB_B
    TaskB-->>Disp: TCB_B loaded
    Note over TaskB: Now executing
```

## 4.6 Diagram 5 — Round-Robin Time Slicing Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1[Task 1 TCB] --> Q2[Task 2 TCB]
    Q2 --> Q3[Task 3 TCB]
    Q3 --> Q4[Task 1 TCB]
    Q4 -. quantum expiry .-> Q2
    Q2 -. quantum expiry .-> Q3
    Q3 -. quantum expiry .-> Q1
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a Process Control Block. List any six fields stored in a typical PCB used in an embedded RTOS.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Process Control Block (PCB)**, also called a **Task Control Block (TCB)**, is a kernel-managed data structure in RAM that stores *all the state information* required to suspend, store, and later resume execution of a process. *(1 mark)*

Typical fields: *(2 marks — list any 6)*

1. `task_id` — unique process identifier
2. `state` — current state (Ready, Running, Blocked, Suspended)
3. `priority` — static or dynamic priority
4. `stack_pointer` — pointer to the saved top-of-stack
5. `program_counter` — saved return address
6. `general_purpose_registers` — saved values of R0–R12
7. `period T_i` and `execution_time C_i` — for real-time analysis
8. `event_flags` / `semaphore_handles` — synchronization primitives

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is context switching in an RTOS? Why is its execution time considered pure overhead?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Context switching** is the kernel mechanism of *saving* the execution context (registers, PC, PSR, stack pointer) of the **outgoing process** into its PCB and *restoring* the context of the **incoming process** from its PCB, so that multi-tasking appears seamless. *(1.5 marks)*

It is considered **pure overhead** because during a context switch: *(1.5 marks)*

- The CPU is **not executing any user task** (no useful work is done).
- It consumes **wall-clock time** that reduces the CPU bandwidth available for periodic deadlines.
- For hard real-time systems, `T_cs` must be **< 1% of the shortest task period**, otherwise schedulability guarantees are violated.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Rule (2024 Scheme):** Each Part B question offers an **internal choice** between two alternatives (`OR`). Both alternatives are given below; students answer **only ONE**.

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 3]

**(a) Explain the four fundamental components that must be "put together" to implement a complete process scheduling mechanism in an embedded RTOS. (7 marks)**

**(b) For the task set given below, determine whether the task set is schedulable under Rate Monotonic Scheduling. Use the Liu & Layland sufficient test AND exact response-time analysis. State clearly all assumptions. (7 marks)**

| Task | Period `T` (ms) | Execution `C` (ms) |
|---|---|---|
| τ1 | 6 | 1 |
| τ2 | 12 | 3 |
| τ3 | 24 | 4 |

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

The four fundamental components that must be "put together" to implement a complete process scheduling mechanism in an embedded RTOS are:

**1. Process Control Block (PCB) — (2 marks)**
The PCB is a kernel-allocated data structure in RAM that serves as the *identity card* of every process. It stores:
- Process state (Ready / Running / Blocked / Suspended)
- Saved CPU registers, stack pointer, program counter
- Priority, period `T_i`, execution time `C_i`
- Synchronization handles (semaphores, queues)

**2. Process States and Transitions — (1 mark)**
A process migrates through the four canonical states: **Ready → Running → Blocked → Ready**, with transitions triggered by the scheduler (dispatch / preempt), events (block / unblock), and kernel API calls (suspend / resume).

**3. Scheduler (Decision Logic) — (2 marks)**
The scheduler is a *pure function* invoked by the kernel tick handler. It examines the ready queue and selects the next process. Examples:
- **Rate Monotonic (RMS):** static priority, shorter period → higher priority.
- **Earliest Deadline First (EDF):** dynamic priority, earliest absolute deadline → highest priority.
- **Round-Robin:** time-sliced circular selection among equal-priority processes.

**4. Dispatcher and Context Switch Mechanism — (2 marks)**
The dispatcher physically transfers the CPU from the outgoing to the incoming process by:
- Saving outgoing PCB state (registers, PC, xPSR, SP) onto the outgoing process's stack.
- Updating the outgoing process's `state` field to `Ready` or `Blocked`.
- Restoring incoming PCB state from the incoming process's stack.
- Updating the incoming process's `state` field to `Running`.
- Branching to the incoming process's program counter.

> The **"putting them altogether"** integration is realized in the kernel's main loop: on every tick, **unblock expired tasks → schedule → dispatch → run**, with the **timer interrupt** providing the cyclic trigger and the **PCB** providing the persistent state.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1: Assumptions (1 mark)**

- Periodic, independent, preemptive tasks.
- $D_i = T_i$ (relative deadline = period).
- Single-processor, static priorities.
- Worst-case execution time `C_i` is constant.
- Context-switch time $T_{cs} = 0$ (ignored, or included in `C_i`).

**Step 2: Assign priorities by RMS rule (0.5 mark)**
- τ1: T = 6 ms → **Highest priority** (P1)
- τ2: T = 12 ms → Middle priority (P2)
- τ3: T = 24 ms → Lowest priority (P3)

**Step 3: Compute CPU Utilization (1 mark)**

$$
U \;=\; \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \frac{C_3}{T_3} \;=\; \frac{1}{6} + \frac{3}{12} + \frac{4}{24}
$$

$$
U \;=\; 0.1667 + 0.2500 + 0.1667 \;=\; 0.5833
$$

**Step 4: Apply Liu & Layland bound for n = 3 (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Bound} \;=\; 3\!\left(2^{1/3} - 1\right) \;\approx\; 0.7798
$$

$$
U = 0.5833 \;\le\; 0.7798 \;\checkmark
$$

The sufficient test **passes**; the task set is potentially RMS-feasible.

**Step 5: Exact Response-Time Analysis (3 marks)**

**For τ1:** $R_1 = C_1 = 1.0$ ms ≤ 6 ms ✓

**For τ2:** iterate $R_2^{(k+1)} = C_2 + \lceil R_2^{(k)}/T_1 \rceil C_1$

$$
R_2^{(0)} = 3.0
$$

$$
R_2^{(1)} = 3.0 + \lceil 3.0/6 \rceil \cdot 1.0 = 3.0 + 1 = 4.0
$$

$$
R_2^{(2)} = 3.0 + \lceil 4.0/6 \rceil \cdot 1.0 = 3.0 + 1 = 4.0 \quad (\text{fixed point})
$$

$R_2 = 4.0$ ms ≤ 12 ms ✓

**For τ3:** iterate $R_3^{(k+1)} = C_3 + \lceil R_3^{(k)}/T_1 \rceil C_1 + \lceil R_3^{(k)}/T_2 \rceil C_2$

$$
R_3^{(0)} = 4.0
$$

$$
R_3^{(1)} = 4.0 + \lceil 4.0/6 \rceil \cdot 1.0 + \lceil 4.0/12 \rceil \cdot 3.0 = 4.0 + 1 + 1 \cdot 3.0 = 8.0
$$

$$
R_3^{(2)} = 4.0 + \lceil 8.0/6 \rceil \cdot 1.0 + \lceil 8.0/12 \rceil \cdot 3.0 = 4.0 + 2 + 1 \cdot 3.0 = 9.0
$$

$$
R_3^{(3)} = 4.0 + \lceil 9.0/6 \rceil \cdot 1.0 + \lceil 9.0/12 \rceil \cdot 3.0 = 4.0 + 2 + 1 \cdot 3.0 = 9.0 \quad (\text{fixed point})
$$

$R_3 = 9.0$ ms ≤ 24 ms ✓

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):** All three tasks meet their deadlines. The task set is **RMS-schedulable** by both the sufficient Liu–Layland test and the exact response-time analysis.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, adapted]

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the process state transition diagram in an RTOS. Discuss the role of the dispatcher and context switcher. (7 marks)**

**(b) Two periodic tasks share a single-processor system: τ1 has T1 = 8 ms, C1 = 2 ms; τ2 has T2 = 16 ms, C2 = 4 ms. (i) Compute the maximum CPU utilization. (ii) Test for RMS schedulability. (iii) Comment on what happens if a third task τ3 with T3 = 32 ms, C3 = 8 ms is added. (7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Process State Diagram (3 marks)** — refer to the diagram in SECTION 4.2:

A newly created process enters the **New** state, is initialized (TCB set up, stack allocated), then moves to **Ready**. The dispatcher grants CPU → **Running**. From Running, three transitions are possible:

- To **Ready**: time-slice expiry (Round-Robin) or preemption by a higher-priority process.
- To **Blocked**: voluntary wait on I/O, semaphore, message queue, or timer.
- To **Terminated**: process finishes execution.

From Blocked, the process returns to Ready when its awaited event fires. Some kernels also support an explicit **Suspended** state, used to pause a task via API call.

**Role of the Dispatcher (2 marks)**
The dispatcher is the kernel module that *physically realizes* the scheduler's decision. It:
- Invokes the **context-save** routine for the outgoing process.
- Updates the outgoing process's PCB (`state`, `stack_pointer`).
- Invokes the **context-restore** routine for the incoming process.
- Performs a context switch via a `PendSV` exception (ARM Cortex-M) or equivalent.

**Role of the Context Switcher (2 marks)**
The context switcher is the low-level assembly routine that:
- Saves the outgoing CPU registers (R0–R12, LR, PC, xPSR) onto the outgoing stack using `PUSH` instructions.
- Stores the updated stack pointer into the outgoing TCB.
- Loads the incoming stack pointer from the incoming TCB.
- Restores the incoming CPU registers using `POP` instructions.
- Executes a `BX` or `ISB` to branch into the incoming process seamlessly.

The combined dispatcher + context switcher is what makes multi-tasking *appear* simultaneous on a single-core CPU.

---

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Step (i): CPU Utilization (1.5 marks)**

$$
U_{\text{original}} \;=\; \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} \;=\; \frac{2}{8} + \frac{4}{16} \;=\; 0.2500 + 0.2500 \;=\; 0.5000
$$

**Step (ii): RMS Schedulability Test (2 marks)**

For `n = 2`:

$$
\text{Bound} \;=\; 2\left(2^{1/2} - 1\right) \;=\; 2(\sqrt{2} - 1) \;\approx\; 0.8284
$$

$$
U = 0.5000 \;\le\; 0.8284 \;\checkmark
$$

The two-task set is **RMS-feasible** by the Liu–Layland sufficient test.

Verify with RTA:
- $R_1 = C_1 = 2$ ms ≤ 8 ms ✓
- $R_2 = 4 + \lceil R_2/8 \rceil \cdot 2$ → $R_2^{(0)} = 4$, $R_2^{(1)} = 4 + 1\cdot 2 = 6$, $R_2^{(2)} = 4 + 1\cdot 2 = 6$ (fixed point). $R_2 = 6$ ms ≤ 16 ms ✓

**Step (iii): Adding τ3 (3.5 marks)**

New total utilization:

$$
U_{\text{new}} \;=\; \frac{2}{8} + \frac{4}{16} + \frac{8}{32} \;=\; 0.2500 + 0.2500 + 0.2500 \;=\; 0.7500
$$

For `n = 3`:

$$
\text{Bound} \;=\; 3(2^{1/3} - 1) \;\approx\; 0.7798
$$

$$
U_{\text{new}} = 0.7500 \;\le\; 0.7798 \;\checkmark
$$

The three-task set is **still RMS-feasible** by the sufficient test.

Verify with exact RTA:
- $R_1 = 2$ ms ≤ 8 ms ✓
- $R_2 = 4 + \lceil R_2/8 \rceil \cdot 2$ → fixed point $R_2 = 6$ ms ≤ 16 ms ✓
- $R_3 = 8 + \lceil R_3/8 \rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil R_3/16 \rceil \cdot 4$:
  - $R_3^{(0)} = 8$
  - $R_3^{(1)} = 8 + 1\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 4 = 14$
  - $R_3^{(2)} = 8 + \lceil 14/8\rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 14/16\rceil \cdot 4 = 8 + 2\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 4 = 16$
  - $R_3^{(3)} = 8 + \lceil 16/8\rceil \cdot 2 + \lceil 16/16\rceil \cdot 4 = 8 + 2\cdot 2 + 1\cdot 4 = 16$ (fixed point)
  - $R_3 = 16$ ms ≤ 32 ms ✓

**Conclusion:** All three tasks are **schedulable under RMS**, but utilization is dangerously close to the bound (0.75 vs 0.7798). A small increase in any `C_i` or addition of a fourth task may push the system over the bound and require a switch to **Earliest Deadline First (EDF)** or a higher-frequency CPU.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **Mixing up C_i/T_i order:** Many students write $\frac{T_i}{C_i}$ in the utilization formula. *Marks deducted: 1–2 per instance.*
> 2. **Forgetting iteration in RTA:** Showing only the first iteration without proving it is a *fixed point* loses the "verify convergence" credit.
> 3. **Confusing sufficient vs necessary test:** The Liu–Layland bound is **sufficient, NOT necessary**. Saying "task set is infeasible" because `U > 0.693` for large n is **wrong** — the exact RTA may still pass.
> 4. **Missing context-switch overhead:** Real schedulability must include $T_{cs}$. KTU 2024 questions often include a sentence like "with $T_{cs} = 0.05$ ms per switch" — if ignored, you lose 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Priority inversion:** If a Part B question mentions shared resources / mutexes, you must discuss **priority inheritance protocol** to avoid losing 2–3 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** — go through this 2 hours before the exam.

- [ ] **"Putting it altogether" = integration of 4 pillars:** PCB + States + Scheduler + Dispatcher (Context Switch).
- [ ] **PCB stores:** state, priority, saved SP/PC/registers, `T_i`, `C_i`, event handles.
- [ ] **Four canonical states:** New, Ready, Running, Blocked (plus Suspended in some kernels).
- [ ] **Context switch time `T_cs` is OVERHEAD** — keep < 1% of shortest period.
- [ ] **CPU utilization:** $U = \sum C_i / T_i$, must be ≤ 1.0 for feasibility.
- [ ] **RMS schedulability bound (Liu & Layland):** $U \le n(2^{1/n} - 1)$, limit ln 2 ≈ 0.693 as n → ∞.
- [ ] **Memorize the bound values:** n=1 → 1.0, n=2 → 0.828, n=3 → 0.780, n=4 → 0.757.
- [ ] **RMS priority rule:** shorter period → higher priority (NOT shortest execution time!).
- [ ] **RTA fixed-point iteration:** $R_i = C_i + \sum_{j \in hp(i)} \lceil R_i / T_j \rceil C_j$, repeat until $R_i$ converges.
- [ ] **EDF feasibility:** $U \le 1.0$ is necessary AND sufficient (single processor).
- [ ] **Hyperperiod** $H = \text{lcm}(T_1, T_2, \dots, T_n)$ — schedule repeats every `H`.
- [ ] **Effective utilization with overhead:** $U_{\text{eff}} = \sum (C_i + T_{cs}) / T_i$.
- [ ] **Priority inversion** can be **unbounded** → solved by **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** or **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)**.
- [ ] **Stack size rule:** `Stack ≥ max_call_depth × frame_size + ISR_margin (64B typical)`.
- [ ] **ARM Cortex-M context switch** uses the **PendSV** exception for deferred switching.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 exam pattern:** 3-mark Part A definitions + 14-mark Part B with sub-parts (a) and (b), internal choice.
- [ ] **Always state assumptions** explicitly in any schedulability question (preemptive, periodic, $D_i = T_i$, independent).
- [ ] **For n > 4 tasks**, the RMS bound becomes tight; consider EDF for higher utilizations.
- [ ] **Round-Robin is NOT suitable for hard real-time** — no deadline guarantees.
- [ ] **Critical instant assumption** of Liu & Layland: all higher-priority tasks released at `t = 0` with task `i`.
- [ ] **KTU 2024 specific:** Questions may ask to **draw the PCB memory layout** with stack + TCB relationships.
- [ ] **Don't forget context-switch cost** when a question provides $T_{cs}$ — this is a common 2-mark trap.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Task Communication
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT861 - ADVANCED EMBEDDED SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Task Communication

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Task Communication** in an embedded Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) refers to the set of mechanisms, protocols, and kernel services that enable two or more concurrent **tasks (threads/processes)** to exchange data, synchronize execution, and coordinate access to shared resources without violating system determinism, consistency, or real-time deadlines.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, task communication is categorized under **Inter-Process Communication (IPC)** in RTOS contexts and is typically studied in the light of kernels such as **FreeRTOS, μC/OS-II/III, VxWorks, and RT-Linux**.

The two principal pillars of task communication are:

1. **Data Exchange (Information Transfer):** Moving a payload (variable, buffer, message) from one task to another.
   - Mechanisms: *Mailboxes, Message Queues, Pipes, Shared Memory, Sockets*.
2. **Synchronization (Event Signaling):** Coordinating the *temporal ordering* of task execution.
   - Mechanisms: *Semaphores (Binary, Counting, Mutex), Event Flags, Signals, Condition Variables*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Terminology Mapping:**
> - "Tasks" = Threads/Processes scheduled by the RTOS kernel scheduler.
> - "Communication" = Both data movement AND synchronization.
> - "Shared Resource" = Any hardware peripheral (UART, ADC) or software object (linked list) accessed by >1 task.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Analogy 🏥

Imagine a busy **hospital emergency room** (the embedded system) with multiple **doctors** (tasks) working simultaneously.

- **Doctors need to talk to each other** about a patient → this is **Mailbox / Message Queue communication** (passing patient files through pneumatic tubes).
- **A doctor needs the X-ray machine** that another doctor is using → this is **Resource Synchronization via Semaphores/Mutexes** (only one key holder can use the machine).
- **A surgeon must wait until the anesthetist gives the green signal** → this is **Event Flag synchronization** (a one-shot or repeated trigger).
- **Two doctors writing on the same patient chart simultaneously** and overwriting each other → this is the **Race Condition**, prevented by Mutual Exclusion.

> [!TIP]
> **Why this analogy works for KTU:** Most Part-A questions in KTU 2024 exams ask "Explain task communication with a suitable example" — a hospital/factory analogy gives you a **3-mark answer in under 90 seconds** with full marks.

---

## 1.3 Key RTOS Metrics & Constants (Bolded for Recall)

| Term | Symbol / Value | KTU Significance |
|------|---------------|------------------|
| **Priority Inversion** | $PI$ | Classic problem costing the Mars Pathfinder mission (1997). |
| **Priority Inheritence Protocol** | $PIP$ | Default solution in μC/OS-II. |
| **Priority Ceiling Protocol** | $PCP$ | Used in VxWorks and POSIX. |
| **Context Switch Time** | $\tau_{cs}$ | Typically **1–10 μs** in modern RTOS. |
| **Interrupt Latency** | $\tau_{int}$ | Must be **bounded** (deterministic). |
| **IPC Overhead** | $O_{ipc}$ | Kernel-mediated, so non-zero. |
| **Jitter** | $J$ | Variation in task release/finish time; must be minimized. |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Synchronization** with **Communication**. Synchronization = *when* to run. Communication = *what* data to exchange. Both are needed in most real designs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Task Communication Mechanisms

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 3 of Advanced Embedded Systems) expects mastery of the following hierarchy:

### A. Message-Based Communication (Data Movement)
1. **Mailbox** — A fixed-size slot holding **one** message. Overwrite-on-full semantics common in μC/OS-II.
2. **Message Queue** — A FIFO buffer holding **N** messages of fixed/variable size. Used in FreeRTOS (`xQueueCreate`, `xQueueSend`, `xQueueReceive`).
3. **Pipe** — A unidirectional byte stream between two tasks (Linux-style, or named pipe in RT-Linux).
4. **Socket** — Network/IPC endpoint; used in networked embedded systems (IoT gateways).

### B. Shared-Memory Communication
5. **Shared Variables / Shared Memory** — Tasks read/write a common global buffer. **Fastest** mechanism but **unsynchronized = unsafe**.
6. **Double Buffering / Ring Buffers** — Lock-free or lock-protected producer-consumer patterns.
7. **Memory-Mapped I/O (MMIO)** — Sharing peripheral registers between an ISR and a task.

### C. Synchronization Primitives
8. **Semaphore** — A non-negative integer counter with atomic `wait()` and `signal()` operations.
9. **Mutex (Mutual Exclusion Lock)** — A *binary semaphore with ownership* (only the locking task can unlock).
10. **Event Flags / Event Groups** — A 32-bit (or larger) bitmask; tasks wait on specific bit patterns (AND/OR). FreeRTOS: `xEventGroupWaitBits`.
11. **Signals** — Asynchronous notifications to a specific task (similar to UNIX signals). FreeRTOS: `xTaskNotify` / `xTaskNotifyGive`.
12. **Condition Variables** — POSIX-style; task waits until a predicate becomes true.

---

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The Producer–Consumer Model

Almost every KTU question reduces to a **Producer** task and a **Consumer** task separated by a bounded buffer.

**Step-by-step logic (Bulleted):**

- **Step 1 — Initialization:** Kernel creates a synchronization primitive (e.g., a counting semaphore `emptySlots = N`, `fullSlots = 0`) and a mutual-exclusion lock on the buffer.
- **Step 2 — Producer Loop:**
  - Produces an item.
  - `wait(emptySlots)` → blocks if buffer is full.
  - `lock(mutex)` → enters critical section.
  - Writes item into buffer.
  - `unlock(mutex)` → exits critical section.
  - `signal(fullSlots)` → wakes consumer.
- **Step 3 — Consumer Loop:**
  - `wait(fullSlots)` → blocks if buffer is empty.
  - `lock(mutex)` → enters critical section.
  - Reads item from buffer.
  - `unlock(mutex)` → exits critical section.
  - `signal(emptySlots)` → wakes producer.
- **Step 4 — Termination:** Tasks delete themselves; kernel cleans up IPC objects.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Question KTU Asks:** Why use *two semaphores* (empty + full) and a mutex separately?
> **Answer:** The semaphores handle **count synchronization** (bounded capacity), while the mutex handles **mutual exclusion on the buffer structure itself** (preventing torn writes). Conflating them is a classic race condition.

---

## 2.3 The Four Conditions for Correct IPC (Kshemkalyani–Singhal Model)

For any message-passing system to be correct, the following four properties are *jointly sufficient* (often tested as a 7-mark sub-question):

1. **Safety (Mutual Exclusion):** No two tasks are simultaneously in the critical section for the same resource.
2. **Liveness (Progress):** If no task is in the CS and some task *wants* to enter, only those not in the remainder section (RS) can participate in decision, and selection cannot be postponed indefinitely.
3. **Bounded Waiting (Starvation Freedom):** There exists a bound on the number of times other tasks can enter CS after a task has requested entry and before it is granted.
4. **Fairness / FIFO Ordering (optional):** Requests are served in the order they are made (stronger condition).

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The table below uses `\mid` instead of vertical pipes inside expressions to preserve markdown table integrity.

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Mailbox Capacity** | $C_{mb} = 1$ message | Fixed slot; 4/8/16/32 bytes typical. |
| 2 | **Message Queue Capacity** | $C_{mq} = N$ messages, size $S$ bytes | Total buffer = $N \times S$ bytes. |
| 3 | **Semaphore Atomicity** | $\text{wait}(S): S \leftarrow S - 1 \text{ if } S > 0$ | **Atomic** — uninterruptible. |
| 4 | **Mutex Ownership** | $\text{owner}(\mu) = T_i \implies \text{only } T_i \text{ may unlock}$ | Differs from binary semaphore. |
| 5 | **Context Switch Time** | $\tau_{cs} = \tau_{\text{save}} + \tau_{\text{restore}} + \tau_{\text{sched}}$ | μs range. |
| 6 | **Priority Inversion Duration (worst case)** | $T_{PI} = \sum_{k \in L} T_k$ | Sum of execution times of **all medium-priority tasks** intervening. |
| 7 | **Event Flag Match (AND)** | $\text{match} = (F \mathbin{\&} M) = M$ | All bits in mask $M$ must be set. |
| 8 | **Event Flag Match (OR)** | $\text{match} = (F \mathbin{\&} M) \neq 0$ | Any bit suffices. |
| 9 | **Pipe Throughput** | $\Theta_{\text{pipe}} = \dfrac{L}{t_{\text{prop}} + t_{\text{copy}}}$ | Bytes per second. |
| 10 | **Deadlock (Coffman)** | Mutual Exclusion + Hold-and-Wait + No Preemption + Circular Wait | All four must hold. |
| 11 | **PIP Effective Priority** | $P_{\text{eff}}(T) = \max(P_{\text{base}}(T),\, P_{\text{waiting}}(R))$ | Boosted to requestor's priority. |
| 12 | **PCP Ceiling** | $\lceil R \rceil = \max_{T \,:\, \text{accesses}(R)} P(T)$ | Static resource ceiling. |
| 13 | **Buffer Occupancy (Producer)** | $n(t) = \text{produced}(t) - \text{consumed}(t)$ | $0 \le n \le N$. |
| 14 | **Bounded Blocking Time (PCP)** | $B_i \le \max_{k} \bigl(\lceil R_k \rceil\bigr)$ | Worst-case blocking per task. |
| 15 | **Bounded Blocking Time (PIP)** | $B_i \le \sum_{k} CS_k^{\text{crit}}$ | Sum of critical sections of lower-priority tasks. |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive (AUTOSAR):** COM module uses signals + PDUs (Protocol Data Units) for ECU-to-ECU task comms.
- **Aerospace (DO-178C):** Ravenscar profile restricts task comms to protected objects only.
- **Industrial IoT (OPC-UA, MQTT):** Message queues route telemetry between edge tasks and cloud proxies.
- **Medical Devices (IEC 62304):** Mailbox-based deterministic IPC for safety-critical patient monitors.
- **Robotics (ROS 2):** DDS (Data Distribution Service) over real-time publish-subscribe message queues.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Bonus Mark Tip:** Drop a 1-line example from **AUTOSAR or ROS 2** in your answer — most KTU paper evaluators award ½ to 1 mark extra for applied context.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Worst-Case Priority Inversion Duration

This is the **most-asked 7-mark derivation** under Module 3 in KTU past papers. We derive the unbounded blocking time that occurs *without* a solution protocol.

### Setup
- Three tasks: $T_H$ (high), $T_M$ (medium), $T_L$ (low) with priorities $P_H > P_M > P_L$.
- $T_L$ acquires a shared resource $R$ (e.g., a UART) at time $t = 0$.
- $T_H$ becomes ready at time $t = t_0$ and preempts $T_L$ (wait — $T_H$ needs $R$, so it *blocks* on $R$).
- $T_M$ (which does **not** need $R$) becomes ready and preempts $T_L$.

### Timeline
$$
\begin{aligned}
t = 0      &: T_L \text{ acquires } R. \\
t = t_0    &: T_H \text{ arrives, requests } R, \text{ blocks. } T_L \text{ resumes.} \\
t = t_1    &: T_M \text{ arrives, preempts } T_L. \\
t = t_2    &: T_M \text{ finishes (length } T_M \text{)}; T_L \text{ resumes.} \\
t = t_3    &: T_L \text{ releases } R; T_H \text{ acquires } R \text{ and runs.}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Derivation of blocking time on $T_H$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{BlockTime}(T_H) &= \text{Time from } t_0 \text{ (request)} \text{ to } t_3 \text{ (acquire)} \\
&= (t_2 - t_0) + (t_3 - t_2) \\
&= T_M + (\text{remainder of } T_L \text{ critical section}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

If **multiple** medium-priority tasks $T_{M_1}, T_{M_2}, \dots, T_{M_k}$ exist and each can preempt $T_L$ before $T_L$ releases $R$:

$$
\boxed{\,B_{\max}(T_H) \;=\; \sum_{j=1}^{k} T_{M_j} \;+\; T_L^{\text{remainder}}\;}
$$

This is **unbounded** because $k$ and $T_{M_j}$ are application-dependent and have no static upper bound enforced by the kernel.

### Effect of Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)

When $T_H$ blocks on $R$ held by $T_L$:
- $T_L$'s effective priority becomes $P_H$.
- All $T_{M_j}$ (with $P_{M_j} < P_H$) can **no longer** preempt $T_L$.
- $T_L$ runs to completion of CS, releases $R$, then $T_H$ acquires $R$ immediately.

Therefore:

$$
B_{\max}^{\text{PIP}}(T_H) \;=\; T_L^{\text{cs}}
$$

That is, $T_H$ blocks for **at most** the critical section length of $T_L$ — **bounded**.

### Effect of Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)

Each resource $R$ is assigned a ceiling $\lceil R \rceil$ (highest priority of any task that may lock it). A task may lock $R$ only if its priority is **strictly greater** than the ceilings of all resources currently locked by *other* tasks. This **prevents deadlocks** and bounds blocking:

$$
B_{\max}^{\text{PCP}}(T_i) \;=\; \max_{R_k \,:\, \text{accessed by lower-prio task}} \text{cs}(R_k)
$$

**A task can be blocked at most once** by at most one lower-priority task's critical section.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Producer–Consumer Throughput with Bounded Queue

Let:
- $T_p$ = period of producer task.
- $T_c$ = period of consumer task.
- $N$ = queue depth.
- $t_{cs}$ = critical section time per access.

### Steady-state condition
The system is in steady state when production rate equals consumption rate **on average**:

$$
\frac{1}{T_p} \;\le\; \frac{1}{T_c} \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad T_c \;\le\; T_p
$$

If $T_c > T_p$, the queue grows unboundedly and eventually overflows; $N$ must satisfy:

$$
N \;\ge\; \left\lceil \frac{T_p - T_c}{T_c} \right\rceil
$$

### Latency for a single message
$$
L_{\text{msg}} \;=\; t_{\text{produce}} \;+\; t_{\text{queue}} \;+\; t_{\text{consume}} \;+\; 2\,t_{\text{cs}}
$$

Where $t_{\text{queue}}$ is the waiting time inside the queue (Jackson's network formula approximates this in M/M/1 models).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Which IPC to Choose?

| Requirement | Best Mechanism | Why |
|-------------|---------------|-----|
| Pass a small fixed packet between 2 tasks | **Mailbox** | Simple, low overhead. |
| Pass variable-sized stream between N tasks | **Message Queue** | FIFO ordering, multiple slots. |
| Producer-Consumer with high throughput | **Shared Memory + Mutex** | Lowest latency (no kernel copy). |
| Synchronize access to a single peripheral | **Binary Semaphore / Mutex** | Standard mutual exclusion. |
| Coordinate multiple conditions at once | **Event Flags** | One primitive, many signals. |
| Wake one task from ISR | **Semaphore `give` from ISR** or **Task Notification** | ISR-safe variants exist. |
| Stream bytes between two tasks (e.g., logger) | **Pipe / Stream Buffer** | Byte-oriented, blocking reads. |
| Distributed embedded nodes | **Socket / CAN / UDP** | Network-transparent IPC. |
| Publish-Subscribe telemetry (IoT) | **Pub-Sub broker / DDS** | Decouples producers from consumers. |

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Classic KTU-Style Problem

> **Problem:** Two tasks $T_1$ and $T_2$ share a resource $R$ protected by a binary semaphore. $T_1$ has priority 5, $T_2$ has priority 2. A third task $T_3$ of priority 3 (which **does not** use $R$) preempts $T_1$ while it holds $R$. $T_2$ now requests $R$. Trace the schedule and compute blocking time with and without PIP.

### Solution Trace
1. $T_1$ (prio 5) runs, acquires $R$ at $t = 0$.
2. $T_3$ (prio 3) arrives, preempts $T_1$ (since $5 > 3$, wait — 5 *is* higher; assumption should be re-stated). **Re-stated:** Let priorities be $T_1=5, T_2=7, T_3=4$ where higher number = higher priority.
3. $T_2$ (prio 7) arrives, requests $R$, **blocks** on $R$ held by $T_1$.
4. $T_3$ (prio 4) preempts $T_1$ (since $4 < 5$ before inheritance).
5. $T_3$ runs for $T_3$ time units.
6. $T_1$ resumes, finishes CS, releases $R$.
7. $T_2$ acquires $R$ and runs.

**Without PIP:**
$$
B(T_2) \;=\; T_3 \;+\; \text{remainder}(T_1^{\text{cs}})
$$

**With PIP:** $T_1$ inherits $T_2$'s priority (7). $T_3$ (prio 4) **cannot preempt** $T_1$ now. $T_1$ finishes CS immediately.

$$
B^{\text{PIP}}(T_2) \;=\; T_1^{\text{cs remain}}
$$

**Conclusion:** PIP converts *unbounded* blocking into *bounded* blocking of duration equal to the remaining CS of the lower-priority task.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 High-Level Task Communication Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Task T1 Producer] -->|xQueueSend| Q1[Message Queue MQ1]
    A -->|xSemaphoreGive| S1[Semaphore S1]
    A -->|Mutex Lock M1| SH1[Shared Buffer BUF]
    Q1 -->|xQueueReceive| B[Task T2 Consumer]
    S1 -->|xSemaphoreTake| B
    B -->|Mutex Unlock M1| SH1
    SH1 --> A
    ISR[Hardware ISR] -->|FromISR Give| S1
    ISR -->|Direct notify| B
    EF1[Event Flag Group] -->|WaitBits| B
    A -->|SetBits| EF1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** $T_1$ is a producer task feeding $T_2$ via three parallel channels — a message queue (bulk data), a semaphore (event signal), and a shared buffer (fastest path) protected by a mutex. An ISR can also signal $T_2$ using the semaphore or task notification — both are ISR-safe in FreeRTOS.

---

## 4.2 Mailbox vs Message Queue — Comparative Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph MBOX[Mailbox - Single Slot]
        direction TB
        MB[Slot of Size S bytes]
        MB -->|overwrite if full| MB
    end
    subgraph MQ[Message Queue - N Slots]
        direction LR
        Q0[Slot 0]
        Q1[Slot 1]
        Q2[Slot 2]
        Qdots[...]
        QN[Slot N-1]
        Q0 --> Q1 --> Q2 --> Qdots --> QN
    end
    PROD[Producer Task] -->|post| MBOX
    CONS[Consumer Task] -->|pend| MBOX
    PROD -->|enqueue| MQ
    CONS -->|dequeue| MQ
```

---

## 4.3 Priority Inversion — Timeline Diagram (Without Solution)

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Inversion - Unbounded Blocking
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section T_H High Prio
    T_H blocked         :crit, 0, 8
    T_H running         :active, 8, 4
    section T_M Medium Prio
    T_M running         :active, 3, 4
    section T_L Low Prio
    T_L running         :0, 3
    T_L resumes CS      :active, 7, 1
```

**Reading the Gantt chart:** $T_L$ holds the resource from $t=0$ to $t=1$, gets preempted, $T_M$ runs from $t=3$ to $t=7$ (a 4-unit slice), then $T_L$ resumes and finally releases at $t=8$. $T_H$ is blocked the **entire** time from $t=0$ to $t=8$ — even though $T_H$ has the highest priority.

---

## 4.4 PIP Resolution — Same Scenario With Protocol

```mermaid
gantt
    title Priority Inheritance Protocol - Bounded Blocking
    dateFormat  X
    axisFormat  %S
    section T_H High Prio
    T_H blocked         :crit, 0, 1
    T_H running         :active, 1, 4
    section T_M Medium Prio
    T_M running         :active, 1, 0
    section T_L Low Prio
    T_L inherited prio  :active, 0, 1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key insight for KTU answers:** Notice that $T_M$'s bar is gone — it could not preempt $T_L$ because $T_L$ was inheriting $T_H$'s priority (7), which is greater than $T_M$'s (4). $T_H$ is blocked for only **1 unit** (the remainder of $T_L$'s critical section).

---

## 4.5 Deadlock — Circular Wait Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    T_A[Task A holds Res X] -->|waits for| T_B[Task B holds Res Y]
    T_B -->|waits for| T_A
    style T_A fill:#ffcccc
    style T_B fill:#ffcccc
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All **four Coffman conditions** are met here:
> 1. Mutual Exclusion ✓ (X and Y are non-sharable).
> 2. Hold-and-Wait ✓ (A holds X, waits for Y).
> 3. No Preemption ✓ (resources cannot be forcibly taken).
> 4. Circular Wait ✓ (A → B → A).
>
> **Break any one** to prevent deadlock. The most common solution in RTOS: impose a **total ordering** on resource acquisition.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model)]**
> **Differentiate between a Mailbox and a Message Queue in RTOS. State one real-time use case of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Mailbox | Message Queue |
|--------|---------|---------------|
| Capacity | **1 message** (single slot) | **N messages** (FIFO buffer) |
| Full-condition behavior | Typically **overwrites** old message or returns error | **Blocks sender** until slot frees |
| Use case | ISR-to-Task flag + small payload (e.g., "ADC sample ready") | Multi-message log buffer, command pipeline |

- **Mailbox example:** A temperature sensor ISR posts a single 4-byte reading into a mailbox; the display task pends on it.
- **Message Queue example:** A command parser task enqueues 10-character CLI commands; a worker task dequeues them in FIFO order.

*(1 mark for definition each + 1 mark for use case = 3 marks)*

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]**
> **What is a Mutex? How is it different from a Binary Semaphore?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- A **Mutex (MUT-ual EX-clusion)** is a locking primitive that ensures only one task can access a shared resource at a time.
- **Key differences:**
  1. **Ownership:** A mutex has an *owner* — only the task that locked it may unlock it. A binary semaphore has *no ownership*; any task may `give()`.
  2. **Priority Inheritance:** Mutexes typically support PIP to prevent priority inversion. Plain binary semaphores do not.
  3. **Recursive Locking:** A task may lock the same mutex multiple times (recursive mutex); not possible with binary semaphore.

*(1 mark each = 3 marks)*

---

## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Two sub-parts, typically **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with internal choice between the two main questions.

---

### ✏️ **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 (Model)]*

**Q. (a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **Producer–Consumer problem** in RTOS. Show how it is solved using **two counting semaphores and a mutex**. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Producer–Consumer Definition (1 mark):**
A classic synchronization problem where one or more *producer* tasks generate data items and place them into a bounded buffer, while one or more *consumer* tasks remove and process them. The buffer has finite capacity $N$.

**Need for synchronization (1 mark):**
- Producer must **wait** if buffer is full.
- Consumer must **wait** if buffer is empty.
- Only **one** task may access the buffer at a time (no torn writes).

**Solution with semaphores + mutex (3 marks):**
Let `empty = N` (counts free slots) and `full = 0` (counts filled slots), and `mutex = 1` (lock on buffer).

```
Producer Task:                  Consumer Task:
while (1) {                     while (1) {
    produce_item();                 wait(full);
    wait(empty);                    wait(mutex);
    wait(mutex);                    item = remove_item();
    put_item();                     signal(mutex);
    signal(mutex);                  signal(empty);
    signal(full);                   consume_item();
}                               }
```

**Explanation of each primitive (1 mark):**
- `wait(empty)` blocks the producer when buffer is full.
- `wait(full)` blocks the consumer when buffer is empty.
- `wait(mutex)` / `signal(mutex)` ensure atomic update of the buffer index.
- `signal(full)` after producer enqueues wakes up the consumer.
- `signal(empty)` after consumer dequeues wakes up the producer.

**Diagram (1 mark):** See Section 4.1 — a flowchart of producer, queue, and consumer with semaphores and mutex.

---

**Q. (b)** Explain **Priority Inversion** with a real-time example. Show how the **Priority Inheritance Protocol (PIP)** resolves it. Derive the worst-case blocking time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Priority inversion is a scheduling anomaly where a **higher-priority** task is forced to wait for the execution of a **lower-priority** task that holds a shared resource, while **medium-priority** tasks (unrelated to the resource) preempt the low-priority task, *indirectly* delaying the high-priority task indefinitely.

**Real-time example (1 mark):**
- $T_H$: Flight controller reading GPS via UART.
- $T_L$: Logger writing to SD card (which also uses UART DMA).
- $T_M$: Telemetry updater (unrelated to UART).
- $T_L$ acquires UART, $T_H$ preempts but blocks on UART, $T_M$ preempts $T_L$ for an unbounded time → $T_H$ misses its 10 ms deadline → **Mars Pathfinder 1997 bug**.

**Without PIP — timeline (2 marks):** See Section 4.3 Gantt chart. Derive:
$$B_{\max}(T_H) = \sum_{j} T_{M_j} + T_L^{\text{remainder}} \quad \text{(unbounded)}$$

**With PIP — resolution (2 marks):** See Section 4.4 Gantt chart.
$$B_{\max}^{\text{PIP}}(T_H) = T_L^{\text{cs remain}} \quad \text{(bounded)}$$

When $T_H$ blocks on $R$ held by $T_L$, the kernel **transiently boosts** $T_L$'s effective priority to $P_H$. Medium-priority tasks can no longer preempt $T_L$. $T_L$ completes its critical section, releases $R$, then $T_H$ acquires $R$ immediately. After release, $T_L$'s priority reverts to its base value.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** PIP bounds the blocking time, restoring real-time determinism.

---

### ✏️ **QUESTION B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Model)]*

**Q. (a)** Explain the following task communication mechanisms with suitable diagrams: **(7 marks)**
   1. **Shared Memory** with mutex protection.
   2. **Event Flag Groups**.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Shared Memory (3 marks):**
- Two or more tasks access a common **global buffer or memory-mapped peripheral register**.
- **Fastest** IPC because no kernel data copy is involved.
- **Risk:** Race conditions, torn reads/writes, cache coherency issues in multicore.
- **Solution:** Protect every access with a **mutex** (or disable interrupts for very short CS in single-core).
- **Code pattern:**
  ```
  taskA: lock(m); buffer[i] = value; unlock(m);
  taskB: lock(m); value = buffer[i];     unlock(m);
  ```
- **Diagram:** See Section 4.1 — `SH1 [Shared Buffer BUF]` node connecting two tasks via `Mutex Lock M1`.

**2. Event Flag Groups (4 marks):**
- A **32-bit (or wider) bitmask** stored in the kernel. Each bit represents an event.
- Tasks can `WaitBits` with an AND-mask (all bits required) or OR-mask (any bit sufficient).
- Tasks or ISRs can `SetBits` to signal events.
- Tasks can `ClearBits` on wakeup (auto-clear or manual).
- **FreeRTOS API:** `xEventGroupCreate`, `xEventGroupSetBits`, `xEventGroupWaitBits`.
- **Example:** A motor-control task waits for `bit0 = "sensor ready"` AND `bit1 = "command received"` before driving the motor. The sensor ISR sets `bit0`; the UART receiver task sets `bit1`.
- **Diagram:**
```
   [ISR] ---SetBit bit0---> [EventGroup EFG] <---SetBit bit1--- [UART Task]
                                  |
                              WaitBits(bit0 AND bit1)
                                  |
                            [MotorTask runs]
```

---

**Q. (b)** Describe the **four Coffman conditions** for deadlock. Explain how the **Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP)** prevents deadlock and bounds blocking time. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Four Coffman Conditions (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**
1. **Mutual Exclusion:** At least one resource is held in a non-sharable mode.
2. **Hold-and-Wait:** A task holding resources is waiting to acquire additional resources held by others.
3. **No Preemption:** Resources cannot be forcibly taken from a holding task; they must be released voluntarily.
4. **Circular Wait:** A circular chain of tasks exists, each waiting for a resource held by the next.

*All four must hold simultaneously for deadlock. Breaking any one prevents it.*

**Priority Ceiling Protocol (PCP) — Mechanism (3 marks):**
- Each resource $R$ is assigned a **ceiling** $\lceil R \rceil = \max$ priority of any task that may lock $R$.
- A task $T_i$ may lock a resource $R$ **only if** $P(T_i)$ is **strictly greater** than the ceilings of all resources currently locked by *other* tasks.
- This **statically prevents circular wait** because a task can never wait for a resource held by a lower-priority task — if it could, it would have had higher priority and could have locked it first.
- **Blocking bound:** A task $T_i$ can be blocked at most once, for at most the length of the **longest critical section** of any lower-priority task that accesses a resource whose ceiling exceeds $P(T_i)$:
$$B_{\max}^{\text{PCP}}(T_i) = \max_{R_k \,:\, \lceil R_k \rceil > P(T_i)} \text{cs}(R_k)$$

**Comparison with PIP (2 marks):**

| Property | PIP | PCP |
|----------|-----|-----|
| Deadlock prevention | ❌ (only inversion) | ✅ |
| Blocking bound | Sum of CS | Max of one CS |
| Runtime cost | Medium (priority boost on block) | Low (static check at lock) |
| Used in | μC/OS-II, FreeRTOS mutexes | VxWorks, POSIX |

**Conclusion:** PCP is **stronger** than PIP — it prevents both deadlock and chained blocking, at the cost of static priority assignment to resources.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Lose Marks on Task Communication Questions:**
>
> 1. **Conflating "Mutex" with "Binary Semaphore"** (–1 to –2 marks). Always state *ownership* as the differentiator.
> 2. **Drawing the Gantt chart without a time axis or task labels** (–1 mark). Examiners want **identifiable tasks** ($T_H, T_M, T_L$) and **time markers**.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention that PIP is "transient"** (–1 mark). The priority is *temporarily* raised and *reverts* after `release()`.
> 4. **Mixing up PCP and PIP formulas** (–1 mark). PCP blocks ≤ *one* CS; PIP blocks ≤ *sum* of CS of lower-priority tasks.
> 5. **Writing "message passing is always faster than shared memory"** (–2 marks). The opposite is true — shared memory is faster but unsafe without protection.
> 6. **Omitting ISR-safety** in answers about semaphores and queues (–1 mark). KTU specifically tests `FromISR` variants.
> 7. **Skipping the diagram** in Producer–Consumer answers (–1 mark). Even a rough box-and-arrow sketch earns partial credit.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Task Communication = Data Exchange + Synchronization.** Don't separate them in definitions.
- [x] **Mailbox = 1 slot; Message Queue = N slots.** Know the overwrite-vs-block behavior.
- [x] **Mutex ≠ Binary Semaphore.** Mutex has *ownership* and *priority inheritance*.
- [x] **Priority Inversion** has **three tasks**: High (blocks on resource), Medium (unrelated, preempts Low), Low (holds resource).
- [x] **PIP** = *Dynamic* priority boost on the holder. Bounded blocking = remaining CS of $T_L$.
- [x] **PCP** = *Static* ceiling on each resource. Prevents **deadlock** and *chained blocking*. Blocking = max of one CS.
- [x] **Four Coffman conditions** must *all* hold for deadlock. Break any one to prevent it.
- [x] **Event Flag Groups** = bitmask; supports AND/OR wait. FreeRTOS `xEventGroupWaitBits`.
- [x] **ISR-safe primitives** are critical: `xQueueSendFromISR`, `xSemaphoreGiveFromISR`, `xTaskNotifyFromISR`.
- [x] **Shared Memory** is *fastest* but *unsafe* without mutex or interrupt disable.
- [x] **Pipe** = byte-stream, unidirectional, between two tasks. Different from a message queue (which is message-oriented).
- [x] **Deadlock vs Priority Inversion** — *different problems*. Inversion is delay; deadlock is permanent stall.
- [x] **Mars Pathfinder (1997)** is the canonical KTU real-world example. Mention it in priority-inversion answers for bonus marks.
- [x] **Memory barriers / volatile** are needed in shared-memory IPC on multicore embedded systems (ARM Cortex-M7, etc.) — not just mutex.
- [x] **Real-world IPCs to name-drop:** AUTOSAR COM, ROS 2 DDS, OPC-UA Pub/Sub, POSIX mqueue, FreeRTOS Stream Buffers.

---

> **End of Module 3 — Task Communication Notes**
